topics.title: Introduction and Relevance of Biomedical Signal Processing; Biomedical Signals - ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Introduction and Relevance of Biomedical Signal Processing; Biomedical Signals - ECG # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Introduction to Biomedical Signal Processing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > *Biomedical Signal Processing (BMSP) is the systematic application of signal processing theories, algorithms, and statistical methods to bio-electric and bio-acoustic signals originating from the human body, in order to extract clinically meaningful information for diagnosis, monitoring, and therapy.* In other words, it is the **engineering discipline** that sits at the intersection of: $$\text{Medicine} \;\cap\; \text{Electrical Engineering} \;\cap\; \text{Computer Science}$$ The signals of interest are non-stationary (their statistical properties change with time), extremely low in amplitude (often in the **microvolt to millivolt** range), and corrupted by multiple noise sources (powerline interference, baseline wander, motion artifacts). > [!NOTE] > **Key Idea:** The body is essentially a *biological circuit*. Every heartbeat, every brain wave, and every muscle contraction produces measurable electrical voltages — biomedical signal processing is the art of *listening to* and *interpreting* these voltages. ### Conceptual Analogy: The Stethoscope of the 21st Century Imagine a doctor using a stethoscope. The stethoscope converts acoustic vibrations from the chest into audible sound. **Biomedical signal processing is the modern, electronic "digital stethoscope"** — except instead of listening with the ear, we: 1. **Acquire** the electrical signal using surface or implantable electrodes. 2. **Amplify** it (since body signals are tiny). 3. **Filter** it to remove noise. 4. **Analyze** it using mathematical transforms (Fourier, Wavelet, etc.). 5. **Display/Classify** it for clinical interpretation. | Traditional Stethoscope | Biomedical Signal Processing | |------------------------|------------------------------| | Acoustic energy in | Electrical/magnetic energy in | | Ear hears the signal | Algorithm processes the signal | | Qualitative diagnosis | Quantitative, automated diagnosis | --- ## 1.2 What is a Biomedical Signal? A **biomedical signal** is a time-varying quantity (voltage, pressure, flow, temperature) that carries information about a physiological process. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Sörnmo & Laguna Convention):** > *A biomedical signal is a measurable bio-physical quantity, as a function of time (or space), that directly or indirectly represents the functional state of a living system.* ### Broad Classification of Biomedical Signals | Origin | Examples | Typical Amplitude | Frequency Band | |--------|----------|------------------|----------------| | **Bio-electric** | ECG, EEG, EMG, EOG, ERG | 0.001 – 5 mV | 0.05 – 10 kHz | | **Bio-mechanical** | Blood pressure, PCG (heart sounds) | mmHg / arbitrary | 0.1 – 2 kHz | | **Bio-chemical** | pH, pO₂, glucose | mg/dL | DC – a few Hz | | **Bio-magnetic** | MCG, MEG | fT to pT | DC – 1 kHz | | **Bio-acoustic** | PCG, speech | Pa | 20 Hz – 20 kHz | | **Bio-optical** | Pulse oximetry (PPG) | % SpO₂ | 0.5 – 5 Hz | The most clinically important and academically foundational of these is the **Electrocardiogram (ECG)**, which we study next. --- ## 1.3 The Electrocardiogram (ECG) — Core Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **ECG Definition (Goldberger / KTU Standard):** > *The Electrocardiogram (ECG or EKG, from the German "Elektrokardiogramm") is a non-invasive recording of the electrical activity of the heart over time, captured at the body surface using skin electrodes. It represents the *summated electrical depolarization and repolarization potentials* of cardiac muscle cells, measured as a voltage difference between two points on the body.* > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic — What ECG actually measures:** It does **NOT** measure mechanical contraction. It measures the **electrical trigger** that *causes* the contraction. The heart is essentially a bio-electric pump. ### Conceptual Analogy: "The Heart as a Dipole Battery" Picture the heart as a **3-volt battery** floating inside a saline (salt-water) medium (the torso, which conducts electricity). The "current" flowing from the positive pole (depolarized tissue) to the negative pole (resting tissue) creates an **electric field** that reaches the skin surface. The ECG electrodes simply tap into this field at the body surface — much like placing two voltmeter probes on the outside of a tank of water to sense a battery hidden inside. - **Depolarization** = activation / contraction trigger (battery ON). - **Repolarization** = recovery / relaxation (battery OFF and reset). - **ECG signal** = the voltage sensed at the skin surface during these events. ### The Standard 12-Lead ECG (Big Picture) A "lead" in ECG terminology is **not a wire** — it is a **viewpoint** of the heart's electrical activity from a specific angle. The standard clinical ECG uses **12 leads** (10 electrodes, 12 different viewpoints): | Lead Group | Count | Names | Type | |------------|-------|-------|------| | Bipolar Limb | 3 | Lead I, II, III | Einthoven's Triangle | | Augmented Unipolar Limb | 3 | aVR, aVL, aVF | Goldberger's | | Precordial (Chest) | 6 | V1, V2, V3, V4, V5, V6 | Wilson's Central Terminal | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The 12-lead ECG is built from only **10 physical electrodes**. The same 4 limb electrodes (RA, LA, RL, LL) are *algebraically combined* to form the augmented leads — clever signal processing embedded in the hardware! --- ## 1.4 Why Study Biomedical Signal Processing? (Relevance) > [!TIP] > **Five Engineering Reasons it Matters:** > 1. **Lives depend on it** — automated arrhythmia detectors in ICU monitors, defibrillators, pacemakers. > 2. **Signals are extremely weak** — requires ultra-low-noise analog front-end design. > 3. **Artifacts dominate** — real signals are buried in noise; *signal processing is the only way out*. > 4. **Huge data volumes** — Holter monitors record 24–48 hours × 1000 Hz × 12 leads = GB-scale data needing compression. > 5. **AI/ML revolution** — modern ECG analysis uses deep learning for *predicting* disease from raw waveforms. The relevance is summarized in the engineering chain: $$\text{Physiological Event} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Sensor} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Amplifier} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ADC} \;\rightarrow\; \text{DSP Algorithm} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Clinical Decision}$$ Every arrow in this chain involves a signal processing decision. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The ECG Waveform — Detailed Anatomy A single cardiac cycle produces a **P-QRS-T-U** complex. Each deflection corresponds to a specific electrophysiological event. ``` ECG Waveform (One Cardiac Cycle) P wave QRS complex T wave ___ ___ ___ / \ / | \ / \ ____/ \____ ____/ | \____ _________ / \___ | \ / S | \/ Q R ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Five Cardinal Waves & Their Meaning:** | Wave / Segment | Electrophysiological Event | Typical Duration | Typical Amplitude | |----------------|---------------------------|------------------|-------------------| | **P wave** | Atrial depolarization (both atria contract) | 80 – 100 ms | 0.10 – 0.25 mV | | **PR Segment** | Conduction delay at AV node | ~ 50 ms | Isoelectric | | **PR Interval** | Start of P → Start of QRS | 120 – 200 ms | — | | **Q wave** | Septal depolarization (L→R) | < 40 ms | < 0.25 mV (25% of R) | | **R wave** | Main ventricular depolarization | — | 0.5 – 2.5 mV (tallest) | | **S wave** | Late ventricular depolarization | — | Variable | | **QRS Complex** | Total ventricular depolarization | 80 – 120 ms | ~ 1 mV (peak) | | **ST Segment** | All ventricles depolarized (plateau) | 80 – 120 ms | Isoelectric | | **T wave** | Ventricular repolarization | 160 ms | 0.10 – 0.5 mV | | **U wave** | Purkinje repolarization (sometimes seen) | ~ 180 ms | < 0.1 mV | | **QT Interval** | Total ventricular systole (electrical) | 350 – 440 ms | — | | **RR Interval** | One complete cardiac cycle | 0.6 – 1.0 s (60–100 bpm) | — | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic:** "**P**lease **Q**uietly **R**eplace **S**ome **T**ea" — but remember, **atrial repolarization (Ta wave) is hidden inside the QRS** because the ventricles are so much more massive. --- ## 2.2 Einthoven's Triangle & the Bipolar Leads Willem Einthoven (Nobel 1924) modeled the torso as a **2D plane** and the heart as a dipole at the center. He placed electrodes: - **RA** (Right Arm) — positive in Lead III - **LA** (Left Arm) — positive in Lead I - **LL** (Left Leg) — positive in Lead II The three bipolar leads are *voltage differences* between pairs: $$\boxed{\;V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}\;}$$ $$\boxed{\;V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}\;}$$ $$\boxed{\;V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}\;}$$ These satisfy the famous **Einthoven's Law**: $$\boxed{\;V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}\;}$$ This is mathematically equivalent to **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** applied to the triangle (RA → LA → LL → RA), since the body is a conductive medium at steady state. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Einthoven's Equilateral Triangle Assumption:** The three limb electrodes form an **equilateral triangle** around the heart, and the heart is at the **electrical centroid**. Reality deviates by < 15%, but for clinical purposes, this is excellent. --- ## 2.3 The Augmented Leads (Goldberger) — Inline Signal Processing The augmented leads are unipolar — they measure the voltage at one limb electrode *with respect to a reference* formed by the other two. $$\boxed{\;V_{aVR} = V_{RA} - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{LA} + V_{LL})\;}$$ $$\boxed{\;V_{aVL} = V_{LA} - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{RA} + V_{LL})\;}$$ $$\boxed{\;V_{aVF} = V_{LL} - \tfrac{1}{2}(V_{RA} + V_{LA})\;}$$ > [!NOTE] > The augmented leads are *augmented* by a factor of **1.5** in the actual hardware (Goldberger's design), giving a clean calibration: the augmented lead's amplitude matches the bipolar leads' amplitude for a balanced cardiac vector. --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — ECG & Biomedical Signal Processing > [!TIP] > **Master this table — these are the only formulas needed for Module 1 KTU questions.** | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Notes / Units | |---|-------------------|-----------|---------------| | 1 | Heart Rate (from RR interval) | $HR = \dfrac{60}{RR_{\text{(sec)}}}$ | beats per minute (bpm) | | 2 | Heart Rate (small-box method) | $HR = \dfrac{1500}{N_{\text{small boxes}}}$ | $N$ = # of 1-mm boxes between two R-peaks | | 3 | Heart Rate (large-box method) | $HR = \dfrac{300}{N_{\text{large boxes}}}$ | 1 large box = 5 mm = 0.20 s @ 25 mm/s | | 4 | QRS axis (mean electrical axis) | $\text{Axis} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{\sum QRS_{aVF}}{\sum QRS_I}\right)$ | degrees (°) | | 5 | Einthoven's law | $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ | Kirchhoff's voltage law | | 6 | Cardiac cycle duration | $T_{cycle} = \dfrac{60}{HR}$ | seconds | | 7 | Powerline interference freq. (India) | $f_{\text{PLI}} = 50\,\text{Hz}$ | add 60 Hz for US | | 8 | ECG main frequency content | $0.05\,\text{Hz} \leq f \leq 150\,\text{Hz}$ | QRS energy concentrated in 10–50 Hz | | 9 | Nyquist sampling rate (ECG) | $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max} = 300\,\text{Hz}$ | Clinical standard: **500 Hz** | | 10 | ADC resolution requirement | $\text{ENOB} \geq 12\,\text{bits}$ | For ≥ 5 μV resolution on ±5 mV range | | 11 | Standard ECG paper speed | $v = 25\,\text{mm/s}$ | 1 mm = 0.04 s (horiz.), 1 mm = 0.1 mV (vert.) | | 12 | Mean QRS vector magnitude | $\vert \vec{M} \vert = \sqrt{QRS_I^2 + QRS_{aVF}^2}$ | mV | | 13 | Cardiac output (related concept) | $CO = HR \times SV$ | L/min, $SV$ = stroke volume | | 14 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | dB | | 15 | Powerline interference period (50 Hz) | $T_{\text{PLI}} = \dfrac{1}{50} = 20\,\text{ms}$ | ms | --- ## 2.5 Frequency Content & Noise — Why Filtering is Essential The ECG signal occupies a wide band, but the **diagnostically useful energy** is narrow: | Wave | Dominant Frequency | |------|-------------------| | P wave | 0.5 – 10 Hz | | QRS | 10 – 50 Hz | | T wave | 1 – 7 Hz | | Noise Source | Frequency | Remedy | |--------------|-----------|--------| | **Baseline wander** (respiration, body movement) | 0.05 – 0.5 Hz | High-pass filter (cutoff ≈ 0.5 Hz) | | **Powerline interference** (50/60 Hz pickup) | 50 Hz (or 60 Hz) | Notch filter @ 50 Hz, driven-right-leg circuit | | **EMG (muscle) noise** | 20 – 500 Hz | Low-pass filter (cutoff ≈ 150 Hz) | | **Electrode contact noise** | Broad, transient | Good skin prep, Ag/AgCl electrodes | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Clinical ECG Bandwidth:** **0.5 Hz – 150 Hz** (diagnostic); **0.5 – 40 Hz** (monitoring). The 0.5 Hz HPF removes baseline wander but slightly distorts the ST segment — a classic engineering trade-off! --- ## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Concepts The signals and formulas above are not academic. They are literally the building blocks of: 1. **ICU Patient Monitors** — real-time QRS detection → heart rate alarm. 2. **Automated External Defibrillators (AEDs)** — R-peak detection to synchronize shock delivery. 3. **Pacemakers** — sense intrinsic P/QRS, deliver pacing pulse if absent. 4. **Holter monitors** — 24-hour ambulatory ECG with automated arrhythmia classification. 5. **AI-based ECG diagnostics** — Deep learning models (e.g., CNNs) trained on 12-lead ECGs can detect *asymptomatic* left ventricular dysfunction, hyperkalemia, even age & sex — all from raw waveforms. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heart Rate from RR Interval > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Problem (KTU-style):** A patient's ECG tracing (recorded at standard 25 mm/s) shows two consecutive R peaks separated by **20 mm of paper**. Compute the heart rate. ### Step 1 — Convert paper distance to time The paper moves at $v = 25\;\text{mm/s}$. A 20 mm separation therefore represents a time interval: $$t = \dfrac{\text{distance}}{\text{speed}} = \dfrac{20\,\text{mm}}{25\,\text{mm/s}} = 0.8\,\text{s}$$ ### Step 2 — Recognize this as the RR interval By definition, the **R–R interval ($RR$)** is the time between two consecutive R peaks and equals one full cardiac cycle duration $T_{\text{cycle}}$: $$RR = T_{\text{cycle}} = 0.8\,\text{s}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the heart-rate formula $$HR = \dfrac{60}{RR_{\text{(sec)}}} = \dfrac{60}{0.8} = 75\;\text{bpm}$$ ### Step 4 — Cross-check with the small-box method 20 mm = 20 small boxes (since 1 small box = 1 mm). Using formula (2) from the cheat sheet: $$HR = \dfrac{1500}{N_{\text{small}}} = \dfrac{1500}{20} = 75\;\text{bpm} \;\;\checkmark$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Nyquist Rate for ECG Sampling ### Problem Determine the minimum sampling frequency for lossless digital acquisition of an ECG signal, given that the highest clinically significant frequency component is **150 Hz**. ### Step 1 — State Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem A band-limited signal with maximum frequency $f_{\max}$ can be reconstructed from its samples *if and only if* the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies: $$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$$ ### Step 2 — Substitute ECG parameters For ECG: $f_{\max} = 150\,\text{Hz}$ (diagnostic bandwidth). Therefore: $$f_s^{\min} = 2 \times 150 = 300\,\text{Hz}$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the engineering safety factor (oversampling) In practice, a **factor of ~1.7×** oversampling is used to allow for realizable anti-aliasing filter roll-off. Hence clinical ECG systems use: $$f_s^{\text{clinical}} = 500\,\text{Hz}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is why every KTU ECG problem states "sampling rate is 500 Hz" — it is the *de-facto* standard. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Einthoven's Law from Kirchhoff's Voltage Law Starting from the three lead definitions: $$V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA}$$ $$V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA}$$ $$V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA}$$ ### Step 1 — Add Lead I and Lead III $$V_I + V_{III} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})$$ ### Step 2 — Cancel $V_{LA}$ terms $$V_I + V_{III} = -V_{RA} + V_{LL}$$ ### Step 3 — Identify the right-hand side $$-V_{RA} + V_{LL} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} = V_{II}$$ ### Step 4 — Conclude $$\boxed{\;V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}\;}$$ Geometrically, this is just **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** on the closed loop **RA → LA → LL → RA** in Einthoven's triangle. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Mean QRS Axis Calculation ### Problem Given a patient's ECG, the net QRS deflection (R minus Q minus S) is measured as: - Lead I: $\sum QRS_I = +1.2\,\text{mV}$ - Lead aVF: $\sum QRS_{aVF} = +0.8\,\text{mV}$ Find the **mean electrical axis** of the heart in the frontal plane. ### Step 1 — Construct the cardiac vector The two orthogonal components (Einthoven's reference system) are: $$X = \sum QRS_I = +1.2\,\text{mV} \quad \text{(horizontal axis)}$$ $$Y = \sum QRS_{aVF} = +0.8\,\text{mV} \quad \text{(vertical axis)}$$ ### Step 2 — Apply the arctan formula $$\text{Axis} = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{Y}{X}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{0.8}{1.2}\right)$$ $$\text{Axis} = \arctan(0.6667) \approx 33.69^{\circ}$$ ### Step 3 — Assign quadrant Both $X > 0$ and $Y > 0$ → vector in **Quadrant I (left, inferior)** → **Normal axis range** ($-30^{\circ}$ to $+90^{\circ}$). ### Step 4 — State clinical conclusion > The mean QRS axis is **+34°**, which lies well within the **normal range ($-30^{\circ}$ to $+90^{\circ}$)**. No axis deviation is present. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Typical ECG Parameter Values | Parameter | Symbol | Normal Range | Clinical Significance if Abnormal | |-----------|--------|--------------|------------------------------------| | Heart Rate | $HR$ | 60 – 100 bpm | < 60 = bradycardia; > 100 = tachycardia | | P wave duration | $t_P$ | 80 – 100 ms | > 120 ms = left atrial enlargement | | PR interval | $t_{PR}$ | 120 – 200 ms | > 200 ms = first-degree AV block | | QRS duration | $t_{QRS}$ | 80 – 120 ms | > 120 ms = bundle branch block | | QT interval | $t_{QT}$ | 350 – 440 ms | Prolonged → torsades de pointes risk | | QTc (corrected) | $QT_c$ | 350 – 450 ms | Bazett's: $QT_c = QT/\sqrt{RR}$ | | Q wave amplitude | $a_Q$ | < 25% of R wave | Deep Q = prior myocardial infarction | | ST elevation | — | < 1 mm (limb), < 2 mm (V1–V3) | Acute MI | | T wave | — | Upright, asymmetric | Inverted T = ischemia | --- ## 3.6 Bazett's QT Correction — Derivation of the Formula QT interval naturally varies with heart rate. Bazett (1920) proposed normalizing it to a heart rate of 60 bpm: $$\boxed{\;QT_c = \dfrac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}\;}$$ where $QT$ is in seconds and $RR$ is in seconds. Derivation logic: - $RR$ is in seconds; for HR = 60, $RR = 1$ s, so $QT_c = QT$. - For HR = 120, $RR = 0.5$ s, so $QT_c = QT/\sqrt{0.5} \approx 1.41 \cdot QT$ — corrects for the natural shortening of QT at high HR. > [!NOTE] > Bazett's correction is *rate-dependent* and slightly overcorrects at high rates. The Fridericia formula ($QT_c = QT / RR^{1/3}$) is sometimes used, but Bazett remains the KTU standard. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Biomedical Signal Acquisition Chain This shows the full signal path from the patient's body to the display, illustrating *where* signal processing occurs. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Patient Body
Physiological Source] --> B[Electrodes
Sensor Transducer] B --> C[Pre-amplifier
Instrumentation Amp] C --> D[Analog Filters
HPF 0.5 Hz, LPF 150 Hz, Notch 50 Hz] D --> E[ADC
Sampling 500 Hz, 12-bit] E --> F[Digital Signal Processor
DSP Microcontroller] F --> G1[Display / Monitor] F --> G2[Feature Extraction] F --> G3[Storage / Telemetry] F --> G4[Alarms / Decision Logic] G2 --> H[Classification Algorithm
Arrhythmia Detection] H --> I[Clinical Decision Support] style A fill:#ffd9d9,stroke:#cc0000 style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38f00 style D fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38f00 style E fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d style F fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394 style H fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394 style I fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of the diagram:** > - **Analog domain** (B → D): amplification, anti-aliasing filtering. > - **Conversion** (E): ADC converts continuous-time signal to discrete samples. > - **Digital domain** (F → I): the *real* signal processing — QRS detection, HR computation, noise cancellation, feature extraction, classification. > - This is the **physical realization of biomedical signal processing** — every clinical device is a variation of this chain. --- ## 4.2 Einthoven's Triangle & 12-Lead ECG — Spatial Architecture ```mermaid graph TB RA[RA Right Arm]:::limb LA[LA Left Arm]:::limb LL[LL Left Leg]:::limb H((Heart
Electrical Dipole)):::heart V1[V1]:::chest V2[V2]:::chest V3[V3]:::chest V4[V4]:::chest V5[V5]:::chest V6[V6]:::chest RA -- Lead I --> LA RA -- Lead II --> LL LA -- Lead III --> LL RA --- H LA --- H LL --- H V1 --- H V2 --- H V3 --- H V4 --- H V5 --- H V6 --- H classDef limb fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38f00 classDef heart fill:#ea9999,stroke:#990000 classDef chest fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Lead Vector Map (frontal plane angles):** > - Lead I = **0°**, Lead II = **+60°**, Lead III = **+120°** > - aVR = **−150°**, aVL = **−30°**, aVF = **+90°** > - V1 = **+110° (horizontal)**, V6 = **0° (horizontal)** — chest leads sweep in the *horizontal* (transverse) plane. --- ## 4.3 ECG Waveform & Frequency Spectrum — Functional Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TIME["Time-Domain Signal"] A1[P wave
0.5 to 10 Hz] A2[PR segment
isoelectric] A3[QRS complex
10 to 50 Hz] A4[ST segment
isoelectric] A5[T wave
1 to 7 Hz] end subgraph FREQ["Frequency-Domain Content"] F1[LF Band
below 5 Hz] F2[MF Band
5 to 25 Hz] F3[HF Band
25 to 150 Hz] end A1 --> F1 A3 --> F2 A3 --> F3 A5 --> F1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bandwidth Insight:** The **QRS complex dominates the high-frequency content** of the ECG (10–50 Hz). This is *why* a 50 Hz notch filter must be carefully designed — too wide a notch will distort the QRS morphology and mask pathology! --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1a, 3 marks] **Define a biomedical signal. List any four examples with their typical frequency ranges.** **Model Answer:** A *biomedical signal* is a measurable bio-physical quantity, as a function of time, that represents the functional state of a living system. Four examples: | Signal | Frequency Range | |--------|-----------------| | ECG (Electrocardiogram) | 0.05 – 150 Hz | | EEG (Electroencephalogram) | 0.5 – 100 Hz | | EMG (Electromyogram) | 20 – 500 Hz | | PCG (Phonocardiogram) | 20 – 2000 Hz | These signals are non-stationary, low-amplitude (μV–mV), and contaminated by multiple noise sources — necessitating dedicated signal processing techniques. > **[Total: 3 marks = 1 (defn) + 2 (table)]** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Q1b, 3 marks] **State Einthoven's law. Mention its physical significance.** **Model Answer:** **Einthoven's Law:** The algebraic sum of the voltages of Lead I and Lead III equals the voltage of Lead II. $$V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$$ **Physical Significance:** It is a direct consequence of **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law** applied to the closed loop formed by the three limb electrodes (RA, LA, LL) which, by Einthoven's assumption, lie on an equilateral triangle with the heart at its electrical center. Clinically, it provides a built-in **consistency check** — if a measured $V_{II}$ does not equal $V_I + V_{III}$, the electrode placement or machine calibration is faulty. > **[Total: 3 marks = 1 (formula) + 1 (significance) + 1 (Kirchhoff link)]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- ### Question A — [14 Marks] #### [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Predicted Pattern)] **(a) Draw the ECG waveform of one cardiac cycle. Label all the waves, segments, and intervals. For each wave, state the corresponding electrophysiological event and the typical duration. (7 marks)** **(b) Explain the standard 12-lead ECG system. With a neat diagram, describe Einthoven's triangle and derive Einthoven's law. (7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Q.A (a) **ECG Waveform (One Cardiac Cycle):** ``` P wave QRS Complex T wave ___ _ _ ___ / \ / \ / \ / \ U (sometimes) / \ / \/ \ / \ / \__/ \____________/ \____ \ / \_/ S ``` **Labelled Annotations:** | Wave / Interval | Electrophysiological Event | Duration (ms) | |-----------------|---------------------------|---------------| | P wave | Atrial depolarization (SA node → both atria) | 80 – 100 | | PR segment | AV nodal conduction delay (isoelectric) | ~ 50 | | PR interval | Start of P → Start of QRS (atrial + AV delay) | 120 – 200 | | Q wave | Initial septal depolarization (L → R) | < 40 | | R wave | Main ventricular depolarization | — | | S wave | Late basal ventricular depolarization | — | | QRS complex | Total ventricular depolarization | 80 – 120 | | ST segment | All ventricles depolarized (plateau phase) | 80 – 120 | | T wave | Ventricular repolarization | ~ 160 | | QT interval | Total ventricular electrical systole | 350 – 440 | | U wave | Purkinje fiber repolarization (often invisible) | ~ 180 | **Key Notes for the Examiner:** - The *atrial repolarization* (Ta wave) is **NOT visible** because it is buried within the much larger QRS complex. - The QRS is tall (1 mV) because the ventricles are a much larger muscle mass than the atria. > **[7 marks = 1 (drawing) + 4 (table) + 2 (narrative)]** --- ### Model Solution to Q.A (b) **The Standard 12-Lead ECG:** The 12-lead ECG uses **10 physical electrodes** to derive **12 distinct "views"** of the heart's electrical activity: - **3 Bipolar limb leads** (Einthoven): I, II, III - **3 Augmented unipolar limb leads** (Goldberger): aVR, aVL, aVF - **6 Precordial (chest) leads** (Wilson): V1 – V6 **Einthoven's Triangle:** Willem Einthoven (1903) proposed that: 1. The three limb electrodes — **RA, LA, LL** — form an **equilateral triangle** with the heart at its electrical centroid. 2. The body acts as a **uniform volume conductor**. 3. The three sides of the triangle represent the three bipolar leads. ``` LA /\ / \ Lead III / \ Lead I / \ / HEART \ / \ /____________\ RA LL \ / \ / Lead II / ``` **Derivation of Einthoven's Law:** By definition: $$V_I = V_{LA} - V_{RA} \qquad (1)$$ $$V_{II} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} \qquad (2)$$ $$V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{LA} \qquad (3)$$ Adding (1) and (3): $$V_I + V_{III} = (V_{LA} - V_{RA}) + (V_{LL} - V_{LA})$$ The $V_{LA}$ terms cancel: $$V_I + V_{III} = V_{LL} - V_{RA} = V_{II}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\,V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}\,}$$ **Physical Meaning:** This is Kirchhoff's Voltage Law applied to the closed loop *RA → LA → LL → RA* in Einthoven's triangle. Clinically it serves as a **calibration check** for the ECG machine. > **[7 marks = 2 (12-lead overview) + 2 (triangle) + 3 (derivation)]** --- ### Question B — [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] #### [KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Predicted Pattern)] **(a) Classify biomedical signals. For each category, give two examples and state the typical amplitude and frequency range. (7 marks)** **(b) Discuss the relevance of biomedical signal processing in modern healthcare. Explain with a neat block diagram the general architecture of a biomedical signal acquisition and processing system. (7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution to Q.B (a) **Classification of Biomedical Signals:** Biomedical signals are classified based on the **physical origin** of the underlying physiological event. | Category | Description | Example 1 | Example 2 | |----------|-------------|-----------|-----------| | **Bio-electric** | Electrical potentials from excitable cells | ECG (0.5 – 5 mV, 0.05 – 150 Hz) | EEG (5 – 300 μV, 0.5 – 100 Hz) | | **Bio-mechanical** | Pressure, force, displacement | Blood pressure (mmHg, DC – 60 Hz) | Respiration airflow (L/s, 0.1 – 20 Hz) | | **Bio-chemical** | Concentration of analytes | Blood glucose (mg/dL, DC) | pH of blood (DC) | | **Bio-magnetic** | Magnetic fields from ionic currents | MCG (fT – pT, DC – 1 kHz) | MEG (fT, DC – 1 kHz) | | **Bio-acoustic** | Sound from biological sources | PCG (Pa, 20 – 2000 Hz) | Bowel sounds (20 – 3000 Hz) | | **Bio-optical** | Light absorption / scattering | PPG / pulse oximetry (%, 0.5 – 5 Hz) | fNIRS (DC – 10 Hz) | **Key Engineering Observations:** - The amplitudes are **extremely small** (μV to mV), demanding low-noise amplifier design. - The frequencies are **predominantly low** (mostly < 1 kHz), enabling modest ADC sampling rates. - Most signals are **non-stationary** and corrupted by overlapping physiological and environmental noise, requiring advanced time-frequency analysis. > **[7 marks = 1 (classification logic) + 4 (table) + 2 (engineering observations)]** --- ### Model Solution to Q.B (b) **Relevance of Biomedical Signal Processing in Modern Healthcare:** > Biomedical Signal Processing (BMSP) is the **enabling technology** behind nearly every modern diagnostic, therapeutic, and monitoring device in clinical use. **Five Pillars of Relevance:** 1. **Diagnosis** — Automated detection of arrhythmias, seizures, sleep disorders. 2. **Monitoring** — Continuous ICU/CCU surveillance with multi-parameter alarms. 3. **Therapy** — Closed-loop control in pacemakers, insulin pumps, deep-brain stimulators. 4. **Rehabilitation** — EMG-based prosthetics, brain-computer interfaces (BCIs). 5. **Predictive Health** — AI on raw ECG predicts atrial fibrillation, heart failure, even diabetes years before symptoms. **Block Diagram of a Generic Biomedical Signal Acquisition & Processing System:** ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Patient / Physiological Source] --> S[Sensor or Electrode] S --> A[Front-End Amplifier
Instrumentation Amplifier] A --> F[Analog Filter Bank
HPF + LPF + Notch] F --> C[ADC
Sampling + Quantization] C --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor
Filtering / Feature Extraction / Classification] DSP --> D1[Visual Display] DSP --> D2[Decision / Alarm Logic] DSP --> D3[Data Storage / Telemetry] DSP --> D4[Therapeutic Actuator
for closed-loop devices] style P fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000 style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38f00 style F fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#b38f00 style C fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d style DSP fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#0b5394 style D4 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#38761d ``` **Block-by-Block Description:** | Block | Function | Typical Specs | |-------|----------|---------------| | **Sensor / Electrode** | Transduces physiological event into electrical signal | Ag/AgCl electrodes for ECG; piezoresistive for BP | | **Front-End Amplifier** | Boosts μV/mV signal to ADC-compatible range | INA128 / AD620 instrumentation amp; CMRR > 90 dB | | **Analog Filter Bank** | Anti-aliasing & initial noise rejection | HPF @ 0.5 Hz, LPF @ 150 Hz, Notch @ 50 Hz | | **ADC** | Converts analog → digital | 12–16 bit, 500 Hz sample rate (ECG) | | **DSP** | Core signal processing — Pan-Tompkins QRS detection, FFT, wavelet, ML | Microcontroller / DSP chip / PC | | **Output Blocks** | Display, alarms, storage, therapy | LCD, buzzer, SD card, defibrillator shock | > **[7 marks = 2 (relevance narrative) + 2 (block diagram) + 3 (block description table)]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students LOSE marks in Module 1 ECG questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting units on durations** — Always write "P wave = 80–100 **ms**" not just "P wave = 80–100". The examiner deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit. > 2. **Confusing Lead and Electrode** — A "lead" is a *viewpoint*; an "electrode" is a *physical sensor*. Students who use the words interchangeably lose 1 mark. > 3. **Missing the AV nodal delay in the PR interval** — Many students forget to mention the **AV node conduction delay** when explaining the PR interval. This is a *favorite* 1-mark deduction. > 4. **Skipping the standard paper speed** — In any heart-rate calculation, **always** state the standard speed $v = 25\;\text{mm/s}$ and the conversions: 1 small box = 1 mm = 0.04 s; 1 large box = 5 mm = 0.20 s. > 5. **Not stating the clinical significance** — Waveform questions often ask "why is this important?" — saying "ST elevation indicates MI" gets you an extra mark compared to just listing durations. > 6. **Using the wrong axis in mean QRS calculation** — Use Leads I and aVF for axis, NOT Lead II and aVL. Examiners specifically test this. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Intro & ECG):** ### Core Definitions - Biomedical signal = measurable bio-physical quantity representing physiological state. - ECG = non-invasive recording of the heart's electrical activity at the body surface. - Lead = a *viewpoint* of cardiac electrical activity; not a wire. ### ECG Waveform Essentials - **P** wave → Atrial depolarization (80–100 ms, 0.10–0.25 mV). - **PR interval** → P start → QRS start (120–200 ms). Reflects AV nodal delay. - **QRS** → Ventricular depolarization (80–120 ms, ~1 mV). - **ST segment** → Isoelectric plateau; **elevation = acute MI**. - **T wave** → Ventricular repolarization (~160 ms). - **QT interval** → Total ventricular electrical systole (350–440 ms). - **Atrial repolarization (Ta)** is hidden inside the QRS. ### The 12-Lead ECG - 10 electrodes → 12 leads: I, II, III (Einthoven) + aVR, aVL, aVF (Goldberger) + V1–V6 (Wilson). - **Einthoven's Law:** $V_I + V_{III} = V_{II}$ (Kirchhoff's voltage law). - Augmented leads use a reference formed from the *average* of the other two limbs. ### Numerical Tools - **HR** = $60/RR_{(\sec)}$ = $1500/N_{\text{small boxes}}$ = $300/N_{\text{large boxes}}$. - **Standard paper speed:** 25 mm/s → 1 mm = 0.04 s, 1 mV = 10 mm. - **QRS axis** = $\arctan(QRS_{aVF}/QRS_I)$ — normal range $-30^{\circ}$ to $+90^{\circ}$. - **QTc (Bazett)** = $QT/\sqrt{RR}$ — normal 350–450 ms. ### Frequency & Noise - ECG bandwidth: 0.05 – 150 Hz (diagnostic); 0.5 – 40 Hz (monitoring). - Sampling rate: ≥ 300 Hz; clinical standard = **500 Hz**. - ADC resolution: **12-bit** minimum for 5 μV resolution. - Noise sources: baseline wander (< 0.5 Hz), powerline interference (50 Hz India / 60 Hz US), EMG noise (20–500 Hz). ### Relevance of BMSP - Enables **diagnosis, monitoring, therapy, rehabilitation, and predictive health**. - Used in ECG monitors, defibrillators, pacemakers, Holter monitors, EEG-based BCIs, AI-based disease prediction. ### Engineering Philosophy - Bio-signals are **weak, noisy, and non-stationary** — *signal processing is the only way to extract reliable clinical information*. - Every clinical device is a chain: **Sensor → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → DSP → Decision**. --- > **END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — Introduction and ECG** > *Continue to Module 2 for EEG, EMG, and Event Detection algorithms.*

topics.title: Carotid Pulse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Carotid Pulse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carotid Pulse (CP):** The carotid pulse is a **mechanical pressure signal** generated by the rhythmic ejection of blood from the left ventricle into the aorta, and subsequently propagated through the elastic walls of the carotid artery, where it is detected non-invasively at the neck region (typically over the common carotid artery, approximately at the level of the **C3–C4 vertebrae**).

From a **biomedical signal processing** perspective, the carotid pulse is a quasi-periodic, deterministic, low-frequency physiological waveform (fundamental frequency typically in the range **0.5 Hz – 2 Hz** at resting heart rate) that carries diagnostic information regarding:
- Left ventricular ejection performance
- Arterial stiffness / compliance
- Systemic vascular resistance
- Pulse wave velocity (PWV)
- Cardiac timing events

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Garden Hose" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a garden hose connected to a pulsating water pump (the heart).** Each time the pump pushes a slug of water, a **bulge of pressure** travels down the hose. If you place your finger gently on the hose wall, you can *feel* each bulge pass by — that is essentially what a clinician feels when placing a hand on the neck to feel the carotid pulse.

Key parallels:
- The **heart** is the pump producing periodic pressure slugs.
- The **aorta and carotid artery** act as the hose.
- The **fingertip / tonometer / piezo sensor** is the transducer converting the mechanical bulge into a recordable signal.
- A **stiff, old hose** transmits the bulge faster and sharper (analogous to an **arteriosclerotic vessel**).
- A **soft, elastic hose** transmits the bulge slower and with damping (analogous to a **healthy, compliant young vessel**).

This is the foundational intuition behind **Pulse Wave Velocity (PWV)** analysis — the very heart of carotid pulse interpretation in modern clinical signal processing.

## 1.3 Why Carotid Pulse? — Clinical & Engineering Relevance

| Aspect | Significance |
|---|---|
| Proximity to heart | Carotid is one of the closest large superficial arteries to the aorta |
| Easy accessibility | Non-invasive tonometry possible at neck |
| Strong signal amplitude | Adequate SNR even in obese patients |
| Reflects central hemodynamics | Better surrogate of aortic pressure than peripheral (radial) pulse |
| Used in PWV computation | Gold-standard surrogate for arterial stiffness |
| Synchronization with ECG | Allows extraction of **Pulse Transit Time (PTT)** |

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Normal resting heart rate:** **60 – 100 beats per minute (bpm)**
- **Pulse Transit Time (PTT):** typically **200 – 400 ms** (carotid site)
- **Carotid pulse amplitude (tonometric):** roughly **80 – 130 mmHg** in systolic excursion
- **Pulse Wave Velocity (PWV) aorta-to-carotid:** approximately **4 – 10 m/s** in healthy adults
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin & Generation of the Carotid Pulse

The carotid pulse is generated through the following sequential chain of events:

1. **Electrical Initiation (ECG QRS complex):** The SA node fires → atrial contraction → AV node conduction → ventricular depolarization (QRS).
2. **Mechanical Latency (Pre-ejection Period, PEP):** ~ 30 – 50 ms delay between QRS onset and actual aortic valve opening (electromechanical coupling).
3. **Ventricular Ejection:** Left ventricle ejects ~ 70 mL of blood (Stroke Volume, SV) into the aorta, raising pressure from diastolic (~ 80 mmHg) to systolic (~ 120 mmHg).
4. **Pressure Wave Propagation:** The pressure front travels along the aorta → brachiocephalic trunk → common carotid artery → measurement site.
5. **Wave Reflection:** At bifurcations and resistance arterioles, the forward wave reflects back, superimposing on the forward wave and creating the characteristic secondary features.

> [!NOTE]
> The carotid pulse is therefore a **superposition of the forward (ejection) wave and the reflected (echo) wave** from peripheral sites.

## 2.2 Detailed Waveform Anatomy

The carotid pulse waveform consists of **five distinct fiducial points** which are critical for KTU-level analysis:

| # | Feature | Clinical/Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Anacrotic Limb (Systolic Upstroke)** | Rapid ascending limb during early systole; slope indicates LV contractility |
| 2 | **Percussion Wave (P-wave)** | The dominant systolic peak caused by peak LV ejection |
| 3 | **Tidal Wave (T-wave)** | Secondary systolic peak caused by reflection of pressure wave from upper body |
| 4 | **Dicrotic Notch (Incisura, N)** | Marks aortic valve closure (A2 of S2 heart sound); end of systole |
| 5 | **Dicrotic Wave (D-wave)** | Small rebound wave after notch from elastic recoil of aorta |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Dicrotic Notch** is the single most important fiducial marker in the carotid pulse — it is used as the timestamp for **end of systole** in many signal processing algorithms.

## 2.3 Temporal Relationship with ECG and Heart Sounds

The carotid pulse is **delayed** relative to the ECG QRS due to electromechanical delay and wave propagation:

$$\text{QRS} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{PEP} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Carotid Upstroke} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Percussion Peak} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Dicrotic Notch}$$

Typical timing (in a healthy adult at HR = 75 bpm):
- QRS onset → Carotid upstroke: **~ 100 ms**
- QRS onset → Percussion peak: **~ 200 – 250 ms**
- QRS onset → Dicrotic notch: **~ 300 – 350 ms**
- Carotid upstroke to Dicrotic notch = **LV Ejection Time (LVET)**, typically **~ 250 – 320 ms**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every high-yield equation you need for KTU 2024 exam on this topic. No pipes inside math — all absolute values use `\vert`.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Heart Rate from Pulse** | $HR = \dfrac{60}{T_{pp}}$ | $T_{pp}$ = peak-to-peak interval (s) | Basic rhythm analysis |
| 2 | **Pulse Wave Velocity (PWV)** | $PWV = \dfrac{L}{\Delta t}$ | $L$ = distance between sites (m); $\Delta t$ = transit time (s) | Arterial stiffness |
| 3 | **Moens – Korteweg Equation** | $PWV = \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot h}{2 \cdot r \cdot \rho}}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus (Pa); $h$ = wall thickness (m); $r$ = radius (m); $\rho$ = blood density (kg/m³) | Theoretical PWV |
| 4 | **Bramwell – Hill Equation** | $PWV = \sqrt{\dfrac{V}{\rho} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta V}}$ | $V$ = volume; $\Delta P$ = pressure change; $\Delta V$ = volume change | Compliance-based PWV |
| 5 | **Arterial Compliance** | $C = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta P}$ | $\Delta V$ (mL); $\Delta P$ (mmHg) | Vessel elasticity |
| 6 | **Pulse Pressure** | $PP = P_{sys} - P_{dia}$ | $PP$ (mmHg) | Ejection strength |
| 7 | **Stroke Volume (from pulse)** | $SV \approx k \cdot \dfrac{PP \cdot A}{Z_c}$ | $A$ = cross-section; $Z_c$ = characteristic impedance; $k$ = constant | CO estimation |
| 8 | **Cardiac Output** | $CO = HR \times SV$ | $CO$ (L/min) | Global perfusion |
| 9 | **Augmentation Index** | $AIx = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{PP} \times 100\%$ | $P_1$ = percussion peak; $P_2$ = tidal peak; $PP$ = pulse pressure | Wave reflection magnitude |
| 10 | **Mean Arterial Pressure (from pulse)** | $MAP = P_{dia} + \dfrac{1}{3} PP$ | $P_{dia}$ (mmHg); $PP$ (mmHg) | Average perfusion pressure |
| 11 | **Stiffness Index (SI)** | $SI = \dfrac{h}{\ln(P_{sys}/P_{dia})}$ | Dimensionless | Local arterial stiffness |
| 12 | **Reflection Index (RI)** | $RI = \dfrac{P_2 - P_{dia}}{PP} \times 100\%$ | Percent | Reflection strength |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The carotid pulse is exploited in:
- **Cardiovascular risk stratification** (Framingham-style studies)
- **Cuff-less blood pressure estimation** (PTT-based wearable devices)
- **Hemodynamic monitoring in ICUs** (less invasive than pulmonary artery catheters)
- **Biometric identification** (pulse waveform uniqueness)
- **Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT)** optimization via LVET extraction
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Moens – Korteweg Equation for PWV

This is a flagship derivation frequently asked in KTU 14-mark questions.

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

We model a cylindrical elastic tube (artery) of:
- Inner radius $r$ (m)
- Wall thickness $h$ (m), with $h \ll r$
- Young's modulus $E$ (Pa) of the wall material
- Filled with an incompressible fluid of density $\rho$ (kg/m³)

We assume:
- Small-amplitude, axisymmetric pressure pulse
- Thin-wall approximation ($h/r \ll 1$)
- No viscous losses (idealized)
- Quasi-steady Newton–Laplace wave propagation regime

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law applied to a fluid slice of length $\delta x$:**

Consider a fluid element of mass $dm = \rho \cdot A \cdot \delta x$, where $A = \pi r^{2}$ is the cross-sectional area.

The net force on the slice due to pressure differential is $dF = -A \cdot \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot \delta x$.

Applying $F = ma$:

$$\rho \cdot A \cdot \delta x \cdot \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -A \cdot \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x} \cdot \delta x$$

Dividing throughout by $A \cdot \delta x$:

$$\rho \cdot \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

**Step 2 — Continuity equation (mass conservation) for an incompressible fluid in an expandable tube:**

The change in volume of the tube due to a pressure increment $dP$ produces a radial expansion $dr$. The rate of fluid accumulation must equal the difference in inflow–outflow:

$$-\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{1}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{dt}$$

Since $A = \pi r^{2}$, we have $\dfrac{dA}{A} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{dr}{r}$, so:

$$-\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{2}{r} \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Hooke's Law for the arterial wall (thin-wall tube law):**

For a thin-walled cylinder under internal pressure, the circumferential (hoop) stress $\sigma$ is:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{h}$$

The circumferential strain (using Hooke's law, $E = \sigma / \varepsilon$) gives radial expansion:

$$\dfrac{dr}{r} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E} = \dfrac{P \cdot r}{h \cdot E}$$

Hence:

$$\dfrac{1}{A} \cdot \dfrac{dA}{dP} = \dfrac{2}{r} \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dP} = \dfrac{2 \cdot r}{h \cdot E}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the two equations (eliminate $u$):**

Differentiate Step 1 with respect to $x$:

$$\rho \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x \partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial x^{2}}$$

Differentiate Step 2 with respect to $t$:

$$-\dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x \partial t} = \dfrac{2}{r} \cdot \dfrac{d^{2} r}{dt^{2}} = \dfrac{2}{r} \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dP} \cdot \dfrac{d^{2} P}{dt^{2}} = \dfrac{2 r}{h E} \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial t^{2}}$$

Substituting the second into the first:

$$\rho \cdot \left(-\dfrac{2 r}{h E} \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial t^{2}}\right) = -\dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial x^{2}}$$

Rearranging:

$$\dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial x^{2}} = \dfrac{2 \rho r}{h E} \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial t^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the standard wave equation form:**

The classical 1-D wave equation is:

$$\dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial x^{2}} = \dfrac{1}{c^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial t^{2}}$$

Comparing both, the wave speed $c$ (which is the PWV) is:

$$\dfrac{1}{c^{2}} = \dfrac{2 \rho r}{h E}$$

$$\boxed{\;c = PWV = \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot h}{2 \rho r}}\;}$$

This is the **Moens – Korteweg equation** (Diederik Johannes Korteweg and his student Adriaan Isebree Moens, 1878).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** PWV scales as $\sqrt{E}$ — so a **doubling of arterial wall stiffness quadruples the PWV**. This is the basis of all arterial stiffness diagnostics.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bramwell – Hill Equation

### 3.2.1 Starting Point

The Bramwell–Hill equation relates PWV to distensibility $D$ and is derived from the tube law.

The local distensibility of an artery is defined as:

$$D = \dfrac{1}{V} \cdot \dfrac{dV}{dP}$$

For a cylindrical vessel of length $L$:

$$V = \pi r^{2} L \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{dV}{V} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{dr}{r}$$

Combined with the thin-wall elasticity:

$$\dfrac{dr}{r} = \dfrac{r}{h E} \cdot dP$$

So:

$$D = \dfrac{2 r}{h E}$$

### 3.2.2 Derivation

Substituting into Moens–Korteweg:

$$PWV^{2} = \dfrac{E h}{2 \rho r} = \dfrac{1}{\rho D}$$

Rearranging:

$$\boxed{\;PWV = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\rho D}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} \cdot \dfrac{V}{\Delta V}}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This form is **clinically powerful** because $D$ (distensibility) can be measured directly from ultrasound diameter changes and tonometric pressure — without needing $E$ or $h$, which are difficult to obtain in vivo.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Augmentation Index from Carotid Pulse

### 3.3.1 Setup

The Augmentation Index (AIx) is the ratio of the pressure contributed by the reflected wave to the total pulse pressure:

$$AIx = \dfrac{\Delta P_{aug}}{PP} \times 100\%$$

### 3.3.2 Derivation from the Waveform

From the carotid pulse, identify the **Percussion Peak ($P_1$)** and the **Tidal Peak ($P_2$)**.

Case 1: If $P_2 > P_1$ (Type C waveform — typical in young, healthy subjects):

$$\Delta P_{aug} = P_2 - P_1$$

$$AIx = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{PP} \times 100\%$$

Case 2: If $P_1 > P_2$ (Type A waveform — typical in older, stiffer subjects):

$$\Delta P_{aug} = P_2 - P_1 \quad (\text{can be negative})$$

$$AIx = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{PP} \times 100\%$$

Pulse pressure is:

$$PP = P_{sys} - P_{dia}$$

Therefore, the final closed-form expression:

$$\boxed{\;AIx = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{P_{sys} - P_{dia}} \times 100\%\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Insight:** In young healthy adults, AIx is **negative** (Type C, reflected wave arrives in diastole). In elderly / arteriosclerotic patients, AIx is **positive** (Type A, reflected wave augments systolic peak) — leading to increased LV afterload.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Linking ECG ↔ Carotid Pulse ↔ Heart Sounds

| Event | ECG | Carotid Pulse | Heart Sound | Approx. Time (ms from QRS) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Atrial depolarization | P-wave | — | S4 (sometimes) | -160 |
| Ventricular depolarization | QRS onset | — | — | 0 |
| Mitral valve closure | — | — | **S1** (M1) | 20 – 40 |
| Aortic valve opens | — | **Upstroke begins** | — | 100 – 120 |
| Peak LV ejection | — | **Percussion wave (P1)** | — | 200 – 250 |
| LV ejection ends | T-wave begins | **Dicrotic notch (N)** | **S2 (A2)** | 300 – 350 |
| Aortic valve closes | — | Dicrotic notch | **S2 (A2)** | 320 – 360 |
| Tidal wave arrives | — | **Tidal peak (P2)** | — | 380 – 450 |
| Ventricular repolarization | T-wave end | — | — | 400 |

This matrix is the **single most important KTU visual** for this topic.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Carotid Pulse Waveform — Fiducial Anatomy

This Mermaid block diagrams the **anatomical/functional flow** of the carotid pulse waveform, mapping every fiducial point to its physiological origin and clinical meaning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ECG QRS Trigger] --> A2[Left Ventricular Contraction]
    A2 --> A3[Aortic Valve Opens]
    A3 --> B1[Anacrotic Limb Upstroke]
    B1 --> B2[Percussion Wave P1 Peak]
    B2 --> B3[LV Ejection Continues]
    B3 --> B4[Tidal Wave P2 Peak due to Wave Reflection]
    B4 --> C1[Aortic Valve Closes]
    C1 --> C2[Dicrotic Notch N Incisura]
    C2 --> C3[Dicrotic Wave D Elastic Rebound]
    C3 --> D1[Diastolic Runoff]
    D1 --> A1
    B2 -. Augmentation Index .-> B4
    A3 -. LV Ejection Time LVET .-> C1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Carotid Pulse Signal Acquisition & Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Carotid Artery Mechanical Pulse] --> S2[Piezoelectric Tonometer Transducer]
    S2 --> S3[Analog Amplifier Gain 1000]
    S3 --> S4[Anti Aliasing Lowpass Filter fc 40 Hz]
    S4 --> S5[16 bit ADC Sampling 500 Hz]
    S5 --> S6[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
    S6 --> S7[Fiducial Point Detection Algorithm]
    S7 --> S8[PWV and AIx Computation Module]
    S8 --> S9[Clinical Display and Storage]
    S6 --> S10[ECG Synchronization Channel]
    S10 --> S8
```

## 4.3 Wave Reflection Concept (Forward + Reflected Wave)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Left Ventricle Ejects Blood] --> F2[Forward Pressure Wave Travels Down Aorta]
    F2 --> F3[Bifurcation at Carotid Site]
    F3 --> F4[Reflection at Peripheral Resistance Sites]
    F4 --> F5[Reflected Wave Returns to Carotid]
    F5 --> F6[Superposition of Forward and Reflected]
    F6 --> F7[Net Carotid Pulse Waveform]
    F2 --> F6
```

> [!NOTE]
> In a young elastic aorta, the reflected wave returns during **diastole** (augmenting coronary perfusion). In a stiff elderly aorta, the reflected wave returns during **late systole** (augmenting LV afterload — a hemodynamic hazard).

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: PTT-Based BP Estimation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[ECG R wave Peak Detection] --> T2[Start Timer]
    T3[Carotid Pulse Foot Detection] --> T4[Stop Timer]
    T2 --> T5[Pulse Transit Time PTT]
    T4 --> T5
    T5 --> T6[Inverse PTT to PWV Conversion]
    T6 --> T7[Linear Regression Model BP]
    T7 --> T8[Estimated Systolic BP mmHg]
    T7 --> T9[Estimated Diastolic BP mmHg]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define carotid pulse. Mention any four of its fiducial points.**

**Model Answer:**

The **carotid pulse** is the mechanical pressure wave recorded non-invasively from the common carotid artery in the neck, generated by left ventricular ejection and propagated through the arterial tree.

Four fiducial points:
1. **Anacrotic limb** — the rapid systolic upstroke
2. **Percussion wave (P1)** — primary systolic peak due to peak LV ejection
3. **Dicrotic notch (Incisura)** — the sharp downward deflection marking aortic valve closure
4. **Dicrotic wave (D)** — the small post-notch rebound from aortic elastic recoil

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for the four points)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the dicrotic notch? How is it clinically significant in carotid pulse analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

The **dicrotic notch (incisura)** is a sharp downward deflection on the descending limb of the carotid pulse waveform, occurring at the end of ventricular systole. It is produced by the brief backflow of blood into the aorta at the moment of **aortic valve closure**, which corresponds to the **A2 component of the second heart sound (S2)**.

**Clinical significance:**
- Marks the **end of left ventricular ejection** (used to compute LVET)
- Acts as a **synchronization fiducial** for deriving Pulse Transit Time
- Pathological alterations (absent, delayed, or exaggerated notch) indicate conditions like aortic regurgitation, hypertension, or aortic stenosis.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for cause, 1 for clinical significance)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** write generic "heart anatomy" essays — examiners specifically want **signal-processing-level** details.
> - **Always label units** in every formula (mmHg, m/s, ms) — unitless answers lose 1–2 marks.
> - **Distinguish** between *theoretical* PWV (Moens–Korteweg) and *measured* PWV (from $\Delta t$).
> - Draw the **waveform with labels** in any 7-marker sub-part — partial answers without diagrams are penalized.
> - **State assumptions** before any derivation — failing to do so costs 1 mark.
> - For LVET, specify that it is the **interval between the carotid upstroke foot and the dicrotic notch** — students often confuse this with PEP.

---

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **carotid pulse waveform** in detail. Discuss the physiological origin of each of its fiducial points and relate it temporally to the ECG and heart sounds.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **Moens–Korteweg equation** for pulse wave velocity in an elastic artery. Discuss the clinical significance of PWV in arterial stiffness assessment.

---

### MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Carotid Pulse Waveform [7 marks]

**Diagram (3 marks):** A clearly labelled waveform must show:

- X-axis: time (s or ms)
- Y-axis: pressure amplitude (mmHg, relative units)
- Five labelled fiducial points: Anacrotic limb, Percussion wave (P1), Tidal wave (P2), Dicrotic notch (N), Dicrotic wave (D)
- Reference: A baseline indicating zero pressure and a diastolic reference

**Physiological Origin Table (2 marks):**

| Fiducial Point | Physiological Origin |
|---|---|
| Anacrotic limb | Rapid LV pressure rise → opening of aortic valve → pressure transmission |
| Percussion wave (P1) | Peak LV ejection velocity; forward wave dominates |
| Tidal wave (P2) | Reflected wave from upper-body bifurcations (carotid, subclavian) |
| Dicrotic notch (N) | Aortic valve closure (corresponds to S2 / A2) |
| Dicrotic wave (D) | Elastic recoil of aortic arch + backflow rebound |

**Temporal Relationship with ECG and Heart Sounds (2 marks):**

- ECG QRS → 100–120 ms → Carotid upstroke foot
- ECG QRS → 200–250 ms → Percussion peak
- ECG QRS → ~ 320 ms → Dicrotic notch (synchronous with S2 A2)
- Interval **Carotid foot → Dicrotic notch** = **LV Ejection Time (LVET), ~ 250 – 320 ms**

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Moens–Korteweg Equation [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State assumptions (1 mark):**
- Thin-walled elastic cylindrical artery ($h \ll r$)
- Incompressible, non-viscous blood
- Small-amplitude linear wave

**Step 2 — Equation of motion (Newton's 2nd law) (2 marks):**

$$\rho \cdot \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3 — Continuity equation (1 mark):**

$$-\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \dfrac{2}{r} \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dt}$$

**Step 4 — Tube law (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{1}{r} \cdot \dfrac{dr}{dP} = \dfrac{r}{h E}$$

**Step 5 — Combine to wave equation and solve (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial x^{2}} = \dfrac{2 \rho r}{h E} \cdot \dfrac{\partial^{2} P}{\partial t^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Final result and significance (1 mark):**

$$PWV = \sqrt{\dfrac{E \cdot h}{2 \rho r}}$$

**Clinical significance of PWV:**
- Aortic PWV > **10 m/s** is considered a marker of significant arterial stiffness
- Gold standard for **subclinical organ damage** assessment (European Society of Hypertension guidelines)
- Doubling of $E$ quadruples PWV — a sensitive marker of arteriosclerosis
- Forms the basis of **cuff-less BP monitoring** via PWV–BP regression

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **Bramwell–Hill equation** and derive it from the Moens–Korteweg equation. Discuss how it is used to compute arterial distensibility from carotid pulse measurements.

**(b) [7 marks]** Define **Pulse Transit Time (PTT)** and **Augmentation Index (AIx)**. With the help of a block diagram, describe a system for cuff-less blood pressure estimation using PTT derived from ECG and carotid pulse.

---

### MODEL ANSWER — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Bramwell–Hill Equation [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** The Bramwell–Hill equation (1909) relates PWV to arterial distensibility, removing the need for direct measurement of $E$ and $h$:

$$PWV = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\rho D}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V}{\rho \cdot \Delta V}}$$

**Derivation from Moens–Korteweg (3 marks):**

Starting from $PWV^{2} = \dfrac{E h}{2 \rho r}$, recall that distensibility $D = \dfrac{2r}{hE}$ (from the tube law). Substituting:

$$PWV^{2} = \dfrac{1}{\rho D}$$

$$\therefore \quad PWV = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\rho D}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{V \Delta P}{\rho \Delta V}}$$

**Use in distensibility computation (3 marks):**
- $\Delta P$ is obtained from the carotid pulse amplitude (PP)
- $\Delta V$ is obtained from ultrasound diameter change ($V = \pi r^{2} L$)
- $L$ (carotid length) and $\rho \approx 1060$ kg/m³ are known
- Compute $D$ directly without $E$ or $h$ — making it feasible in vivo
- $D$ values of **~ 10–20 × 10⁻³ /kPa** are normal in young adults; lower values indicate stiffening

---

#### Part (b) — PTT, AIx, and Cuff-less BP System [7 marks]

**Definitions (2 marks):**

$$PTT = t_{carotid\_foot} - t_{ECG\_Rpeak}$$

Typically **200 – 400 ms**. It is the time taken for the pressure pulse to travel from the aortic valve to the carotid measurement site.

$$AIx = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{P_{sys} - P_{dia}} \times 100\%$$

It quantifies the contribution of wave reflection to central systolic pressure.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

The system should include (in order):
1. **ECG amplifier + R-peak detector**
2. **Carotid tonometer + pressure pulse amplifier**
3. **Carotid pulse foot detector** (second-derivative zero-crossing or tangent-intersection method)
4. **PTT measurement module** (timer between R-peak and carotid foot)
5. **PWV calculator:** $PWV = L / PTT$
6. **BP estimation block:** Empirical regression $BP = a + b \cdot PWV$ (or $BP = a - b \cdot PTT$, since PTT and BP are inversely related)
7. **Display and storage unit**

**Justification (2 marks):** Because PWV depends on arterial stiffness, and stiffness depends on mean arterial pressure (via the nonlinear $E$–$P$ relationship of collagen/elastin), PWV can be calibrated against BP. The relation:

$$PWV^{2} = \dfrac{E(P) \cdot h}{2 \rho r} \quad \text{with} \quad E(P) = E_0 \cdot e^{\gamma P}$$

yields a near-linear inverse relation between PTT and BP in physiological ranges, enabling continuous cuff-less BP tracking.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision List for Carotid Pulse:**

- ☐ Carotid pulse is a **mechanical pressure signal** at the common carotid artery, not electrical.
- ☐ The **dicrotic notch** is the end-systolic fiducial — corresponds to **A2 of S2** and **aortic valve closure**.
- ☐ **Five fiducials**: Anacrotic limb, Percussion (P1), Tidal (P2), Dicrotic notch, Dicrotic wave (D).
- ☐ **Carotid upstroke foot → Dicrotic notch** interval = **LVET** (~ 250–320 ms).
- ☐ **Pulse Transit Time (PTT)** = time from ECG R-wave to carotid pulse foot (~ 200–400 ms).
- ☐ **Moens–Korteweg**: $PWV = \sqrt{\dfrac{E h}{2 \rho r}}$ — derived from Newton's law + continuity + Hooke's law.
- ☐ **Bramwell–Hill**: $PWV = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P \cdot V}{\rho \cdot \Delta V}}$ — clinically usable because $E$ and $h$ are not needed.
- ☐ **Augmentation Index**: $AIx = \dfrac{P_2 - P_1}{PP} \times 100\%$ — measures wave reflection.
- ☐ **Normal PWV (aorta-to-carotid)** is **4–10 m/s**; PWV > 10 m/s = arterial stiffness.
- ☐ **Young arteries** → reflected wave in **diastole** (Type C, AIx negative — *good*).
- ☐ **Old / stiff arteries** → reflected wave in **late systole** (Type A, AIx positive — *worse*).
- ☐ Carotid pulse is the **gold-standard central pressure surrogate**, preferable to radial pulse for central hemodynamic studies.
- ☐ Cuff-less BP estimation uses the **inverse linear relation** between PTT and BP.
- ☐ Units in every formula: **PWV in m/s, PTT in s or ms, E in Pa, ρ in kg/m³, r and h in m**.
- ☐ In derivations, **always state assumptions** (thin wall, incompressible, linear, inviscid) before starting — earns the first mark.
- ☐ Examiner's red flag: **never** confuse **PEP** (pre-ejection period) with **LVET** (left ventricular ejection time) — PEP is QRS-to-upstroke, LVET is upstroke-to-notch.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speech Signal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Speech Signal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speech Signal (KTU Definition):** A *speech signal* is a one-dimensional, time-varying, non-stationary acoustic pressure waveform generated by the human vocal apparatus. Mathematically, it is a function $s(t)$ representing variations in air pressure (in **Pascals, Pa**) measured at a microphone as a function of time, carrying linguistic, paralinguistic, and emotional information encoded through the coordinated activity of the lungs, larynx, and vocal tract.

In the context of **Biomedical Signal Processing (PBEBT504)**, the speech signal is treated as a *physiological bio-signal* because it originates from a coordinated neuromuscular activity of the human body. It is therefore a primary example used in Module 1 to introduce the concepts of signal acquisition, modeling, and analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight — Bio-Signal Classification:**
> The speech signal belongs to the family of *electrophysiological / mechanical bio-signals*. Unlike ECG or EEG, the speech signal is an *acoustic bio-signal* that can be converted into an electrical signal using a transducer (microphone) and subsequently digitized for processing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Think of the speech signal as a **"musical instrument played from the inside."** Just as a guitar player plucks a string (the **source**) and the body of the guitar shapes the sound (the **filter**), a human being pushes air from the lungs (source of energy), vibrates the vocal folds at the larynx (the "string"), and shapes that vibration using the tongue, lips, teeth, and jaw (the "guitar body"). The final sound that reaches your ear is the *filtered version of the source*.

So when you say the word *"bat,"* your brain, lungs, vocal folds, and mouth all collaborate in roughly **50–100 milliseconds** to produce a complex pressure wave that uniquely identifies the word. A biomedical engineer simply *captures* this wave, *digitizes* it, and then *decodes* the hidden information inside it.

## 1.3 Key Physical & Statistical Metrics

The following are the canonical parameters used in KTU 2024 valuation to characterize a speech signal:

- **Fundamental Frequency (Pitch, $F_0$):** Typically **80–250 Hz** for adult humans; lies in the **300–3400 Hz** range for standard telephony.
- **Intensity (Loudness):** Measured in **decibels (dB SPL)**; normal conversational speech ≈ **60 dB SPL**.
- **Sampling Frequency:** Per the **Nyquist criterion**, $F_s \geq 2 F_{max}$. The standard value used clinically is **$F_s = 16$ kHz** (telephony) or **$F_s = 44.1$ kHz** (CD quality).
- **Quasi-Stationarity Window:** Speech is treated as *stationary* over short frames of **10–30 ms** because the vocal tract shape does not change appreciably in that interval.
- **Bit Depth:** Typically **16 bits/sample** for high-fidelity clinical recording.

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU tests this:** A 3-mark question often asks, *"Why is speech considered a non-stationary signal but assumed stationary over short frames?"* — the answer hinges on the **quasi-stationarity** concept above.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Speech Production Mechanism (Step-by-Step)

Speech is produced through a coordinated three-stage physiological pipeline. KTU Module 1 requires a precise functional understanding of each stage.

### Stage 1 — Respiratory (Power Supply)
- The **diaphragm** and **lungs** expel a continuous stream of pressurized air.
- This air stream is the *energy source* — without it, no sound is produced.
- The airflow is quasi-periodic for voiced sounds and turbulent (noise-like) for unvoiced sounds.

### Stage 2 — Laryngeal (Source Generation)
- The **vocal folds** (popularly called vocal cords) inside the **larynx** vibrate at a fundamental frequency $F_0$ for *voiced* sounds (vowels, /m/, /n/, /z/).
- For *unvoiced* sounds (/s/, /sh/, /f/, /p/, /t/, /k/), the vocal folds remain open and air becomes turbulent — equivalent to a *noise generator*.
- The output of Stage 2 is a **glottal waveform** $g(t)$ — a quasi-periodic pulse train for voiced sounds.

### Stage 3 — Articulatory (Filtering)
- The **vocal tract** (pharynx, oral cavity, nasal cavity) acts as a **variable acoustic filter** $H(f)$.
- The tongue, lips, teeth, and jaw reshape this filter to produce different *phonemes* (the smallest distinct units of sound).
- The radiated sound at the lips is $s(t)$.

## 2.2 Mathematical Source-Filter Model (KTU Key Equation)

The complete speech signal in the time domain is expressed as the **convolution** of the excitation source with the vocal-tract impulse response:

$$s(t) = g(t) * h(t) * r(t)$$

where:
- $g(t)$ = glottal pulse train (excitation source)
- $h(t)$ = vocal tract impulse response (filter)
- $r(t)$ = lip radiation impedance model
- $*$ denotes linear convolution

In the frequency domain (using the Convolution Theorem):

$$S(f) = G(f) \cdot H(f) \cdot R(f)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the lip radiation term?** The lips convert the internal acoustic pressure to free-space acoustic pressure, modeled as a **6 dB/octave high-pass filter**, so $R(f) \propto j2\pi f$.

## 2.3 Classification of Speech Sounds (Voiced vs Unvoiced)

| Property | Voiced Sounds (Vowels, /b/, /d/, /g/) | Unvoiced Sounds (/p/, /t/, /k/, /s/, /f/) |
|---|---|---|
| Vocal fold vibration | Yes (quasi-periodic) | No |
| Source signal | Periodic pulse train | Random noise |
| Energy distribution | Low frequency dominant | High frequency spread |
| Pitch ($F_0$) | Clearly defined | Absent |
| Zero crossing rate | Low | High |
| Short-time energy | High | Low |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Speech waveform | $s(t) = g(t) * h(t) * r(t)$ | Pressure (Pa) |
| 2 | Frequency-domain model | $S(f) = G(f) \cdot H(f) \cdot R(f)$ | Hz |
| 3 | Pitch period | $T_0 = 1/F_0$ | seconds (s) |
| 4 | Pitch range (adult) | $80 \le F_0 \le 250$ | Hz |
| 5 | Nyquist sampling | $F_s \ge 2 F_{max}$ | Hz |
| 6 | Telephony bandwidth | $300 \le f \le 3400$ | Hz |
| 7 | Short-Time Energy | $E_n = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} [s(m) \cdot w(n-m)]^2$ | Energy units |
| 8 | Short-Time Zero-Crossing Rate | $Z_n = \tfrac{1}{2} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \text{sgn}[s(m)] - \text{sgn}[s(m-1)] \vert \cdot w(n-m)$ | crossings/frame |
| 9 | Autocorrelation (voicing detection) | $R_n(k) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} s(m) \cdot s(m+k) \cdot w(n-m)$ | Energy units |
| 10 | Autoregressive (AR) model | $s(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k) + e(n)$ | Coefficients $a_k$ |
| 11 | dB conversion | $X_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert X \vert / X_{ref})$ | dB |
| 12 | Decibel intensity | $I_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(I / I_{ref})$ | dB SPL |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The autocorrelation-based pitch detector is the *single most-tested* concept in Module 1. Memorize the fact that for **voiced** segments, $R_n(k)$ shows strong peaks at multiples of $T_0$, whereas for **unvoiced** segments, $R_n(k)$ rapidly decays.

## 2.5 Quasi-Stationarity Assumption

Speech is fundamentally *non-stationary* (its statistics change constantly), but it can be modeled as **locally stationary** over short windows of 10–30 ms. This is the foundation of **Short-Time Analysis**, introduced formally in Module 2 of PBEBT504. The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically requires students to justify this assumption by referencing the **physiological inertia of the articulators** — the tongue, lips, and jaw cannot physically reposition in less than ~10 ms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Short-Time Energy (STE)

The **Short-Time Energy** of a discrete speech signal $s(n)$ is defined as the energy of the signal within a finite-duration analysis window $w(n)$ centered at sample $n$.

**Step 1 — Define the windowed speech segment.**
A rectangular (or Hamming/Hanning) window of length $N$ slides across the signal:

$$s_w(m) = s(m) \cdot w(n-m)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the instantaneous energy of each sample inside the window.**

$$e(m) = s_w^2(m) = [s(m)]^2 \cdot [w(n-m)]^2$$

**Step 3 — Sum the energy contributions over the window length $N$.**

$$
E_n = \sum_{m=n-N+1}^{n} [s(m)]^2 \cdot [w(n-m)]^2
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using the standard symmetric form (most common in textbooks).**

$$
E_n = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} [s(m) \cdot w(n-m)]^2
$$

This is the **canonical KTU form** for Short-Time Energy.

> **Engineering Utility:** STE is used to distinguish voiced segments (high energy) from silence or unvoiced fricatives (low energy). It is the simplest energy-based Voice Activity Detector (VAD).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Short-Time Zero-Crossing Rate (ZCR)

The ZCR counts how many times the signal crosses the zero amplitude axis within the analysis window — a powerful discriminator between voiced (low ZCR) and unvoiced (high ZCR) speech.

**Step 1 — Define the signum function of the speech signal.**

$$\text{sgn}[s(m)] = \begin{cases} 1, & s(m) \ge 0 \\ -1, & s(m) < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — A zero crossing occurs between samples $m-1$ and $m$ when their signs differ.**

$$\vert \text{sgn}[s(m)] - \text{sgn}[s(m-1)] \vert = 2 \quad \text{(zero crossing)}$$

$$\vert \text{sgn}[s(m)] - \text{sgn}[s(m-1)] \vert = 0 \quad \text{(no zero crossing)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the window and divide by 2 to count each crossing once.**

$$
Z_n = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \text{sgn}[s(m)] - \text{sgn}[s(m-1)] \vert \cdot w(n-m)
$$

**Step 4 — Typical values (for $F_s = 10$ kHz):**
- Voiced speech: $Z_n \approx 20$–$50$ crossings per 10 ms
- Unvoiced speech: $Z_n \approx 100$–$200$ crossings per 10 ms

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Autocorrelation Function for Pitch Detection

**Step 1 — Continuous-time autocorrelation of a periodic signal $s(t)$ with period $T_0$:**

$$
R(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} s(t) \cdot s(t+\tau)\, dt
$$

**Step 2 — For a strictly periodic signal, the autocorrelation is also periodic with the same period $T_0$:**

$$
R(\tau + T_0) = R(\tau)
$$

**Step 3 — The peaks of $R(\tau)$ occur at integer multiples of $T_0$:**

$$
R(0) \ge R(T_0) \ge R(2T_0) \ge \ldots
$$

**Step 4 — Discretize for digital signal processing with shift $k$:**

$$
R_n(k) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} s(m) \cdot s(m+k) \cdot w(n-m)
$$

**Step 5 — Pitch detection algorithm:**
1. Compute $R_n(k)$ for lags $k = k_{min}, \ldots, k_{max}$ corresponding to plausible pitch periods.
2. Find the lag $k^*$ where $R_n(k)$ attains its first major peak (excluding $k=0$).
3. The estimated pitch is $\hat{T}_0 = k^* / F_s$ and $\hat{F}_0 = F_s / k^*$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Discriminator Property:** For **unvoiced** segments, $R_n(k)$ shows no periodic peaks and decays rapidly — the peak search returns an ambiguous result, which is exactly how the algorithm flags a frame as *unvoiced*.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A speech signal is sampled at $F_s = 8000$ Hz. The autocorrelation of a voiced frame shows its first non-zero peak at lag $k^* = 62$ samples. Compute the pitch frequency.

**Solution:**

$$
\hat{F}_0 = \frac{F_s}{k^*} = \frac{8000}{62} \approx 129.03 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Result:** $\hat{F}_0 \approx 129$ Hz — typical for an adult male speaker.

---

## 3.5 KTU Analytical Matrix — Speech Analysis Tools

| Analysis Tool | Mathematical Basis | What It Detects | KTU Cognitive Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short-Time Energy | Squaring and windowing | Voiced vs silence | Apply / Analyze |
| Zero-Crossing Rate | Sign changes per frame | Voiced vs unvoiced | Apply |
| Autocorrelation | Time-shift correlation | Pitch period $T_0$ | Analyze |
| AMDF | Mean Absolute Difference | Pitch period (alt. method) | Analyze |
| Autoregressive (AR) modeling | Linear prediction | Formant estimation | Analyze / Evaluate |
| Cepstral Analysis | Deconvolution via log-spectrum | Source-filter separation | Evaluate |
| Spectrogram | STFT magnitude squared | Time-frequency content | Apply |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Human Speech Production Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Lungs and Diaphragm] --> B[Pressurized Airflow]
    B --> C{Laryngeal Source}
    C -->|Voiced Vibration| D[Quasi-Periodic Glottal Pulse g of t]
    C -->|Turbulent Noise| E[Unvoiced Noise Source]
    D --> F[Vocal Tract Filter h of t]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Lip Radiation r of t]
    G --> H[Acoustic Pressure Wave s of t]
    H --> I[Microphone Transducer]
    I --> J[Analog Electrical Signal]
    J --> K[Anti-Aliasing Filter]
    K --> L[ADC Sampling at Fs]
    L --> M[Digital Speech Signal s of n]
    M --> N[Biomedical Signal Processor]
```

> **Figure Interpretation:** The diagram traces the *end-to-end* flow from physiological energy (lungs) through the source-filter-radiation model to the digitized signal ready for digital processing. KTU examiners frequently test the ability to map each block to its mathematical counterpart ($g(t)$, $h(t)$, $r(t)$).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Speech Analysis Framework (Receiver Side)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Speech s of n] --> B[Pre-Emphasis Filter]
    B --> C[Frame Blocking 10 to 30 ms Windows]
    C --> D[Windowing Hamming or Hanning]
    D --> E[Feature Extraction]
    E --> F1[Short-Time Energy STE]
    E --> F2[Zero-Crossing Rate ZCR]
    E --> F3[Autocorrelation Function]
    E --> F4[Linear Prediction Coefficients]
    F1 --> G[Voiced Unvoiced Silence Decision]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> H[Pitch Estimation]
    F4 --> I[Formant Estimation]
    G --> J[Application Layer Recognition or Coding]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

> **Figure Interpretation:** This block diagram represents the *analysis chain* applied to a digital speech signal. Each feature extractor produces parameters that feed clinical or engineering applications — e.g., **Parkinson's disease diagnosis** (pitch tremor), **dysphonia detection** (jitter/shimmer), and **speaker recognition**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Source-Filter Model (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Excitation Source e of n] -->|Convolution| B[Vocal Tract Filter H of z]
    B -->|Convolution| C[Lip Radiation R of z]
    C --> D[Output Speech s of n]
    A1[Voiced Pulse Train] -.-> A
    A2[Unvoiced Random Noise] -.-> A
```

> **Figure Interpretation:** This is the **canonical DSP representation** of the source-filter model. In the *z-domain*, the transfer function becomes $S(z) = E(z) \cdot H(z) \cdot R(z)$, which forms the basis of **Linear Predictive Coding (LPC)** — heavily tested in KTU Module 2.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Speech Signal Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Speech Signal] --> B[Voiced Sounds]
    A --> C[Unvoiced Sounds]
    A --> D[Silence or Background Noise]
    B --> B1[Low ZCR]
    B --> B2[High STE]
    B --> B3[Periodic Autocorrelation Peak at T0]
    C --> C1[High ZCR]
    C --> C2[Low STE]
    C --> C3[No Autocorrelation Peak]
    D --> D1[Very Low STE]
    D --> D2[Variable ZCR]
```

> **Figure Interpretation:** This decision tree is the foundation of the simplest *endpoint detector* and *voicing classifier* — a high-yield KTU topic. The next step in classification involves pitch extraction and formant tracking.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
Define a speech signal. Why is it classified as a *non-stationary* bio-signal, and why can it be treated as *quasi-stationary* for analysis purposes?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A speech signal is a one-dimensional time-varying acoustic pressure waveform $s(t)$ produced by the coordinated action of the lungs, larynx, and vocal tract, carrying linguistic and paralinguistic information **(1 mark)**.

It is non-stationary because its statistical properties (mean, variance, spectral content) change continuously as the speaker utters different phonemes **(1 mark)**.

It is treated as *quasi-stationary* over short windows of 10–30 ms because the human articulators (tongue, lips, jaw) cannot physically reposition faster than this limit. Hence, within such short frames, the signal's statistics are approximately constant **(1 mark)**.

---
