### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
Differentiate between **voiced** and **unvoiced** speech sounds. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Voiced | Unvoiced |
|---|---|---|
| Vocal fold vibration | Present | Absent |
| Source type | Quasi-periodic glottal pulse | Random turbulent noise |
| Examples | /a/, /i/, /m/, /n/, /b/ | /s/, /f/, /p/, /t/, /sh/ |

**(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for the comparison table, 1 mark for the examples).**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always mention *Hz* for frequency, *Pa* for pressure, *dB SPL* for intensity. Marks are routinely deducted when units are omitted.
> 2. **Confusing pitch and formant:** Pitch ($F_0$) is the *source* property; formants are *filter* properties. Examiners give 0 marks if these are mixed up.
> 3. **Skipping the quasi-stationarity justification:** Any answer involving short-time analysis *must* justify why the 10–30 ms window is chosen.
> 4. **Writing the autocorrelation without normalization:** Always mention that $R_n(0)$ represents the signal's energy, and the peak search is restricted to the plausible pitch-lag range.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **source-filter model** of speech production with a neat block diagram. Derive the time-domain and frequency-domain equations relating the source, the vocal tract filter, and the radiated speech signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With relevant mathematical expressions, describe the **Short-Time Energy (STE)** and **Short-Time Zero-Crossing Rate (ZCR)** analysis methods. Explain how these two parameters are used together to distinguish voiced, unvoiced, and silence segments of a speech signal. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Source-Filter Model (7 marks):**

The human speech production system is modeled as a three-component cascade: an **excitation source**, a **vocal tract filter**, and a **lip radiation model**.

- **Source** $g(t)$: Generated at the larynx. For voiced sounds, the vocal folds vibrate quasi-periodically at pitch $F_0$, producing a glottal pulse train. For unvoiced sounds, the vocal folds remain open and air turbulence produces a noise-like source **(1 mark)**.
- **Vocal tract filter** $h(t)$: The pharyngeal, oral, and nasal cavities form a variable acoustic tube (length ≈ 17 cm in adults) that resonates at specific frequencies called **formants**. The filter is shaped dynamically by the tongue, lips, and jaw **(1 mark)**.
- **Lip radiation** $r(t)$: The conversion of internal acoustic volume velocity to free-space pressure is modeled as a 6 dB/octave high-pass differentiator, $R(f) = j 2 \pi f$ **(1 mark)**.

**Time-domain equation** (convolution):

$$s(t) = g(t) * h(t) * r(t) \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Frequency-domain equation** (using the convolution theorem):

$$S(f) = G(f) \cdot H(f) \cdot R(f) \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

**Block diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes. The student should reproduce a clean three-block cascade with the source feeding the filter, feeding the radiation, producing $s(t)$ **(2 marks — 1 for diagram, 1 for label clarity)**.

---

**Part (b) — STE and ZCR (7 marks):**

**Short-Time Energy (STE):** Defined as the energy of the speech signal within a sliding window $w(n)$ of length $N$:

$$E_n = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} [s(m) \cdot w(n-m)]^2 \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

- Voiced segments → high STE (typically 0 to –6 dB range relative to peak) **(0.5 mark)**
- Silence / unvoiced fricatives → low STE **(0.5 mark)**

**Short-Time Zero-Crossing Rate (ZCR):** Counts the number of zero amplitude crossings within the analysis window:

$$Z_n = \frac{1}{2} \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert \text{sgn}[s(m)] - \text{sgn}[s(m-1)] \vert \cdot w(n-m) \quad \text{...(1 mark)}$$

- Voiced sounds (low frequencies) → low ZCR (≈ 20–50 crossings per 10 ms) **(0.5 mark)**
- Unvoiced sounds (high frequencies) → high ZCR (≈ 100–200 crossings per 10 ms) **(0.5 mark)**

**Combined Classification Rule (decision logic — KTU favorite):** **(2 marks)**

| Segment Type | STE | ZCR |
|---|---|---|
| Voiced | High | Low |
| Unvoiced | Low to Medium | High |
| Silence | Very Low | Low to Medium |

The two features together form a 2-D feature space in which a simple threshold-based classifier (or a more sophisticated Gaussian discriminator) can segment the speech signal into its constituent phonetic categories. The two features are computed *frame-by-frame* (window length 10–30 ms) to respect the quasi-stationarity assumption.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Alternative Module Choice]**

**(a)** What is the **autocorrelation function** of a speech signal? Derive its mathematical expression and explain how it can be used to detect the **pitch period** $T_0$ of a voiced speech segment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A speech signal is sampled at $F_s = 10$ kHz. The autocorrelation of a 30 ms voiced frame exhibits its first non-zero peak at a lag of $k^* = 80$ samples. Compute the **pitch period** and **pitch frequency**. Justify whether this speaker is more likely to be an adult male or adult female. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Autocorrelation and Pitch Detection (7 marks):**

The autocorrelation function measures the similarity of a signal with a time-shifted version of itself. For a discrete speech signal $s(n)$, the short-time autocorrelation is **(1 mark)**:

$$R_n(k) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} s(m) \cdot s(m+k) \cdot w(n-m)$$

where $w(n-m)$ is the analysis window and $k$ is the lag in samples.

**Key Properties (1 mark each, totaling 3 marks):**
1. $R_n(0)$ equals the short-time energy of the frame (the signal correlates perfectly with itself at zero lag).
2. $R_n(k) \le R_n(0)$ for all $k$ (maximum at zero lag).
3. For a *strictly periodic* voiced signal, $R_n(k)$ is also periodic with the same period $T_0$, exhibiting peaks at $k = 0, T_0, 2T_0, \ldots$

**Pitch Detection Algorithm (2 marks):**
1. Compute $R_n(k)$ for the range of lags $k_{min} \le k \le k_{max}$ corresponding to plausible pitch periods.
   - For $F_s = 10$ kHz and pitch range 80–250 Hz: $k_{min} = F_s / F_{0,max} = 10000 / 250 = 40$ and $k_{max} = F_s / F_{0,min} = 10000 / 80 = 125$.
2. Identify the lag $k^*$ at which $R_n(k)$ attains its first major peak (excluding $k = 0$).
3. The estimated pitch period is $\hat{T}_0 = k^* / F_s$ and pitch frequency is $\hat{F}_0 = F_s / k^*$.
4. For unvoiced segments, $R_n(k)$ shows no clear periodic peak — the algorithm flags the frame as unvoiced.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Pitch Computation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $F_s = 10{,}000$ Hz, $k^* = 80$ samples, $N = 30$ ms frame.

**Step 1 — Compute the pitch period:**

$$
\hat{T}_0 = \frac{k^*}{F_s} = \frac{80}{10000} = 0.008 \text{ s} = 8 \text{ ms} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the pitch frequency:**

$$
\hat{F}_0 = \frac{F_s}{k^*} = \frac{10000}{80} = 125 \text{ Hz} \quad \text{...(2 marks)}
$$

**Step 3 — Speaker gender identification (1.5 marks):**

The typical pitch ranges are:
- Adult male: **$F_0$ ≈ 85–155 Hz**
- Adult female: **$F_0$ ≈ 165–255 Hz**
- Child: **$F_0$ ≈ 250–300 Hz**

Since $\hat{F}_0 = 125$ Hz falls comfortably within the adult male range, the speaker is **most likely an adult male** **(1 mark)**.

**Step 4 — Validation (0.5 mark):** The lag $k^* = 80$ lies within the plausible lag range $[40, 125]$ computed in Part (a), confirming that the peak is a genuine pitch peak and not a search-boundary artifact.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Module 1 Revision — Speech Signal Cheat Sheet:**

- **Speech Signal Definition:** Acoustic pressure waveform produced by lungs + larynx + vocal tract, measured in **Pa**.
- **Bio-signal category:** Acoustic bio-signal (converted to electrical via microphone).
- **Speech production model:** Source $\rightarrow$ Filter $\rightarrow$ Radiation $\rightarrow$ Output, mathematically $s(t) = g(t) * h(t) * r(t)$.
- **Voiced vs Unvoiced:** Quasi-periodic pulses (vocal fold vibration) vs random noise (turbulence). Examples: voiced — /a/, /m/; unvoiced — /s/, /p/.
- **Pitch $F_0$:** Source property; adult range **80–250 Hz**; male lower, female higher.
- **Formants:** Filter property; resonances of the vocal tract; typically 3–5 formants in the 0–4 kHz range.
- **Sampling:** Use $F_s \ge 2 F_{max}$ (Nyquist); telephony $F_s = 8$ kHz, high-fidelity $F_s = 16$–$44.1$ kHz.
- **Quasi-stationarity:** 10–30 ms window — justified by articulator inertia.
- **Short-Time Energy (STE):** High for voiced, low for silence/unvoiced.
- **Zero-Crossing Rate (ZCR):** Low for voiced, high for unvoiced.
- **Autocorrelation:** Peak at $k = T_0$ gives pitch; $R_n(0)$ = frame energy.
- **AR Model:** $s(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k) + e(n)$ — used for formant estimation via Linear Predictive Coding (LPC).
- **dB Conversion:** Use $20 \log_{10}$ for amplitude, $10 \log_{10}$ for power/intensity.
- **Standard telephony bandwidth:** **300 Hz – 3400 Hz**.
- **Clinical applications:** Voice disorder diagnosis (Parkinson's, dysphonia), hearing aids, speech recognition, speaker identification, cochlear implants.

> **End of Module 1 — Speech Signal Notes. Good luck with your KTU 2024 ESE preparation!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concurrent Signals; Signal Representation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concurrent Signals; Signal Representation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Signal?

A **signal** is any physical quantity that varies with time, space, or any other independent variable and conveys information about the behavior or nature of a phenomenon. In the context of biomedical engineering, a signal represents a measurable biological activity, such as the electrical activity of the heart or brain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A signal $x(t)$ is a function of one or more independent variables (most commonly time $t$) that carries information about the physical phenomenon under observation. Mathematically, it represents a mapping from the independent variable domain to a dependent variable range: $x: \mathbb{R} \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ (or $\mathbb{C}$).

## 1.2 What are Concurrent Signals?

**Concurrent signals** are multiple signals that occur simultaneously in time. In biomedical signal processing, we rarely deal with a single signal in isolation. The body produces several signals at the same instant, and analyzing them together yields far more diagnostic information than any one signal alone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concurrent Signal Definition:**
> Two or more signals $x_1(t), x_2(t), \ldots, x_n(t)$ are said to be *concurrent* if they exist and are observable over the **same time interval** $t \in [t_1, t_2]$. This concurrency is the foundation of **multi-modal biomedical analysis** (e.g., simultaneous ECG + EEG + EMG recording).

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Hospital Monitoring Ward

Imagine a **Cardiac Intensive Care Unit (ICU)**. Multiple patients are monitored at the same time, but more importantly, **a single patient is connected to several machines simultaneously**:

- The **ECG machine** records the heart's electrical activity.
- The **EEG headset** records the brain's electrical activity.
- The **EMG patch** records muscle activity.
- The **respiration belt** measures breathing.

All these signals are happening **at the exact same moment in time** — they are *concurrent*. A cardiologist does not just look at the ECG; she correlates the heart rhythm with the breathing pattern and the brain state. **This is concurrency in action.**

## 1.4 Signal Representation — What Does It Mean?

Signal representation refers to the mathematical or graphical framework used to describe a signal so that it can be analyzed, stored, transmitted, and processed. A signal can be represented in multiple equivalent ways:

| Representation Type | Description |
|---|---|
| **Mathematical Function** | $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$ |
| **Graphical Plot** | Amplitude vs. time waveform |
| **Tabular** | Sample values at discrete time instants |
| **Frequency Domain** | Magnitude and phase spectrum |
| **Sequence Notation** | $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$ for discrete-time signals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** In biomedical signal processing, signal representation bridges the gap between a *physical biological event* (e.g., heartbeat) and a *mathematical model* that can be manipulated by digital processors.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Signals

Signals in biomedical engineering are classified along several orthogonal axes. Understanding these classifications is critical for selecting the correct processing algorithm.

### 2.1.1 Continuous-Time vs. Discrete-Time Signals

- **Continuous-Time Signal** $x(t)$: Defined for every instant of time $t \in \mathbb{R}$. The amplitude is also continuous. *Example:* The analog output of an ECG electrode before sampling.
- **Discrete-Time Signal** $x[n]$: Defined only at specific (discrete) time instants $n$, where $n$ is an integer. *Example:* The digitized ECG stored in a computer at a sampling rate of 360 Hz.
- **Digital Signal**: A discrete-time signal whose amplitude is also quantized (finite number of bits). *Example:* A 12-bit ECG sample stored as an integer between 0 and 4095.

### 2.1.2 Deterministic vs. Random Signals

- **Deterministic Signal**: Can be completely described by a mathematical equation. *Example:* $x(t) = 5\sin(100\pi t)$.
- **Random Signal**: Cannot be described by a closed-form equation; characterized statistically. *Example:* Background noise picked up by an EEG electrode.

### 2.1.3 Periodic vs. Aperiodic Signals

A signal $x(t)$ is **periodic** with period $T$ if:
$$x(t + T) = x(t) \quad \forall t$$

The smallest positive $T$ satisfying this is the **fundamental period**. The fundamental frequency is $f_0 = 1/T$.

A signal is **aperiodic** if no such $T > 0$ exists. *Example:* A single heartbeat waveform is aperiodic; a steady-state ECG is periodic.

### 2.1.4 Even and Odd Signals

- **Even Signal:** $x(-t) = x(t)$ (symmetric about the vertical axis). *Example:* $x(t) = \cos(t)$.
- **Odd Signal:** $x(-t) = -x(t)$ (antisymmetric). *Example:* $x(t) = \sin(t)$.

Any arbitrary signal $x(t)$ can be decomposed into its even and odd parts:
$$x_e(t) = \frac{x(t) + x(-t)}{2}, \quad x_o(t) = \frac{x(t) - x(-t)}{2}$$

### 2.1.5 Energy vs. Power Signals

The **normalized energy** of a signal over interval $[t_1, t_2]$ is:
$$E = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

The **average power** is:
$$P = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification Rule:**
> - If $0 < E < \infty$ (and $P = 0$) → **Energy Signal** (e.g., a single ECG beat).
> - If $0 < P < \infty$ (and $E = \infty$) → **Power Signal** (e.g., a continuous periodic ECG).
> - Signals cannot be both energy and power simultaneously.

## 2.2 Concurrent Signal Operations

When dealing with concurrent signals $x(t)$ and $y(t)$, the standard operations are:

| Operation | Formula | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Addition** | $z(t) = x(t) + y(t)$ | Mixing EMG + ECG onto one channel |
| **Multiplication** | $z(t) = x(t) \cdot y(t)$ | Amplitude modulation of a carrier by a biosignal |
| **Scaling** | $z(t) = A \cdot x(t)$ | Amplifying a faint fetal heart sound |
| **Time Shifting** | $z(t) = x(t - t_0)$ | Aligning two recordings in time |
| **Time Scaling** | $z(t) = x(at)$ | Speeding up playback of a recorded signal |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Signal energy | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Joules (normalized) |
| 2 | Signal power | $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$ | Watts (normalized) |
| 3 | Periodicity | $x(t+T) = x(t)$ | $T > 0$ |
| 4 | Fundamental frequency | $f_0 = 1/T$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| 5 | Even part | $x_e(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) + x(-t)]$ | — |
| 6 | Odd part | $x_o(t) = \tfrac{1}{2}[x(t) - x(-t)]$ | — |
| 7 | Sinusoidal signal | $x(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$ | $A$: amplitude, $\phi$: phase (rad) |
| 8 | Sampling relation | $x[n] = x(nT_s)$ | $T_s$: sampling period |
| 9 | Unit step | $u(t) = 1$ for $t \geq 0$, else $0$ | — |
| 10 | Unit impulse | $\delta(t) = 0$ for $t \neq 0$, $\int \delta(t)dt = 1$ | — |

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **ECG Machines** use even/odd decomposition to isolate QRS complexes from baseline wander.
- **EEG Analyzers** classify brain rhythms (delta, theta, alpha, beta) as either energy or power signals for seizure detection.
- **Hearing Aids** rely on concurrent signal addition to mix microphone input with feedback cancellation signals.
- **Patient Monitors** perform time-scaling to compress 24 hours of recorded ECG into a screen view.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy of a Single Rectangular Pulse

**Problem:** Find the energy of the rectangular pulse $x(t) = A$ for $0 \leq t \leq T_0$, and $x(t) = 0$ elsewhere.

**Step 1 — Write the energy integral definition.**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Identify the non-zero region of integration.**

Since $x(t)$ is zero outside $[0, T_0]$, the integral reduces to:

$$E = \int_{0}^{T_0} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the constant amplitude.**

$$E = \int_{0}^{T_0} A^2 \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral (constant integrand).**

$$E = A^2 \int_{0}^{T_0} dt = A^2 \cdot T_0$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

$$\boxed{E = A^2 T_0}$$

> This is finite, hence the rectangular pulse is an **energy signal**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power of a Sinusoidal Signal

**Problem:** Find the average power of $x(t) = A\sin(2\pi f_0 t)$.

**Step 1 — Write the average power definition.**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Square the signal.**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} A^2 \sin^2(2\pi f_0 t) \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Apply the trigonometric identity.**

Using $\sin^2(\theta) = \tfrac{1}{2}(1 - \cos(2\theta))$:

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{2T} \int_{-T}^{T} \frac{1 - \cos(4\pi f_0 t)}{2} \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Split the integral.**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T} \left[ \int_{-T}^{T} 1 \, dt - \int_{-T}^{T} \cos(4\pi f_0 t) \, dt \right]$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate each integral.**

- First integral: $\int_{-T}^{T} 1 \, dt = 2T$
- Second integral: $\int_{-T}^{T} \cos(4\pi f_0 t) \, dt = \left[\frac{\sin(4\pi f_0 t)}{4\pi f_0}\right]_{-T}^{T} = \frac{\sin(4\pi f_0 T) - \sin(-4\pi f_0 T)}{4\pi f_0} = 0$ for integer cycles; bounded otherwise.

In the limit $T \to \infty$, the second term vanishes when divided by $T$.

**Step 6 — Combine.**

$$P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{A^2}{4T} \cdot 2T = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{A^2}{2}}$$

> This is finite, so the sinusoid is a **power signal**. Its energy is $E = \infty$ over infinite duration.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Even/Odd Decomposition of a Biomedical Pulse

**Problem:** Decompose $x(t) = e^{-t}u(t)$ (an exponentially decaying pulse, e.g., a postsynaptic potential) into its even and odd parts.

**Step 1 — Compute $x(-t)$.**

$$x(-t) = e^{t}u(-t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the even part formula.**

$$x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{-t}u(t) + e^{t}u(-t)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the odd part formula.**

$$x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{-t}u(t) - e^{t}u(-t)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Verify the decomposition.**

$$x_e(t) + x_o(t) = e^{-t}u(t) = x(t) \checkmark$$

**Step 5 — Verify even/odd properties.**

$$x_e(-t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{t}u(-t) + e^{-t}u(t)\right] = x_e(t) \quad \text{(even)} \checkmark$$

$$x_o(-t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{t}u(-t) - e^{-t}u(t)\right] = -x_o(t) \quad \text{(odd)} \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Periodicity of a Sum of Concurrent Sinusoids

**Problem:** Determine if $x(t) = \sin(2\pi \cdot 50 t) + \sin(2\pi \cdot 75 t)$ is periodic.

**Step 1 — Identify the two periods.**

For $f_1 = 50$ Hz: $T_1 = 1/50 = 0.02$ s.
For $f_2 = 75$ Hz: $T_2 = 1/75 \approx 0.01333$ s.

**Step 2 — Form the ratio of periods.**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{0.02}{0.01333\ldots} = \frac{1/50}{1/75} = \frac{75}{50} = \frac{3}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the rationality condition.**

A sum of two sinusoids is periodic **if and only if** the ratio $T_1/T_2$ is a **rational number**. Here $3/2$ is rational.

**Step 4 — Compute the fundamental period.**

$$T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2) = \text{LCM}(2, 3) \cdot \frac{1}{150} = \frac{6}{150} = 0.04 \text{ s}$$

Equivalently, $T_0 = \tfrac{1}{\gcd(50, 75)} = \tfrac{1}{25} = 0.04$ s.

$$\boxed{T_0 = 0.04 \text{ s}, \quad f_0 = 25 \text{ Hz}}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Signals (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    sigA[Signals] --> sigB[Continuous Time x of t]
    sigA --> sigC[Discrete Time x of n]
    sigB --> sigB1[Analog Signal]
    sigB --> sigB2[Quantized Continuous]
    sigC --> sigC1[Digital Signal]
    sigC --> sigC2[Sampled Data Signal]
    sigA --> sigD[Deterministic Signal]
    sigA --> sigE[Random Signal]
    sigA --> sigF[Periodic Signal]
    sigA --> sigG[Aperiodic Signal]
    sigA --> sigH[Energy Signal]
    sigA --> sigI[Power Signal]
    sigA --> sigJ[Even Signal]
    sigA --> sigK[Odd Signal]
    sigA --> sigL[Concurrent Signals]
    sigL --> sigL1[ECG and EEG Recorded Simultaneously]
    sigL --> sigL2[EMG and Force Sensor Aligned in Time]
    sigL --> sigL3[Blood Pressure and Heart Rate Synchronized]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Generic Biomedical Signal Acquisition and Representation Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    bioA[Patient Physiological Event] --> bioB[Biological Source Heart Brain Muscle]
    bioB --> bioC[Transducer Electrode Sensor]
    bioC --> bioD[Analog Signal x of t]
    bioD --> bioE[Anti Aliasing Filter]
    bioE --> bioF[Sampler and ADC]
    bioF --> bioG[Digital Signal x of n]
    bioG --> bioH[Mathematical Model xn]
    bioH --> bioI[Signal Processor DSP CPU]
    bioI --> bioJ[Display and Diagnosis]
    bioD --> bioK[Time Domain Plot]
    bioG --> bioL[Sequence Plot Stem Diagram]
    bioH --> bioM[Frequency Domain FFT Spectrum]
```

## 4.3 Concurrent Signal Processing — Conceptual Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    archA[Input Channel 1 ECG] --> muxA[Synchronization Buffer]
    archB[Input Channel 2 EEG] --> muxA
    archC[Input Channel 3 EMG] --> muxA
    muxA --> procA[Feature Extraction Module]
    procA --> procB[Time Domain Features Mean Variance]
    procA --> procC[Frequency Domain Features PSD Band Power]
    procA --> procD[Time Frequency Features STFT Wavelets]
    procB --> fusion[Multi Modal Fusion Layer]
    procC --> fusion
    procD --> fusion
    fusion --> outA[Diagnostic Output 1 Arrhythmia Detection]
    fusion --> outB[Diagnostic Output 2 Sleep Stage Classification]
    fusion --> outC[Diagnostic Output 3 Seizure Alert]
```

## 4.4 Even/Odd Decomposition — Signal Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA[Input Signal x of t] --> split1{Decomposition}
    split1 --> partA[Time Reversed x of minus t]
    partA --> add1[Adder]
    inA --> add1
    add1 --> div1[Scale by 1 over 2]
    div1 --> outA[Even Part x sub e of t]
    split1 --> partB[Time Reversed minus x of minus t]
    partB --> add2[Adder]
    inA --> add2
    add2 --> div2[Scale by 1 over 2]
    div2 --> outB[Odd Part x sub o of t]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define a continuous-time signal and a discrete-time signal. Give one biomedical example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **continuous-time signal** $x(t)$ is defined for every instant of time $t \in \mathbb{R}$, with amplitude that may also be continuous. *Biomedical example:* The unfiltered analog ECG waveform obtained directly from skin electrodes.

A **discrete-time signal** $x[n]$ is defined only at discrete instants, usually $n = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$ The amplitude may be continuous or quantized. *Biomedical example:* The digitized ECG sample values stored in a Holter monitor's memory at a sampling rate of, say, 250 Hz.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always mention both the *time* discretization and the *amplitude* quantization distinction to score the full 3 marks.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is meant by concurrent signals? Why are they important in biomedical diagnostics?**

**Model Answer:**
**Concurrent signals** are two or more signals $x_1(t), x_2(t), \ldots, x_n(t)$ that are observed over the **same time interval** simultaneously. They originate from different physiological sources or different sensing modalities but are time-aligned.

**Importance in biomedical diagnostics:**
1. **Cross-correlation reveals pathologies** invisible in a single signal — e.g., synchronizing ECG R-peaks with blood pressure dips reveals baroreflex dysfunction.
2. **Multi-modal fusion** improves diagnostic accuracy (e.g., combining EEG + ECG for sleep staging).
3. **Noise cancellation** by subtracting a concurrent reference signal (e.g., powerline interference picked up by two electrodes).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the signal $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)$, where $u(t)$ is the unit step function:
1. Sketch the waveform for $t \in [-2, 3]$ s.
2. Determine whether it is an energy signal, a power signal, or neither. Justify with calculation.
3. Find its even and odd components.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Two concurrent signals are given as $x_1(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t)$ and $x_2(t) = 3\sin(200\pi t + \pi/4)$. Determine:
1. The fundamental period of each signal.
2. Whether the sum $y(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$ is periodic. If yes, find the fundamental period.
3. The average power of $y(t)$.

---

### Question A — Complete Model Solution

#### Part (a) Solution

**(i) Sketch:**
For $t < 0$: $x(t) = e^{-2t} \cdot 0 = 0$.
For $t \geq 0$: $x(t) = e^{-2t}$ (exponentially decaying from 1 at $t=0$ toward 0 as $t \to \infty$).

A textual description of the sketch: the signal is zero for $t < 0$, jumps to 1 at $t = 0$, and decays smoothly, reaching approximately 0.018 at $t = 2$ s and 0.0025 at $t = 3$ s.

**(ii) Energy or Power classification:**

Compute the energy:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(e^{-2t}\right)^2 \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt$$

$$E = \left[ \frac{e^{-4t}}{-4} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left(\frac{-1}{4}\right) = \frac{1}{4} \text{ J}$$

Since $0 < E < \infty$, $x(t)$ is an **energy signal** with $E = 0.25$ J. Its average power is $P = 0$.

**(iii) Even and odd decomposition:**

First compute $x(-t) = e^{-(-2t)} u(-t) = e^{2t} u(-t)$.

Even part:

$$x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{-2t} u(t) + e^{2t} u(-t)\right]$$

Odd part:

$$x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{-2t} u(t) - e^{2t} u(-t)\right]$$

**Verification:** $x_e(t) + x_o(t) = e^{-2t} u(t) = x(t)$ ✓.

#### Part (b) Solution

**(i) Periods of individual signals:**

For $x_1(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t)$: angular frequency $\omega_1 = 100\pi$ rad/s, so $f_1 = 50$ Hz and $T_1 = 1/50 = 0.02$ s.

For $x_2(t) = 3\sin(200\pi t + \pi/4)$: $\omega_2 = 200\pi$ rad/s, so $f_2 = 100$ Hz and $T_2 = 1/100 = 0.01$ s.

**(ii) Periodicity of the sum:**

$$T_1 / T_2 = 0.02 / 0.01 = 2 \quad \text{(rational)}$$

Since the ratio is rational, the sum is periodic. The fundamental period is:

$$T_0 = \text{LCM}(T_1, T_2) = \text{LCM}(0.02, 0.01) = 0.02 \text{ s}$$

So $f_0 = 1/T_0 = 50$ Hz.

**(iii) Average power of $y(t) = x_1(t) + x_2(t)$:**

For sinusoids of *different frequencies*, the cross-term averages to zero over a common period. Therefore:

$$P_y = P_1 + P_2 = \frac{A_1^2}{2} + \frac{A_2^2}{2} = \frac{5^2}{2} + \frac{3^2}{2} = \frac{25 + 9}{2} = \frac{34}{2} = 17 \text{ W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often compute $P = (A_1 + A_2)^2 / 2$. This is **wrong** unless the two sinusoids have the *same* frequency. The correct rule is $P = (A_1^2 + A_2^2)/2$ for orthogonal (different-frequency) sinusoids. Losing 2 marks here is very common.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** For the signal $x(t)$ defined as:
$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 2, & -1 \leq t \leq 1 \\ 0, & \text{elsewhere} \end{cases}$$
1. Find the total energy of $x(t)$.
2. Find the average power of $x(t)$ over the interval $[-2, 2]$.
3. Is $x(t)$ an energy signal, power signal, or neither?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to biomedical signal processing:
1. Define the terms **sampling period** and **sampling frequency**.
2. The analog ECG signal has a maximum frequency component of 150 Hz. Determine the minimum sampling frequency required to avoid aliasing. State the theorem used.
3. Briefly explain why concurrent recording of ECG and arterial blood pressure (ABP) is clinically useful.

---

### Question B — Complete Model Solution

#### Part (a) Solution

**(i) Total energy:**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{-1}^{1} (2)^2 \, dt = 4 \int_{-1}^{1} dt = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ J}$$

**(ii) Average power over $[-2, 2]$:**

$$P = \frac{1}{2 - (-2)} \int_{-2}^{2} \vert x(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{4} \int_{-1}^{1} 4 \, dt = \frac{1}{4} \times 4 \times 2 = 2 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Classification:**

Total energy is finite ($E = 8$ J), so the signal is an **energy signal**. The average power over infinite duration is 0, which is consistent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When a problem specifies an interval, you must use the *finite-interval* energy/power formulas, not the limit definitions. The interval $[-2, 2]$ here is finite, so the formulas with explicit limits are correct.

#### Part (b) Solution

**(i) Definitions:**

- **Sampling Period** $T_s$: The uniform time interval between two successive samples of a continuous-time signal. Measured in seconds.
- **Sampling Frequency** $f_s$: The number of samples taken per second, equal to $1/T_s$. Measured in Hertz (Hz).

**(ii) Minimum sampling frequency:**

By the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, to avoid aliasing, the sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest frequency component present in the signal:

$$f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$$

With $f_{\max} = 150$ Hz:

$$f_{s,\min} = 2 \times 150 = 300 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{s,\min} = 300 \text{ Hz}}$$

**(iii) Clinical utility of concurrent ECG and ABP:**

Concurrent recording of ECG and arterial blood pressure (ABP) waveforms is clinically useful because:

1. **Timing correlation:** The R-peak of the QRS complex in ECG precedes the systolic upstroke in ABP by a known interval (electromechanical delay). Loss of this delay indicates conduction or vascular pathology.
2. **Pulse transit time (PTT) estimation:** PTT = time from ECG R-peak to ABP foot. PTT correlates with blood pressure and arterial stiffness; useful for cuffless BP monitoring.
3. **Arrhythmia detection:** A premature ventricular contraction visible in ECG causes a corresponding abnormal ABP waveform beat.
4. **Hemodynamic assessment:** Doctors can correlate electrical events (ECG) with mechanical outcomes (ABP) for comprehensive cardiac evaluation.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Confusing $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ (Nyquist rate) with $f_s \geq f_{\max}$. Always state the **Nyquist Theorem** explicitly.
> 2. Forgetting to mention that $f_s = 300$ Hz is the *minimum*; in practice, $f_s = 500$–1000 Hz is preferred for ECG.
> 3. Writing PTT in reverse order (ABP to ECG instead of ECG to ABP). Direction matters — 1 mark lost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Concurrent Signals & Signal Representation**

- **Signal**: A function conveying information, mapped from an independent variable (usually time) to a value.
- **Concurrent signals** occur over the same time interval; foundation of multi-modal biomedical analysis.
- **Continuous-time** $x(t)$: defined for all $t$; **Discrete-time** $x[n]$: defined at $n \in \mathbb{Z}$; **Digital**: discrete in time *and* amplitude.
- **Deterministic** signals have closed-form expressions; **Random** signals are described by statistics.
- **Periodic** condition: $x(t+T) = x(t)$ for some $T > 0$. Sum of sinusoids is periodic only if $T_i/T_j$ is **rational**.
- **Energy signal**: $0 < E < \infty$ and $P = 0$. **Power signal**: $0 < P < \infty$ and $E = \infty$.
- **Even–odd decomposition** is always possible: $x(t) = x_e(t) + x_o(t)$.
- **Nyquist sampling**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}$ (in practice, $f_s$ is chosen 2–5× the Nyquist rate).
- **Standard signal operations** (sum, product, scale, shift, reversal) must be applied to each concurrent channel independently unless fusion is intended.
- **Units check**: Energy in joules, power in watts, frequency in Hz, angular frequency in rad/s.
- **Cross-term power rule**: For orthogonal sinusoids $A_1\sin(\omega_1 t) + A_2\sin(\omega_2 t)$ with $\omega_1 \neq \omega_2$, $P = (A_1^2 + A_2^2)/2$, NOT $(A_1 + A_2)^2/2$.
- **Always sketch** the signal first; visual interpretation prevents algebraic sign errors.
- **Biomedical examples** to remember: ECG (periodic, 0.5–150 Hz, ~1 mV), EEG (alpha rhythm 8–13 Hz), EMG (20–500 Hz).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aliasing and Sampling Theorem; Elementary Signals; Classification of Discrete Signals - Energy and Power Signals, Periodic and Aperiodic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Aliasing and Sampling Theorem; Elementary Signals; Classification of Discrete Signals - Energy and Power Signals, Periodic and Aperiodic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Foundations of Discrete-Time Signal Processing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon Theorem)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Sampling Theorem** states that a band-limited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above $f_m$ Hz (i.e., its spectrum is zero for $\vert f \vert > f_m$) can be **uniquely and completely recovered** from its samples, provided the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:
> $$f_s \geq 2 f_m$$
> The minimum sampling rate $f_N = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**, and $f_s/2$ is the **Nyquist Frequency** (folding frequency).

#### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stroboscope" Effect
Imagine a spoked wheel rotating at a high speed. If you film it with a normal camera (high sampling rate), you see the wheel spinning realistically. But if you film it with a slow-frame camera (low sampling rate), the wheel may appear to rotate **backwards** or **stand still** — even though it is actually spinning forward. This illusion is **aliasing**. Just as you need enough frames per rotation to capture the true motion, you need enough samples per period of the highest frequency in your biomedical signal (like an ECG or EMG) to faithfully record it. Sample too slowly, and the high-frequency content "folds back" and masquerades as a lower-frequency signal.

### 1.2 Aliasing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which a high-frequency component of a continuous-time signal, when sampled at a rate lower than the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2 f_m$), appears in the reconstructed signal as a **false lower-frequency component**. Mathematically, a sinusoid of frequency $f$ sampled at $f_s$ becomes indistinguishable from sinusoids of frequencies:
> $$f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert, \quad k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$$
> provided $f_{alias}$ lies in the principal range $[-f_s/2, \, f_s/2]$.

#### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wagon-Wheel" Effect in Real Life
You have seen the *wagon-wheel effect* in movies: a rotating wheel that appears stationary or to rotate slowly backward. This is precisely aliasing in the visual domain. The camera's frame rate ($f_s$) is too low to capture the wheel's true rotation frequency ($f$). The brain (acting as a reconstruction filter) interprets the insufficient samples as a *different, slower* motion. In biomedical signal processing, aliasing can make a 150 Hz EMG interference look like a 20 Hz muscle tremor — a clinical disaster.

### 1.3 Elementary (Basic) Discrete-Time Signals

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 syllabus treats the following five as **elementary signals**. All are defined for discrete time $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ (integers).

1. **Unit Impulse Sequence** $\delta(n)$: equals $1$ at $n=0$, else $0$.
2. **Unit Step Sequence** $u(n)$: equals $1$ for $n \geq 0$, else $0$.
3. **Unit Ramp Sequence** $r(n)$: equals $n$ for $n \geq 0$, else $0$.
4. **Exponential Sequence** $x(n) = a^n$ for all $n$.
5. **Sinusoidal Sequence** $x(n) = A \sin(\omega_0 n + \phi)$.

### 1.4 Classification of Discrete Signals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy vs. Power Signals:**
> - **Energy Signal**: Has finite total energy $0 < E < \infty$ and average power $P = 0$.
> - **Power Signal**: Has finite average power $0 < P < \infty$ and infinite total energy $E = \infty$.
> - A signal can be **neither** (in which case it is non-energy, non-power).

> **Periodic vs. Aperiodic:**
> - **Periodic Signal**: $x(n) = x(n + N)$ for some smallest positive integer $N$ and **all** $n$.
> - **Aperiodic Signal**: Not periodic; no finite $N$ exists such that the condition holds.

#### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — Energy and Power
Think of a single hand-clap (an *energy signal*) — it is brief, contains finite "effort" (energy), and dissipates instantly. Now think of a sustained musical note (a *power signal*) — it has been going on forever and will continue forever, so its total energy is infinite, but the **rate** at which energy is delivered (power) is finite and meaningful. Energy signals are transients; power signals are steady-state.

#### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — Periodic and Aperiodic
A periodic signal is like the heartbeat of a metronome: it repeats the same pattern over and over. An aperiodic signal is like a single spoken word — it happens once, and you cannot predict when (or whether) it will happen again in exactly the same way.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Conditions for Sampling Theorem

The **Sampling Theorem** rests on three conditions that must be simultaneously satisfied for **perfect reconstruction**:

1. The original signal $x(t)$ must be **band-limited** (no infinite-frequency content).
2. The sampling rate must satisfy $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (no aliasing).
3. Reconstruction must use an **ideal low-pass filter** with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ (the Nyquist frequency).

> [!NOTE]
> The reconstruction formula (ideal interpolation) is:
> $$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \, \frac{\sin\!\left[\pi (t - nT)/T\right]}{\pi (t - nT)/T}$$
> where $T = 1/f_s$ is the sampling period. The bracketed term is the **sinc interpolation kernel**.

## 2.2 Anti-Aliasing (Pre-sampling) Filter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, no real biomedical signal is truly band-limited (e.g., EMG contains noise above 1 kHz). Therefore, a **low-pass anti-aliasing filter** with cutoff $f_c < f_s/2$ is mandatory **before** sampling. This aggressively attenuates frequencies above $f_c$ to prevent them from folding back into the baseband.

## 2.3 Elementary Signals — Detailed Definitions

| Signal | Symbol | Mathematical Definition | Value at $n=0$ | Energy / Power |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unit Impulse | $\delta(n)$ | $\delta(n) = 1$ if $n=0$, else $0$ | $1$ | $E = 1$ (energy) |
| Unit Step | $u(n)$ | $u(n) = 1$ if $n \geq 0$, else $0$ | $1$ | $P = 1/2$ (power, ambiguous) |
| Unit Ramp | $r(n)$ | $r(n) = n$ for $n \geq 0$, else $0$ | $0$ | $E = \infty$ (neither) |
| Exponential | $a^n u(n)$ | $a^n$ for $n \geq 0$ | $1$ | $E = \frac{1}{1-\vert a\vert^2}$ if $\vert a\vert<1$ |
| Sinusoidal | $A\sin(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ | Defined for all $n$ | $A\sin(\phi)$ | $P = A^2/2$ (power if periodic) |

> [!NOTE]
> The relation $\delta(n) = u(n) - u(n-1)$ and $u(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\delta(n-k)$ are **critical identities** for KTU derivations.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **canonical reference** for solving Module-1 problems. **Memorize every entry.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Condition / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | $f_s$ in Hz |
| 2 | Nyquist Rate | $f_N = 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 3 | Nyquist Frequency | $f_N/2 = f_s/2$ | Hz (folding freq.) |
| 4 | Sampling Period | $T = 1/f_s$ | seconds |
| 5 | Aliased Frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ | Hz, principal range |
| 6 | Discrete Energy | $E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n)\vert^2$ | Joules (discrete) |
| 7 | Discrete Power | $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x(n)\vert^2$ | Watts (discrete) |
| 8 | Energy of $a^n u(n)$ | $E = \frac{1}{1-\vert a\vert^2}$ | $\vert a \vert < 1$ |
| 9 | Power of $A\sin(\omega_0 n)$ | $P = A^2/2$ | Periodic |
| 10 | Power of $u(n)$ | $P = 1/2$ | Step starts at $n=0$ |
| 11 | Periodic Condition | $x(n) = x(n+N)$ | $N$ smallest positive integer |
| 12 | Continuous $\to$ Discrete | $x(n) = x(t)\big\vert_{t=nT}$ | Mapping rule |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **ECG Digitization**: A typical clinical ECG has frequency content up to ~150 Hz (pediatric). Nyquist demands $f_s \geq 300$ Hz; in practice $f_s = 500$ Hz is used with an anti-alias filter at ~150 Hz.
> - **EMG**: Surface EMG has content up to 500 Hz, requiring $f_s \geq 1$ kHz.
> - **EEG**: Generally below 100 Hz, but for sleep staging $f_s = 200$ Hz is standard.
> - **Audio CDs**: $f_s = 44.1$ kHz to capture up to 20 kHz (human hearing).
> Failing to apply these principles produces **clinically misleading recordings** — a cornerstone reason this topic is Module 1 of a biomedical engineering course.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy of a Causal Exponential $x(n) = a^n u(n)$, $\vert a\vert<1$

**Step 1 — Recall the energy definition** for a discrete-time signal:

$$E = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n)\vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** the causal exponential. Since $u(n) = 0$ for $n < 0$, the sum effectively starts at $n = 0$:

$$E = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert a^n u(n)\vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Simplify** using $u(n)=1$ for $n\geq 0$ and $\vert a^n\vert^2 = \vert a\vert^{2n}$:

$$E = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert a\vert^{2n}$$

**Step 4 — Recognize** this as an infinite geometric series with first term $1$ and common ratio $r = \vert a\vert^2$:

$$E = 1 + \vert a\vert^2 + \vert a\vert^4 + \ldots$$

**Step 5 — Apply** the geometric-series sum formula $S = \frac{1}{1-r}$ valid for $\vert r \vert < 1$:

$$\boxed{\,E = \frac{1}{1 - \vert a\vert^2}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the standard result KTU expects. If $\vert a\vert \geq 1$, the energy **diverges** and the signal is **not an energy signal**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Power of a Discrete Sinusoid $x(n) = A\sin(\omega_0 n)$

**Step 1 — Recall** the discrete average-power definition:

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x(n)\vert^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute** the sinusoid:

$$P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} A^2 \sin^2(\omega_0 n)$$

**Step 3 — Pull out** the constant $A^2$:

$$P = A^2 \, \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \sin^2(\omega_0 n)$$

**Step 4 — Use** the trigonometric identity $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$P = \frac{A^2}{2} \, \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \bigl[1 - \cos(2\omega_0 n)\bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Split** the sum:

$$P = \frac{A^2}{2} \left[ 1 - \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \cos(2\omega_0 n) \right]$$

**Step 6 — Recognize** that the average of a discrete cosine over an integer number of its periods equals zero. Hence:

$$P = \frac{A^2}{2} \bigl[ 1 - 0 \bigr]$$

$$\boxed{\,P = \frac{A^2}{2}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: This is the **discrete-time** power of a sinusoid, identical in form to the continuous-time result. The energy of a sinusoid is **infinite** ($E = \infty$).

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Demonstrating Aliasing Numerically

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **type of numerical problem** KTU frequently asks in Part B.

**Problem**: A continuous-time sinusoid $x(t) = \cos(2\pi \cdot 90 \, t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 100$ Hz. Determine the *apparent* (aliased) frequency in the discrete signal.

**Step 1 — Identify** $f = 90$ Hz and $f_s = 100$ Hz. Compute Nyquist rate:

$$f_N = 2 f = 180 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Check** the condition: $f_s = 100 < 180 = f_N$. **Aliasing occurs.**

**Step 3 — Compute** the discrete-time angular frequency:

$$\omega_0 = 2\pi \frac{f}{f_s} = 2\pi \frac{90}{100} = 1.8\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 4 — Find** the equivalent principal frequency in the range $(-\pi, \pi]$. Since $1.8\pi > \pi$, we subtract $2\pi$:

$$\omega_{alias} = 1.8\pi - 2\pi = -0.2\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 5 — Convert** back to Hz:

$$f_{alias} = \frac{\vert \omega_{alias}\vert}{2\pi} f_s = \frac{0.2\pi}{2\pi} \cdot 100 = 10 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6 — Verify** with the alias formula. Choose $k = 1$:

$$f_{alias} = \vert 90 - 1 \cdot 100 \vert = 10 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> The original 90 Hz signal is **misrepresented** as a 10 Hz signal — a 9× fold-down error. In ECG monitoring, similar errors can mis-diagnose a tachyarrhythmia as bradycardia.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Discrete Signal Classification

| Signal $x(n)$ | Energy $E$ | Power $P$ | Periodic? | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\delta(n)$ | $1$ | $0$ | No (aperiodic) | **Energy** |
| $u(n)$ | $\infty$ | $1/2$ | No (aperiodic) | **Power** |
| $r(n)$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | No (aperiodic) | **Neither** |
| $a^n u(n)$, $\vert a\vert<1$ | $\frac{1}{1-\vert a\vert^2}$ | $0$ | No (aperiodic) | **Energy** |
| $a^n u(n)$, $\vert a\vert>1$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | No (aperiodic) | **Neither** |
| $A\sin(\omega_0 n)$ | $\infty$ | $A^2/2$ | **Yes** (if $2\pi/\omega_0$ rational) | **Power** |
| $e^{j\omega_0 n}$ | $\infty$ | $1$ | **Yes** (if $2\pi/\omega_0$ rational) | **Power** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: A discrete sinusoid $\sin(\omega_0 n)$ is periodic **only if** $2\pi/\omega_0$ is a **rational** number $P/Q$ (in lowest terms), in which case the period is $N = Q$. If $2\pi/\omega_0$ is irrational (e.g., $\omega_0 = 1$ rad), the signal is **aperiodic** yet still has finite power $A^2/2$. This subtle distinction is a favorite KTU trick question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Sampling and Reconstruction Chain

> [!NOTE]
> The following **block-level functional architecture** maps the entire signal chain from a biomedical source (e.g., ECG electrode) to a digital processor, highlighting the anti-aliasing filter's role.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Biomedical Source x of t"] --> aaf["Anti Aliasing LPF cutoff fc = fs over 2"]
    aaf --> adc["Analog to Digital Converter fs samples per sec"]
    adc --> proc["Digital Signal Processor PBEBT504 core"]
    proc --> dac["Digital to Analog Converter Zero Order Hold"]
    dac --> rfilter["Reconstruction LPF cutoff fc = fs over 2"]
    rfilter --> out["Reconstructed Continuous Signal y of t"]

    style src fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style aaf fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style adc fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style proc fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style dac fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style rfilter fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style out fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram**: The yellow **Anti-Aliasing LPF** must be placed *before* the ADC, never after. Once aliasing has occurred in the digital samples, **no filter can undo it** — information has been irreversibly lost. This is a one-way trap.

## 4.2 Classification Tree: Elementary Discrete-Time Signals

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["Discrete Time Signals x of n"]

    root --> es["Elementary Signals"]
    root --> es2["Classification by Energy or Power"]
    root --> es3["Classification by Periodicity"]

    es --> imp["Unit Impulse delta of n"]
    es --> step["Unit Step u of n"]
    es --> ramp["Unit Ramp r of n"]
    es --> exp["Exponential a power n"]
    es --> sin["Sinusoidal A sin omega n plus phi"]

    es2 --> en["Energy Signal 0 less than E less than infinity"]
    es2 --> pw["Power Signal 0 less than P less than infinity"]
    es2 --> ne["Neither Energy nor Power"]

    es3 --> per["Periodic x of n equals x of n plus N"]
    es3 --> ape["Aperiodic No finite N satisfies the condition"]

    imp -. example .-> en
    step -. example .-> pw
    sin -. example .-> per
    exp -. example .-> ne

    style root fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style es fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d
    style es2 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d
    style es3 fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#6c757d
```

## 4.3 Frequency-Domain View: Origin of Aliasing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a["Continuous Spectrum X of f band limited at fm"] --> b["After Sampling X of f replicated every fs"]
    b --> c1["fs greater than 2fm No overlap Spectra do not touch"]
    b --> c2["fs less than 2fm Spectral overlap Aliasing occurs"]

    c1 --> d["Ideal LPF recovers original"]
    c2 --> e["Cannot recover lost information"]

    style a fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd
    style b fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    style c1 fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style c2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    style d fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754
    style e fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning**: In the frequency domain, sampling creates **periodic replicas** of the original spectrum centered at $k f_s$. When $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, the replicas do not touch and a low-pass filter can cleanly extract the central one. When $f_s < 2 f_m$, the replicas **overlap**, and the overlapping regions sum — this overlap **is** aliasing.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State the Sampling Theorem. Define the Nyquist Rate and Nyquist Frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The Sampling Theorem states that a band-limited continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely recovered from its discrete samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$.
>
> - **Nyquist Rate**: $f_N = 2 f_m$ Hz — the *minimum* sampling rate that avoids aliasing. **(1 mark)**
> - **Nyquist Frequency**: $f_s/2$ Hz — the highest frequency that can be unambiguously represented; also the cutoff of the ideal reconstruction filter. **(1 mark)**
> - Statement of the theorem with the inequality $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. **(1 mark)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between an energy signal and a power signal. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Property | Energy Signal | Power Signal |
> |---|---|---|
> | Total Energy $E$ | Finite ($0 < E < \infty$) | Infinite ($E = \infty$) |
> | Average Power $P$ | Zero | Finite ($0 < P < \infty$) |
> | Nature | Transient (decays) | Persistent (steady-state) |
> | Example | $x(n) = a^n u(n)$, $\vert a\vert<1$ | $x(n) = A\sin(\omega_0 n)$ |
>
> **(2 marks)** for the table. **(1 mark)** for the examples. KTU strictly requires the example to be a *discrete* signal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern: **Choice between two full 14-mark questions** in this module. Each is split into sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of **aliasing** in detail. Derive the mathematical expression for the aliased frequency of a sinusoid sampled below the Nyquist rate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal $x(t) = 5 \cos(200\pi t) + 3 \sin(400\pi t) + 2 \cos(500\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 400$ Hz. Determine whether aliasing occurs. If yes, find the aliased frequencies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Aliasing & Aliased Frequency**

Aliasing is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a continuous-time signal, when sampled at a rate below the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2 f_m$), appear as false low-frequency components in the discrete-time signal. **(2 marks)**

**Derivation of the aliased frequency:**

Consider $x(t) = \cos(2\pi f t)$ sampled at $f_s = 1/T$. The sampled signal is:

$$x(nT) = \cos(2\pi f nT) = \cos\!\left(2\pi \frac{f}{f_s} n\right)$$

For the discrete-time signal, frequencies that differ by integer multiples of $2\pi$ (or equivalently, by integer multiples of $f_s$ in continuous terms) are **indistinguishable**. Hence for any integer $k$:

$$2\pi \frac{f}{f_s} n \equiv 2\pi \frac{f - k f_s}{f_s} n \pmod{2\pi}$$

Therefore the **apparent (aliased) frequency** is:

$$\boxed{\,f_{alias} = \bigl\vert f - k f_s \bigr\vert, \quad k = \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots\,}$$

provided $f_{alias} \in [-f_s/2, \, f_s/2]$. **(3 marks) for the derivation, 2 marks for the explanation and boxed formula.**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Analysis**

**Step 1 — Identify** the three frequency components:

$$f_1 = 100 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_2 = 200 \text{ Hz}, \quad f_3 = 250 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Find** the maximum frequency: $f_m = 250$ Hz. **(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Nyquist rate** is $f_N = 2 \times 250 = 500$ Hz. Since $f_s = 400 < 500$ Hz, **aliasing occurs**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Compute** the discrete angular frequencies $\omega_i = 2\pi f_i / f_s$:

$$\omega_1 = 2\pi \cdot 100/400 = 0.5\pi \text{ rad/sample} \quad \text{(within } [-\pi,\pi]\text{ — no alias)}$$

$$\omega_2 = 2\pi \cdot 200/400 = \pi \text{ rad/sample} \quad \text{(at the boundary — no alias)}$$

$$\omega_3 = 2\pi \cdot 250/400 = 1.25\pi \text{ rad/sample} \quad \text{(outside range — aliased)}$$

**Step 5 — Alias the 250 Hz component**. Subtract $2\pi$ to bring it into the principal range:

$$\omega_{3,alias} = 1.25\pi - 2\pi = -0.75\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 6 — Convert** to Hz:

$$f_{3,alias} = \frac{\vert -0.75\pi \vert}{2\pi} \cdot 400 = 150 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 7 — Verification** with the alias formula ($k=1$):

$$f_{3,alias} = \vert 250 - 1 \cdot 400 \vert = 150 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final aliased signal:** $x_d(n) = 5 \cos(0.5\pi n) + 3 \sin(\pi n) + 2 \cos(-0.75\pi n)$ = $5 \cos(0.5\pi n) + 3 \sin(\pi n) + 2 \cos(0.75\pi n)$. **(3 marks) for the algebra; 1 mark for the final statement.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Students frequently forget to verify that $f_s < 2 f_m$ *before* diving into the calculation. **(Lose 1 mark)**. Also, students sometimes state the aliased frequency without restricting it to the principal range $[-f_s/2, f_s/2]$. **(Lose 1 mark)**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define and sketch the following elementary discrete-time signals: (i) Unit impulse $\delta(n)$, (ii) Unit step $u(n)$, (iii) Unit ramp $r(n)$. Show that $\delta(n) = u(n) - u(n-1)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine whether the following signals are energy, power, or neither; periodic or aperiodic. Compute the energy or power value where applicable. **(7 marks)**
  - (i) $x_1(n) = (0.5)^n u(n)$
  - (ii) $x_2(n) = 4 \cos(0.25\pi n)$

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Elementary Signals and Impulse–Step Relation**

**(i) Unit Impulse** $\delta(n)$:

$$\delta(n) = \begin{cases} 1, & n = 0 \\ 0, & n \neq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Sketch**: A vertical arrow of height $1$ at $n=0$; the value at $n=0$ is explicitly $1$, not "undefined". **(1 mark)**

**(ii) Unit Step** $u(n)$:

$$u(n) = \begin{cases} 1, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Sketch**: A sequence that is $0$ for $n<0$ and equals $1$ for $n \geq 0$. At $n=0$, the convention is $u(0) = 1$. **(1 mark)**

**(iii) Unit Ramp** $r(n)$:

$$r(n) = \begin{cases} n, & n \geq 0 \\ 0, & n < 0 \end{cases}$$

**Sketch**: A linearly increasing sequence: $0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ for $n \geq 0$ and zeros for $n < 0$. **(1 mark)**

**Proof that $\delta(n) = u(n) - u(n-1)$:**

| $n$ | $u(n)$ | $u(n-1)$ | $u(n) - u(n-1)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $n < 0$ | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| $n = 0$ | $1$ | $0$ | $1$ |
| $n > 0$ | $1$ | $1$ | $0$ |

The right-hand column matches the definition of $\delta(n)$ exactly. **Q.E.D.** **(4 marks) for the proof and the table.**

---

**Part (b) — Classification**

**(i) $x_1(n) = (0.5)^n u(n)$:**

**Energy test:**

$$E = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \vert 0.5^n \vert^2 = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.25)^n = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{4}{3}$$

Using the formula $E = \frac{1}{1 - \vert a\vert^2}$ with $a = 0.5$:

$$E = \frac{1}{1 - 0.25} = \frac{4}{3} \quad \text{(finite)}$$

**Conclusion**: $x_1(n)$ is an **energy signal** (aperiodic) with $E = 4/3$ and $P = 0$. **(3 marks)**

**(ii) $x_2(n) = 4 \cos(0.25\pi n)$:**

**Periodic test**: We seek the smallest positive integer $N$ such that $\cos(0.25\pi (n+N)) = \cos(0.25\pi n)$. This requires:

$$0.25\pi N = 2\pi k \implies N = 8k$$

Smallest $N = 8$ (with $k=1$). So $x_2(n)$ is **periodic** with period $N = 8$. **(1 mark)**

**Power calculation**: Using $P = A^2/2$ with $A = 4$:

$$P = \frac{4^2}{2} = 8$$

**Energy** is infinite. **Conclusion**: $x_2(n)$ is a **power signal**, periodic with $N = 8$, $P = 8$. **(3 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: For (ii), students often forget to verify that $2\pi/\omega_0$ is *rational*. Here $2\pi/0.25\pi = 8$ is rational — the signal **is** periodic. Had $\omega_0$ been $1$ rad (irrational case), it would be aperiodic but **still have power** $A^2/2$. Losing this distinction costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1**

- [ ] **Sampling Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ (memorize the inequality, not just "equal to").
- [ ] **Nyquist Rate** = $2 f_m$ (minimum); **Nyquist Frequency** = $f_s/2$ (cutoff of reconstruction filter).
- [ ] **Aliasing formula**: $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$, restricted to $[-f_s/2, f_s/2]$.
- [ ] **Anti-aliasing filter** is **mandatory** before the ADC; once aliased, data is lost forever.
- [ ] **Elementary signals**: $\delta(n)$, $u(n)$, $r(n)$, $a^n$, $A\sin(\omega_0 n + \phi)$ — know the formula and sketch of each.
- [ ] **Key identity**: $\delta(n) = u(n) - u(n-1)$ and $u(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\delta(n-k)$.
- [ ] **Energy** $E = \sum \vert x(n)\vert^2$ — finite only for transients.
- [ ] **Power** $P = \lim \frac{1}{2N+1}\sum \vert x(n)\vert^2$ — finite for persistent signals.
- [ ] **Energy of $a^n u(n)$**: $E = \frac{1}{1 - \vert a\vert^2}$ provided $\vert a\vert < 1$.
- [ ] **Power of sinusoid**: $P = A^2/2$ (regardless of $\omega_0$).
- [ ] **Periodic in discrete time**: $x(n) = x(n+N)$ for some *smallest* positive integer $N$.
- [ ] **Discrete sinusoid periodic iff $2\pi/\omega_0$ is rational**; smallest period $N$ is the denominator in lowest terms.
- [ ] **Classification rule**: Energy ↔ $P=0, E<\infty$ · Power ↔ $0<P<\infty, E=\infty$ · Neither ↔ both infinite.
- [ ] **Practical biomedical rates**: ECG ~ 500 Hz, EMG ~ 1 kHz, EEG ~ 200 Hz, audio CD ~ 44.1 kHz.
- [ ] **Time mapping**: $x(n) = x(t)\big\vert_{t=nT}$ with $T = 1/f_s$.
- [ ] **Pitfall #1**: Sampling at exactly $f_s = 2 f_m$ is theoretically possible but practically risky — always use a **guard band** of 10–20% (e.g., $f_s \geq 2.2 f_m$).
- [ ] **Pitfall #2**: A signal can be periodic but **not** an energy signal; a signal can be aperiodic and still a **power** signal (e.g., $\cos(n)$ with $\omega_0=1$).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Even and Odd Signals; Discrete Time Systems - Properties of Discrete Systems; LTI System - Impulse Response of a Linear Time-Invariant System, Convolution, Correlation, and Difference Equation Representation of Discrete Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Even and Odd Signals; Discrete Time Systems - Properties of Discrete Systems; LTI System - Impulse Response of a Linear Time-Invariant System, Convolution, Correlation, and Difference Equation Representation of Discrete Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Even and Odd Signals (Discrete-Time Domain)

A discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is classified as **even** or **odd** based on its symmetry properties with respect to the origin (n = 0). This classification is foundational in DSP because **any arbitrary signal can be uniquely decomposed into its even and odd components**, which dramatically simplifies analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Even/Odd decomposition is treated as a direct application of signal symmetry and is frequently tested as a 3-mark short answer or as a sub-part in a 14-mark module question.

### Formal Definition (KTU Standard Terminology)

A discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is said to be:

- **Even Signal:** $x[-n] = x[n]$ for all integer $n$. The signal is perfectly **mirror-symmetric** about the vertical axis at $n = 0$.
- **Odd Signal:** $x[-n] = -x[n]$ for all integer $n$. The signal is **anti-symmetric** about the origin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention for Discrete Time:** In DSP, the symmetry of a signal is judged by substituting $n \rightarrow -n$ in the discrete index. For example, $x[n] = \cos(\omega n)$ is even because $\cos(-\omega n) = \cos(\omega n)$.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing in front of a mirror placed vertically through the origin:

- An **even signal** is like your **reflection in a regular mirror** — the left half is an exact mirror copy of the right half. Examples: $x[n] = 1$, $x[n] = \cos(0.5\pi n)$, or a rectangular pulse centered at the origin.
- An **odd signal** is like the **reflection in a fun-house mirror that inverts depth** — if the right side goes up, the left side goes down by the same amount. Examples: $x[n] = n$, $x[n] = \sin(0.5\pi n)$.

### Even–Odd Decomposition Theorem

Any signal $x[n]$ can be uniquely expressed as the sum of an even part $x_e[n]$ and an odd part $x_o[n]$:

$$x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]$$

where:

$$x_e[n] = \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}, \quad x_o[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$$

The product of an even and an odd signal is always **odd**, and the product of two even (or two odd) signals is always **even**. Also, the area (DC value) of any odd signal evaluated symmetrically is always **zero**.

---

## 1.2 Discrete-Time Systems — Properties

A **discrete-time system** is a mathematical operator that maps an input discrete-time signal $x[n]$ to an output discrete-time signal $y[n]$. We denote this transformation as $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$.

In KTU terminology, the system is analyzed using five **canonical properties** that govern its behavior, stability, and predictability. These are essential for analyzing biomedical signals (ECG, EEG, EMG) where filtering and feature extraction demand rigorous system characterization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Significance:** KTU 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on testing the *static properties* (with/without memory, invertibility) and *dynamic properties* (linearity, time-invariance, causality, stability) of discrete systems. Expect a direct 7-mark question in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### Static (Memory-Related) Properties
- **System with Memory / Dynamic:** Output $y[n]$ depends on past/future input values (e.g., $y[n] = x[n-1]$).
- **Memoryless / Static System:** Output depends only on the present input $x[n]$ (e.g., $y[n] = (x[n])^2$).
- **Invertible System:** Distinct inputs always produce distinct outputs, allowing recovery of $x[n]$ from $y[n]$.
- **Stable System (BIBO):** A bounded input sequence produces a bounded output sequence for all $n$.

### Dynamic (Operational) Properties
- **Linearity:** The system obeys the **Superposition Principle** — it is both *additive* and *homogeneous (scaled)*.
- **Time-Invariance:** A shift in the input by $n_0$ produces an identical shift in the output.
- **Causality:** The output $y[n]$ depends only on present and past input values, never on future values. **Biomedical systems must be causal** because they operate in real-time.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a discrete-time system as a **black-box medical diagnostic filter**:

- *Linearity* is like saying the filter's response to a 2x amplified heart sound is exactly 2x the response to a normal heart sound.
- *Time-invariance* means the filter's behavior does not change with time — it doesn't "get tired" or "drift" after 24 hours of use.
- *Causality* means the filter cannot predict the future (since it has not happened yet in the patient).
- *Stability* guarantees the filter will never blow up (output to infinity) when the input is finite — critical for ICU monitors.

---

## 1.3 LTI Systems — Impulse Response

A **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) System** is a system that satisfies **both linearity and time-invariance**. The fundamental theorem of LTI systems states that **such a system is completely characterized by its impulse response $h[n]$** — the output when the input is a unit impulse $\delta[n]$.

$$h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Impulse Response is the "DNA" of an LTI System.** Once $h[n]$ is known, the output for **any** input $x[n]$ can be computed via **convolution**. This is the most powerful concept in DSP and is heavily tested in KTU exams.

---

## 1.4 Convolution

**Convolution** is the mathematical operation that describes the output of an LTI system to an arbitrary input. The input signal is decomposed into a sum of weighted, shifted impulses, and the output is the sum of weighted, shifted impulse responses.

The **linear convolution sum** for two discrete-time sequences $x[n]$ and $h[n]$ is defined as:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Context:** Convolution is the mathematical model of how a **filter** modifies a **biomedical signal** (e.g., how a low-pass filter smooths an ECG signal to remove 50 Hz powerline interference). The impulse response $h[n]$ is the "fingerprint" of the filter.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine pouring water into a series of connected buckets, where each bucket represents one impulse response shifted in time. The total water level in the receiving bucket is the **convolution sum** — a weighted sum of past inputs.

---

## 1.5 Correlation

**Correlation** is a mathematical measure of **similarity** between two signals as a function of the time-lag applied to one of them. Unlike convolution, correlation does **not** involve time-reversal of one signal.

- **Auto-correlation** $r_{xx}[l]$: Measures the similarity of a signal with a time-shifted version of **itself**.
- **Cross-correlation** $r_{xy}[l]$: Measures the similarity between **two different** signals $x[n]$ and $y[n]$ as one is shifted.

$$r_{xy}[l] = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, y[n-l]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Application:** Cross-correlation is used to estimate the **time delay** between two ECG leads (for heart axis determination), between mother and fetal ECG during labor, and to detect **template-matched QRS complexes** in Holter monitors.

---

## 1.6 Difference Equation Representation

A discrete LTI system can be represented by a **linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE)** of order $N$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k]$$

The most common normalized form (with $a_0 = 1$) is:

$$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The difference equation is the **preferred representation** for recursive (IIR) and non-recursive (FIR) systems. It is the discrete analog of the differential equation in continuous-time systems and is fundamental for digital filter design in biomedical instrumentation.

### Conceptual Analogy
A difference equation is like a **recipe**: to cook today's output $y[n]$, you need ingredients from today's input $x[n]$, yesterday's output $y[n-1]$, and possibly older outputs and inputs. The coefficients $a_k$ and $b_k$ are the "amounts" of each ingredient.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Properties of Even and Odd Signals

The even/odd decomposition yields three major insights used repeatedly in DSP:

1. **DC Component Extraction:** The value of an even signal at $n=0$ equals the signal's instantaneous DC (average) value. The odd component is always zero at $n=0$, i.e., $x_o[0] = 0$.

2. **Energy Decomposition:** The total energy of a signal equals the sum of the energies of its even and odd parts (this is **Parseval-like orthogonality**):

$$E_{\text{total}} = E_e + E_o = \sum_{n} \mid x_e[n] \mid^2 + \sum_{n} \mid x_o[n] \mid^2$$

3. **Multiplication Rules:**
   - Even $\times$ Even = Even
   - Odd $\times$ Odd = Even
   - Even $\times$ Odd = Odd

These rules simplify the analysis of products such as $x[n]\cos(\omega n)$ (used in modulation theory).

---

## 2.2 Properties of Discrete-Time Systems — Detailed Classification

### 2.2.1 System with vs. without Memory
- **Memoryless:** $y[n]$ depends only on $x[n]$. Example: $y[n] = (x[n])^2$.
- **With Memory:** $y[n]$ depends on $x[n-k]$ for $k \neq 0$. Example: $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$ (a simple averaging filter).

### 2.2.2 Invertibility
A system is **invertible** if distinct inputs yield distinct outputs. The **inverse system** $T^{-1}\{\cdot\}$ is defined by $T^{-1}\{T\{x[n]\}\} = x[n]$.

**Example:** $y[n] = 2x[n]$ is invertible, with $x[n] = \frac{1}{2}y[n]$. However, $y[n] = x^2[n]$ is **not invertible** because both $x[n] = +a$ and $x[n] = -a$ yield the same $y[n] = a^2$.

### 2.2.3 Stability (BIBO Criterion)
A system is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if and only if for every bounded input (i.e., $\mid x[n] \mid \leq M_x < \infty$), the output is also bounded ($\mid y[n] \mid \leq M_y < \infty$). For **LTI systems**, this simplifies to the **Absolute Summability Condition** on the impulse response:

$$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \mid h[n] \mid < \infty$$

### 2.2.4 Linearity
A system is **linear** if it satisfies the **Superposition Principle** for any inputs $x_1[n]$, $x_2[n]$ and any scalars $a, b$:

$$T\{a x_1[n] + b x_2[n]\} = a\, T\{x_1[n]\} + b\, T\{x_2[n]\}$$

- **Additivity:** $T\{x_1[n] + x_2[n]\} = T\{x_1[n]\} + T\{x_2[n]\}$
- **Homogeneity (Scaling):** $T\{a x_1[n]\} = a\, T\{x_1[n]\}$

### 2.2.5 Time-Invariance
A system is **time-invariant** if a shift in the input produces an identical shift in the output. Formally, if $y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$, then for any integer $n_0$:

$$y[n - n_0] = T\{x[n - n_0]\}$$

The system's behavior **does not change** with time. This is essential for repeatable biomedical measurements.

### 2.2.6 Causality
A system is **causal** if the output at time $n$ depends only on present and past input values, never on future values. For an LTI system, causality is equivalent to:

$$h[n] = 0 \quad \text{for } n < 0$$

(i.e., the impulse response is a **right-sided sequence** — the system is realizable in real-time hardware/software).

---

## 2.3 LTI System Representation

The **fundamental property** of an LTI system is that its response to any input is uniquely determined by the **convolution** of the input with the system's **impulse response**:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$$

Equivalently, by changing the index of summation:

$$y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h[k]\, x[n-k]$$

This dual representation is the key to evaluating convolution by either **flipping-and-shift** of $h[n]$ or **flipping-and-shift** of $x[n]$.

### Properties of Convolution
- **Commutative:** $x[n] * h[n] = h[n] * x[n]$
- **Associative:** $(x * h_1) * h_2 = x * (h_1 * h_2)$
- **Distributive:** $x * (h_1 + h_2) = x * h_1 + x * h_2$
- **Identity Element:** $x[n] * \delta[n] = x[n]$
- **Cascade Equivalence:** Two LTI systems in cascade have an overall impulse response $h[n] = h_1[n] * h_2[n]$.

---

## 2.4 Correlation — Detailed Theory

### 2.4.1 Auto-correlation
The **auto-correlation sequence** of an energy signal $x[n]$ is:

$$r_{xx}[l] = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, x[n-l]$$

Key properties:
- **Even symmetry:** $r_{xx}[l] = r_{xx}[-l]$
- **Maximum at zero lag:** $\mid r_{xx}[l] \mid \leq r_{xx}[0] = E_x$ (signal energy)

### 2.4.2 Cross-correlation
The **cross-correlation sequence** between $x[n]$ and $y[n]$ is:

$$r_{xy}[l] = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\, y[n-l]$$

Note: Cross-correlation is **not commutative** and **not associative**. However:

$$r_{xy}[l] = r_{yx}[-l]$$

### 2.4.3 Relation between Convolution and Correlation
The crucial link between correlation and convolution is:

$$r_{xy}[l] = x[l] * y[-l]$$

That is, cross-correlation is **convolution with one of the signals time-reversed**. This is why MATLAB implementations of correlation often use `xcorr(x,y) = conv(x, fliplr(y))`.

---

## 2.5 Difference Equation Representation — LCCDE

A general **Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** of order $N$ is:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k]$$

- The order of the system is $\max(N, M)$.
- **Recursive (IIR) System:** If $N \geq 1$, the output depends on past outputs (feedback). For example: $y[n] = y[n-1] + x[n]$.
- **Non-recursive (FIR) System:** If $N = 0$, the output depends only on inputs. For example: $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1] + x[n-2]$.

### Block Diagram Elements
- **Delay Element ($z^{-1}$):** Stores the previous sample.
- **Multiplier:** Multiplies by a coefficient $a_k$ or $b_k$.
- **Adder:** Sums the weighted signals.

### Direct Form I vs. Direct Form II
- **Direct Form I:** Implements the LCCDE directly as two cascades — first the **all-zero (FIR) part** and then the **all-pole (IIR) part**.
- **Direct Form II:** A more compact structure obtained by reversing the order of the two parts, exploiting the linearity of LTI systems.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below consolidates all high-yield formulas for Module 1 — bookmark this for revision.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Condition / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Even Signal | $x[-n] = x[n]$ | Mirror symmetry about $n=0$ |
| 2 | Odd Signal | $x[-n] = -x[n]$ | Anti-symmetry; $x[0] = 0$ |
| 3 | Even Part | $x_e[n] = \frac{x[n] + x[-n]}{2}$ | Always exists uniquely |
| 4 | Odd Part | $x_o[n] = \frac{x[n] - x[-n]}{2}$ | Always exists uniquely |
| 5 | Linearity Test | $T\{a x_1 + b x_2\} = a T\{x_1\} + b T\{x_2\}$ | Superposition |
| 6 | Time-Invariance | $T\{x[n - n_0]\} = y[n - n_0]$ | For all $n_0$ |
| 7 | Causality (LTI) | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ | Right-sided impulse response |
| 8 | Stability (LTI) | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \mid h[n] \mid < \infty$ | Absolute summability |
| 9 | Impulse Response | $h[n] = T\{\delta[n]\}$ | Complete LTI characterization |
| 10 | Linear Convolution | $y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k} x[k] h[n-k]$ | Bounded for finite-length signals |
| 11 | Convolution Length | $L_y = L_x + L_h - 1$ | For finite-length sequences |
| 12 | Auto-correlation | $r_{xx}[l] = \sum_{n} x[n] x[n-l]$ | Even: $r_{xx}[l] = r_{xx}[-l]$ |
| 13 | Cross-correlation | $r_{xy}[l] = \sum_{n} x[n] y[n-l]$ | $r_{xy}[l] = r_{yx}[-l]$ |
| 14 | Correlation-Convol. | $r_{xy}[l] = x[l] * y[-l]$ | Time-reverse one signal |
| 15 | LCCDE (General) | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | Order = $\max(N, M)$ |
| 16 | LCCDE (Normalized) | $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | With $a_0 = 1$ |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Biomedical Filter Design (ECG/EEG Clean-up):** An LTI system's impulse response $h[n]$ is designed to remove 50/60 Hz powerline interference and baseline wander from ECG signals.

2. **Heart Rate Variability (HRV) Analysis:** Auto-correlation of RR-interval sequences is used clinically to assess autonomic nervous system function.

3. **Evoked Potential Detection:** Cross-correlation between a recorded EEG and a known stimulus template extracts the time-locked brain response (e.g., auditory evoked potentials).

4. **Pacemaker Logic Circuits:** Implemented as recursive difference equations that respond to sensed cardiac events in real-time (causal systems).

5. **Adaptive Noise Cancellation:** Used in hearing aids to subtract ambient noise from the desired signal — a direct application of LTI system theory with time-varying coefficients.
