topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Time Shifting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Time Shifting in Biomedical Signals

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Time Shifting** is a fundamental signal transformation operation in which a signal $x(t)$ (continuous-time) or $x[n]$ (discrete-time) is translated along the horizontal time axis by a fixed amount $t_0$ (or $n_0$), without altering its amplitude, shape, energy, or any other intrinsic characteristic.

Mathematically, the time-shifted version of a continuous-time signal is given by:

$$y(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

And for a discrete-time signal:

$$y[n] = x[n - n_0]$$

The parameter $t_0 \in \mathbb{R}$ (or $n_0 \in \mathbb{Z}$) is called the **shift amount** or **time shift constant**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - If $t_0 > 0$ (positive shift) → the signal is shifted to the **right** → this represents a **DELAY** (the signal occurs later in time).
> - If $t_0 < 0$ (negative shift) → the signal is shifted to the **left** → this represents an **ADVANCE** (the signal occurs earlier in time).

> [!NOTE]
> Time shifting is a **memory-less algebraic operation** on the independent variable (time). It does NOT modify the dependent variable (amplitude) of the signal. The vertical axis (amplitude) remains completely unchanged.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have recorded a patient's **ECG (Electrocardiogram)** signal on a strip of paper. Now, the doctor wants to compare this ECG with another patient's ECG, but the second recording started exactly **2 seconds later** than the first one.

What you essentially do is **slide the entire waveform horizontally** by 2 seconds so that both signals line up properly — this sliding action is precisely what **Time Shifting** does mathematically.

- **Right Shift (Delay)** → Imagine pressing the "play" button on a video 3 seconds late. Everything you see is delayed by 3 seconds.
- **Left Shift (Advance)** → Imagine fast-forwarding backward in time. The signal appears earlier than its original occurrence.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** *"Positive shift → goes to the RIGHT → like reading a clock that is behind schedule (DELAYED). Negative shift → goes to the LEFT → like reading a clock that is ahead of schedule (ADVANCED)."*

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Time Shifting

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous-time shift | $t_0$ | **seconds (s)** | Real-valued scalar |
| Discrete-time shift | $n_0$ | **samples** | Integer-valued scalar |
| Delay | $t_0 > 0$ | s | Right shift |
| Advance | $t_0 < 0$ | s | Left shift |
| Zero shift | $t_0 = 0$ | s | Identity operation $y(t) = x(t)$ |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

Let us analyze how the time-shifting operation $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$ works internally:

1. **Identify the original signal $x(t)$** — Note the location of important features (peaks, zero crossings, edges) along the time axis.
2. **Apply the substitution $t \rightarrow (t - t_0)$** — Every occurrence of $t$ inside $x(\cdot)$ is replaced by $(t - t_0)$.
3. **Evaluate the new function** — For each new time instant $t$, we look up the value of the original signal at an earlier (or later) instant $(t - t_0)$.
4. **Plot the new waveform** — The entire shape is preserved; only the horizontal positions of all features are translated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The value that $x(t)$ had at $t = t_1$ now appears in $y(t)$ at the new time $t_2 = t_1 + t_0$. In other words, every point on the signal moves horizontally by exactly $t_0$ units.

### 2.2 Properties Preserved Under Time Shifting

The following signal properties remain **invariant** under the time-shifting operation:

- **Shape of the waveform** — unchanged.
- **Amplitude (peak value)** — unchanged.
- **Energy** $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ — **invariant** for finite-energy signals.
- **Power** $P = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^{T} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ — **invariant** for power signals.
- **RMS value** — unchanged.
- **Autocorrelation function** — $R_{xx}(\tau)$ is **shift-invariant**.
- **Spectral magnitude** $\vert X(f) \vert$ — **invariant** (only phase changes).
- **Duration / Width** of the signal — unchanged.

### 2.3 Effect on Energy (Detailed Proof Concept)

If $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$, then by the substitution $u = t - t_0 \Rightarrow du = dt$:

$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert y(t) \vert^2 dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t - t_0) \vert^2 dt$$

Substituting $u = t - t_0$:

$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(u) \vert^2 du = E_x$$

Hence, **energy is preserved** under time shifting.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Operation | Continuous-Time Formula | Discrete-Time Formula | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Right Shift (Delay) | $y(t) = x(t - t_0), \; t_0 > 0$ | $y[n] = x[n - n_0], \; n_0 > 0$ | Signal appears later |
| 2 | Left Shift (Advance) | $y(t) = x(t + t_0), \; t_0 > 0$ | $y[n] = x[n + n_0], \; n_0 > 0$ | Signal appears earlier |
| 3 | Identity (No Shift) | $y(t) = x(t), \; t_0 = 0$ | $y[n] = x[n], \; n_0 = 0$ | No change |
| 4 | Energy Invariance | $E_y = E_x$ | $E_y = E_x$ | Preserved |
| 5 | Power Invariance | $P_y = P_x$ | $P_y = P_x$ | Preserved |
| 6 | Time-axis mapping | Original at $t_1$ → New at $t_1 + t_0$ | Original at $n_1$ → New at $n_1 + n_0$ | Horizontal translation |
| 7 | Delay operator | $T_{t_0}\{x(t)\} = x(t-t_0)$ | $T_{n_0}\{x[n]\} = x[n-n_0]$ | — |
| 8 | Linearity | $T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a T\{x_1\} + b T\{x_2\}$ | Same as continuous | Linear system |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Biomedical Field

Time shifting is extensively used in:

- **ECG Signal Alignment** — Synchronizing multiple lead recordings (Lead I, II, III) for accurate cardiac diagnosis.
- **EEG Coherence Analysis** — Aligning signals from different scalp electrodes to study brain connectivity.
- **Propagation Delay Removal** — Compensating for sensor-to-acquisition-system latency in patient monitoring.
- **Template Matching in QRS Detection** — Shifting a reference QRS template to align with the incoming heartbeat.
- **Phonocardiogram (PCG) Synchronization** — Aligning heart sound recordings with ECG for valve-disease analysis.
- **Hearing Aids & Cochlear Implants** — Compensating for processing delays so output matches natural perception.
- **Averaging of Evoked Potentials** — Aligning repeated stimulus responses before averaging to enhance SNR.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Continuous-Time Time Shifting

**Statement:** Given a signal $x(t)$, derive the expression for the time-shifted signal $y(t)$ delayed by $t_0$ seconds.

**Step 1 — Define the original signal.**
Let $x(t)$ be a generic continuous-time signal defined for all $t \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Step 2 — Define the shift operator.**
The time-shift operator $T_{t_0}$ acts on $x(t)$ as:

$$T_{t_0}\{x(t)\} = x(t - t_0)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the value transfer principle.**
Consider a specific feature of $x(t)$ at $t = t_1$, where the value is $x(t_1) = A$. In the shifted signal $y(t)$, this same value $A$ must appear at a new time $t_2$ such that:

$$y(t_2) = x(t_2 - t_0) = A \Rightarrow t_2 - t_0 = t_1 \Rightarrow t_2 = t_1 + t_0$$

This proves that **every point shifts by exactly $+t_0$ on the time axis**.

**Step 4 — Formal statement.**
The time-shifted signal is:

$$\boxed{y(t) = x(t - t_0)}$$

**Step 5 — Energy invariance (exhaustive derivation).**

$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert y(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

Substitute $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$:

$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t - t_0) \vert^2 \, dt$$

Apply the change of variable: let $u = t - t_0$, so $du = dt$, and when $t \to -\infty$, $u \to -\infty$; when $t \to \infty$, $u \to \infty$:

$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(u) \vert^2 \, du = E_x$$

**Conclusion:** $\; E_y = E_x$. The energy of the signal is **completely preserved** under time shifting. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Discrete-Time Time Shifting

**Statement:** Given $x[n]$, find $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$.

**Step 1 — Define the discrete signal.**
$x[n]$ is defined for integer indices $n \in \mathbb{Z}$.

**Step 2 — Apply the discrete shift.**
For delay ($n_0 > 0$):

$$y[n] = x[n - n_0]$$

**Step 3 — Index mapping.**
The sample that was originally at index $n_1$ now appears at index $n_2$:

$$n_2 = n_1 + n_0$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example.**
Let $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$ with $x[0] = 1, x[1] = 2, x[2] = 3, x[3] = 4, x[4] = 5$.

Apply $n_0 = 2$ (delay by 2 samples):

$$y[n] = x[n - 2]$$

So $y[0] = x[-2] = 0$, $y[1] = x[-1] = 0$, $y[2] = x[0] = 1$, $y[3] = x[1] = 2$, $y[4] = x[2] = 3$, $y[5] = x[3] = 4$, $y[6] = x[4] = 5$.

Hence:

$$y[n] = \{0, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}$$

The signal is delayed by 2 samples; the first 2 samples are zero (causal padding with zero).

---

### 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example — Biomedical Context

**Problem:** An ECG signal $x(t)$ has its R-peak (the tallest spike) at $t = 0.4$ seconds. After passing through an acquisition system, the R-peak is observed at $t = 0.6$ seconds due to a system delay of $0.2$ s. Express the delayed signal mathematically.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify the shift amount.
The system introduces a delay of $0.2$ s, so:

$$t_0 = +0.2 \; \text{seconds} \;(\text{positive} \Rightarrow \text{right shift})$$

**Step 2:** Write the mathematical expression.

$$y(t) = x(t - 0.2)$$

**Step 3:** Verify the R-peak position.
Original R-peak at $t_1 = 0.4$ s.
New R-peak position:

$$t_2 = t_1 + t_0 = 0.4 + 0.2 = 0.6 \; \text{s} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4:** State the compensation operation.
To restore alignment, the engineer must apply a **time advance** of $0.2$ s:

$$x_{compensated}(t) = y(t + 0.2)$$

**Result:** The R-peak is now back at $t = 0.4$ s in the compensated signal. $\blacksquare$

---

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Time Shifting System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x of t] --> B[Time Shift Operator T sub t0]
    B --> C[Output Signal y of t equals x of t minus t0]
    D[Shift Constant t0 greater than 0] --> B
    E[Shift Constant t0 less than 0] --> B
    F[Shift Constant t0 equals 0 Identity] --> B
```

### 4.2 Detailed Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Orig[Original Biomedical Signal x of t] --> Step1[Identify Key Features Peaks Zero Crossings Edges]
    Step1 --> Step2[Apply Time Shift Operator T sub t0]
    Step2 --> DelayCheck{Is t0 positive}
    DelayCheck -- Yes Right Shift Delay --> RightSig[Output Signal y of t equals x of t minus t0 Delayed]
    DelayCheck -- No Left Shift Advance --> LeftSig[Output Signal y of t equals x of t plus mod t0 Advanced]
    RightSig --> Use1[ECG Lead Alignment]
    RightSig --> Use2[QRS Template Matching]
    LeftSig --> Use3[Latency Compensation]
    LeftSig --> Use4[Predictive Filtering]
```

### 4.3 Graphical Interpretation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Before [Original x of t]
        A1[Feature at t equals 0]
        A2[Feature at t equals 1]
        A3[Feature at t equals 2]
    end
    subgraph After [Shifted y of t equals x of t minus 1]
        B1[Feature now at t equals 1]
        B2[Feature now at t equals 2]
        B3[Feature now at t equals 3]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B2
    A3 --> B3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above diagrams represent the **logical flow** of the time-shifting operation. In an examination, students are expected to draw a clear before-and-after plot showing the original waveform and the shifted waveform with the shift amount $t_0$ explicitly labeled using arrows on the time axis.

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
*Define the time-shifting operation on a continuous-time signal $x(t)$. Explain with a suitable biomedical example why time alignment of signals is important in clinical diagnosis.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Time shifting is the operation of translating a signal $x(t)$ along the horizontal time axis by an amount $t_0$ without changing its amplitude characteristics. The shifted signal is given by:

$$y(t) = x(t - t_0)$$

If $t_0 > 0$, the signal is delayed (shifted right); if $t_0 < 0$, the signal is advanced (shifted left).

**Biomedical Example (1 mark):** In multi-lead ECG recording, the 12 leads do not acquire the cardiac signal simultaneously due to electrode impedance and acquisition latency. Time shifting is used to align the QRS complexes across all 12 leads so that the cardiologist can accurately interpret the timing relationships between leads — crucial for detecting bundle branch blocks and myocardial infarction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*State any three properties of a signal that remain invariant under time shifting. Justify briefly.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The following properties are preserved under time shifting $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$:

1. **Shape of the waveform** — Time shifting is a pure horizontal translation; vertical structure remains identical. **(1 mark)**

2. **Energy of the signal** — Since $E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t - t_0) \vert^2 dt = E_x$ (by substitution $u = t - t_0$), energy is invariant. **(1 mark)**

3. **Power of the signal** — Similarly, $P_y = P_x$ for periodic/power signals, since the time average over an infinite interval is unaffected by a finite shift. **(1 mark)**

*(Additionally, spectral magnitude $\vert X(f) \vert$ and autocorrelation $R_{xx}(\tau)$ are also invariant, but only three were asked.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3, Choice A]**

**(a)** Consider the continuous-time biomedical signal:

$$x(t) = \begin{cases} 2, & 0 \leq t \leq 4 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

This represents a rectangular pulse of amplitude 2 V (analog voltage from a pacemaker output monitor) with duration 4 seconds.

**(i)** Sketch the original signal $x(t)$ clearly labeling the time axis.

**(ii)** Now consider the time-shifted signal $y(t) = x(t - 3)$. Sketch $y(t)$ and identify the new boundaries of the pulse. Verify mathematically that the energy is preserved. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the discrete-time ECG amplitude samples $x[n] = \{2, 4, 6, 4, 2\}$ where the index starts from $n = 0$:

**(i)** Compute the time-shifted sequence $y[n] = x[n - 2]$ and $z[n] = x[n + 1]$. Show both sequences with their indices.

**(ii)** Determine whether the discrete-time signal is causal before and after the shift of $n_0 = -1$. Justify your answer with reference to the KTU definition of causality. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Solution to (a)(i): [3.5 Marks]**

The original signal $x(t)$ is a rectangular pulse:

- $x(t) = 2$ for $0 \leq t \leq 4$
- $x(t) = 0$ elsewhere

The signal starts at $t = 0$ and ends at $t = 4$, with constant amplitude 2.

**Sketch (description for valuation):**
- Horizontal axis: $t$ (seconds), with markings at $0, 2, 4, 6$.
- Vertical axis: $x(t)$ (volts), with a horizontal line at amplitude 2.
- The line begins at $t = 0$, stays at 2 until $t = 4$, then drops to 0.

---

**Solution to (a)(ii): [3.5 Marks]**

For $y(t) = x(t - 3)$, the substitution $t \to t - 3$ shifts the entire pulse to the right by 3 seconds.

**New boundaries:**
The original pulse exists for $0 \leq t \leq 4$. After shifting, it exists for $0 \leq (t - 3) \leq 4$, which gives $3 \leq t \leq 7$.

So $y(t) = 2$ for $3 \leq t \leq 7$, and $y(t) = 0$ elsewhere.

**Sketch (description):**
- Same rectangular pulse of height 2, but now from $t = 3$ to $t = 7$.

**Energy Verification:**

$$E_x = \int_{0}^{4} (2)^2 dt = \int_{0}^{4} 4 \, dt = 4 \times 4 = 16 \; \text{J}$$

$$E_y = \int_{3}^{7} (2)^2 dt = \int_{3}^{7} 4 \, dt = 4 \times 4 = 16 \; \text{J}$$

Since $E_x = E_y = 16$ J, **energy is preserved**. $\checkmark$ (This is a special case where energy invariance can be verified directly because the integrand is constant.)

In the general case, energy invariance follows from the change of variable $u = t - t_0$:

$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t - t_0) \vert^2 dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(u) \vert^2 du = E_x$$

---

**Solution to (b)(i): [3.5 Marks]**

Original sequence (with $n = 0$ as the first index):

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x[n]$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | 4 | 2 |

**For $y[n] = x[n - 2]$ (delay by 2 samples):**

The value at index $n$ in $y$ comes from index $(n - 2)$ in $x$.

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y[n] = x[n-2]$ | $x[-2]=0$ | $x[-1]=0$ | $x[0]=2$ | $x[1]=4$ | $x[2]=6$ | $x[3]=4$ | $x[4]=2$ |

So $y[n] = \{0, 0, 2, 4, 6, 4, 2\}$ — the original sequence is **delayed** by 2 samples, with two leading zeros (causal zero-padding).

**For $z[n] = x[n + 1]$ (advance by 1 sample):**

| $n$ | -1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $z[n] = x[n+1]$ | $x[0]=2$ | $x[1]=4$ | $x[2]=6$ | $x[3]=4$ | $x[4]=2$ |

So $z[n] = \{2, 4, 6, 4, 2\}$ but starting from index $n = -1$. The sequence is **advanced** by 1 sample.

---

**Solution to (b)(ii): [3.5 Marks]**

**KTU Definition of Causality:** A discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is **causal** if $x[n] = 0$ for all $n < 0$.

**Original signal $x[n]$:**
$x[n]$ is non-zero for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$ and zero for $n < 0$. Therefore, $x[n]$ is **CAUSAL**. $\checkmark$

**After applying shift $n_0 = -1$, consider $w[n] = x[n - (-1)] = x[n + 1]$:**

We already computed $z[n] = x[n + 1]$ in part (b)(i). It has a non-zero value at $n = -1$ (since $z[-1] = x[0] = 2$).

Since $w[-1] = 2 \neq 0$, the shifted signal is **NON-CAUSAL**.

**Justification (KTU Standard):** When a causal signal is advanced (left shift, $n_0 < 0$), the resulting signal acquires non-zero values for $n < 0$, violating the causality condition. Conversely, delaying (right shift, $n_0 > 0$) a non-causal signal can make it causal by zero-padding the negative indices.

**Conclusion:** Time shifting can **change the causality** of a signal. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 3, Choice B]**

**(a)** A biomedical sensor produces the following continuous-time signal:

$$x(t) = e^{-2t} \cdot u(t)$$

where $u(t)$ is the unit step function.

**(i)** Determine the time-shifted signal $y(t) = x(t - 5)$ and clearly state whether this represents a delay or an advance. Sketch both $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ on the same axis.

**(ii)** Compute the energy of $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ over $t \in [0, \infty)$. Show that the energies are equal, verifying the energy-invariance property of time shifting. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the discrete-time biomedical signal:

$$x[n] = \begin{cases} n, & 0 \leq n \leq 3 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**(i)** Plot $x[n]$ as a stem plot. Then plot $y[n] = x[n - 2]$ and $z[n] = x[n + 2]$ on the same figure with proper labeling.

**(ii)** If this signal represents the envelope of a recorded nerve impulse response, explain in 4–5 lines how time shifting helps in synchronizing this recording with a reference template. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Solution to (a)(i): [3.5 Marks]**

The original signal is:

$$x(t) = e^{-2t} \cdot u(t) = \begin{cases} e^{-2t}, & t \geq 0 \\ 0, & t < 0 \end{cases}$$

This is a decaying exponential starting at $t = 0$ with initial value $x(0) = 1$ and decreasing toward 0 as $t \to \infty$.

**Time-shifted signal $y(t) = x(t - 5)$:**

By substitution, the shifted signal exists where $(t - 5) \geq 0$, i.e., for $t \geq 5$:

$$y(t) = x(t - 5) = e^{-2(t - 5)} \cdot u(t - 5) = \begin{cases} e^{-2(t-5)}, & t \geq 5 \\ 0, & t < 5 \end{cases}$$

**Interpretation:** Since the shift constant is $t_0 = +5 > 0$, this is a **DELAY** (right shift) by 5 seconds. The exponential now begins at $t = 5$ instead of $t = 0$.

**Sketch description:**
- $x(t)$: starts at $(0, 1)$ and decays exponentially toward 0.
- $y(t)$: identical shape, but shifted right so it starts at $(5, 1)$ and decays toward 0.

---

**Solution to (a)(ii): [3.5 Marks]**

**Energy of $x(t)$:**

$$E_x = \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert e^{-2t} \vert^2 dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt$$

$$E_x = \left[ \frac{e^{-4t}}{-4} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = 0 - \left( \frac{-1}{4} \right) = \frac{1}{4} \; \text{J}$$

**Energy of $y(t)$:**

$$E_y = \int_{5}^{\infty} \vert e^{-2(t-5)} \vert^2 dt = \int_{5}^{\infty} e^{-4(t-5)} \, dt$$

Substitute $u = t - 5$, $du = dt$; when $t = 5$, $u = 0$; when $t \to \infty$, $u \to \infty$:

$$E_y = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4u} \, du = \frac{1}{4} \; \text{J}$$

**Conclusion:** $E_x = E_y = \frac{1}{4}$ J. Energy is preserved under time shifting. $\blacksquare$

---

**Solution to (b)(i): [3.5 Marks]**

**Original signal $x[n]$:**

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x[n]$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |

This is a **ramp signal** (linear increase from 0 to 3) for $0 \leq n \leq 3$, and 0 elsewhere.

**Delayed signal $y[n] = x[n - 2]$ (delay by 2):**

| $n$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y[n]$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |

The ramp now spans $n = 2$ to $n = 5$.

**Advanced signal $z[n] = x[n + 2]$ (advance by 2):**

| $n$ | -2 | -1 | 0 | 1 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $z[n]$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 |

The ramp now starts at $n = -2$ and ends at $n = 1$ (non-causal).

**Stem plot description:**
- Three stem plots on the same figure.
- $x[n]$: stems at $n=0,1,2,3$ with heights $0,1,2,3$.
- $y[n]$: stems at $n=2,3,4,5$ with heights $0,1,2,3$ (delayed by 2).
- $z[n]$: stems at $n=-2,-1,0,1$ with heights $0,1,2,3$ (advanced by 2).

---

**Solution to (b)(ii): [3.5 Marks]**

**Model Answer (4–5 lines as required by KTU):**

Time shifting is crucial for synchronizing nerve impulse recordings with a reference template in clinical neurophysiology. The recorded nerve response $x[n]$ is first compared with a stored reference template $r[n]$ using cross-correlation. The lag $n_0$ at which the cross-correlation peak occurs indicates the temporal mismatch between the two signals. To align the recording with the template, the engineer applies a compensating time shift: $x_{aligned}[n] = x[n - n_0]$ (if $n_0 > 0$) or $x_{aligned}[n] = x[n + n_0]$ (if $n_0 < 0$). This ensures that the peak of the nerve response coincides with the peak of the reference, enabling accurate latency measurements which are critical for diagnosing neuropathies such as carpal tunnel syndrome or multiple sclerosis.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently write $x(t+5)$ for a delay and $x(t-5)$ for an advance. **Remember:** $+5$ inside the argument → right shift → **DELAY**. Many KTU answer scripts lose marks for this exact error.
>
> 2. **Confusing Time Shifting with Time Scaling (–1 mark):** Time shifting **adds/subtracts** a constant inside the argument. Time scaling **multiplies** the argument by a constant ($x(2t)$ or $x(t/2)$). These are completely different operations. Do not mix them up.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Update Boundaries (–1 mark):** When sketching the shifted signal, students often forget to recompute the start and end times of pulses. Always rewrite the inequalities with the new variable.
>
> 4. **Causality Trap in Discrete-Time (–1 mark):** A right-shift (delay) of a causal signal keeps it causal, but a left-shift (advance) makes it non-causal. The reverse is not always true without context. Be precise.
>
> 5. **Energy/Power Calculation Errors (–1 mark):** When verifying energy invariance using substitution $u = t - t_0$, students forget to change the limits of integration. The limits remain $(-\infty, \infty)$ because the shift is finite.
>
> 6. **Not Labeling Units:** $t_0$ is in **seconds**; $n_0$ is in **samples**. Mixing them up is a common KTU valuation pitfall.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Time Shifting (Module 3):**
>
> ☐ **Core Formula (CT):** $y(t) = x(t - t_0)$
>
> ☐ **Core Formula (DT):** $y[n] = x[n - n_0]$
>
> ☐ **Sign Rule:** $t_0 > 0$ → Right shift → DELAY ; $t_0 < 0$ → Left shift → ADVANCE
>
> ☐ **Index Mapping:** A feature at $t_1$ in $x(t)$ appears at $t_1 + t_0$ in $y(t)$.
>
> ☐ **Invariants:** Energy $E$, Power $P$, Shape, Amplitude, RMS, Autocorrelation, $\vert X(f) \vert$ are **all preserved** under time shifting.
>
> ☐ **Spectral Effect:** Only the **phase spectrum** changes; magnitude spectrum is preserved.
>
> ☐ **Linearity:** Time shift is a **linear, time-invariant** operation.
>
> ☐ **Biomedical Applications:** ECG lead alignment, EEG coherence, QRS template matching, evoked-response averaging, hearing-aid delay compensation.
>
> ☐ **Causality:** A causal signal remains causal under **delay** ($t_0 > 0$) but becomes **non-causal** under **advance** ($t_0 < 0$).
>
> ☐ **Distinguish from:** Time scaling ($x(at)$), time reversal ($x(-t)$), and amplitude scaling ($A \cdot x(t)$) — these are different operations.
>
> ☐ **Exam Tip:** Always **sketch** the original and shifted waveforms side by side with clear axis labels — KTU examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Signal Scaling

In the context of **Biomedical Signal Processing**, **Scaling** refers to the systematic transformation of a biomedical signal along two independent axes: the **time axis** (horizontal scaling) and the **amplitude axis** (vertical scaling). It is one of the fundamental independent variable transformations applied to physiological signals such as **ECG (Electrocardiogram)**, **EEG (Electroencephalogram)**, and **EMG (Electromyogram)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> Scaling is defined as the operation performed on a continuous-time or discrete-time signal $x(t)$ such that the signal is either compressed, expanded, or amplified mathematically. The two principal forms are **Time Scaling** ($x(t) \rightarrow x(at)$) and **Amplitude Scaling** ($x(t) \rightarrow A \cdot x(t)$).

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Types of Scaling

**A. Time Scaling (Horizontal Scaling):**
The independent variable $t$ is multiplied by a scaling constant $a$.
$$x(t) \rightarrow x(at) \quad \text{or} \quad x(t) \rightarrow x(t/a)$$
where $a$ is a **non-zero real scaling constant** ($a \neq 0$).

**B. Amplitude Scaling (Vertical Scaling):**
The dependent variable (signal amplitude) is multiplied by a constant $A$.
$$x(t) \rightarrow A \cdot x(t)$$
where $A$ is a **real scaling constant** ($A \neq 0$).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The ECG Playback Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: The "Medical Monitor Playback" Concept**
>
> Imagine you are a cardiologist reviewing an **ECG recording** of a patient. You have two controls on the monitor:
>
> 1. **Playback Speed Control (Time Scaling):** If you fast-forward the ECG playback to $2\times$ speed, the entire waveform (P-QRS-T complex) appears compressed — what took 1 second now takes 0.5 seconds. This is **time compression** ($a > 1$). Conversely, slow-motion playback ($0.5\times$) is **time expansion** ($0 < a < 1$).
>
> 2. **Gain Control Knob (Amplitude Scaling):** Turning the vertical gain knob amplifies the R-wave peaks so a 1 mV signal now shows as 2 mV. This is **amplitude scaling** ($A > 1$). Reducing the gain attenuates the signal ($0 < A < 1$).
>
> In both cases, the underlying cardiac event hasn't changed — only how we **observe** and **represent** it on the screen.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Sampling Frequency ($f_s$):** Typically **250 Hz to 1000 Hz** for ECG, **100 Hz to 500 Hz** for EEG.
- **Signal Bandwidth:** ECG occupies **0.05 Hz – 150 Hz**, EEG occupies **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz**.
- **Scaling Constant $a$:** Dimensionless, with $a > 0$ for non-reversing transformations.
- **Amplitude Scaling Factor $A$:** Dimensionless, can be positive or negative.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always clarify in KTU exams whether the scaling is on the **time axis** or the **amplitude axis** — these are independent operations and frequently confused.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Time Scaling — Operational Logic

The general operation of time scaling transforms a signal $x(t)$ into a new signal $y(t)$ where:
$$y(t) = x(at) \quad \text{with} \quad a \in \mathbb{R}, a \neq 0$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Identify the original event at time $t_0$:** The original signal $x(t)$ has a feature (e.g., R-peak) at time $t = t_0$.
- **Step 2 — Apply the transformation:** In the scaled signal $x(at)$, the same feature now occurs when $at = t_0$, i.e., at $t = t_0/a$.
- **Step 3 — Interpret the shift:** The feature has been relocated on the time axis based on the value of $a$.

**Three Critical Cases of Time Scaling:**

- **Case 1: $a > 1$ → Time Compression**
  The signal is compressed along the time axis. All features occur **faster**. The duration of the signal reduces by factor $1/a$.
  $$\text{New duration} = \frac{\text{Original duration}}{a}$$

- **Case 2: $0 < a < 1$ → Time Expansion**
  The signal is stretched along the time axis. All features occur **slower**. The duration of the signal increases by factor $1/a$.
  $$\text{New duration} = \frac{\text{Original duration}}{a}$$

- **Case 3: $a < 0$ → Time Scaling with Reversal**
  The signal is both reversed in time and scaled. For example, $a = -2$ reverses and compresses.

### 2.2 Amplitude Scaling — Operational Logic

The general operation of amplitude scaling is:
$$y(t) = A \cdot x(t) \quad \text{with} \quad A \in \mathbb{R}, A \neq 0$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1 — Identify amplitude at $t_0$:** The original signal has amplitude $x(t_0)$.
- **Step 2 — Multiply by constant $A$:** New amplitude becomes $A \cdot x(t_0)$.
- **Step 3 — Preserve time-axis:** The time at which features occur is **unchanged**.

**Three Critical Cases of Amplitude Scaling:**

- **Case 1: $A > 1$ → Amplification**
  Signal magnitude is increased. Power scales by $A^2$.
  $$P_{\text{new}} = A^2 \cdot P_{\text{original}}$$

- **Case 2: $0 < A < 1$ → Attenuation**
  Signal magnitude is reduced. Useful when biomedical signal exceeds ADC input range.

- **Case 3: $A < 0$ → Inversion (Polarity Reversal)**
  Signal is inverted vertically. Common in **differential ECG lead systems**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the EXAM-READY formula reference for Scaling in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.**

| Operation Type | Mathematical Form | Effect on Time Axis | Effect on Amplitude | Biomedical Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time Compression | $y(t) = x(at), \; a > 1$ | Compressed (faster) | Unchanged | ECG playback at 2x speed |
| Time Expansion | $y(t) = x(at), \; 0 < a < 1$ | Expanded (slower) | Unchanged | EEG slow-motion analysis |
| Time Reversal | $y(t) = x(-t)$ | Mirrored about $t = 0$ | Unchanged | Backward signal play |
| Amplitude Amplification | $y(t) = A \cdot x(t), \; A > 1$ | Unchanged | Magnified | Boosting weak EMG signal |
| Amplitude Attenuation | $y(t) = A \cdot x(t), \; 0 < A < 1$ | Unchanged | Reduced | ADC range matching |
| Amplitude Inversion | $y(t) = A \cdot x(t), \; A < 0$ | Unchanged | Flipped polarity | Lead-II inversion correction |
| Combined Scaling | $y(t) = A \cdot x(at)$ | Compressed/Expanded | Scaled | Full display rescaling |

**Key Boundary Conditions for KTU Problems:**

- For $y(t) = x(at)$: Original event at $t_0$ → New event at $t_0 / a$.
- For $y(t) = A \cdot x(t)$: Original amplitude $x_0$ → New amplitude $A \cdot x_0$.
- Power relationship: $P_y = A^2 \cdot P_x$ for amplitude scaling only.
- Energy relationship: $E_y = A^2 \cdot E_x$ for amplitude scaling only.
- For time scaling alone: $E_y = \frac{E_x}{\vert a \vert}$ (energy inversely scales).

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Biomedical Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Applications of Scaling:**
>
> 1. **Telemetry and Teleradiology:** Patient ECG data transmitted over low-bandwidth channels is time-expanded at the receiving end to recover original duration.
> 2. **Hearing Aid Signal Processing:** Weak auditory signals are amplitude-amplified using $A > 1$ scaling before output to the speaker.
> 3. **Fetal ECG Extraction:** Maternal ECG is amplitude-scaled to match fetal ECG levels for cancellation algorithms.
> 4. **Pacemaker Signal Conditioning:** R-wave detection circuits use amplitude scaling to normalize the input for consistent threshold detection.
> 5. **Medical Image Registration (1-D projections):** Time scaling aligns signals from different sampling rates for comparison.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Locating a Feature After Time Scaling

**Problem Setup:** A continuous-time biomedical signal $x(t)$ has an **R-peak** (the most prominent feature of an ECG) at $t_0 = 1.2$ seconds. The signal is time-scaled as $y(t) = x(2t)$. Determine the new time location of the R-peak.

**Derivation:**

$$y(t) = x(at) \quad \text{where} \quad a = 2$$

The R-peak in the original signal satisfies: $x(t_0) = x(1.2)$ is the peak amplitude.

In the transformed signal, the R-peak occurs when:
$$at_{\text{new}} = t_0$$

$$2 \cdot t_{\text{new}} = 1.2$$

$$t_{\text{new}} = \frac{1.2}{2} = 0.6 \text{ seconds}$$

**Conclusion:** The R-peak originally at $t = 1.2$ s now appears at $t = 0.6$ s. Since $a = 2 > 1$, this is a **time compression**, consistent with theory.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy of a Time-Scaled Signal

**Problem Setup:** A finite-energy biomedical signal $x(t)$ has total energy $E_x$. Find the energy of the time-scaled signal $y(t) = x(at)$.

**Derivation:**

By definition, the energy of $y(t)$ is:
$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert y(t) \vert^2 \, dt$$

Substituting $y(t) = x(at)$:
$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(at) \vert^2 \, dt$$

Apply substitution: let $\tau = at$, so $d\tau = a \, dt$, which gives $dt = \frac{d\tau}{a}$.

**When $a > 0$:**
$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2 \, \frac{d\tau}{a} = \frac{1}{a} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2 \, d\tau$$

$$E_y = \frac{E_x}{a}$$

**When $a < 0$:** The integration limits swap, but $\vert a \vert$ governs the result:
$$E_y = \frac{E_x}{\vert a \vert}$$

**Generalized Result:**
$$E_y = \frac{E_x}{\vert a \vert}$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Time compression ($a > 1$) **reduces** total energy. Time expansion ($0 < a < 1$) **increases** total energy. This is because the signal energy is spread over different time durations.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Combined Amplitude and Time Scaling

**Problem Setup:** Given $y(t) = A \cdot x(at)$, derive the relationship between the energies of $x(t)$ and $y(t)$.

**Derivation:**

$$E_y = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert y(t) \vert^2 \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert A \cdot x(at) \vert^2 \, dt$$

$$E_y = A^2 \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(at) \vert^2 \, dt$$

Applying the same substitution $\tau = at$, $dt = \frac{d\tau}{\vert a \vert}$:
$$E_y = A^2 \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(\tau) \vert^2 \, \frac{d\tau}{\vert a \vert}$$

$$E_y = \frac{A^2}{\vert a \vert} \cdot E_x$$

**Final Combined Scaling Energy Relation:**
$$E_y = \frac{A^2}{\vert a \vert} \cdot E_x$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (KTU Typical 7-Mark Style):**
>
> A triangular biomedical pulse $x(t)$ is defined as:
> $$x(t) = \begin{cases} 1 - \vert t \vert, & \vert t \vert \leq 1 \\ 0, & \vert t \vert > 1 \end{cases}$$
>
> The original peak amplitude is $1$ at $t = 0$, and the duration is $2$ seconds. Determine the peak amplitude, new duration, and total energy of $y(t) = 3 \cdot x(4t)$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the scaling parameters:**
- $A = 3$ (amplitude scaling factor)
- $a = 4$ (time scaling factor)

**Step 2 — Compute the new peak amplitude:**
$$y_{\text{peak}} = A \cdot x_{\text{peak}} = 3 \cdot 1 = 3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the new duration:**
$$\text{New duration} = \frac{\text{Original duration}}{\vert a \vert} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the energy of the original signal:**
$$E_x = \int_{-1}^{1} (1 - \vert t \vert)^2 \, dt = 2 \int_{0}^{1} (1 - t)^2 \, dt$$

$$E_x = 2 \left[ -\frac{(1-t)^3}{3} \right]_0^1 = 2 \cdot \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \text{ joules}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the energy of the scaled signal using the derived formula:**
$$E_y = \frac{A^2}{\vert a \vert} \cdot E_x = \frac{3^2}{4} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{9}{4} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{18}{12} = \frac{3}{2} \text{ joules}$$

**Step 6 — Verification by direct integration:**
$$E_y = \int_{-0.25}^{0.25} (3(1 - \vert 4t \vert))^2 \, dt = 9 \int_{-0.25}^{0.25} (1 - 4\vert t \vert)^2 \, dt$$

$$E_y = 18 \int_{0}^{0.25} (1 - 4t)^2 \, dt = 18 \left[ -\frac{(1-4t)^3}{12} \right]_0^{0.25}$$

$$E_y = 18 \cdot \frac{1}{12} = \frac{18}{12} = \frac{3}{2} \text{ joules} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:** Peak amplitude $= 3$, new duration $= 0.5$ s, energy $= 1.5$ J.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Scaling Operations in a Biomedical Signal Processing Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src1[BIOELECTRIC SOURCE]:::src --> acq[Analog Acquisition Module]:::proc
    acq --> cond[Signal Conditioning Filter]:::proc
    cond --> gain{Amplitude Gain Control A}:::ctrl
    gain --> |A equals 1| bypass[Unity Gain Pass Through]:::proc
    gain --> |A greater than 1| amp[Amplification Stage]:::proc
    gain --> |A between 0 and 1| atten[Attenuation Stage]:::proc
    gain --> |A less than 0| inv[Polarity Inversion Stage]:::proc
    bypass --> tscl{Time Scaling Control a}:::ctrl
    amp --> tscl
    atten --> tscl
    inv --> tscl
    tscl --> |a greater than 1| comp[Time Compression Buffer]:::proc
    tscl --> |a between 0 and 1| exp[Time Expansion Interpolator]:::proc
    tscl --> |a equals 1| tpas[Time Identity Pass Through]:::proc
    comp --> adc[Analog to Digital Converter]:::out
    exp --> adc
    tpas --> adc
    adc --> dsp[Digital Signal Processor]:::out
    dsp --> disp[Clinical Display Output]:::out

    classDef src fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#0a1a2e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#2d6a9f,stroke:#1a3a5c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctrl fill:#d97706,stroke:#7a3e00,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#1b5e20,stroke:#0a3009,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram illustrates how a raw bioelectric signal (e.g., ECG) passes through amplitude scaling (gain control block) followed by time scaling (playback rate control block) before digitization and clinical display. The orange decision blocks represent the multiplicative constants $A$ and $a$ that govern each scaling type.

### 4.2 Mathematical Mapping Flow for $y(t) = A \cdot x(at)$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigin[Original Signal x of t]:::in --> tstep1[Step 1 Locate Feature at t0]:::step
    tstep1 --> tstep2[Step 2 Compute New Time t new = t0 divided by a]:::step
    tstep2 --> tstep3[Step 3 Apply Time Map to All Features]:::step
    tstep3 --> tstep4[Step 4 Multiply Amplitude by A]:::step
    tstep4 --> sigout[Transformed Signal y of t equals A times x of at]:::out

    tstep4 --> eng[Energy E y equals A squared divided by absolute a times E x]:::formula

    classDef in fill:#37474f,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef step fill:#0277bd,stroke:#01365e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef out fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#0a3a0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef formula fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#2e0a4a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Effect-of-Scaling Comparison Table (Visual Reference)

| Scaling Parameter | Before Scaling (Visual Cue) | After Scaling (Visual Cue) |
|---|---|---|
| $a = 2, A = 1$ (Time Compression) | Wide waveform, slow peaks | Narrow waveform, fast peaks |
| $a = 0.5, A = 1$ (Time Expansion) | Narrow waveform, fast peaks | Wide waveform, slow peaks |
| $a = 1, A = 2$ (Amplitude Amplification) | Short vertical peaks | Tall vertical peaks |
| $a = 1, A = -1$ (Polarity Inversion) | Upward R-wave | Downward R-wave |
| $a = 2, A = 2$ (Combined) | Slow short signal | Fast tall signal |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle]**
**Define time scaling and amplitude scaling of a continuous-time signal. State the effect of each on the signal duration and amplitude respectively.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Time Scaling:** The operation that transforms a signal $x(t)$ into $x(at)$ where $a$ is a non-zero real constant, thereby changing the duration of the signal along the time axis.
>
> **Amplitude Scaling:** The operation that transforms a signal $x(t)$ into $A \cdot x(t)$ where $A$ is a non-zero real constant, thereby changing the magnitude of the signal along the amplitude axis.
>
> **Effect Comparison:**
> - Time scaling $x(at)$: Duration is multiplied by $\frac{1}{\vert a \vert}$, amplitude is **unchanged**.
> - Amplitude scaling $A \cdot x(t)$: Duration is **unchanged**, amplitude is multiplied by $A$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**
**A biomedical signal has an event at $t = 4$ s. If the signal is transformed as $y(t) = x(2t)$, where will the event occur in $y(t)$? What type of scaling is this?**

**Model Answer:**

> Given: $y(t) = x(at)$ with $a = 2$ and original event at $t_0 = 4$ s.
>
> The new event location is:
> $$t_{\text{new}} = \frac{t_0}{a} = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \text{ seconds}$$
>
> Since $a = 2 > 1$, this is **time compression** (signal is compressed along the time axis). The event now occurs at $t = 2$ s, half of the original time.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Define the two types of scaling operations performed on continuous-time signals. For each type, explain the effect of the scaling constant being greater than 1, between 0 and 1, and less than 0. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A continuous-time biomedical signal $x(t)$ has total energy $E_x = 12$ J. The signal is transformed as $y(t) = 4 \cdot x(2t)$. Calculate:
  - (i) The new peak amplitude if the original peak was $5$ mV.
  - (ii) The new signal duration if the original duration was $3$ s.
  - (iii) The total energy of $y(t)$ using the energy scaling formula.
  - (iv) Verify your answer to (iii) by direct integration if $x(t) = 3e^{-2t}u(t)$. **(7 Marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation:**

> **Type 1: Time Scaling** $y(t) = x(at)$
>
> - **$a > 1$:** Time compression. The signal is squeezed along the time axis. All features occur in $\frac{1}{a}$ times the original duration. Example: $x(2t)$ means an event at $t_0$ moves to $\frac{t_0}{2}$.
> - **$0 < a < 1$:** Time expansion. The signal is stretched along the time axis. All features occur in $\frac{1}{a}$ times the original duration. Example: $x(0.5t)$ means an event at $t_0$ moves to $2t_0$.
> - **$a < 0$:** Time reversal combined with scaling. The signal is first reversed and then scaled by $\vert a \vert$. Example: $x(-2t)$ reverses and compresses.
>
> **Type 2: Amplitude Scaling** $y(t) = A \cdot x(t)$
>
> - **$A > 1$:** Amplification. The signal amplitude is multiplied by $A$, increasing the magnitude. Power increases by factor $A^2$.
> - **$0 < A < 1$:** Attenuation. The signal amplitude is reduced by factor $A$.
> - **$A < 0$:** Polarity inversion. The signal is multiplied by a negative constant, flipping it across the time axis. Common in differential biomedical lead systems.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

> Given: $A = 4$, $a = 2$, $E_x = 12$ J, original peak $x_p = 5$ mV, original duration $T_x = 3$ s.
>
> **(i) New Peak Amplitude:**
> $$y_p = A \cdot x_p = 4 \cdot 5 = 20 \text{ mV}$$
>
> **(ii) New Signal Duration:**
> $$T_y = \frac{T_x}{\vert a \vert} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ s}$$
>
> **(iii) Energy using scaling formula:**
> $$E_y = \frac{A^2}{\vert a \vert} \cdot E_x = \frac{4^2}{2} \cdot 12 = \frac{16}{2} \cdot 12 = 8 \cdot 12 = 96 \text{ J}$$
>
> **(iv) Verification by direct integration for $x(t) = 3e^{-2t}u(t)$:**
> $$E_x = \int_{0}^{\infty} (3e^{-2t})^2 \, dt = 9 \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt = 9 \cdot \frac{1}{4} = \frac{9}{4} \text{ J}$$
>
> Wait — this $E_x = 2.25$ J does not match the stated $12$ J. We use the formula directly with the **given** $E_x = 12$ J:
> $$E_y = \frac{A^2}{\vert a \vert} \cdot 12 = \frac{16}{2} \cdot 12 = 96 \text{ J}$$
>
> Verification by direct integration using $y(t) = 4 \cdot 3 e^{-2(2t)} u(2t) = 12 e^{-4t} u(t)$:
> $$E_y = \int_{0}^{\infty} (12 e^{-4t})^2 \, dt = 144 \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-8t} \, dt = 144 \cdot \frac{1}{8} = 18 \text{ J}$$
>
> The discrepancy arises because the verification integral computes the energy for a *different* $E_x$ than 12 J. The formula method is correct **only** when the stated $E_x$ is used. Hence, with $E_x = 12$ J as given, the answer is $E_y = 96$ J.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Differentiate between time scaling and time shifting operations. A signal $x(t)$ has a triangular pulse of duration 6 seconds with peak amplitude 4 V. The signal is then time-scaled by $a = 3$ and amplitude-scaled by $A = 0.5$. Compute the new duration, new peak amplitude, and the new total energy if the original signal energy is 48 J. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the discrete-time biomedical signal $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ with $n = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$:
  - (i) Plot $x[n]$ and $y[n] = x[2n]$.
  - (ii) Identify which operation is performed.
  - (iii) List the non-zero samples of $y[n]$ and their indices.
  - (iv) Determine if the operation preserves total energy. Justify with a brief calculation. **(7 Marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Differentiation and Computation:**

> **Difference between Time Scaling and Time Shifting:**
>
> | Aspect | Time Scaling $x(at)$ | Time Shifting $x(t-t_0)$ |
> |---|---|---|
> | Purpose | Change duration of signal | Shift signal in time |
> | Time-axis effect | Stretches or compresses | Translates left or right |
> | Parameter meaning | $a$ controls rate | $t_0$ controls shift amount |
> | Example | $x(2t)$: compress by 2 | $x(t-3)$: shift right by 3 |
>
> **Numerical Computations:**
>
> Given: $T_x = 6$ s, $x_p = 4$ V, $E_x = 48$ J, $a = 3$, $A = 0.5$.
>
> - **New duration:**
> $$T_y = \frac{T_x}{\vert a \vert} = \frac{6}{3} = 2 \text{ s}$$
>
> - **New peak amplitude:**
> $$y_p = A \cdot x_p = 0.5 \cdot 4 = 2 \text{ V}$$
>
> - **New total energy:**
> $$E_y = \frac{A^2}{\vert a \vert} \cdot E_x = \frac{(0.5)^2}{3} \cdot 48 = \frac{0.25}{3} \cdot 48 = 4 \text{ J}$$

**Part (b) — Discrete-Time Signal Analysis:**

> Given: $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ with indices $n = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}$.
>
> **(i) Plot of $x[n]$ and $y[n] = x[2n]$:**
>
> - $x[n]$: Triangular sequence peaking at $n = 0$ with value 3.
> - $y[n] = x[2n]$ evaluates $x$ at even indices only.
>
> | $n$ | $2n$ | $x[2n]$ |
> |---|---|---|
> | $-1$ | $-2$ | $1$ |
> | $0$  | $0$  | $3$ |
> | $1$  | $2$  | $1$ |
>
> **(ii) Operation identified:** $y[n] = x[2n]$ with $a = 2$ is **discrete-time compression** (down-sampling by factor 2).
>
> **(iii) Non-zero samples of $y[n]$:**
> $$y[n] = \{1, 3, 1\} \quad \text{at} \quad n = \{-1, 0, 1\}$$
>
> **(iv) Energy check:**
> $$E_x = 1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + 2^2 + 1^2 = 1 + 4 + 9 + 4 + 1 = 19$$
> $$E_y = 1^2 + 3^2 + 1^2 = 1 + 9 + 1 = 11$$
>
> Energy is **not preserved** in discrete-time down-sampling. The general rule $E_y = \frac{E_x}{\vert a \vert}$ applies to continuous-time signals. For discrete signals, energy conservation depends on whether samples are preserved or discarded.

---

### 5.1 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing time scaling with time shifting:** Students write $x(t-2)$ when the question asks for $x(2t)$. Remember: scaling **multiplies** the argument; shifting **subtracts/adds** to it.
>
> 2. **Forgetting absolute value in duration formula:** Writing $T_y = \frac{T_x}{a}$ instead of $\frac{T_x}{\vert a \vert}$. Duration is always positive.
>
> 3. **Sign error in energy formula:** Writing $E_y = \frac{A^2}{a}$ instead of $\frac{A^2}{\vert a \vert}$. Energy must be positive, so absolute value is mandatory.
>
> 4. **Confusing amplitude scaling with DC offset:** Adding a constant $C$ to $x(t)$ is **not** scaling. Scaling requires multiplicative constant $A$.
>
> 5. **Incorrect index mapping in discrete signals:** For $y[n] = x[an]$, sample $x[k]$ appears at $n = k/a$. Using $n = k \cdot a$ is the most common error.
>
> 6. **Mixing up energy and power relations:** Amplitude scaling scales energy by $A^2$. Time scaling scales energy by $\frac{1}{\vert a \vert}$. Combined: factor is $\frac{A^2}{\vert a \vert}$.

---

### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Scaling in Biomedical Signal Processing**
>
> - **Two Types of Scaling:** Time scaling $x(at)$ and amplitude scaling $A \cdot x(t)$.
> - **Time Compression:** $a > 1$ → signal squashed in time, duration shrinks by $\frac{1}{a}$.
> - **Time Expansion:** $0 < a < 1$ → signal stretched, duration grows by $\frac{1}{a}$.
> - **Amplitude Amplification:** $A > 1$ → signal magnitude grows, power scales by $A^2$.
> - **Amplitude Attenuation:** $0 < A < 1$ → signal magnitude shrinks.
> - **Polarity Inversion:** $A < 0$ → signal flipped about time axis (used in differential ECG).
> - **Time Reversal:** $a < 0$ → signal mirrored about $t = 0$ (used in matched filtering).
> - **Energy of Amplitude-Scaled Signal:** $E_y = A^2 \cdot E_x$.
> - **Energy of Time-Scaled Signal:** $E_y = \frac{E_x}{\vert a \vert}$.
> - **Energy of Combined Scaling:** $E_y = \frac{A^2}{\vert a \vert} \cdot E_x$.
> - **Biomedical Applications:** ECG playback rate adjustment, hearing aid amplification, fetal ECG extraction, pacemaker signal conditioning, ADC range matching.
> - **Discrete-Time Caveat:** Down-sampling by integer $a$ discards samples; energy is **not** simply scaled by $\frac{1}{\vert a \vert}$.
> - **Sampling Theorem Relevance:** Time scaling in discrete domain must respect Nyquist criterion to avoid aliasing.
> - **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** 3-mark direct questions, 7-mark sub-problems on energy and duration computation, 14-mark combined questions with verification steps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Time Reversal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Time Reversal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Time Reversal** is a fundamental signal transformation operation in which a discrete-time biomedical signal $x[n]$ (or continuous-time $x(t)$) is reflected about the vertical (amplitude) axis by replacing the independent time variable with its negative. The resulting time-reversed signal is mathematically denoted as $x[-n]$ (discrete) or $x(-t)$ (continuous).

In the context of biomedical signal processing (BSP), time reversal is a *linear, invertible* transformation used extensively for analyzing the inherent symmetry properties of physiological signals such as the **ECG (Electrocardiogram)**, **EEG (Electroencephalogram)**, and **EMG (Electromyogram)**. It is the operational backbone for *even-odd decomposition*, *cross-correlation* (since $x[n] \star y[n] = x[-n] * y[n]$), and the symmetry properties of the **Z-transform** and **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]  
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Rewind Tape" Analogy:**
> Imagine you have recorded a patient's heartbeat (ECG) on a magnetic tape recorder. If you play the tape backwards, every peak that originally appeared at $t = 0.4$ s now appears at $t = -0.4$ s. The waveform looks like a mirror reflection of itself when viewed in a vertical mirror placed at the origin.  
>  
> Mathematically, this "rewinding" is precisely what **time reversal** does. The signal's *energy* and *frequency content* are preserved, but its temporal order is flipped. Critically, *even-shaped signals* (like an idealized R-wave envelope) look identical after this operation, while *odd-shaped signals* (like a biphasic QRS deflection) are *inverted in amplitude* as well.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]  
> - **Sampling frequency ($f_s$):** Standard clinical value is **$\mathbf{360 \text{ Hz}}$** for ECG, **$\mathbf{500 \text{ Hz}}$** for EMG, and **$\mathbf{256 \text{ Hz}}$** for EEG.  
> - **Time-reversal index shift:** If original sample at index $n = k$ contains amplitude $x[k]$, the time-reversed counterpart is at $n = -k$.  
> - **Energy invariance:** The total energy of the signal is strictly preserved: $E_x = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x[-n] \vert^2$.

### 1.4 Why Time Reversal Matters in Biomedical Signal Processing

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Time reversal is **not a mere theoretical exercise**. It is a *working tool* in the following clinical DSP pipelines:  
> 1. **QRS Detection Algorithms:** Pan-Tompkins and matched filter detectors often correlate an inverted template of the QRS complex with the incoming ECG.  
> 2. **EEG Epilepsy Spike Detection:** Templates are time-reversed before matched filtering to handle polarity ambiguity.  
> 3. **Speech and Hearing Aids:** Cochlear implant DSP pipelines use time reversal in their adaptive feedback cancellation routines.  
> 4. **Auscultation (PCG):** Phonocardiogram murmurs (e.g., systolic vs. diastolic) are classified using symmetry-based features derived from even/odd decomposition.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Time Reversal — Step by Step

Let $x[n]$ be a discrete-time biomedical signal sampled at $f_s$. The time-reversed version $x_r[n]$ is constructed by executing the following logical pipeline:

1. **Step 1 — Index Reflection:**  
   Replace every occurrence of the time index $n$ with $-n$ in the functional form of the signal.  
   Example: $x[n] = \delta[n-3] \;\Longrightarrow\; x_r[n] = \delta[-n-3] = \delta[-(n+3)]$.

2. **Step 2 — Re-indexing via Shift Property:**  
   Use the identity $\delta[-n - k] = \delta[n + k]$ (impulse is an even function) to obtain a causal/standard form.  
   Result: $\delta[-n-3] = \delta[n+3]$, which is an impulse located at $n = -3$.

3. **Step 3 — Verification of Symmetry:**  
   - If $x_r[n] = x[n]$ for all $n$, the signal is **even**: $x_e[n] = \frac{1}{2}(x[n] + x[-n])$.  
   - If $x_r[n] = -x[n]$ for all $n$, the signal is **odd**: $x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}(x[n] - x[-n])$.  
   - Any arbitrary signal decomposes uniquely: $x[n] = x_e[n] + x_o[n]$.

4. **Step 4 — Cascade with Z-Transform:**  
   Apply the Z-transform definition. Because the summation index flips, we obtain a *reciprocal* in the Z-domain — this is the famous time-reversal property (detailed in §2.3).

5. **Step 5 — Cascade with DTFT:**  
   Substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$ yields a frequency-domain phase inversion. This is critical for matched filtering and spectral analysis.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why index reflection?** Because the *physical interpretation* of a signal depends on the order of samples; reversing the order is needed for backward-playback applications and for cross-correlation operations where the template is "slid" across the signal in reverse.
- **Why re-index using shift property?** Standard signal processing literature writes impulses in terms of positive shifts $\delta[n - k]$; conversion to $\delta[n + k]$ keeps the form canonical.
- **Why symmetry check?** A surprising number of physiological signals (e.g., the central R-wave of an ECG) are approximately symmetric, making even/odd decomposition a powerful feature-extraction tool.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The following table consolidates **every KTU-exam-relevant equation** on time reversal. Note the use of `\vert` and `\mid` in place of the vertical pipe symbol to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Property / Operation | Discrete-Time Expression $x[n] \rightarrow x[-n]$ | Continuous-Time Expression $x(t) \rightarrow x(-t)$ | Domain / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Basic Definition | $x_r[n] = x[-n]$ | $x_r(t) = x(-t)$ | Time Domain |
| 2 | Z-Transform Property | $X(z) \rightarrow X(z^{-1})$ | Not applicable directly | ROC inverts: $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$ becomes $1/r_1 < \vert z \vert < 1/r_2$ |
| 3 | DTFT Property | $X(e^{j\omega}) \rightarrow X(e^{-j\omega})$ | Not applicable directly | Phase is negated; magnitude unchanged |
| 4 | CTFT Property | Not applicable directly | $X(j\omega) \rightarrow X(-j\omega)$ | Symmetric conjugate folding |
| 5 | Energy | $E = \sum \vert x[n] \vert^2 = \sum \vert x[-n] \vert^2$ | $E = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int \vert x(-t) \vert^2 dt$ | Invariant |
| 6 | Power | $P = \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{2N+1} \sum_{n=-N}^{N} \vert x[n] \vert^2$ | Same as discrete (with integral) | Invariant |
| 7 | Even Part | $x_e[n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] + x[-n]\}$ | $x_e(t) = \frac{1}{2}\{x(t) + x(-t)\}$ | Symmetric about $n = 0$ / $t = 0$ |
| 8 | Odd Part | $x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] - x[-n]\}$ | $x_o(t) = \frac{1}{2}\{x(t) - x(-t)\}$ | Anti-symmetric about $n = 0$ / $t = 0$ |
| 9 | Convolution with Reversed | $y[n] = x[n] * h[-n]$ | $y(t) = x(t) * h(-t)$ | Time-reversed impulse response (feedback systems) |
| 10 | Cross-Correlation | $r_{xy}[n] = x[n] \star y[n] = \sum_k x[k]\,y[k-n] = x[-n] * y[n]$ | $R_{xy}(\tau) = \int x(t)\,y(t-\tau)\,dt = x(-\tau) * y(\tau)$ | Time reversal in cross-correlation |
| 11 | Effect on Delay | $x[n - n_0] \rightarrow x[-n - n_0] = x[-(n + n_0)]$ | $x(t - t_0) \rightarrow x(-t - t_0)$ | Delay preserved with sign flip |
| 12 | Linearity | $a\,x[n] + b\,y[n] \rightarrow a\,x[-n] + b\,y[-n]$ | $a\,x(t) + b\,y(t) \rightarrow a\,x(-t) + b\,y(-t)$ | Linear operator |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **clinical engineering**, time-reversal is the silent workhorse behind:

- **Automated defibrillator (AED) shock timing:** R-wave detection algorithms correlate a *time-reversed template* with the incoming ECG to maximize the SNR ratio. The matched filter is mathematically $h[n] = x[N-1-n]$, a time-reversed copy of the template.
- **Hearing aid feedback cancellation:** Adaptive filters model the feedback path $h(t)$ and the anti-feedback filter uses $h(-t)$ as an estimate.
- **Ultrasound hyperthermia and tissue ablation:** *Acoustic time-reversal mirrors* (TRM) focus high-intensity sound waves at pathological tissues by exploiting the time-reversal invariance of the wave equation in lossy media.
- **Brain-computer interfaces (BCI):** ERP (event-related potential) extraction uses the time-reversed average of pre-stimulus EEG as the baseline template.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Z-Transform of the Time-Reversed Signal

> [!NOTE]  
> **Goal:** Starting from the definition of the Z-transform, prove the property $x[-n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} X(z^{-1})$.

The bilateral Z-transform of a discrete-time signal $x[n]$ is defined as:

$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,z^{-n}$$

Now, compute the Z-transform of the time-reversed signal $x_r[n] = x[-n]$. By direct substitution of $-n$ in place of $n$:

$$X_r(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[-n]\,z^{-n}$$

**Step 1 — Change of variable:**  
Let $m = -n$. Then $n = -m$ and $z^{-n} = z^{m}$. When $n$ ranges over $(-\infty, \infty)$, so does $m$.

$$X_r(z) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\,z^{m}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the bilateral sum form:**  
The expression $\sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\,z^{m}$ is *not* the Z-transform definition (which uses $z^{-n}$), but the *inverse* Z-transform integral evaluated at the point $z$. We can convert it by replacing $z$ with $z^{-1}$:

$$X_r(z) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\,(z^{-1})^{-m} = X(z^{-1})$$

**Step 3 — Conclude the property:**

$$\boxed{\;x[-n] \;\xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}}\; X(z^{-1})\;}$$

**Step 4 — Region of Convergence (ROC) update:**  
If the original ROC is $r_1 < \vert z \vert < r_2$, then the new ROC of $X(z^{-1})$ is obtained by substituting $z \to z^{-1}$:

$$\frac{1}{r_2} < \vert z \vert < \frac{1}{r_1}$$

This is the **reciprocal ROC** — a critical KTU exam point.

### 3.2 Derivation II: DTFT Property for Time Reversal

> [!NOTE]  
> **Goal:** Show that the magnitude spectrum is preserved while the phase is negated.

The DTFT of $x[n]$ is:

$$X(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n]\,e^{-j\omega n}$$

For the time-reversed signal $x[-n]$:

$$X_r(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[-n]\,e^{-j\omega n}$$

Apply the same change of variable $m = -n$:

$$X_r(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{m=-\infty}^{\infty} x[m]\,e^{j\omega m} = X(e^{-j\omega})$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\;x[-n] \;\xleftrightarrow{\text{DTFT}}\; X(e^{-j\omega})\;}$$

**Magnitude and phase decomposition:**

$$\vert X_r(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \vert X(e^{-j\omega}) \vert = \vert X(e^{j\omega}) \vert$$

$$\angle X_r(e^{j\omega}) = -\angle X(e^{j\omega})$$

The *magnitude spectrum is symmetric (even) about $\omega = 0$* for real signals, so it is invariant under time reversal. The *phase spectrum is negated*, which is precisely why matched filters work: the time-reversed template cancels the phase dispersion of the original signal.

### 3.3 Derivation III: Even-Odd Decomposition

Any real signal $x[n]$ can be uniquely decomposed into its even and odd components:

**Step 1 — Define the even part:**

$$x_e[n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] + x[-n]\}$$

Verify: $x_e[-n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[-n] + x[n]\} = x_e[n]$. ✓ (Even)

**Step 2 — Define the odd part:**

$$x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] - x[-n]\}$$

Verify: $x_o[-n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[-n] - x[n]\} = -x_o[n]$. ✓ (Odd)

**Step 3 — Add the two parts:**

$$x_e[n] + x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] + x[-n]\} + \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] - x[-n]\} = x[n] \;\;\checkmark$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A finite-duration ECG segment is represented as $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ with the arrow $\uparrow$ under $n=0$. Find (a) the time-reversed signal $x[-n]$; (b) the even and odd decompositions.

**Solution:**

**(a) Time reversal:** The signal has samples at $n = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2$ with values $1, 2, 3, 2, 1$. Reversing flips the order so that value at $n = -k$ is the original value at $n = k$:

| Index $n$ | $-2$ | $-1$ | $0$ | $1$ | $2$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $x[n]$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 1 |
| $x[-n]$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 2 | 1 |

Therefore: $x[-n] = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$ — the signal is **even** because $x[n] = x[-n]$.

**(b) Decomposition:**

$$x_e[n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] + x[-n]\} = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] + x[n]\} = x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 2, 1\}$$

$$x_o[n] = \frac{1}{2}\{x[n] - x[-n]\} = 0$$

**Conclusion:** The original signal is purely even. No odd component exists.

### 3.5 Cross-Correlation and Convolution Relationship

> [!NOTE]  
> **Crucial KTU Relationship:** Cross-correlation is convolution with one signal time-reversed.

$$r_{xy}[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\,y[k-n] = x[-n] * y[n]$$

**Derivation sketch:** Let $m = k - n$, so $k = m + n$:

$$r_{xy}[n] = \sum_{m} x[m+n]\,y[m]$$

Compare with convolution $x[-n] * y[n] = \sum_{m} x[-m]\,y[n-m]$. Substituting $m \to m-n$ in the convolution gives the cross-correlation expression. This is why *time reversal* is the bridge between matched filtering (used in QRS detection) and template matching.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Time Reversal Block Diagram (Mermaid Flowchart)

> [!NOTE]  
> The following Mermaid block depicts the operational flow of time reversal as a signal processing block, with the input signal $x[n]$ transformed into $x[-n]$ and simultaneously decomposed into even and odd sub-components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Original Biomedical Signal x of n] --> B[Time Reversal Operator T]
    B --> C[Time-Reversed Signal x of minus n]
    C --> D{Symmetry Test}
    D -- Equal to x of n --> E[Even Component x_e of n]
    D -- Negative of x of n --> F[Odd Component x_o of n]
    D -- Neither --> G[General Decomposition]
    E --> H[Feature Extraction for QRS Detection]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Clinical Decision Support Output]
```

### 4.2 Signal Decomposition Block Architecture

> [!NOTE]  
> Block-level functional architecture showing the *parallel* decomposition of an arbitrary biomedical signal into its even and odd parts via two parallel time-reversal branches.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputNode[Input Signal x of n] --> splitNode{Signal Splitter}
    splitNode --> pathA[Direct Path]
    splitNode --> pathB[Time-Reversed Path x of minus n]
    pathA --> sumAdd[Summing Junction Plus]
    pathB --> sumAdd
    sumAdd --> scaleA[Scale by 1 over 2]
    scaleA --> evenOut[Even Part x_e of n]
    pathA --> sumSub[Summing Junction Minus]
    pathB --> sumSub
    sumSub --> scaleB[Scale by 1 over 2]
    scaleB --> oddOut[Odd Part x_o of n]
    evenOut --> combine[Combiner]
    oddOut --> combine
    combine --> recon[Reconstructed x of n equals x_e plus x_o]
```

### 4.3 Z-Domain Property Mapping

> [!NOTE]  
> Mapping of time reversal in the time domain to its corresponding Z-domain operation, highlighting ROC reciprocity.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    timeDomain[Time Domain x of n] -->|Apply Time Reversal| timeRev[Time Domain x of minus n]
    timeRev -->|Take Z Transform| zDomain[Z Domain X of z inverse]
    zDomain -->|ROC reciprocal| rocOut[New ROC 1 over r1 to 1 over r2]
    timeDomain -->|Direct Z Transform| zOrig[Z Domain X of z]
    zOrig -->|Original ROC| rocIn[ROC r1 to r2]
```

### 4.4 Clinical Pipeline: QRS Detection via Time-Reversed Matched Filter

> [!NOTE]  
> End-to-end DSP pipeline used in clinical ECG monitors where time-reversed templates are cross-correlated with incoming samples to detect R-peaks in real time.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ecgIn[Raw ECG Input x of n] --> preFilter[Bandpass Filter 5 to 15 Hz]
    preFilter --> diffFilter[Differentiator]
    diffFilter --> squareOp[Squaring Operation]
    squareOp --> movAvg[Moving Window Integrator]
    movAvg --> matchedFilt[Matched Filter with Time-Reversed Template h of n equals x of N minus 1 minus n]
    matchedFilt --> threshold[Adaptive Thresholding]
    threshold --> rPeakDetect[R-Peak Detection Output]
```
