- If $\tau < 0$: $\int_{-\tau}^{\infty}$ (lower limit is $-\tau$, since $-\tau > 0$)

*(2 marks for correctly setting up the integration limits)*

**Step 3:** For $\tau \geq 0$:

$$r_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} \cdot e^{-a(t+\tau)} \, dt = e^{-a\tau} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2at} \, dt = e^{-a\tau} \cdot \frac{1}{2a}$$

*(2 marks for the integration)*

**Step 4:** For $\tau < 0$:

$$r_{xx}(\tau) = \int_{-\tau}^{\infty} e^{-at} \cdot e^{-a(t+\tau)} \, dt = e^{-a\tau} \int_{-\tau}^{\infty} e^{-2at} \, dt = e^{-a\tau} \cdot \frac{e^{2a\tau}}{2a} = \frac{e^{a\tau}}{2a}$$

*(2 marks for the integration)*

**Step 5:** Combined result and feature summary:

$$r_{xx}(\tau) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{e^{a\tau}}{2a}, & \tau < 0 \\[6pt] \dfrac{e^{-a\tau}}{2a}, & \tau \geq 0 \end{cases}$$

*Salient features:*
- **Maximum at origin:** $r_{xx}(0) = \dfrac{1}{2a}$ (the energy of the signal)
- **Even symmetry:** $r_{xx}(-\tau) = r_{xx}(\tau)$ ✓
- **Decay:** Exponential decay as $\vert \tau \vert \to \infty$, with rate constant $a$
- **Always positive:** $r_{xx}(\tau) > 0$ for all $\tau$ (no negative excursions)

*(1 mark for the features)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative**

**(a)** [7 Marks] For the discrete-time signals $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3\}$ and $y[n] = \{0, 1, 0.5\}$, compute the cross-correlation sequence $r_{xy}[l]$ for $l = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2$ using $r_{xy}[l] = \sum_{n} x[n] y[n+l]$. Assume zero-padding outside the defined ranges.

**(b)** [7 Marks] State and prove the maximum property of the auto-correlation function $r_{xx}(\tau)$ for continuous-time energy signals. Hence show that the value of $r_{xx}(0)$ equals the total energy $E$ of the signal.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Define the signals with index notation. Let $x[0]=1, x[1]=2, x[2]=3$ and $y[0]=0, y[1]=1, y[2]=0.5$. All other samples are zero.

**Step 2:** Compute $r_{xy}[l]$ for each lag. Use the formula $r_{xy}[l] = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] y[n+l]$.

**$l = -2$:**
$$r_{xy}[-2] = \sum_{n} x[n] y[n-2]$$
The product $x[n] y[n-2]$ is non-zero only when both $n \in \{0,1,2\}$ and $n-2 \in \{0,1,2\}$, i.e., $n = 2$. Then $x[2] y[0] = 3 \times 0 = 0$.
$$r_{xy}[-2] = 0$$

**$l = -1$:**
$$r_{xy}[-1] = \sum_{n} x[n] y[n-1]$$
Non-zero at $n = 1$ ($x[1]y[0] = 2 \times 0 = 0$) and $n = 2$ ($x[2]y[1] = 3 \times 1 = 3$).
$$r_{xy}[-1] = 0 + 3 = 3$$

**$l = 0$:**
$$r_{xy}[0] = \sum_{n} x[n] y[n]$$
Non-zero at $n = 0$ ($1 \times 0 = 0$), $n = 1$ ($2 \times 1 = 2$), $n = 2$ ($3 \times 0.5 = 1.5$).
$$r_{xy}[0] = 0 + 2 + 1.5 = 3.5$$

**$l = 1$:**
$$r_{xy}[1] = \sum_{n} x[n] y[n+1]$$
Non-zero at $n = 0$ ($x[0]y[1] = 1 \times 1 = 1$), $n = 1$ ($x[1]y[2] = 2 \times 0.5 = 1$).
$$r_{xy}[1] = 1 + 1 = 2$$

**$l = 2$:**
$$r_{xy}[2] = \sum_{n} x[n] y[n+2]$$
Non-zero at $n = 0$ ($x[0]y[2] = 1 \times 0.5 = 0.5$).
$$r_{xy}[2] = 0.5$$

*(6 marks distributed across the five lag computations, 1 mark for presenting the final sequence clearly)*

**Final Cross-Correlation Sequence:**

$$r_{xy}[l] = \{\,0, \, 3, \, 3.5, \, 2, \, 0.5\,\} \quad \text{for } l = -2, -1, 0, 1, 2$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Statement:** For any finite-energy real signal $x(t)$, the auto-correlation function $r_{xx}(\tau)$ satisfies $r_{xx}(0) \geq \vert r_{xx}(\tau) \vert$ for all $\tau \in \mathbb{R}$.

**Proof:** See the complete nine-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes.

**Energy Equivalence:**
By definition, $r_{xx}(0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot x(t+0) \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x^2(t) \, dt = E$ (the total energy of the signal). This follows directly from substituting $\tau = 0$ into the auto-correlation integral. *(2 marks for this energy identification)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Critical for Scoring 14/14):**
> 
> 1. **Sign Error in the $\tau < 0$ Branch:** When computing the auto-correlation of $x(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$, approximately 30% of students incorrectly set the lower limit to $0$ for $\tau < 0$, leading to the wrong answer. The correct lower limit is $-\tau$ because $u(t+\tau) = 1$ requires $t \geq -\tau$.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $r_{xy}$ with Convolution:** Students sometimes write $r_{xy}[l] = \sum x[n+l] y[n]$ and call it convolution, or they reverse $y[n]$ while computing correlation. Always remember: **Correlation does not flip the signal.**
> 
> 3. **Missing the Energy Statement:** In derivation questions, students often prove the bound $\vert r_{xx}(\tau) \vert \leq r_{xx}(0)$ but forget to explicitly state that $r_{xx}(0) = E$ (the signal energy). This omission costs 1-2 marks.
> 
> 4. **Units and Normalization:** When asked for the correlation coefficient, students must report a dimensionless $\rho \in [-1, +1]$. Reporting raw correlation values with units of $\text{mV}^2$ where a coefficient is expected will cost 2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Lock-in:** Correlation = integral of $x(t) \cdot y(t+\tau) \, dt$. It is a **measure of similarity as a function of lag** $\tau$. Auto-correlation is the special case where $x(t) = y(t)$.
- **The Three Cardinal Properties of $r_{xx}(\tau)$:** (i) Maximum at origin ($r_{xx}(0) = E$), (ii) Even symmetry ($r_{xx}(\tau) = r_{xx}(-\tau)$), (iii) $r_{xx}(0) \geq 0$ for all $\tau$.
- **Correlation vs. Convolution — The Golden Rule:** Correlation = $x(t) \cdot y(t+\tau)$ (no flip). Convolution = $x(t) \cdot y(t-\tau)$ (one signal flipped).
- **Boundedness of Normalized Coefficient:** $\rho_{xy} \in [-1, +1]$ — dimensionless, scale-invariant, derived from Cauchy-Schwarz.
- **Deterministic vs. Statistical:** Use $r_{xy}(\tau)$ for energy/power biosignals (ECG, PCG, PPG). Use $R_{XY}(\tau) = E[X(t)Y(t+\tau)]$ for stochastic biosignals (EEG, EMG, HRV series).
- **Discrete-Time Index Shift:** $r_{xy}[l] = \sum_n x[n] y[n+l]$. A positive lag $l$ means $y$ is shifted to the **left** relative to $x$.
- **Clinical Applications to Memorize:** Fetal ECG extraction, pulse transit time, evoked potential averaging, HRV analysis, QRS template matching, EEG coherence mapping.
- **Common Unit Pitfall:** Raw correlation of voltage signals has units of $\text{(voltage)}^2$ (power). Normalize before comparing across channels of different gains.
- **Peak-Location Interpretation:** The lag $\tau^*$ that maximizes $r_{xy}(\tau)$ is the **time delay of arrival** between the two signals — central to all TDOA-based biomedical measurements.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Multiplication # MODULE 3 — MULTIPLICATION OF SIGNALS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of biomedical signal processing, **Multiplication** is a fundamental point-by-point (sample-by-sample) algebraic operation performed between two time-domain signals. When two continuous-time signals $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ are multiplied, the resultant signal $y(t)$ is defined as: $$y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$$ For discrete-time signals, the same operation is performed sample-by-sample: $$y[n] = x_1[n] \cdot x_2[n]$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multiplication in the time domain is equivalent to CONVOLUTION in the frequency domain.** This is one of the most powerful dualities in signal processing and is the foundation of **Amplitude Modulation (AM)**, which is widely used in biomedical telemetry (e.g., wireless transmission of ECG, EEG, and EMG signals from a patient to a monitoring station). ### 1.2 The Two Critical Forms of Multiplication in Biomedical Engineering | S.No | Form | Description | Biomedical Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | **Modulation** | $s(t) = x(t) \cdot c(t)$ (signal × carrier) | Wireless telemetry, transmission of physiological signals | | 2 | **Windowing** | $x_w(t) = x(t) \cdot w(t)$ (signal × window) | Spectral analysis, finite-duration signal extraction, noise reduction | | 3 | **Sampling** | Modulation by an impulse train (a special case) | A/D conversion of ECG, EEG signals | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — "Two Voices in a Hall":** > Imagine two singers (Signal A and Signal B) singing simultaneously in a hall. A microphone placed in the hall **multiplies** both voices air-pressure-wise at every instant. The output of the microphone is the **product** of the two voices, moment by moment. If one voice is silent, the output is zero; if both sing, the output is louder. This is exactly what happens in a multiplier circuit (or mixer) — it produces an output whose amplitude at every instant equals the product of the instantaneous amplitudes of the two input signals. > > A second, more technical analogy: **Multiplying a low-frequency ECG signal (0.5–100 Hz) by a high-frequency carrier (e.g., 100 kHz)** shifts the ECG spectrum up to a higher frequency band. This is like placing the ECG signal "on the back of a faster horse" so it can be transmitted wirelessly over long distances without attenuation. ### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Carrier Frequency ($f_c$)**: Typically **100 kHz – 2 GHz** for biomedical telemetry. - **Message (Modulating) Signal Bandwidth ($W$)**: **0.5 Hz – 1 kHz** for most biosignals. - **Modulation Index ($\mu$)**: A dimensionless ratio, must satisfy $\mu \le 1$ for distortion-free envelope detection. - **Multiplier Output**: Units depend on inputs (e.g., Volts × Volts = Volts² for analog multipliers, but the output is usually scaled to Volts). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Multiplication Theorem (Time–Frequency Duality) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** If $x(t) \leftrightarrow X(f)$ and $y(t) \leftrightarrow Y(f)$ are Fourier Transform pairs, then the pointwise product in the time domain corresponds to **convolution in the frequency domain**, scaled by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ (or $\frac{1}{T_s}$ in discrete time). ### 2.2 Operational Logic (Step-by-Step) 1. **Take the two signals** $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ in the time domain. 2. **Multiply them point-by-point** to get $z(t) = x(t) \cdot y(t)$. 3. **Take the Fourier Transform** of $z(t)$. By the multiplication theorem, this equals a **convolution** of the two individual spectra. 4. **Mathematically**: $$Z(f) = X(f) * Y(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\lambda) \, Y(f - \lambda) \, d\lambda$$ 5. **Implication:** When you multiply two time signals, you are essentially **smearing / blending their frequency spectra** together. The output spectrum is the convolution of the input spectra. ### 2.3 The Modulation Theorem (Special Case of Multiplication) > [!NOTE] > **The Modulation Theorem** is the single most important application of multiplication in biomedical signal processing. It states that multiplying a signal $x(t)$ by a sinusoid $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ **shifts** its spectrum to $\pm f_c$. If $x(t) \leftrightarrow X(f)$, then: $$x(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t) \longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}\big[X(f - f_c) + X(f + f_c)\big]$$ This is the **foundation of all amplitude modulation (AM) systems**. ## 2.4 Amplitude Modulation (AM) — Full Mathematical Framework ### 2.4.1 Standard AM Signal (with Carrier) The general form of an amplitude-modulated signal is: $$s(t) = A_c \, [1 + k_a \, m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$ where: - $A_c$ = Carrier amplitude (in Volts) - $k_a$ = Amplitude sensitivity of the modulator (in $\text{V}^{-1}$) - $m(t)$ = Message (modulating) signal — e.g., ECG waveform - $f_c$ = Carrier frequency (Hz) For convenience, define the **modulation index** $\mu = k_a \cdot \max \vert m(t) \vert$. ### 2.4.2 Modulation Index (Critical Parameter) $$\mu = \frac{m_p}{A_c} = k_a \cdot m_p$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** For distortion-free envelope detection, **$\mu \le 1$** is mandatory. If $\mu > 1$ (over-modulation), the envelope becomes distorted, and information is lost. This is a common **2-mark trap** in KTU papers. ### 2.4.3 Spectrum of AM Signal The AM spectrum consists of three components: - **Carrier component** at $\pm f_c$ with amplitude $A_c$ - **Upper Sideband (USB)** — shifted copy of $X(f)$ centered at $+f_c$ - **Lower Sideband (LSB)** — mirror image of $X(f)$ centered at $-f_c$ ### 2.4.4 Power in AM Signal $$P_t = P_c \, \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$ where $P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$ is the carrier power, and the total sideband power is $P_c \cdot \frac{\mu^2}{2}$. > [!NOTE] > At $\mu = 1$ (100% modulation), only **33.3%** of the total transmitted power carries information (the rest is in the carrier). This is why **DSB-SC** (suppressed carrier) systems are more power-efficient. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Condition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Signal Multiplication | $y(t) = x_1(t) \cdot x_2(t)$ | Volts (after scaling) | | 2 | **Multiplication Theorem (Time)** | $x(t) \cdot y(t) \leftrightarrow X(f) * Y(f)$ | Convolution in frequency | | 3 | **Multiplication Theorem (Discrete)** | $x[n] \cdot y[n] \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2\pi} X(e^{j\omega}) * Y(e^{j\omega})$ | DTFT, periodic in $2\pi$ | | 4 | **Modulation Theorem** | $x(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}[X(f-f_c) + X(f+f_c)]$ | Spectrum shift by $\pm f_c$ | | 5 | AM Signal | $s(t) = A_c[1 + k_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Carrier + message product | | 6 | Modulation Index | $\mu = k_a \cdot m_p = \frac{m_p}{A_c}$ | $\mu \le 1$ for no distortion | | 7 | Total AM Power | $P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$ | Watts | | 8 | AM Bandwidth | $BW = 2 W$ | Twice the message bandwidth | | 9 | DSB-SC Signal | $s(t) = m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Carrier suppressed | | 10 | DSB-SC Power | $P_t = \frac{m_{rms}^2}{2}$ | All power is informational | | 11 | Coherent Demodulation | $r(t) = s(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Followed by LPF | | 12 | Efficiency of AM | $\eta = \frac{\mu^2}{2 + \mu^2}$ | Max $\eta = 33.33\%$ at $\mu=1$ | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Biotelemetry**: Wireless transmission of ECG from an ambulatory Holter monitor to a receiver. - **EEG/EMG Transmission**: Carrying low-frequency biosignals over a high-frequency RF link. - **Chopper-Stabilized Amplifiers**: Multiplying a low-frequency biomedical signal by a square wave to shift it away from the $1/f$ (flicker) noise region of op-amps. - **Multiplexing**: Many patients' biosignals can be modulated onto different carrier frequencies and transmitted over a single channel. - **Spectrum Shifting for Anti-Aliasing**: Modulating a signal to a higher frequency band before sampling so that it lies above the $1/f$ noise corner of the ADC. - **Narrowband Noise Rejection**: Once modulated, a biosignal occupies a narrow band; narrowband filters can then be used to reject wideband noise. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Multiplication Theorem (Time Domain) > **Goal:** Show that if $x(t) \leftrightarrow X(f)$ and $y(t) \leftrightarrow Y(f)$, then $z(t) = x(t) \cdot y(t) \leftrightarrow Z(f) = X(f) * Y(f)$. ### Step 1: Write the inverse Fourier Transform of $X(f)$ and $Y(f)$ $$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) e^{j 2\pi f t} \, df$$ $$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} Y(\lambda) e^{j 2\pi \lambda t} \, d\lambda$$ ### Step 2: Form the product $z(t) = x(t) \cdot y(t)$ $$z(t) = \left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) e^{j 2\pi f t} \, df\right] \cdot \left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} Y(\lambda) e^{j 2\pi \lambda t} \, d\lambda\right]$$ This is the product of two integrals, which is a **double integral**. ### Step 3: Combine into a single double integral $$z(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) \, Y(\lambda) \, e^{j 2\pi (f + \lambda) t} \, df \, d\lambda$$ ### Step 4: Take the Fourier Transform of $z(t)$ $$Z(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} z(t) e^{-j 2\pi \nu t} \, dt$$ Substitute $z(t)$: $$Z(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) Y(\lambda) e^{j 2\pi (f + \lambda) t} \, df \, d\lambda \right] e^{-j 2\pi \nu t} \, dt$$ ### Step 5: Rearrange the order of integration $$Z(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) \, Y(\lambda) \left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j 2\pi (\nu - f - \lambda) t} \, dt \right] \, df \, d\lambda$$ The inner integral is a Dirac delta: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j 2\pi (\nu - f - \lambda) t} \, dt = \delta(\nu - f - \lambda)$. ### Step 6: Apply the sifting property of the delta $$Z(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) \, Y(\lambda) \, \delta(\nu - f - \lambda) \, df \, d\lambda$$ The delta is non-zero when $\lambda = \nu - f$. Substitute: $$\boxed{Z(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) \, Y(\nu - f) \, df = X(\nu) * Y(\nu)}$$ **Conclusion:** $z(t) = x(t) \cdot y(t) \longleftrightarrow Z(f) = X(f) * Y(f)$. $\blacksquare$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Modulation Theorem (Special Case) > **Goal:** Show that $x(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}[X(f-f_c) + X(f+f_c)]$. ### Step 1: Express cosine as two complex exponentials (Euler's identity) $$\cos(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[e^{j 2\pi f_c t} + e^{-j 2\pi f_c t}\right]$$ ### Step 2: Form the modulated signal $$s(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{1}{2}\left[x(t) e^{j 2\pi f_c t} + x(t) e^{-j 2\pi f_c t}\right]$$ ### Step 3: Apply the **Frequency Shifting Property** of the Fourier Transform If $x(t) \leftrightarrow X(f)$, then: - $x(t) e^{j 2\pi f_c t} \leftrightarrow X(f - f_c)$ - $x(t) e^{-j 2\pi f_c t} \leftrightarrow X(f + f_c)$ ### Step 4: Combine $$S(f) = \frac{1}{2}\big[X(f - f_c) + X(f + f_c)\big]$$ $$\boxed{x(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}\big[X(f - f_c) + X(f + f_c)\big]} \quad \blacksquare$$ This shows that the spectrum of $x(t)$ is **replicated** and **shifted** to $\pm f_c$, each copy scaled by $\frac{1}{2}$. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Numerical) > **Problem:** A message signal $m(t) = 3 \cos(200\pi t)$ Volts is amplitude-modulated onto a carrier $c(t) = 10 \cos(2000\pi t)$ Volts. The AM signal is $s(t) = [A + m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ with $A = 5$ V. Find: > (i) Modulation index, (ii) AM expression in time domain, (iii) AM spectrum and sketch it, (iv) Total transmitted power across $1\,\Omega$, (v) Bandwidth. ### Solution **Given:** - Message: $m(t) = 3\cos(200\pi t)$ → message frequency $f_m = 100$ Hz, peak $m_p = 3$ V. - Carrier: $c(t) = 10\cos(2000\pi t)$ → carrier frequency $f_c = 1000$ Hz, amplitude $A_c = 10$ V. - DC offset: $A = 5$ V. #### (i) Modulation Index $$\mu = \frac{m_p}{A} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 \; (< 1, \text{ no distortion})$$ #### (ii) AM Expression in Time Domain $$s(t) = [A + m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t) = [5 + 3\cos(200\pi t)] \cos(2000\pi t)$$ Expand: $s(t) = 5\cos(2000\pi t) + 3\cos(200\pi t)\cos(2000\pi t)$ Apply product-to-sum identity: $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A+B) + \cos(A-B)]$ $$s(t) = 5\cos(2000\pi t) + \frac{3}{2}\big[\cos(2200\pi t) + \cos(1800\pi t)\big]$$ #### (iii) AM Spectrum (Fourier Transform of each term) | Term | Frequency | Amplitude | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $5\cos(2000\pi t)$ | $\pm 1000$ Hz (Carrier) | $\frac{5}{2} = 2.5$ V (each side) | | $\frac{3}{2}\cos(2200\pi t)$ | $\pm 1100$ Hz (USB) | $\frac{3}{4} = 0.75$ V (each side) | | $\frac{3}{2}\cos(1800\pi t)$ | $\pm 900$ Hz (LSB) | $\frac{3}{4} = 0.75$ V (each side) | **Spectral Components:** - Carrier at $f = \pm 1000$ Hz, magnitude 2.5 V (one-sided: 5 V) - USB at $f = \pm 1100$ Hz, magnitude 0.75 V (one-sided: 1.5 V) - LSB at $f = \pm 900$ Hz, magnitude 0.75 V (one-sided: 1.5 V) #### (iv) Total Transmitted Power (across $1\,\Omega$) Carrier power: $P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2} = \frac{10^2}{2} = 50$ W Total power: $P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right) = 50\left(1 + \frac{0.36}{2}\right) = 50(1.18) = 59$ W Sideband power: $P_{SB} = P_c \cdot \frac{\mu^2}{2} = 50 \cdot 0.18 = 9$ W (split equally: 4.5 W each) #### (v) Bandwidth $$BW = 2 f_m = 2 \times 100 = 200 \text{ Hz}$$ (ranging from 900 Hz to 1100 Hz) **Final Answers:** - $\mu = 0.6$ - $s(t) = 5\cos(2000\pi t) + 1.5\cos(2200\pi t) + 1.5\cos(1800\pi t)$ - $P_t = 59$ W - $BW = 200$ Hz --- ## 3.4 Summary of Key Derivation Insights | Property | Time Domain | Frequency Domain | Signal Processing Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Multiplication Theorem | $x(t) \cdot y(t)$ | $X(f) * Y(f)$ | Spectrum blurring | | Convolution Theorem | $x(t) * y(t)$ | $X(f) \cdot Y(f)$ | Filtering (multiply by $H(f)$) | | Modulation Theorem | $x(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $\frac{1}{2}[X(f-f_c)+X(f+f_c)]$ | Spectrum shifting | | Time Scaling | $x(at)$ | $\frac{1}{\vert a \vert}X(f/a)$ | Compression/expansion | | Duality | $X(t)$ | $x(-f)$ | Swaps domains | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Amplitude Modulation System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Message Signal m of t
ECG or EEG] --> M[Multiplier] B[Carrier Oscillator
cos of 2 pi f_c t] --> M M --> H[Bandpass Filter
Removes harmonics] H --> ANT[Antenna
Wireless Channel] ANT --> RX[Receiver Antenna] RX --> DM[Demodulator
Multiplier plus LPF] C[Local Oscillator
cos of 2 pi f_c t] --> DM DM --> OUT[Recovered m of t] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Multiplication in Frequency Domain (Convolution) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Signal x of t] -->|FT| AX[Spectrum X of f] B[Signal y of t] -->|FT| BY[Spectrum Y of f] AX --> CONV[Convolve X of f with Y of f] BY --> CONV CONV --> CZ[Spectrum Z of f] CZ -->|Inverse FT| CZ1[Product z of t equals x of t times y of t] ``` ## 4.3 Spectral Diagram: AM Signal (Two-Sided) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph S[AM Spectrum Two Sided] direction LR N1[ f equals minus 1100 Hz
USB mirror, amp 0.75] --> N2[ f equals minus 1000 Hz
Carrier, amp 2.5] N2 --> N3[ f equals minus 900 Hz
LSB mirror, amp 0.75] N3 --> N4[ f equals 0 Hz] N4 --> P1[ f equals plus 900 Hz
LSB, amp 0.75] P1 --> P2[ f equals plus 1000 Hz
Carrier, amp 2.5] P2 --> P3[ f equals plus 1100 Hz
USB, amp 0.75] end ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Chopper-Stabilized Biomedical Amplifier (Application of Multiplication) ```mermaid flowchart LR BIO[Low Frequency Biosignal
0 to 100 Hz] --> M1[Multiplier
Chopper 500 Hz square wave] CLK[Clock 500 Hz] --> M1 M1 --> AC[AC Coupled Amplifier
Removes 1 by f noise] AC --> M2[Multiplier
Same chopper signal] CLK --> M2 M2 --> LPF[Low Pass Filter
Cutoff 100 Hz] LPF --> OUT[Clean Recovered Biosignal] ``` ## 4.5 Decision Tree: When to Use Which Form of Multiplication ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[Need to Apply Multiplication] --> Q1{Goal?} Q1 -->|Wireless transmission| A1[Use AM Modulation Theorem] Q1 -->|Spectral analysis| A2[Use Windowing] Q1 -->|Noise rejection| A3[Use Chopper Stabilization] Q1 -->|A to D conversion| A4[Use Sampling Multiplication] Q1 -->|Multiplexing| A5[Use FDM Frequency Division Multiplexing] A1 --> R1[Result: Spectrum shifted by plus or minus f_c] A2 --> R2[Result: Spectrum convolved with window spectrum] A3 --> R3[Result: Signal moved to higher frequency] A4 --> R4[Result: Periodic spectrum] A5 --> R5[Result: Multiple spectra placed side by side] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State and explain the Multiplication Theorem in signal processing. Mention its significance in biomedical applications.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Statement:** The Multiplication Theorem states that multiplication of two signals in the time domain is equivalent to convolution of their spectra in the frequency domain. Mathematically, > $$x(t) \cdot y(t) \longleftrightarrow X(f) * Y(f)$$ > where $*$ denotes convolution. > > **Significance (1.5 marks):** This theorem is the foundation of amplitude modulation. Multiplying a low-frequency biomedical signal (e.g., ECG) by a high-frequency carrier shifts its spectrum to the carrier frequency, enabling wireless transmission (biotelemetry) of physiological signals. It also underlies windowing, sampling, and chopper-stabilized amplification. > > **Keywords for full marks:** Dual operation, time-frequency duality, convolution in frequency, modulation, biotelemetry. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define modulation index. What is its significance in AM? What happens if $\mu > 1$?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1 mark):** The modulation index $\mu$ of an AM signal is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal $m_p$ to the carrier amplitude $A_c$: > $$\mu = \frac{m_p}{A_c}$$ > > **Significance (1 mark):** $\mu$ determines the depth of modulation and the relative strength of the sidebands (which carry information). It affects transmitted power: > $$P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$$ > Maximum power efficiency ($\eta = 33.3\%$) is achieved at $\mu = 1$. > > **Consequence of $\mu > 1$ (1 mark):** This is called **over-modulation**. The envelope of the AM signal becomes distorted (the troughs cross zero), making envelope detection impossible. The signal suffers from phase reversal and information loss. In KTU exams, **always state that $\mu \le 1$ is required for distortion-free demodulation**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **KTU Format Note:** Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically carrying 7 marks each. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]` **(a)** State and prove the **Multiplication Theorem** of Fourier Transform. (7 marks) **(b)** A message signal $m(t) = 4\cos(100\pi t)$ V modulates a carrier $c(t) = 20\cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t)$ V in a standard AM system. The DC offset is $A = 5$ V. (i) Write the AM signal expression. (ii) Determine the modulation index and comment on its value. (iii) Sketch the two-sided spectrum and find the bandwidth. (7 marks) --- ### **Model Solution for Question A** #### Part (a) — Multiplication Theorem Proof (7 marks) **Statement:** If $x(t) \leftrightarrow X(f)$ and $y(t) \leftrightarrow Y(f)$, then $$z(t) = x(t) \cdot y(t) \longleftrightarrow Z(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\lambda) Y(f - \lambda) \, d\lambda = X(f) * Y(f)$$ **Proof (4 steps, each carrying ~1.5–2 marks):** **Step 1:** Express $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ using inverse Fourier Transforms: $$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) e^{j 2\pi f t} \, df \quad ; \quad y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} Y(\lambda) e^{j 2\pi \lambda t} \, d\lambda$$ **Step 2:** Form the product $z(t) = x(t) \cdot y(t)$ and combine integrals: $$z(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) Y(\lambda) e^{j 2\pi (f + \lambda) t} \, df \, d\lambda$$ **Step 3:** Take the Fourier Transform of $z(t)$: $$Z(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) Y(\lambda) \left[\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-j 2\pi (\nu - f - \lambda) t} dt\right] df \, d\lambda$$ **Step 4:** The inner integral equals $\delta(\nu - f - \lambda)$. Apply sifting property: $$Z(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) Y(\nu - f) df = X(\nu) * Y(\nu) \quad \blacksquare$$ --- #### Part (b) — AM Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** $m(t) = 4\cos(100\pi t)$, $c(t) = 20\cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t)$, $A = 5$ V. **(i) AM Expression (2 marks):** $$s(t) = [A + m(t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t) = [5 + 4\cos(100\pi t)] \cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t)$$ Expand and apply product-to-sum: $$s(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t) + 2\cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t)\cos(100\pi t)$$ $$s(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t) + 2\cos[2\pi(10^4 + 50)t] + 2\cos[2\pi(10^4 - 50)t]$$ $$\boxed{s(t) = 5\cos(2\pi \times 10^4 t) + 2\cos(2\pi \times 10050 \, t) + 2\cos(2\pi \times 9950 \, t)}$$ **(ii) Modulation Index (2 marks):** $$\mu = \frac{m_p}{A} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$ Since $\mu = 0.8 < 1$, the modulation is **under-modulated (no distortion)**. **(iii) Spectrum and Bandwidth (3 marks):** - Carrier at $\pm 10$ kHz, amplitude 5 V (one-sided). - USB at $\pm 10.05$ kHz, amplitude 2 V (one-sided). - LSB at $\pm 9.95$ kHz, amplitude 2 V (one-sided). - **Bandwidth:** $BW = 2 f_m = 2 \times 50 = 100$ Hz. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Modulation Theorem** and derive the spectrum of an amplitude-modulated signal. (7 marks) **(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a **coherent (synchronous) detector** for AM demodulation. Why is the local carrier synchronized in phase? Discuss any biomedical application. (7 marks) --- ### **Model Solution for Question B** #### Part (a) — Modulation Theorem & AM Spectrum (7 marks) **Statement (2 marks):** Multiplying a message signal $x(t)$ by a sinusoidal carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ produces two shifted copies of the message spectrum, centered at $\pm f_c$, each scaled by $\frac{1}{2}$: $$x(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}[X(f - f_c) + X(f + f_c)]$$ **Derivation (3 marks):** 1. Euler expansion: $\cos(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{1}{2}(e^{j2\pi f_c t} + e^{-j2\pi f_c t})$ 2. Product: $s(t) = \frac{1}{2}x(t)e^{j2\pi f_c t} + \frac{1}{2}x(t)e^{-j2\pi f_c t}$ 3. Apply frequency shifting property: $s(t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}X(f - f_c) + \frac{1}{2}X(f + f_c)$ **AM Spectrum (2 marks):** For $s(t) = [A + m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$: - Carrier impulses at $\pm f_c$ of magnitude $\frac{A}{2}$. - USB centered at $+f_c$, LSB at $-f_c$, each a copy of $M(f)$ scaled by $\frac{1}{2}$. - Bandwidth = $2W$ (twice the message bandwidth). --- #### Part (b) — Coherent Detector (7 marks) **Block Diagram (3 marks):** ``` Received AM → [Multiplier] → [Low-Pass Filter] → Recovered m(t) Local Carrier cos(2πf_c t) → [Multiplier] ``` **Operation (2 marks):** Let received signal $r(t) = [A + m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. Multiply by local carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$: $$r(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t) = [A + m(t)]\cos^2(2\pi f_c t) = \frac{[A + m(t)]}{2}[1 + \cos(4\pi f_c t)]$$ After low-pass filtering (which removes the $2f_c$ component): $$y_{LPF}(t) = \frac{A + m(t)}{2}$$ A DC block recovers $m(t)$. **Why Synchronized Phase (1 mark):** If the local carrier has a phase error $\phi$, the output becomes $\frac{[A + m(t)]\cos\phi}{2}$, causing amplitude attenuation (or full cancellation if $\phi = 90°$). Synchronization preserves the message amplitude. **Biomedical Application (1 mark):** Used in ECG/EEG telemetry receivers, where the locally generated carrier must match the transmitter's carrier in both frequency and phase to recover the biosignal faithfully. --- ## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Multiplication / AM Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ factor** in the Modulation Theorem — examiners *will* deduct 1 mark. Always write $\frac{1}{2}[X(f-f_c) + X(f+f_c)]$. > 2. **Conflating AM with DSB-SC:** In DSB-SC there is **no carrier term** and no $\mu$ (modulation index). In standard AM, **there is** a carrier term. Read the question carefully. > 3. **Bandwidth errors:** AM bandwidth is **$2W$** (twice the message bandwidth), **not** $W$. Single-sideband systems use $W$ only. > 4. **Modulation index:** Many students write $\mu = \frac{m_p}{A}$ and forget the condition $\mu \le 1$. Always mention the condition. > 5. **Power calculations:** Use $P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2}(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2})$ — note the $\frac{1}{2}$ inside the brackets, not outside. > 6. **Sketches:** In spectrum questions, KTU examiners expect **two-sided** (positive and negative frequency) sketches with **impulses at $\pm f_c$** for the carrier. Failing to do so costs 1–2 marks. > 7. **Coherent demodulation:** Students often forget to LPF after the multiplier; the demodulator is **multiplier + LPF**, not just a multiplier. --- ## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **Multiplication Theorem (Time Domain):** $x(t) \cdot y(t) \longleftrightarrow X(f) * Y(f)$ — pointwise time product ↔ frequency convolution. - ✅ **Modulation Theorem:** $x(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) \longleftrightarrow \frac{1}{2}[X(f-f_c) + X(f+f_c)]$ — spectrum is **shifted to $\pm f_c$** and scaled by $\frac{1}{2}$. - ✅ **AM Signal:** $s(t) = A_c[1 + k_a m(t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — contains carrier + two sidebands. - ✅ **Modulation Index:** $\mu = \frac{m_p}{A_c}$; must satisfy **$\mu \le 1$** for distortion-free demodulation. - ✅ **AM Bandwidth:** $BW = 2W$ (twice the message bandwidth). - ✅ **AM Power:** $P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2}\left(1 + \frac{\mu^2}{2}\right)$; maximum efficiency = **33.3%** at $\mu = 1$. - ✅ **DSB-SC Signal:** $s(t) = m(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ — **no carrier**; bandwidth = $2W$; power efficiency = 100%. - ✅ **Coherent Demodulation:** Multiply received signal by locally generated $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$, then LPF. Requires phase/frequency synchronization. - ✅ **Envelope Detection:** Simple RC diode detector, works **only when $\mu \le 1$** (no phase reversal). - ✅ **Chopper Stabilization:** Multiplying biosignal by square wave moves it to a higher frequency, away from $1/f$ noise of op-amps. - ✅ **Biomedical Applications:** Biotelemetry, EEG/ECG wireless transmission, multiplexer, narrowband interference rejection. - ✅ **Time-Frequency Duality Table (must memorize):** - Multiplication in time $\Leftrightarrow$ Convolution in frequency. - Convolution in time $\Leftrightarrow$ Multiplication in frequency. - Modulation in time $\Leftrightarrow$ Shift in frequency. - ✅ **In exams:** Always state units, conditions ($\mu \le 1$), and draw two-sided spectrum sketches. - ✅ **For 14-mark questions:** Provide (i) theorem statement, (ii) full mathematical derivation, (iii) spectrum sketch, (iv) bandwidth & power calculations, and (v) one biomedical application — covering all of these guarantees full marks.

topics.title: Parseval's Relation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parseval's Relation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Parseval's Relation** (also known as **Parseval's Theorem**, **Rayleigh's Energy Theorem**, or **Parseval's Identity**) is a fundamental result in Fourier analysis that expresses the **conservation of signal energy** between the time domain and the frequency domain. It states that the total energy computed by integrating the squared magnitude of a signal over time is exactly equal to the total energy computed by integrating the squared magnitude of its Fourier transform over frequency (normalized by $2\pi$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Form):**
> For an energy signal $x(t)$ with Fourier Transform $X(\omega)$, Parseval's Relation is:
> $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x(t) \mid^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid X(\omega) \mid^2 \, d\omega$$
> The left side is the **time-domain energy**; the right side is the **frequency-domain (spectral) energy**.

> [!NOTE]
> In biomedical signal processing, Parseval's Relation is a **diagnostic tool** — it allows us to verify that no energy is "lost" or "created" when we transform an ECG, EEG, or EMG signal from time to frequency domain. This is critical for validating digital filters, FFT computations, and spectral estimators used in patient monitoring equipment.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

Imagine you have a tank of water with a total volume of **100 litres**. Now, you pump this water out through a pipe and measure the *flow rate* (litres per second) at the pipe's mouth. If you integrate the flow rate over the entire duration of the pump operation, you will recover exactly **100 litres** — not more, not less.

**Parseval's Relation works on the exact same principle:**

| Domain | What You Measure | Physical Interpretation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Time Domain** | $\int \mid x(t) \mid^2 dt$ | Total "energy" of the raw signal as it occurs over time |
| **Frequency Domain** | $\frac{1}{2\pi} \int \mid X(\omega) \mid^2 d\omega$ | Total "energy" summed across all frequency components |

> The signal $x(t)$ is like the water — the water itself doesn't change. You are just **changing the lens** through which you observe it. Parseval's theorem guarantees that both lenses report the **same total quantity** (energy). If they don't match, you have introduced an error (e.g., wrong scaling in your FFT, an improper filter, or computational loss).

### 1.3 Why This Matters in Biomedical Engineering

- **ECG Signal Analysis:** Verifies that the energy in the QRS complex is preserved in its frequency representation — used in R-peak detection algorithms.
- **EEG Spectral Analysis:** Confirms that power spectral density (PSD) computations (Welch's method) are mathematically consistent.
- **Filter Validation:** After applying a digital filter to clean an EMG signal, Parseval's theorem confirms the noise energy removed.
- **Compression Algorithms (e.g., wavelets for ECG compression):** Ensures that compressed-then-reconstructed signals retain their clinical diagnostic energy.

### 1.4 Important Physical & Mathematical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Normalization constant** for continuous-time Parseval's relation: $\frac{1}{2\pi}$
> - **Normalization constant** for discrete-time Parseval's relation: $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ (DTFT form) or $\frac{1}{N}$ (DFT form)
> - **Energy of a signal $E$** is measured in **Joules (J)** for physical signals, or in **arbitrary units (a.u.)** for normalized bioelectric signals.
> - **Parseval's relation is a direct consequence of the orthogonality** of complex exponentials $e^{j\omega t}$ over the interval $(-\infty, \infty)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core "Why" — Orthogonality of Complex Exponentials

Parseval's Relation is not a coincidence — it is a direct mathematical consequence of the **orthogonality principle** of the complex exponential basis functions $e^{j\omega t}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Orthogonality Statement:**
> $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega_1 t} \cdot e^{-j\omega_2 t} \, dt = 2\pi \, \delta(\omega_1 - \omega_2)$$
> This means distinct frequency components are "mathematically perpendicular" to one another, so their energy contributions simply add up — they do not interfere. This additive energy property is what allows Parseval's theorem to hold.

---

### 2.2 Forms of Parseval's Relation (Continuous-Time)

| Form # | Domain Pair | Mathematical Statement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **CT-1** | CTFT (Continuous-Time Fourier Transform) | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x(t) \mid^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid X(\omega) \mid^2 d\omega$ |
| **CT-2** | CTFT (using frequency $f$ in Hz) | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x(t) \mid^2 dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid X(f) \mid^2 df$ |
| **CT-3** | Fourier Series (Periodic) | $\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \mid x(t) \mid^2 dt = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \mid a_k \mid^2$ |

> **Engineering Utility:** The CT-2 form is preferred in **biomedical instrumentation** because clinical devices (EEG machines, ECG monitors) record signals in **Hz**, not radians/sec. Notice the absence of the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor — it is absorbed into the change of variables $d\omega = 2\pi \, df$.

---

### 2.3 Forms of Parseval's Relation (Discrete-Time)

| Form # | Domain Pair | Mathematical Statement |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **DT-1** | DTFT (Discrete-Time Fourier Transform) | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x[n] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \mid X(e^{j\omega}) \mid^2 d\omega$ |
| **DT-2** | DFT (Discrete Fourier Transform) | $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mid x[n] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \mid X[k] \mid^2$ |
| **DT-3** | Energy in windowed segment | $E_{window} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \mid X[k] \mid^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** The $\frac{1}{N}$ scaling in DT-2 depends on the **DFT convention** used. Always check whether the problem uses the *signal-analysis convention* (forward DFT uses $\frac{1}{N}$, inverse uses $1$) or the *signal-processing convention* (forward uses $1$, inverse uses $\frac{1}{N}$). Most KTU textbooks follow the convention where $\frac{1}{N}$ appears in Parseval's identity as shown in DT-2.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Power Spectral Density (PSD) Computation:** Welch's method uses Parseval's theorem to convert a frequency-domain power estimate back to a time-domain variance.
2. **Filter Design Verification:** If a low-pass filter has frequency response $H(\omega)$, the output energy is $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int \mid X(\omega) H(\omega) \mid^2 d\omega$.
3. **Wavelet Transform Validation:** In wavelet-based ECG denoising, Parseval's theorem (generalized) ensures energy preservation across scales.
4. **Medical Device Calibration:** Devices like pulse oximeters and ECG monitors use Parseval's theorem in their DSP pipelines to verify signal integrity.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol/Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $E = \int \mid x(t) \mid^2 dt$ | Total energy of $x(t)$ | Joules (J) for physical signals |
| $E = \sum \mid x[n] \mid^2$ | Total energy of $x[n]$ | Dimensionless (a.u.) |
| $\frac{1}{2\pi}\int \mid X(\omega) \mid^2 d\omega$ | Spectral energy density integrated | Equals $E$ (Parseval) |
| $\mid X(\omega) \mid^2$ | **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)** | $\text{J} \cdot \text{s/rad}$ |
| $\frac{\mid X(\omega) \mid^2}{2\pi}$ | **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** form | For random/stochastic signals |
| $\sum_{k} \mid a_k \mid^2$ | Energy of Fourier Series coefficients | Equals $\frac{1}{T}\int_T \mid x(t) \mid^2 dt$ |
| $\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \mid X[k] \mid^2$ | Energy from DFT coefficients | Equals $\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mid x[n] \mid^2$ |
| $E_{out} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int \mid X(\omega)H(\omega) \mid^2 d\omega$ | Output energy through filter $H$ | Used in filter validation |
| $\delta(\omega_1 - \omega_2)$ | Dirac delta (orthogonality kernel) | $\int \delta = 1$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Continuous-Time Parseval's Relation

**Goal:** Prove that
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x(t) \mid^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid X(\omega) \mid^2 \, d\omega$$

#### Step 1: Start with the Right-Hand Side (Frequency Domain Energy)

We begin with the energy expressed in the frequency domain. We define $E_f$ as the integral of the squared magnitude of the Fourier transform:

$$E_f = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega) \, X^*(\omega) \, d\omega$$

> **Logic:** Since $\mid X(\omega) \mid^2 = X(\omega) \cdot X^*(\omega)$, we explicitly write it as a product to substitute the inverse transform later. This is the standard trick for Parseval-type proofs.

#### Step 2: Substitute the Inverse Fourier Transform for $X^*(\omega)$

The inverse Fourier transform is given by:
$$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt$$

Taking the complex conjugate of both sides (noting that $x(t)$ is real for physical bio-signals, so $x^*(t) = x(t)$):
$$X^*(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, e^{+j\omega \tau} \, d\tau$$

> **Logic:** We use a dummy variable $\tau$ (different from $t$) to avoid confusion in the next step when we have two integrals. This is a critical notational step that students often miss in KTU exams.

#### Step 3: Substitute Back into the Energy Integral

$$E_f = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{-j\omega t} \, dt \right] \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) \, e^{+j\omega \tau} \, d\tau \right] d\omega$$

#### Step 4: Rearrange the Order of Integration

Since the integrals are over independent variables, we can rearrange the order of integration:

$$E_f = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, x(\tau) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega(\tau - t)} \, d\omega \right] dt \, d\tau$$

> **Logic:** Group the exponentials together so that we can perform the inner $\omega$-integral first. This isolates the orthogonality structure.

#### Step 5: Evaluate the Inner $\omega$-Integral Using the Dirac Delta Identity

Using the standard Fourier identity:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega(\tau - t)} \, d\omega = 2\pi \, \delta(\tau - t)$$

Substituting this:
$$E_f = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, x(\tau) \cdot 2\pi \, \delta(\tau - t) \, dt \, d\tau$$

#### Step 6: Simplify the Constants

The $2\pi$ in the numerator cancels the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ prefactor:

$$E_f = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, x(\tau) \, \delta(\tau - t) \, dt \, d\tau$$

#### Step 7: Apply the Sifting Property of the Dirac Delta

The sifting property states that:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(\tau) \, \delta(\tau - t) \, d\tau = f(t)$$

Applying this to integrate over $\tau$ first:
$$E_f = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot x(t) \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x(t) \mid^2 \, dt$$

> **Logic:** The delta function "collapses" the $\tau$-integral, leaving us with a single time integral of $\mid x(t) \mid^2$.

#### Step 8: Conclusion

We have shown:
$$E_f = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x(t) \mid^2 \, dt = E_t$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x(t) \mid^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid X(\omega) \mid^2 \, d\omega} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Discrete-Time Parseval's Relation (DFT Form)

**Goal:** Prove that
$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mid x[n] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \mid X[k] \mid^2$$

#### Step 1: Start with the Right-Hand Side (Frequency Domain Energy)

$$E_f = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] \, X^*[k]$$

#### Step 2: Substitute the DFT Definition

The DFT is defined as:
$$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}$$

Its conjugate is:
$$X^*[k] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \, e^{+j\frac{2\pi}{N}km}$$

> **Logic:** Again, we use a different dummy index $m$ (instead of $n$) to distinguish the two sums.

#### Step 3: Substitute and Rearrange

$$E_f = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[ \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn} \right] \left[ \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] \, e^{+j\frac{2\pi}{N}km} \right]$$

$$E_f = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, x[m] \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}k(m - n)} \right]$$

#### Step 4: Evaluate the Inner Geometric Series

The inner sum is a geometric series:
$$S = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}k(m - n)}$$

Using the closed-form identity for geometric series:
$$S = \begin{cases} N, & \text{if } m = n \pmod{N} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

> **Logic:** This is the **discrete orthogonality** of the complex exponentials — the discrete analog of the Dirac delta in the continuous case. Since $0 \leq n, m \leq N-1$, the condition $m = n \pmod{N}$ reduces to $m = n$.

#### Step 5: Apply the Sifting Property

$$E_f = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, x[m] \cdot N \cdot \delta[m - n]$$

$$E_f = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[n] \, x[m] \, \delta[m - n]$$

#### Step 6: Collapse the $m$-Sum

The Kronecker delta $\delta[m-n]$ is $1$ only when $m = n$, so:
$$E_f = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] \cdot x[n] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mid x[n] \mid^2$$

#### Step 7: Conclusion

$$\boxed{\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mid x[n] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \mid X[k] \mid^2} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (KTU Numerical Type)

**Problem:** Consider the discrete-time signal $x[n] = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ (with $N=4$). Compute the energy in the time domain and verify Parseval's relation using the DFT.

#### Step 1: Time Domain Energy

$$E_t = \sum_{n=0}^{3} \mid x[n] \mid^2 = 1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + 4^2 = 1 + 4 + 9 + 16 = 30$$

#### Step 2: Compute the DFT

The DFT is $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] \, e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{4}kn} = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] \, e^{-j\frac{\pi}{2}kn}$.

For $k=0$:
$$X[0] = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

For $k=1$:
$$X[1] = 1 + 2e^{-j\pi/2} + 3e^{-j\pi} + 4e^{-j3\pi/2}$$
$$= 1 + 2(-j) + 3(-1) + 4(j) = 1 - 2j - 3 + 4j = -2 + 2j$$

For $k=2$:
$$X[2] = 1 + 2e^{-j\pi} + 3e^{-j2\pi} + 4e^{-j3\pi}$$
$$= 1 + 2(-1) + 3(1) + 4(-1) = 1 - 2 + 3 - 4 = -2$$

For $k=3$:
$$X[3] = 1 + 2e^{-j3\pi/2} + 3e^{-j3\pi} + 4e^{-j9\pi/2}$$
$$= 1 + 2(j) + 3(-1) + 4(-j) = 1 + 2j - 3 - 4j = -2 - 2j$$

So $X[k] = \{10, \, -2+2j, \, -2, \, -2-2j\}$.

#### Step 3: Compute Squared Magnitudes

$$\mid X[0] \mid^2 = 100$$
$$\mid X[1] \mid^2 = (-2)^2 + (2)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8$$
$$\mid X[2] \mid^2 = (-2)^2 + 0^2 = 4$$
$$\mid X[3] \mid^2 = (-2)^2 + (-2)^2 = 4 + 4 = 8$$

#### Step 4: Apply Parseval's Relation

$$E_f = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{3} \mid X[k] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{4}(100 + 8 + 4 + 8) = \frac{120}{4} = 30$$

#### Step 5: Verify

$$E_t = E_f = 30 \quad \checkmark$$

> **Result:** Parseval's relation is satisfied, confirming that the DFT computation preserved the signal energy.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Flow in the Parseval Framework (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following diagram illustrates how energy is preserved as a biomedical signal passes through the Fourier transform pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sigA[Biomedical Signal x(t)] --> node1{Is the signal periodic?}
    node1 -- Yes --> sigB[Periodic: x(t) = x(t + T)]
    node1 -- No --> sigC[Aperiodic: Finite Energy Signal]
    sigB --> transA[Fourier Series Decomposition]
    sigC --> transB[Continuous Time Fourier Transform]
    transA --> coefA[Set of Fourier Coefficients a sub k]
    transB --> specA[Spectrum X of omega]
    coefA --> energyA[Time Domain Energy Calculation]
    specA --> energyB[Frequency Domain Energy Calculation]
    energyA --> compA[Integral of absolute value of x(t) squared dt]
    energyB --> compB[1 over 2 pi times Integral of absolute value of X omega squared d omega]
    compA --> verify{Parseval Equality Check}
    compB --> verify
    verify -- Equal --> passA[Energy Preserved: Computation Valid]
    verify -- Not Equal --> failA[Energy Mismatch: Error in Pipeline]
    failA --> debugA[Check Sampling Rate, Windowing, and DFT Scaling]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** A biomedical signal $x(t)$ is decomposed via Fourier methods. The energy is independently computed in both domains. Parseval's theorem asserts that the two computations must be equal — failure indicates a numerical or procedural error in the signal processing pipeline.

---

### 4.2 DFT Energy Conservation Pipeline (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    smpA[Analog Bio Signal] --> adcA[Analog to Digital Converter]
    adcA --> smpB[Sampled Signal x of n]
    smpB --> winA[Apply Window Function]
    winA --> smpC[Windowed Finite Length Sequence]
    smpC --> dftA[Compute N point DFT]
    dftA --> coefB[DFT Coefficients X of k]
    smpC --> engA[Time Domain Energy: Sum of absolute value of x of n squared]
    coefB --> engB[Frequency Domain Energy: 1 over N times Sum of absolute value of X of k squared]
    engA --> compC{Parseval Equality}
    engB --> compC
    compC -- True --> outA[Valid DSP Output]
    compC -- False --> outB[Pipeline Error]
```

> **Engineering Insight:** This pipeline mirrors the actual workflow inside ECG and EEG digital signal processors. Windowing (e.g., Hamming, Hanning) is applied before the DFT to reduce spectral leakage, and Parseval's theorem is used as a final integrity check.

---

### 4.3 Conceptual Map of Parseval's Theorem (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Parseval Relation] --> branchA[Continuous Time]
    rootA --> branchB[Discrete Time]
    branchA --> ctA[CTFT Form]
    branchA --> ctB[Fourier Series Form]
    ctA --> ctA1[Energy in Time equals 1 over 2 pi times Energy in Frequency]
    ctB --> ctB1[Average Power equals Sum of absolute value of a sub k squared]
    branchB --> dtA[DTFT Form]
    branchB --> dtB[DFT Form]
    dtA --> dtA1[Sum of absolute value of x of n squared equals 1 over 2 pi times Integral over negative pi to pi]
    dtB --> dtB1[Sum of absolute value of x of n squared equals 1 over N times Sum of absolute value of X of k squared]
    ctA1 --> appA[Application: PSD Computation]
    ctB1 --> appA
    dtA1 --> appA
    dtB1 --> appA
    appA --> appA1[Biomedical DSP, Filter Design, Compression]
```
