## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct short-answer conceptual questions, each carrying **3 marks** as per KTU 2024 ESE Part A structure. Answers should be concise (3–5 lines) and formula-driven.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

State and explain Parseval's theorem for continuous-time signals. What is its physical significance?

**Model Answer:**

Parseval's theorem for a continuous-time energy signal $x(t)$ with Fourier transform $X(\omega)$ states that:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x(t) \mid^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid X(\omega) \mid^2 \, d\omega$$

**Physical Significance:** The theorem expresses the **conservation of energy** between the time and frequency domains. The total energy of a signal, computed in the time domain by integrating the squared magnitude, is exactly equal to the energy computed in the frequency domain by integrating the squared magnitude of its spectrum (scaled by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$). This implies that the Fourier transform is a **lossless operation** — it neither creates nor destroys signal energy. In biomedical signal processing, this property is used to verify the correctness of spectral computations, design energy-preserving filters, and validate FFT-based diagnostic algorithms.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Write the discrete-time Parseval's relation for an $N$-point DFT. How does it differ from the continuous-time version?

**Model Answer:**

For an $N$-point DFT pair $x[n] \leftrightarrow X[k]$, Parseval's relation is:

$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mid x[n] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \mid X[k] \mid^2$$

**Differences from the Continuous-Time Version:**

1. The continuous-time version uses integrals over infinite domains, while the discrete version uses finite sums.
2. The normalization factor changes from $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ in CTFT to $\frac{1}{N}$ in DFT (with the convention where the forward DFT is unnormalized and the inverse DFT has the $\frac{1}{N}$ factor).
3. The discrete version is a **numerically computable identity** suitable for digital processors, whereas the continuous version is primarily used in analytical derivations.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 3 carries 14 marks. Students are given an internal choice between two questions. Each question typically has sub-parts (a) carrying 7 marks and (b) carrying 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**Q3(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3 Pattern]**

Derive Parseval's theorem for continuous-time Fourier transform. Clearly state the assumptions and the role of orthogonality.

**Model Solution:**

We want to prove:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x(t) \mid^2 \, dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid X(\omega) \mid^2 \, d\omega$$

**Assumption:** $x(t)$ is a real-valued, finite-energy signal (square-integrable), and its Fourier transform $X(\omega)$ exists.

**Step 1:** Start with the energy in the frequency domain:
$$E_f = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega) X^*(\omega) \, d\omega$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ and $X^*(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) e^{j\omega \tau} d\tau$:

$$E_f = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt \right] \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) e^{j\omega \tau} d\tau \right] d\omega$$

**Step 3:** Interchange the order of integration:

$$E_f = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) x(\tau) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega(\tau - t)} d\omega \right] dt \, d\tau$$

**Step 4:** Use the orthogonality identity:
$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j\omega(\tau - t)} d\omega = 2\pi \delta(\tau - t)$$

This identity is the **orthogonality of complex exponentials** over the entire real line — a consequence of the fact that exponentials at different frequencies are "perpendicular" in $L^2$ function space.

**Step 5:** Substitute the delta function:
$$E_f = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) x(\tau) \delta(\tau - t) \, dt \, d\tau$$

**Step 6:** Apply the sifting property $\int f(\tau)\delta(\tau - t) d\tau = f(t)$:
$$E_f = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot x(t) \, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x(t) \mid^2 \, dt$$

Therefore, $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x(t) \mid^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \mid X(\omega) \mid^2 d\omega$. $\blacksquare$

**Role of Orthogonality:** Orthogonality ensures that the energy contributions from distinct frequency components do not cross-couple. Without orthogonality, the energy integral would have cross-terms, and Parseval's relation would not hold in its simple form.

---

**Q3(b). [7 Marks]**

A discrete-time signal $x[n] = \{1, 1, 1, 1\}$ is sampled from a 1-second ECG segment. Compute the 4-point DFT, then verify Parseval's relation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Time Domain Energy**
$$E_t = \sum_{n=0}^{3} \mid x[n] \mid^2 = 1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 + 1^2 = 4$$

**Step 2: Compute the 4-point DFT**

Using $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{4}kn} = \sum_{n=0}^{3} e^{-j\frac{\pi}{2}kn}$:

$$X[0] = 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4$$

$$X[1] = 1 + e^{-j\pi/2} + e^{-j\pi} + e^{-j3\pi/2} = 1 - j - 1 + j = 0$$

$$X[2] = 1 + e^{-j\pi} + e^{-j2\pi} + e^{-j3\pi} = 1 - 1 + 1 - 1 = 0$$

$$X[3] = 1 + e^{-j3\pi/2} + e^{-j3\pi} + e^{-j9\pi/2} = 1 + j - 1 - j = 0$$

So $X[k] = \{4, 0, 0, 0\}$.

**Step 3: Frequency Domain Energy**

$$E_f = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{3} \mid X[k] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{4}(16 + 0 + 0 + 0) = 4$$

**Step 4: Verification**
$$E_t = 4 = E_f \quad \checkmark$$

Parseval's relation is verified. The constant DC signal has all its energy concentrated in the $k=0$ (DC) bin, with no energy in any other frequency component — consistent with the physical intuition that a constant has zero frequency content.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**Q4(a). [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3 Pattern]**

State and prove Parseval's theorem for the discrete Fourier transform (DFT) of an $N$-point sequence. Mention its application in computing the energy of a finite-length biomedical signal.

**Model Solution:**

**Statement:** For an $N$-point sequence $x[n]$ with DFT $X[k]$:
$$\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mid x[n] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \mid X[k] \mid^2$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Begin with the right-hand side:
$$E_f = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X[k] X^*[k]$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}$ and $X^*[k] = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}km}$:

$$E_f = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x[n] e^{-j\frac{2\pi}{N}kn}\right] \left[\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[m] e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}km}\right]$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange:
$$E_f = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[n] x[m] \left[\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}k(m-n)}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the inner geometric sum. Let $\alpha = e^{j\frac{2\pi}{N}(m-n)}$:
$$S = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \alpha^k = \frac{1 - \alpha^N}{1 - \alpha}$$

If $m = n$, $\alpha = 1$, so $S = N$.
If $m \neq n$, $\alpha^N = e^{j2\pi(m-n)} = 1$ and $\alpha \neq 1$, so $S = 0$.

Thus $S = N \cdot \delta[m - n]$, where $\delta$ is the Kronecker delta.

**Step 5:** Apply the sifting property:
$$E_f = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1}\sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x[n] x[m] \cdot N \cdot \delta[m - n] = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \mid x[n] \mid^2$$

$\blacksquare$

**Application in Biomedical Signal Processing:** Parseval's theorem is used to compute the energy of a finite-length ECG/EEG/EMG segment from its DFT coefficients. This is computationally efficient because **squaring $N$ time-domain samples and summing them** is equivalent to **squaring $N$ frequency-domain coefficients, summing them, and dividing by $N$** — both give the same result. This is exploited in **real-time heart rate variability (HRV) monitors** and **EEG band-power calculators** (e.g., delta, theta, alpha, beta band energy).

---

**Q4(b). [7 Marks]**

A 4-point sequence $x[n] = \{1, 0, -1, 0\}$ is the first cycle of an ECG-derived respiration signal. Compute the energy using both time-domain summation and Parseval's relation. Comment on the spectral energy distribution.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Time Domain Energy**
$$E_t = \sum_{n=0}^{3} \mid x[n] \mid^2 = 1^2 + 0^2 + (-1)^2 + 0^2 = 2$$

**Step 2: Compute the 4-point DFT**

$X[k] = \sum_{n=0}^{3} x[n] e^{-j\frac{\pi}{2}kn}$

$$X[0] = 1 + 0 - 1 + 0 = 0$$

$$X[1] = 1 + 0 \cdot e^{-j\pi/2} + (-1)e^{-j\pi} + 0 = 1 - (-1)(-1) = 1 - 1 = 0$$

*Correction:* $X[1] = 1 + 0 \cdot e^{-j\pi/2} + (-1)e^{-j\pi} + 0 \cdot e^{-j3\pi/2} = 1 + (-1)(-1) = 1 + 1 = 2$

$$X[2] = 1 + 0 + (-1)e^{-j2\pi} + 0 = 1 + (-1)(1) = 0$$

$$X[3] = 1 + 0 + (-1)e^{-j3\pi} + 0 = 1 + (-1)(-1) = 2$$

So $X[k] = \{0, 2, 0, 2\}$.

**Step 3: Frequency Domain Energy**
$$E_f = \frac{1}{4}\sum_{k=0}^{3} \mid X[k] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{4}(0 + 4 + 0 + 4) = \frac{8}{4} = 2$$

**Step 4: Verification**
$$E_t = 2 = E_f \quad \checkmark$$

**Spectral Energy Distribution Comment:**

All the energy of $x[n] = \{1, 0, -1, 0\}$ is concentrated in the **odd-indexed DFT bins** ($k=1$ and $k=3$), with zero energy in the DC component ($k=0$) and the Nyquist bin ($k=2$). This is consistent with the fact that the sequence is an **odd-symmetric alternating pattern** — such signals have no DC offset and no energy at the Nyquist frequency. In biomedical terms, this represents a **pure alternating signal** with no baseline wander — characteristic of a clean, high-pass-filtered ECG or respiration waveform with no DC drift.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Missing the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor:** Students often write $\int \mid x(t) \mid^2 dt = \int \mid X(\omega) \mid^2 d\omega$ (without the normalization constant). This is a **3-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
>
> 2. **Confusing the CTFT and DFT conventions:** In CTFT, the inverse transform has the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ factor; in some DFT conventions, the forward transform has the $\frac{1}{N}$ factor. Mixing these leads to incorrect Parseval identities. Always **explicitly state the convention** you are using.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the dummy variable change:** When deriving Parseval's theorem, students often write $X(\omega) = \int x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ and $X^*(\omega) = \int x(t) e^{j\omega t} dt$ using the **same variable $t$** in both integrals. This is mathematically ambiguous. Use **$t$ and $\tau$** as distinct dummy variables. Examiners specifically look for this.
>
> 4. **Sign error in the exponent:** The sifting integral requires $e^{j\omega(\tau - t)}$, not $e^{j\omega(t - \tau)}$. A sign error propagates and changes the delta function to $\delta(t - \tau)$, which is still valid by symmetry, but the intermediate step must be shown correctly for full marks.
>
> 5. **DFT magnitude computation errors:** For complex $X[k] = a + jb$, the magnitude squared is $a^2 + b^2$, not $a^2 + b$ or $a + b^2$. A common student error.
>
> 6. **In numerical problems, not showing the time-domain energy separately:** KTU board examiners expect **two independent computations** of energy (time and frequency) to demonstrate the verification. Just showing the frequency-domain calculation without the time-domain counterpart loses 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Parseval's Relation**

- **Core Identity (CTFT):** $\int \mid x(t) \mid^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int \mid X(\omega) \mid^2 d\omega$ — **memorize verbatim**.

- **Core Identity (DFT):** $\sum \mid x[n] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{N}\sum \mid X[k] \mid^2$ — **memorize verbatim**.

- **Core Identity (CTFT in Hz):** $\int \mid x(t) \mid^2 dt = \int \mid X(f) \mid^2 df$ — **note absence of $\frac{1}{2\pi}$**.

- **Core Identity (DTFT):** $\sum \mid x[n] \mid^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} \mid X(e^{j\omega}) \mid^2 d\omega$.

- **Physical meaning:** Energy is **conserved** between time and frequency domains. The Fourier transform is a **lossless, unitary** operation on $L^2$ signals.

- **Underlying principle:** **Orthogonality of complex exponentials** — both continuous ($2\pi\delta$ form) and discrete ($N \cdot$ Kronecker delta form).

- **Standard derivation steps:** (1) Express frequency energy, (2) substitute inverse transform, (3) interchange integration order, (4) apply orthogonality identity, (5) apply sifting property, (6) obtain time-domain energy.

- **Biomedical Applications:**
  - ECG QRS energy computation
  - EEG band-power (delta, theta, alpha, beta) calculation
  - EMG signal energy monitoring
  - Filter validation (output energy calculation)
  - Compression algorithm verification (wavelets for ECG)
  - PSD computation verification (Welch's method)

- **Key normalization factors to remember:**
  - CTFT Parseval: **$\frac{1}{2\pi}$**
  - DFT Parseval: **$\frac{1}{N}$** (signal-analysis convention)
  - DTFT Parseval: **$\frac{1}{2\pi}$**

- **Conditions for validity:** Signal must be **square-integrable** (finite energy) for CTFT version; sequence must be of **finite length** for DFT version.

- **Related theorem:** **Plancherel theorem** is a more general form of Parseval's theorem that applies to all $L^2$ functions, not just Fourier-transformable ones.

- **Most common exam question type:** Numerical verification (compute energy in both domains and check equality) — **practice at least 3 such problems**.

- **Mistakes to avoid:** Missing normalization factor, mixing up CTFT/DFT conventions, sign errors in exponents, using same dummy variable in two integrals.

- **One-line memory hook:** *"Energy in, energy out — Parseval is what it's about."* (Energy is preserved across domains.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Initial Value Theorem; Rational Z Transform; Poles and Zeros; LTI System Function; Inverse Z Transform - Long Division, Partial Fraction Expansion Method, Residue Method, Convolution Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Initial Value Theorem; Rational Z Transform; Poles and Zeros; LTI System Function; Inverse Z Transform - Long Division, Partial Fraction Expansion Method, Residue Method, Convolution Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Initial Value Theorem (IVT) in Z-Domain

**Formal Definition:**
The Initial Value Theorem states that for a causal sequence $x[n]$ whose Z-Transform $X(z)$ exists and has no poles on or outside the unit circle except possibly at $z = \infty$, the initial value $x[0]$ can be directly obtained from $X(z)$ as $z \to \infty$:

$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** IVT is essential for verifying the correctness of a Z-Transform expression without performing the full inverse Z-Transform operation. It is a high-yield topic for short-answer questions.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a movie reel. The Initial Value Theorem is like "fast-forwarding" the mathematical representation $X(z)$ to its highest frequency (infinity), which corresponds to time zero — the very first frame of the movie. At $z = \infty$, all the "history" (past samples) is suppressed, and we are left with only the starting value of the signal. Mathematically, as $z \to \infty$, all terms with $z^{-1}, z^{-2}, \ldots$ vanish, leaving only the constant term $x[0]$.

### 1.2 Rational Z-Transform

**Formal Definition:**
A Rational Z-Transform is one that can be expressed as a ratio of two polynomials in $z^{-1}$ (or in $z$):

$$X(z) = \frac{N(z)}{D(z)} = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_M z^{-M}}{1 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N}}$$

where $b_k$ and $a_k$ are real constants, $M$ is the order of the numerator, and $N$ is the order of the denominator.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of a Rational Z-Transform as a "recipe ratio" — like a chemical formula $C_6H_{12}O_6$. Just as the recipe expresses glucose as a ratio of constituent atoms, the rational Z-Transform expresses a signal as a ratio of "input ingredients" (numerator) to "system memory" (denominator). The numerator coefficients $b_k$ define the zeros; the denominator coefficients $a_k$ define the poles and system stability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biomedical Relevance:** Rational Z-Transforms model **almost all biomedical filter systems** — ECG filters, EMG denoising filters, EEG band-pass filters — because real-world systems have finite memory (described by a finite-order denominator polynomial).

### 1.3 Poles and Zeros

**Formal Definition:**
- **Zeros of $X(z)$:** Values of $z$ for which $X(z) = 0$ (roots of the numerator polynomial $N(z) = 0$).
- **Poles of $X(z)$:** Values of $z$ for which $X(z) = \infty$ (roots of the denominator polynomial $D(z) = 0$).

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine the signal $X(z)$ as a landscape. Zeros are like **valleys** where the function touches the ground (becomes zero), and poles are like **mountains or singularities** where the function shoots up to infinity. The shape of this landscape completely determines the signal behavior. A pole **on or outside the unit circle** means the system is unstable — the signal grows without bound (think of a heart pacemaker that runs away).

### 1.4 LTI System Function $H(z)$

**Formal Definition:**
For a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) discrete-time system, the **system function** $H(z)$ is the Z-Transform of the impulse response $h[n]$:

$$H(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{h[n]\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n] \, z^{-n}$$

The system function completely characterizes an LTI system in the Z-domain, equivalent to the transfer function in analog systems.

### 1.5 Inverse Z-Transform Methods

The Inverse Z-Transform recovers $x[n]$ from $X(z)$. The four primary methods per the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

| Method | Best Suited For | Core Idea |
|---|---|---|
| **Long Division (Power Series)** | Non-rational or high-order rational $X(z)$ | Polynomial long division of $N(z)/D(z)$ to get $x[0] + x[1]z^{-1} + \cdots$ |
| **Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)** | Rational $X(z)$ with distinct real/complex poles | Decompose into simple first-order fractions, then invert term-by-term |
| **Residue Method** | Rational $X(z)$ with simple or repeated poles | Contour integration using Cauchy's Residue Theorem |
| **Convolution Method** | $X(z) = X_1(z) \cdot X_2(z)$ | Use $x[n] = x_1[n] * x_2[n]$ in time domain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** A **causal LTI system** has a Region of Convergence (ROC) of the form $\vert z \vert > R$, where $R$ is the radius of the outermost pole. For a stable causal system, **all poles must lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Initial Value Theorem — Detailed Logic

The IVT is derived by examining the Z-Transform as $z \to \infty$:

**Step 1:** Write the bilateral Z-Transform definition:
$$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n} = \cdots + x[-2]z^{2} + x[-1]z + x[0] + x[1]z^{-1} + x[2]z^{-2} + \cdots$$

**Step 2:** Take the limit $z \to \infty$. All terms with $z^{-n}$ (for $n \geq 1$) tend to zero, and all terms with $z^{+n}$ (for $n \geq 1$) tend to infinity.

**Step 3:** For a **causal** sequence, $x[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$, so all positive-power terms vanish, leaving only the $n = 0$ term:
$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

**Why it matters:** IVT provides a quick verification check. If you derive a Z-Transform and $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ does not match the first sample of your original signal, your derivation is wrong.

### 2.2 Rational Z-Transform — Pole-Zero Factorization

Any rational $X(z)$ can be factored as:
$$X(z) = \frac{b_0 \prod_{k=1}^{M}(1 - z_k z^{-1})}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(1 - p_k z^{-1})} = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{M}(z - z_k)}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(z - p_k)}$$

where $z_k$ are zeros, $p_k$ are poles, and $K$ is a gain constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When representing poles/zeros on the Z-plane, always mark the **unit circle** ($\vert z \vert = 1$) — examiners look for it. Stability requires all poles inside this circle.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | Initial Value Theorem (causal sequence) |
| 2 | $x[\infty] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)X(z)$ | Final Value Theorem (for stable causal systems) |
| 3 | $X(z) = \frac{N(z)}{D(z)} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | General rational form |
| 4 | $X(z) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{M}(1 - z_k z^{-1})}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(1 - p_k z^{-1})}$ | Pole-zero factored form |
| 5 | ROC: stable causal LTI $\Rightarrow \vert p_k \vert < 1 \;\; \forall k$ | Stability condition |
| 6 | $x[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{X(z)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C X(z) z^{n-1} dz$ | Inverse Z-Transform (contour integral) |
| 7 | $h[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{H(z)\}$ | Impulse response from system function |
| 8 | $H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)}$ | System function as input-output ratio |
| 9 | PFE: $\frac{N(z)}{D(z)} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{R_k}{1 - p_k z^{-1}}$ | Partial fraction decomposition (simple poles) |
| 10 | $R_k = \left. (1 - p_k z^{-1}) \frac{X(z)}{z} \right\vert_{z = p_k}$ | Residue computation for PFE |
| 11 | $x[n] = \sum_{k} \text{Res}\left[ X(z) z^{n-1}, z = p_k \right]$ | Residue method formula |
| 12 | $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{X_1(z) X_2(z)\} = x_1[n] * x_2[n]$ | Convolution property |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility in biomedical engineering:** In DSP design for ECG/EEG processing, the IVT is used to check that the filter's transient response begins at zero (or the correct initial sample) before processing begins — critical for diagnostic systems where artifacts at startup could be misread as pathology.

### 2.4 Inverse Z-Transform: When to Use Which Method

**Long Division** is used when:
- $X(z)$ is non-rational (e.g., $X(z) = \log(1 + az^{-1})$)
- The first few samples of $x[n]$ are needed
- The exact closed-form $x[n]$ is not required

**Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)** is used when:
- $X(z)$ is a proper rational function ($M < N$)
- We need the closed-form expression for $x[n]$
- The system is described by a difference equation

**Residue Method** is used when:
- $X(z)$ has complex poles (most rigorous method)
- PFE becomes algebraically messy
- For exam questions involving systems with complex conjugate pole pairs

**Convolution Method** is used when:
- $X(z)$ factorizes naturally as a product of two known Z-Transforms
- E.g., $X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} \cdot \frac{z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}}$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Initial Value Theorem

We start from the one-sided Z-Transform of a causal sequence $x[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n} \\
X(z) &= x[0] + x[1]z^{-1} + x[2]z^{-2} + x[3]z^{-3} + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

Taking the limit as $z \to \infty$ on both sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) &= \lim_{z \to \infty} \left( x[0] + x[1]z^{-1} + x[2]z^{-2} + \cdots \right) \\
\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) &= x[0] + 0 + 0 + 0 + \cdots \\
\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) &= x[0]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** All terms with $z^{-n}$ (for $n \geq 1$) vanish as $z \to \infty$, isolating the initial sample $x[0]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existence condition:** The theorem requires $X(z)$ to have a finite limit as $z \to \infty$. This is satisfied when $M \leq N$ in the rational form, i.e., the number of zeros is less than or equal to the number of poles. If $M > N$, the limit is infinite and the theorem does not directly apply.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Long Division Method

**Problem:** Find the first 5 samples of $x[n]$ using long division, given:
$$X(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5z^{-1} + 0.06z^{-2}}$$

**Step 1 — Set up the long division.** Divide the numerator polynomial (1) by the denominator polynomial $(1 - 0.5z^{-1} + 0.06z^{-2})$.

**Step 2 — Perform the division** to get the power series in $z^{-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&1 \div (1 - 0.5z^{-1} + 0.06z^{-2}) \\
&= 1 + 0.5z^{-1} + 0.19z^{-2} + 0.037z^{-3} - 0.00798z^{-4} + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Match coefficients.** The coefficients in ascending powers of $z^{-1}$ are the samples of $x[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[0] &= 1 \\
x[1] &= 0.5 \\
x[2] &= 0.19 \\
x[3] &= 0.037 \\
x[4] &= -0.00798
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by IVT:** $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = 1 = x[0]$ ✓ (matches our result).

### 3.3 Worked Example: Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE) Method

**Problem:** Find $x[n]$ using PFE, given:
$$X(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})(1 - 0.2z^{-1})}, \quad \vert z \vert > 0.5$$

**Step 1 — Identify the poles.** Denominator factored form gives poles at $z = 0.5$ and $z = 0.2$ (both inside the unit circle, so system is stable and causal).

**Step 2 — Set up the PFE:**
$$X(z) = \frac{R_1}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} + \frac{R_2}{1 - 0.2z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute residues** using the cover-up method:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_1 &= \left. \frac{1}{1 - 0.2z^{-1}} \right\vert_{z^{-1} = 2} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.2(2)} = \frac{1}{0.6} = \frac{5}{3} \\
R_2 &= \left. \frac{1}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} \right\vert_{z^{-1} = 5} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5(5)} = \frac{1}{-1.5} = -\frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the PFE** by recombining:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= \frac{5/3}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} - \frac{2/3}{1 - 0.2z^{-1}} \\
&= \frac{(5/3)(1 - 0.2z^{-1}) - (2/3)(1 - 0.5z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})(1 - 0.2z^{-1})} \\
&= \frac{(5/3 - 2/3) + (-1/3 + 1/3)z^{-1}}{D(z)} = \frac{1}{D(z)} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the inverse Z-Transform pair** $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\frac{1}{1 - az^{-1}}\right\} = a^n u[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[n] &= \frac{5}{3}(0.5)^n u[n] - \frac{2}{3}(0.2)^n u[n] \\
x[n] &= \left[ \frac{5}{3}(0.5)^n - \frac{2}{3}(0.2)^n \right] u[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verify with IVT:** $x[0] = \frac{5}{3}(1) - \frac{2}{3}(1) = 1$ ✓

### 3.4 Worked Example: Residue Method

**Problem:** Find $x[n]$ using the residue method, given:
$$X(z) = \frac{z^2}{(z - 0.5)(z - 0.2)}, \quad \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > 0.5$$

**Step 1 — Form the inverse Z-integral:**
$$x[n] = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C \frac{X(z)}{z} z^n \, dz = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \oint_C \frac{z^{n+1}}{(z - 0.5)(z - 0.2)} \, dz$$

**Step 2 — Identify poles inside contour $C$** (a circle of radius $> 0.5$): $z = 0.5$ and $z = 0.2$.

**Step 3 — Compute residues** at each pole:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Res}_{z = 0.5} &= \left. \frac{z^{n+1}}{z - 0.2} \right\vert_{z = 0.5} = \frac{(0.5)^{n+1}}{0.5 - 0.2} = \frac{(0.5)^{n+1}}{0.3} \\
\text{Res}_{z = 0.2} &= \left. \frac{z^{n+1}}{z - 0.5} \right\vert_{z = 0.2} = \frac{(0.2)^{n+1}}{0.2 - 0.5} = \frac{(0.2)^{n+1}}{-0.3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Sum the residues** to get $x[n]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[n] &= \text{Res}_{z=0.5} + \text{Res}_{z=0.2} \\
x[n] &= \frac{(0.5)^{n+1}}{0.3} - \frac{(0.2)^{n+1}}{0.3} \\
x[n] &= \frac{1}{0.3} \left[ (0.5)^{n+1} - (0.2)^{n+1} \right] u[n] \\
x[n] &= \frac{10}{3} \left[ (0.5)^{n+1} - (0.2)^{n+1} \right] u[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.5 Worked Example: Convolution Method

**Problem:** Find $x[n]$ using convolution, given:
$$X(z) = \frac{z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.4z^{-1})(1 - 0.6z^{-1})}$$

**Step 1 — Factor** $X(z)$ into two known Z-Transforms:
$$X_1(z) = z^{-1}, \quad X_2(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - 0.4z^{-1})(1 - 0.6z^{-1})}$$

**Step 2 — Inverse Z-Transform of $X_1(z)$:**
$$x_1[n] = \delta[n-1] \quad \text{(unit delayed impulse)}$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Z-Transform of $X_2(z)$** using PFE:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_2(z) &= \frac{A}{1 - 0.4z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - 0.6z^{-1}} \\
A &= \left. \frac{1}{1 - 0.6z^{-1}} \right\vert_{z^{-1} = 2.5} = \frac{1}{1 - 1.5} = -2 \\
B &= \left. \frac{1}{1 - 0.4z^{-1}} \right\vert_{z^{-1} = 1.667} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.667} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_2[n] &= \left[ -2(0.4)^n + 3(0.6)^n \right] u[n]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convolve** $x[n] = x_1[n] * x_2[n] = \delta[n-1] * x_2[n] = x_2[n-1]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x[n] &= \left[ -2(0.4)^{n-1} + 3(0.6)^{n-1} \right] u[n-1]
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pole-Zero Plot of a Stable Causal Biomedical Filter

The following block-level functional architecture illustrates how a rational system function $H(z)$ is structured for a typical ECG denoising low-pass filter.

```mermaid
graph TD
    InputSignal[Input Signal X z] --> GainK[Gain Constant K]
    GainK --> NumPoly[Numerator Polynomial N of z]
    NumPoly --> Zeros[Zeros z sub k on Z plane]
    NumPoly --> DivOp{Division Operation}
    DenPoly[Denominator Polynomial D of z] --> Poles[Poles p sub k on Z plane]
    DenPoly --> DivOp
    DivOp --> SysFunc[System Function H of z]
    Poles --> UnitCircle[Unit Circle vert z vert equals 1]
    Zeros --> UnitCircle
    SysFunc --> StabilityCheck{All poles inside unit circle?}
    StabilityCheck -->|Yes| Stable[Stable LTI System]
    StabilityCheck -->|No| Unstable[Unstable System]
    Stable --> OutputSignal[Output Signal Y of z]
    Unstable --> OutputSignal
```

### 4.2 Inverse Z-Transform Method Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start[Given X of z] --> Q1{Is X of z rational?}
    Q1 -->|No| LongDiv[Use Long Division Method]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Do you need closed form x of n?}
    Q2 -->|No| LongDiv
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Does X of z have complex poles?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| Residue[Use Residue Method]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Does X of z factor as product of known Z transforms?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| Convolve[Use Convolution Method]
    Q4 -->|No| PFE[Use Partial Fraction Expansion]
    LongDiv --> Result[Obtain x of n]
    Residue --> Result
    Convolve --> Result
    PFE --> Result
```

### 4.3 LTI System Analysis Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    Xin[X of z Input] --> Hblock[System Function H of z]
    Hblock --> Yout[Y of z Output]
    Hblock --> ImpulseResp[h of n Impulse Response]
    Hblock --> PoleZero[Pole Zero Plot]
    ImpulseResp --> Stability[Stability Check]
    PoleZero --> Stability
    Stability --> IVT[Initial Value Theorem]
    Stability --> FVT[Final Value Theorem]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The pole-zero plot is the **fingerprint** of an LTI system. Two systems can have the same magnitude response but different phase responses — only the pole-zero positions on the Z-plane (with their multiplicities) give a complete picture. In biomedical DSP, this is used to design filters with specific phase linearity (critical for preserving ECG waveform morphology).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] State and prove the Initial Value Theorem for Z-Transforms.**

**Model Answer:**
The Initial Value Theorem states that for a causal sequence $x[n]$ with Z-Transform $X(z)$, the initial sample $x[0]$ is given by:
$$x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$$

**Proof:** Starting from the one-sided Z-Transform:
$$X(z) = x[0] + x[1]z^{-1} + x[2]z^{-2} + \cdots$$
Taking the limit $z \to \infty$, all terms except $x[0]$ vanish, giving $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$. The theorem is valid only for causal sequences and requires that the limit exists (i.e., $M \leq N$ in the rational form).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] What is meant by poles and zeros of a Z-Transform? Explain their significance in determining system stability.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Zeros** are values of $z$ where $X(z) = 0$ (roots of the numerator).
- **Poles** are values of $z$ where $X(z) = \infty$ (roots of the denominator).

**Significance:** Poles determine the **stability** and **natural response** of an LTI system. For a stable causal system, all poles must lie **strictly inside the unit circle** ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$). If any pole lies on or outside the unit circle, the system becomes unstable and the output grows without bound. Zeros affect the **frequency response shape** but not stability.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Find the inverse Z-Transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.5z^{-1})(1 - 0.3z^{-1})}$ using the **Partial Fraction Expansion method**. Verify your answer using the **Initial Value Theorem**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the inverse Z-Transform of $X(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.8z^{-1} + 0.15z^{-2}}$ using the **long division method** to find the first 5 samples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Apply PFE:**
$$X(z) = \frac{A}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - 0.3z^{-1}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute residues (cover-up method):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \left. \frac{1}{1 - 0.3z^{-1}} \right\vert_{z^{-1} = 2} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.6} = \frac{1}{0.4} = 2.5 \\
B &= \left. \frac{1}{1 - 0.5z^{-1}} \right\vert_{z^{-1} = 3.333} = \frac{1}{1 - 1.667} = \frac{1}{-0.667} = -1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Z-Transform:**
$$x[n] = 2.5(0.5)^n u[n] - 1.5(0.3)^n u[n]$$

**Step 4 — IVT verification:** $x[0] = 2.5 - 1.5 = 1 = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ ✓

**Model Solution to (b):**

Performing long division of $1 \div (1 - 0.8z^{-1} + 0.15z^{-2})$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X(z) &= 1 + 0.8z^{-1} + 0.49z^{-2} + 0.242z^{-3} + 0.0936z^{-4} + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**First 5 samples:** $x[0] = 1, \; x[1] = 0.8, \; x[2] = 0.49, \; x[3] = 0.242, \; x[4] = 0.0936$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 — Alternative]**

**(a)** For the system function $H(z) = \dfrac{1 + 0.5z^{-1}}{1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2}}$, determine the **poles and zeros**, plot them on the Z-plane, and comment on **stability**. Find the impulse response $h[n]$ using the **residue method**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **convolution method**, find the inverse Z-Transform of:
$$X(z) = \frac{z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.2z^{-1})(1 - 0.4z^{-1})}$$ **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Zeros:** $1 + 0.5z^{-1} = 0 \Rightarrow z = -0.5$

**Step 2 — Poles:** $1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2} = 0 \Rightarrow 0.1z^{-2} - 0.7z^{-1} + 1 = 0$

Let $w = z^{-1}$: $0.1w^2 - 0.7w + 1 = 0 \Rightarrow w = \dfrac{0.7 \pm \sqrt{0.49 - 0.4}}{0.2} = \dfrac{0.7 \pm 0.3}{0.2}$
So $w = 5$ or $w = 2$, meaning $z = 0.2$ and $z = 0.5$.

**Step 3 — Stability check:** Both poles satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$, so the system is **stable**.

**Step 4 — Impulse response via residues:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h[n] &= \text{Res}_{z=0.5} \left[ \frac{(1 + 0.5z^{-1})z^{n-1}}{1 - 0.7z^{-1} + 0.1z^{-2}} \right] + \text{Res}_{z=0.2}[\cdots] \\
h[n] &= \sum_k \text{Res}\left[ H(z) z^{n-1}, z = p_k \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

Computing residues (after PFE on the proper form $H(z)$ in $z^{-1}$): $h[n] = [3(0.5)^n - 2(0.2)^n] u[n]$

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Factor** as $X(z) = z^{-1} \cdot \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.2z^{-1})(1 - 0.4z^{-1})}$

**Step 2 — Identify:** $x_1[n] = \delta[n-1]$ and $x_2[n]$ from PFE of the second factor.

**Step 3 — PFE for $X_2(z)$:** $A = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.4/0.2} \cdot (-1) = -2$ and $B = 3$

**Step 4 — Result:**
$$x[n] = \left[ -2(0.2)^{n-1} + 3(0.4)^{n-1} \right] u[n-1]$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Wrong ROC for PFE** — The Inverse Z-Transform result depends on the ROC. Forgetting the unit step $u[n]$ loses **1-2 marks**.
> 2. **Sign errors in PFE residues** — The cover-up method requires $z^{-1} = 1/p_k$, NOT $z = p_k$. Mixing these up is the #1 error in IVT and PFE questions.
> 3. **Not verifying with IVT** — Examiners explicitly check for IVT verification. A complete solution without this loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **Mismatched partial fractions** — When $M \geq N$, students forget to first perform polynomial division to extract the constant term, leading to an incorrect PFE. Always check: degree of numerator $<$ degree of denominator.
> 5. **Forgetting the unit circle** in pole-zero plots — Marking only poles/zeros without the unit circle reference costs **0.5 to 1 mark**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Initial Value Theorem:** $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ — applicable to **causal sequences** with $M \leq N$.
- **Final Value Theorem:** $x[\infty] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1)X(z)$ — applicable to **stable causal systems** (all poles inside unit circle, none at $z = 1$).
- **Rational Z-Transform:** Ratio of two polynomials; fully characterized by its **poles, zeros, and gain**.
- **Stability condition (causal LTI):** Every pole must satisfy $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ (strictly inside the unit circle).
- **Long Division** gives a power series; samples are read off as coefficients of $z^{-n}$. Best for the **first few samples**.
- **PFE** is the **most commonly tested** inverse method; remember the cover-up formula $R_k = \left.(1 - p_k z^{-1}) X(z)\right\vert_{z^{-1} = 1/p_k}$.
- **Residue Method** is mathematically rigorous and handles complex poles elegantly; formula: $x[n] = \sum_k \text{Res}[X(z) z^{n-1}, z = p_k]$.
- **Convolution Method** exploits the property $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{X_1(z) X_2(z)\} = x_1[n] * x_2[n]$ — extremely fast for factored forms.
- **Biomedical Application:** All four methods are used in **ECG filter design, EEG denoising, EMG rectification, and biomedical system identification**.
- **Quick check formula:** For a causal sequence, $\lim_{z \to \infty} X(z) = x[0]$ — always use this to validate your PFE or residue result.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Digital Filtering
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Concept of Digital Filtering

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

A **Digital Filter** is a discrete-time, linear time-invariant (LTI) computational system that processes a sampled input signal $x(n)$ by performing mathematical operations (multiplication, addition, and time-delay) on its numerical samples to produce an output signal $y(n)$ with selectively modified spectral content.

Mathematically, a digital filter is described by a **Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** of the general form:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k) \; - \; \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k)$$

where the set of coefficients $\{b_k, a_k\}$ completely characterizes the filter's behavior.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Digital filtering in biomedical engineering refers to the algorithmic removal of physiological and environmental artifacts (e.g., 50/60 Hz powerline interference, baseline wander, motion artifact) from biosignals like ECG, EEG, and EMG using numerical processors or microcontrollers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen water filter** connected to your tap:
- The **input water** (muddy, contaminated) is like our raw biosignal $x(n)$ — it contains the desired physiological information mixed with noise.
- The **filter cartridge** (activated charcoal layers) selectively traps impurities while letting clean water through — this is exactly what a digital filter does to specific frequency components.
- The **output water** (clean and drinkable) is the filtered biosignal $y(n)$ — the cleaned ECG or EEG waveform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as different filter cartridges trap different particle sizes, different digital filter designs (LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF) trap or pass different *frequency ranges* of the signal spectrum.

---

## 1.3 Why Digital Filtering? — The Biomedical Motivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Mandate (KTU 2024 — Module 4):** Students must understand that analog (hardware) filters suffer from temperature drift, component aging, and limited precision. **Digital filters** are immune to these issues and offer:
> - **Precision:** Determined by word-length (e.g., 16-bit ADC ⇒ $\pm 0.0015$% quantization precision).
> - **Flexibility:** Coefficients can be reprogrammed in software.
> - **Reproducibility:** Identical performance across units.
> - **Phase linearity:** Achievable in FIR designs (critical for preserving ECG morphology).
> - **Adaptive capability:** Coefficients can update in real-time for noise cancellation.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Digital Filters (At a Glance)

| Filter Family | Pole-Zero Structure | Computational Load | Stability | Phase Response |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **FIR** (Finite Impulse Response) | All-Zero | High ($M+1$ taps) | Always Stable | Strictly Linear Phase possible |
| **IIR** (Infinite Impulse Response) | Poles + Zeros | Low | Conditionally Stable | Non-Linear (distorts waveform) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Difference Equation — Engine of a Digital Filter

A causal LTI digital filter maps input samples $x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-M)$ and past outputs $y(n-1), \ldots, y(n-N)$ to the present output $y(n)$ via:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k) \; - \; \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k)$$

### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

- **Step 1 — Sample Ingestion:** At time index $n$, the processor reads the current input $x(n)$ and stores it in a tapped delay line along with $M$ previous samples.
- **Step 2 — Weighted Summation (Feed-Forward):** Each stored sample $x(n-k)$ is multiplied by a coefficient $b_k$. This is the **Moving Average (MA)** part.
- **Step 3 — Feedback Accumulation (Recursive):** Past outputs $y(n-k)$ are multiplied by coefficients $a_k$ and subtracted — this is the **Auto-Regressive (AR)** part.
- **Step 4 — Output Generation:** The new sample $y(n)$ is computed and pushed into the output register; the entire delay line shifts by one sample.
- **Step 5 — Iteration:** The process repeats for $n \rightarrow n+1$ at every sampling clock tick.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** If **all $a_k = 0$**, the filter has *no memory of its past output* → it is an **FIR** filter. If *any $a_k \neq 0$*, the output depends on past outputs → it is an **IIR** filter with feedback.

---

## 2.2 Transfer Function via Z-Transform

Applying the Z-transform to the difference equation (assuming zero initial conditions):

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}} = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)}$$

This is the **Transfer Function** — a rational polynomial in $z^{-1}$. Its roots determine the filter's behavior:
- **Zeros of $B(z)$:** Frequencies that are *blocked*.
- **Poles of $A(z)$:** Frequencies that are *emphasized*.

---

## 2.3 Frequency Response

Substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$ (i.e., evaluating on the unit circle) yields the **Frequency Response**:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, e^{-j\omega k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, e^{-j\omega k}}$$

This is a complex-valued function, decomposed into:

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \text{Magnitude Response (Gain in dB: } 20 \log_{10}\vert H \vert\text{)}$$

$$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \text{Phase Response}$$

$$\tau_g(\omega) = -\frac{d}{d\omega}\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = \text{Group Delay}$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Concept of Digital Filtering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Ready Reference Table:** Memorize these core equations — they appear in nearly every KTU 2024 ESE paper from this module.

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Variables / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Difference Equation | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y(n-k)$ | $b_k$ = feed-forward taps; $a_k$ = feedback taps |
| Transfer Function | $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ | $B(z) = \sum b_k z^{-k}$; $A(z) = 1 + \sum a_k z^{-k}$ |
| Frequency Response | $H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} h(k) e^{-j\omega k}$ | $\omega$ is normalized digital frequency, $\omega \in [-\pi, \pi]$ |
| Magnitude (dB) | $A(\omega) = 20 \log_{10}\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert$ | Negative dB = attenuation; 0 dB = unity gain |
| FIR Condition | $a_k = 0 \;\;\forall k$ | All-zero; always BIBO stable |
| IIR Condition | $\exists \; k \; \vert \; a_k \neq 0$ | Stable iff all poles lie inside unit circle ($\vert p_i \vert < 1$) |
| Linear Phase Condition | $h(n) = \pm h(M-n)$ | Symmetric or anti-symmetric impulse response |
| Group Delay (FIR) | $\tau_g = M/2$ | Constant for symmetric FIR |
| Convolution Sum | $y(n) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} h(k) x(n-k)$ | FIR uses truncated sum; IIR uses recursive equation |
| Sampling Relation | $f = \omega F_s / (2\pi)$ | $F_s$ = sampling frequency in Hz |
| Windowed FIR Design | $h_d(n) = h_{ideal}(n) \cdot w(n)$ | $w(n)$ = Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning, Blackman |
| 3 dB Cutoff | $\vert H(e^{j\omega_c})\vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ | Power falls to half of passband value |

---

## 2.5 Types of Digital Filters Based on Frequency Selectivity

| Filter Type | Symbol | Passes | Blocks | Biomedical Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | LPF | $0 \le \omega \le \omega_c$ | $\omega_c < \omega \le \pi$ | Removing high-frequency EMG noise from ECG |
| **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** | HPF | $\omega_c \le \omega \le \pi$ | $0 \le \omega < \omega_c$ | Removing baseline wander (respiration) from ECG |
| **Band-Pass Filter (BPF)** | BPF | $\omega_1 \le \omega \le \omega_2$ | Otherwise | Isolating EEG alpha band (8–13 Hz) |
| **Band-Stop Filter (BSF)** | BSF / Notch | All except $\omega_1 \le \omega \le \omega_2$ | $\omega_1 \le \omega \le \omega_2$ | Removing 50/60 Hz powerline interference |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Function from Difference Equation

**Goal:** Starting from the general LCCDE, derive the closed-form $H(z)$.

**Step 1:** Write the general difference equation.

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x(n-k) \; - \; \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y(n-k)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Time-Shift Property of the Z-Transform** to every term.

The Z-transform of a time-shifted sequence is:
$$Z\{x(n-k)\} = z^{-k} \, X(z)$$

Applying this to both sides of the equation:

$$Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k} \, X(z) \; - \; \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k} \, Y(z)$$

**Step 3:** Collect $Y(z)$ terms on the Left-Hand Side (LHS).

$$Y(z) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k} \, Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k} \, X(z)$$

$$Y(z) \left[1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}\right] = X(z) \left[\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}\right]$$

**Step 4:** Isolate the ratio $Y(z)/X(z)$ to obtain $H(z)$.

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The numerator $B(z) = \sum b_k z^{-k}$ defines the **zeros** (roots of $B(z) = 0$). The denominator $A(z) = 1 + \sum a_k z^{-k}$ defines the **poles** (roots of $A(z) = 0$). The pole-zero plot is the most powerful visualization of a digital filter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frequency Response from Transfer Function

**Goal:** Derive $H(e^{j\omega})$ as a function of physical frequency.

**Step 1:** Recall that the Z-transform evaluated on the **unit circle** gives the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)**.

The unit circle in the Z-plane is parametrized as:
$$z = e^{j\omega} \quad \text{where} \quad \omega = 2\pi f / F_s$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $z = e^{j\omega}$ into the transfer function.

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, e^{-j\omega k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, e^{-j\omega k}}$$

**Step 3:** Use Euler's formula to expand exponentials into cosine and sine components.

$$e^{-j\omega k} = \cos(\omega k) - j \sin(\omega k)$$

**Step 4:** Apply the **modulus operation** to extract the magnitude response.

$$\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert = \frac{\left\vert \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert}$$

**Step 5:** Express the magnitude in decibels for engineering use.

$$A(\omega) \; (\text{in dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\left[\vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert\right]$$

**Step 6:** Extract the phase response.

$$\phi(\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left[\frac{\text{Im}\{H(e^{j\omega})\}}{\text{Re}\{H(e^{j\omega})\}}\right]$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linear Phase Condition for FIR Filter

**Goal:** Prove that an FIR filter with symmetric impulse response has *constant* group delay.

**Step 1:** The frequency response of an FIR filter ($a_k = 0$) of length $M+1$ is:

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{M} h(n) \, e^{-j\omega n}$$

**Step 2:** Impose the **symmetry constraint** $h(n) = h(M-n)$.

**Step 3:** Substitute and factor out the linear-phase term $e^{-j\omega M/2}$.

$$H(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega M/2} \sum_{n=0}^{M} h(n) \, e^{-j\omega(n - M/2)}$$

**Step 4:** Observe that the remaining sum is purely real for symmetric $h(n)$, so it contributes only magnitude, not additional phase.

**Step 5:** Therefore, the total phase is:

$$\angle H(e^{j\omega}) = -\frac{\omega M}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Compute the group delay.

$$\tau_g = -\frac{d}{d\omega}\left(-\frac{\omega M}{2}\right) = \frac{M}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Implication:** Constant group delay means *all* frequency components are delayed by exactly the same time $M/2$ samples. This preserves the **waveform shape** of the QRS complex in ECG — a property analog filters cannot match.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Notch Filter to Remove 50/60 Hz Powerline Interference

**Goal:** Design a second-order IIR notch filter.

**Step 1:** Specify notch (rejection) frequency: $\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 / F_s$, where $f_0 = 50$ Hz (India) and $F_s = 500$ Hz.

$$\omega_0 = 2\pi \times 50 / 500 = 0.2\pi \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2:** Write the transfer function with zeros on the unit circle at $\pm \omega_0$ and poles inside the unit circle at radius $r$ (for bandwidth control).

$$H(z) = \frac{(1 - 2\cos\omega_0 \, z^{-1} + z^{-2})}{(1 - 2r\cos\omega_0 \, z^{-1} + r^2 z^{-2})}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\omega_0 = 0.2\pi$ and choose $r = 0.95$ (sharp but stable notch).

$$\cos(0.2\pi) \approx 0.8090$$

$$H(z) = \frac{1 - 1.618 z^{-1} + z^{-2}}{1 - 1.538 z^{-1} + 0.9025 z^{-2}}$$

**Step 4:** Stability check: $\vert \text{poles} \vert = \sqrt{0.9025} = 0.95 < 1$ ✓. The system is **BIBO stable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** The 50 Hz notch is one of the most common exam problems in biomedical signal processing. Always verify the stability condition $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Digital Filtering System (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    biosigA[BIOELECTRIC SOURCE ECG EEG EMG] --> adcin[ANTI ALIASING LPF]
    adcin --> adcsys[ADC SAMPLER Fs Hz]
    adcsys --> proc[DIGITAL FILTER H OF Z]
    proc --> dacsys[DAC RECONSTRUCTOR]
    dacsys --> outa[FILTERED OUTPUT DISPLAY OR STORAGE]

    style biosigA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style adcin fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style adcsys fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style proc fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style dacsys fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style outa fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — FIR Filter Direct-Form Structure (Tapped Delay Line)

```mermaid

    xn[INPUT X OF N] --> d0[DELAY Z MINUS 1]

    xn --> m0[MULT BY B0]
    d0 --> m1[MULT BY B1]
    d1 --> m2[MULT BY B2]
    d2 --> m3[MULT BY B3]
    d3 --> m4[MULT BY B4]

    m0 --> sumx[ADDER SUMMER]
    m1 --> sumx
    m2 --> sumx
    m3 --> sumx
    m4 --> sumx

    sumx --> yn[OUTPUT Y OF N]

    style xn fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000

    style d2 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000
    style d3 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000

    style m1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style m2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style m3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style m4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000

    style yn fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — IIR Filter Direct-Form-I Structure (With Feedback)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xinp[INPUT X OF N] --> fb1[FEED FORWARD B0 B1 B2]
    fb1 --> adder1[ADDER]

    adder1 --> y1[OUTPUT Y OF N]
    y1 --> fbb[FEEDBACK A1 A2 LOOP]
    fbb --> adder1

    style xinp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style fb1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style adder1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
```
