
## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Filter Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root[DIGITAL FILTERS] --> fir[FIR FINITE IMPULSE RESPONSE]
    root --> iir[IIR INFINITE IMPULSE RESPONSE]

    fir --> win1[WINDOW METHOD RECTANGULAR HAMMING HANNING BLACKMAN]
    fir --> freq1[FREQUENCY SAMPLING METHOD]

    iir --> analog[ANALOG PROTOTYPE BUTTERWORTH CHEBYSHEV ELLIPTIC]
    analog --> method1[BILINEAR TRANSFORMATION]
    analog --> method2[IMPULSE INVARIANCE METHOD]

    style root fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style fir fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style iir fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style win1 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000
    style freq1 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000
    style analog fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style method1 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000
    style method2 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]`

**Define a digital filter. With a neat block diagram, explain the basic digital filtering process of a biomedical signal.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A digital filter is a discrete-time linear time-invariant (LTI) system that performs numerical operations on sampled signal values to selectively pass or block specific frequency components.

**Block Diagram of the Process:**

$$\text{Biosignal} \rightarrow \text{Anti-Aliasing Filter} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{Digital Processor} \rightarrow \text{DAC} \rightarrow \text{Output}$$

**Explanation (for 3 marks):** The raw biomedical signal (e.g., ECG) is first low-pass filtered to prevent aliasing, then sampled and quantized by an ADC. The resulting digital sequence $x(n)$ enters the digital filter implementing $H(z)$, which produces a cleaned output $y(n)$. This output is reconstructed by a DAC for display. *(Marks distribution: 1 for definition, 1 for diagram, 1 for explanation.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]`

**Differentiate between FIR and IIR digital filters. State any three advantages of FIR filters in biomedical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | FIR Filter | IIR Filter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Impulse Response | Finite, settles to zero in $M+1$ samples | Infinite, theoretically never settles |
| Feedback | Absent ($a_k = 0$) | Present ($a_k \neq 0$) |
| Stability | Always stable | Conditionally stable |
| Phase | Linear (with symmetric $h(n)$) | Non-linear |
| Order for sharp cutoff | High | Low |

**Three advantages of FIR in biomedical applications:** (1) Guaranteed stability — essential for patient safety, (2) Linear phase preserves ECG waveform morphology without distortion, (3) No limit cycles or overflow oscillations. *(1 mark for table, 2 marks for advantages.)*

---

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]` (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Derive the transfer function of a general IIR digital filter from its difference equation. Explain the significance of poles and zeros. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Start with the general LCCDE.

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y(n-k)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Z-transform using the time-shift property.

$$Y(z) + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k} Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k} X(z)$$

**Step 3:** Factor and solve for $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$.

$$H(z) = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$$

**Step 4:** Express in factored (pole-zero) form.

$$H(z) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{M}(1 - z_i z^{-1})}{\prod_{j=1}^{N}(1 - p_j z^{-1})}$$

**Step 5 — Significance of Zeros** (1.5 marks): Zeros $z_i$ are frequencies *attenuated* by the filter. A zero on the unit circle at $\omega_0$ creates a notch at that frequency — used in 50 Hz powerline removal.

**Step 6 — Significance of Poles** (1.5 marks): Poles $p_j$ near the unit circle at angle $\omega_0$ create a *resonance* (peak gain) at that frequency — used in BPF design for EEG band extraction. Stability requires $\vert p_j \vert < 1$ for all poles.

---

#### Part (b) — With neat frequency response sketches, explain the four basic types of digital filters (LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF) and give one biomedical application of each. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Type 1 — Low-Pass Filter (LPF):**
- Passes $0 \le \omega \le \omega_c$, blocks $\omega_c < \omega \le \pi$.
- **Biomedical Application:** Removing high-frequency EMG artifact contamination from ECG signals (cutoff ~ 35 Hz).

**Type 2 — High-Pass Filter (HPF):**
- Blocks $0 \le \omega < \omega_c$, passes $\omega_c \le \omega \le \pi$.
- **Biomedical Application:** Removing baseline wander caused by patient respiration in ECG (cutoff ~ 0.5 Hz).

**Type 3 — Band-Pass Filter (BPF):**
- Passes $\omega_1 \le \omega \le \omega_2$, blocks others.
- **Biomedical Application:** Extracting the EEG alpha rhythm (8–13 Hz) for sleep-stage monitoring.

**Type 4 — Band-Stop (Notch) Filter (BSF):**
- Blocks $\omega_1 \le \omega \le \omega_2$, passes others.
- **Biomedical Application:** Eliminating 50 Hz (India) or 60 Hz (USA) powerline interference in ECG, EEG, and EMG recordings.

*(Marks: 1.5 marks per filter type — 0.5 for sketch description, 0.5 for formula, 0.5 for application.)*

---

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]` (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Explain the window method of designing an FIR filter. Compare the Rectangular, Hamming, and Blackman windows in terms of main-lobe width and side-lobe attenuation. (7 Marks)

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept of Windowing (2 marks):**
An ideal filter $H_d(e^{j\omega})$ has an infinite impulse response $h_d(n)$ that is non-causal and non-realizable. Multiplying $h_d(n)$ by a finite-length window $w(n)$ yields a causal, realizable FIR filter:

$$h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n), \quad 0 \le n \le M$$

**Step 2 — Rectangular Window (1 mark):**
$$w(n) = 1, \quad 0 \le n \le M$$
- **Main-lobe width:** $4\pi / (M+1)$ (narrowest).
- **Side-lobe level:** $-13$ dB (highest, most leakage).

**Step 3 — Hamming Window (1 mark):**
$$w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46 \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{M}\right)$$
- **Main-lobe width:** $8\pi / M$ (wider).
- **Side-lobe level:** $-41$ dB (good attenuation).

**Step 4 — Blackman Window (1 mark):**
$$w(n) = 0.42 - 0.5 \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{M}\right) + 0.08 \cos\!\left(\frac{4\pi n}{M}\right)$$
- **Main-lobe width:** $12\pi / M$ (widest).
- **Side-lobe level:** $-58$ dB (excellent attenuation).

**Step 5 — Trade-off Conclusion (2 marks):**
- Narrow main-lobe → sharp transition (Rectangular).
- Low side-lobe → low ripple (Blackman).
- **Hamming is the engineering compromise** — used in most biomedical FIR designs.

---

#### Part (b) — Describe the block diagram of an adaptive digital filter for noise cancellation in biomedical signals. Mention the role of the LMS algorithm. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
An adaptive noise canceller (ANC) has **two inputs**:
- **Primary input** $d(n)$: the desired biosignal corrupted by noise (e.g., ECG + 50 Hz interference).
- **Reference input** $x(n)$: a noise reference correlated with the corrupting noise (e.g., a 50 Hz sine picked up by an auxiliary electrode).

The reference $x(n)$ is filtered by an adaptive FIR filter $W(z)$ whose coefficients $w_k(n)$ are updated by the LMS algorithm. The filter output $y(n)$ is subtracted from $d(n)$ to produce the error $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$, which is the cleaned signal and also drives the LMS update.

**LMS Algorithm (4 marks):**
The Least Mean Squares algorithm updates each coefficient as:

$$w_k(n+1) = w_k(n) + 2\mu \, e(n) \, x(n-k)$$

where:
- $\mu$ = step-size (small positive constant controlling convergence speed vs. stability).
- $e(n)$ = error signal.
- $x(n-k)$ = reference input sample at delay $k$.

**Role:** LMS minimizes the mean-square error $E[e^2(n)]$ iteratively without requiring prior knowledge of signal statistics, making it ideal for non-stationary biomedical noise (e.g., motion artifacts in ECG Holter recordings).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting zero initial conditions** when applying Z-transform → loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $\omega$ (digital frequency) with $f$ (analog frequency)** — KTU 2024 specifically tests the relation $f = \omega F_s / (2\pi)$.
> 3. **Omitting stability check** for IIR filters ($\vert p_i \vert < 1$).
> 4. **Mixing up FIR and IIR formulas** — using $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ for FIR where $A(z) = 1$.
> 5. **Not specifying units** of cutoff frequency (Hz vs. rad/sample) in the answer.
> 6. **Missing pole-zero plot** in transfer function derivations — examiners expect at least a verbal mention of pole/zero locations.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List — Concept of Digital Filtering**

- **Digital Filter Definition:** A discrete-time LTI system described by an LCCDE; implemented on numerical hardware.
- **Core Equation:** $y(n) = \sum b_k x(n-k) - \sum a_k y(n-k)$.
- **Transfer Function:** $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ — a rational polynomial in $z^{-1}$.
- **Frequency Response:** Obtained by substituting $z = e^{j\omega}$ into $H(z)$.
- **FIR Filter:** $a_k = 0$ for all $k$ → always stable → linear phase possible.
- **IIR Filter:** At least one $a_k \neq 0$ → recursive → conditionally stable.
- **Stability Criterion (IIR):** All poles must satisfy $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ (inside the unit circle).
- **Linear Phase Condition:** $h(n) = \pm h(M-n)$ → group delay $= M/2$ (constant).
- **Filter Types:** LPF (low-pass), HPF (high-pass), BPF (band-pass), BSF (band-stop/notch).
- **Window Method:** Multiply ideal $h_d(n)$ by $w(n)$ → Rectangular, Hamming, Hanning, Blackman.
- **Notch Filter Application:** Removing 50/60 Hz powerline interference from ECG/EEG.
- **Adaptive Filter:** Coefficients update in real-time; LMS algorithm: $w_k(n+1) = w_k(n) + 2\mu \, e(n) x(n-k)$.
- **Biomedical Applications:** ECG denoising, EEG band extraction, EMG artifact removal, heart-rate variability analysis.
- **Convolution Output:** $y(n) = \sum h(k) x(n-k)$ — fundamental for FIR filter implementation.
- **Sampling Relation:** $\omega = 2\pi f / F_s$ — link between analog frequency and digital frequency.
- **dB Conversion:** $A(\omega) = 20 \log_{10}\vert H(e^{j\omega})\vert$ — used in Bode plots.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Digital Filters; Comparison with Analog Filters; FIR Filter Design Using Fourier Series - Use of Window Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Digital Filters; Comparison with Analog Filters; FIR Filter Design Using Fourier Series - Use of Window Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Digital Filter?

A **Digital Filter** is a discrete-time linear time-invariant (LTI) system that processes a discrete input signal $x(n)$ to produce a discrete output signal $y(n)$ by performing mathematical operations (multiplication, addition, and delay) on numerical samples. Mathematically, the difference equation governing a digital filter is:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y(n-k)$$

where $b_k$ and $a_k$ are the **filter coefficients**, and the presence (or absence) of feedback terms $a_k y(n-k)$ determines whether the filter is recursive or non-recursive.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of biomedical signal processing (ECG, EEG, EMG), digital filters are the workhorses for removing **50/60 Hz powerline interference**, **baseline wander**, and **high-frequency noise** — all without altering the diagnostic content of the signal.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Sieve and the Sieve-Master"

Imagine a chef sieving flour. The **mesh size** of the sieve decides what passes through (low-frequency signal content) and what is blocked (high-frequency noise). A digital filter is exactly that — a programmable, mathematically-defined "mesh" operating on digital numbers instead of flour:

- **Low-Pass Filter (LPF):** Lets slow-changing (low-frequency) signals pass — like a sieve that retains only fine flour.
- **High-Pass Filter (HPF):** Lets fast-changing (high-frequency) signals pass — like a coarse filter that retains only large particles.
- **Band-Pass Filter (BPF):** Lets a specific band of frequencies pass — like a slotted spoon that retains only medium-sized chunks.
- **Band-Stop / Notch Filter:** Blocks a specific band — like a sieve with one specific hole patched (e.g., the **50 Hz notch** used to remove powerline hum from ECG).

## 1.2 Types of Digital Filters

Digital filters are broadly classified into two main families based on the structure of their difference equation:

### 1.2.1 Finite Impulse Response (FIR) Filter

An FIR filter has an impulse response $h(n)$ that is of **finite duration**. The output depends *only* on current and past input samples — **no feedback** is present.

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} b_k \, x(n-k)$$

- **Structure:** Non-recursive (all $a_k = 0$).
- **Order:** $N-1$, where $N$ is the filter length.
- **Stability:** Always BIBO stable (because $h(n)$ is absolutely summable by definition).
- **Phase:** Can be designed to have **exactly linear phase**.

### 1.2.2 Infinite Impulse Response (IIR) Filter

An IIR filter has an impulse response $h(n)$ that extends to **infinite duration** because of feedback (recursive) terms. The output depends on past inputs **and** past outputs.

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x(n-k) - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y(n-k)$$

- **Structure:** Recursive ($a_k \ne 0$ for some $k$).
- **Order:** $\max(M, N)$.
- **Stability:** Must be checked via pole locations (must lie inside the unit circle of the z-plane).
- **Efficiency:** Achieves sharper magnitude response with **fewer coefficients** than FIR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "list the types of digital filters," examiners expect you to present the **Recursive (IIR) vs. Non-Recursive (FIR)** classification *first*, then the **frequency-domain classification** (LPF, HPF, BPF, BSF), and finally the **linear-phase vs. non-linear-phase** classification. Covering all three axes fetches full marks.

## 1.3 Analog vs. Digital Filters — The Head-to-Head Comparison

| Parameter | Analog Filter | Digital Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Signal type | Continuous-time | Discrete-time |
| Components | R, L, C, Op-amps | Multipliers, Adders, Delays |
| Implementation | Hardware (circuit) | Software / DSP chip |
| Frequency range | Limited by component tolerances | Limited by sampling rate |
| Accuracy | Component drift, aging | Quantized but reproducible |
| Phase linearity | Poor for most types | Easily linear (FIR) |
| Adaptability | Difficult to change | Coefficients easily updated |
| Cost for complex responses | High | Low |
| Speed | Real-time, very fast | Limited by ADC / clock |
| Noise immunity | Lower (thermal, shot) | Higher (rejected by logic levels) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Biomedical Context:** A classic ECG amplifier chain uses a **passive RC analog high-pass filter** (to remove DC offset), then a **digital FIR band-pass filter** (to isolate the 0.5–40 Hz QRS complex band) inside the microprocessor. The hybrid architecture exploits the strengths of *both* worlds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Digital Filter Design Philosophy — A Two-Stage Process

Designing a digital filter involves two distinct stages:

**Stage 1 — Specification:** Define the desired frequency response (passband edge $\omega_p$, stopband edge $\omega_s$, passband ripple $A_p$, stopband attenuation $A_s$, sampling frequency $F_s$).

**Stage 2 — Approximation:** Find the filter coefficients $h(n)$ (for FIR) or $\{b_k, a_k\}$ (for IIR) that best approximate the specification. This is the "engineering design" step where Fourier series + windowing or bilinear transform methods are applied.

## 2.2 Why FIR for Biomedical Signals?

- FIR filters have **zero phase distortion** if designed with symmetric coefficients — this is critical for preserving the **timing morphology** of ECG/EEG waveforms (e.g., the QRS onset must not shift in time).
- FIR filters are inherently stable — vital in medical devices where malfunction could mean misdiagnosis.
- FIR design methods (windowing, frequency sampling, Parks-McClellan) are **mathematically closed-form** and predictable.

## 2.3 FIR Filter Design Using Fourier Series — Theoretical Framework

The standard window-method design of an FIR filter proceeds in three logical steps:

### Step 1 — Define the Ideal (Desired) Frequency Response

The ideal frequency response $H_d(e^{j\omega})$ is *piecewise constant* with sharp transitions. For example, an ideal low-pass filter with cutoff $\omega_c$ is:

$$H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} 1 \cdot e^{-j\alpha\omega}, & -\omega_c \le \omega \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The term $e^{-j\alpha\omega}$ represents a **linear-phase delay** of $\alpha$ samples, where $\alpha = (N-1)/2$.

### Step 2 — Compute the Ideal Impulse Response by Inverse DTFT

The ideal impulse response is found by applying the **Inverse Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (IDTFT)** to $H_d(e^{j\omega})$:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{-j\alpha\omega} \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega$$

Evaluating the integral gives the closed-form sinc function:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{\sin[\omega_c(n - \alpha)]}{\pi (n - \alpha)} = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} \cdot \text{sinc}\left[\frac{\omega_c(n - \alpha)}{\pi}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The function $h_d(n)$ is **infinite in length** and **non-causal** (it extends from $n = -\infty$ to $+\infty$). A practical FIR filter must be *finite* and *causal*, so we truncate and shift $h_d(n)$ — this is where **windowing** enters.

### Step 3 — Truncate Using a Window Function

Multiply the ideal impulse response $h_d(n)$ by a finite-length window $w(n)$ of length $N$:

$$h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n), \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N-1$$

The window $w(n)$ is non-zero only for $0 \le n \le N-1$, which forces $h(n)$ to be finite and causal. Common window choices are given below.

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — these formulas appear in nearly every Part-B 14-mark FIR design problem.

| Concept | Formula | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal LPF impulse response | $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\sin[\omega_c(n - \alpha)]}{\omega_c(n - \alpha)}$ | $\omega_c$: rad/sample, $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ |
| Ideal HPF impulse response | $h_d(n) = \delta(n-\alpha) - \dfrac{\omega_c}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{\sin[\omega_c(n - \alpha)]}{\omega_c(n - \alpha)}$ | High-pass by spectral subtraction |
| Ideal BPF impulse response | $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\omega_2}{\pi}\text{sinc}[\omega_2(n-\alpha)/\pi] - \dfrac{\omega_1}{\pi}\text{sinc}[\omega_1(n-\alpha)/\pi]$ | $\omega_1, \omega_2$: lower, upper cutoffs |
| Windowed FIR coefficient | $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$ | $0 \le n \le N-1$ |
| Rectangular window | $w(n) = 1, \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$ | Simplest, sharpest transition, worst stopband |
| Hanning window | $w(n) = 0.5 - 0.5\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | Main-lobe width = $8\pi / N$ |
| Hamming window | $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | Main-lobe width = $8\pi / N$, **A = 53 dB** |
| Blackman window | $w(n) = 0.42 - 0.5\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right) + 0.08\cos\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ | Main-lobe width = $12\pi/N$, **A = 74 dB** |
| Bartlett (triangular) | $w(n) = 1 - \dfrac{\vert n - \alpha \vert}{\alpha}$ | Endpoints touch zero |
| Kaiser window (general) | $w(n) = \dfrac{I_0\!\left[\beta\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{2n}{N-1}-1\right)^2}\right]}{I_0[\beta]}$, $0 \le n \le N-1$ | $\beta$: shape parameter, $I_0$: Bessel function |
| Main-lobe width (rectangular) | $\Delta\omega_{ML} = 4\pi/N$ | Fundamental trade-off |
| Stopband attenuation | $A = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\text{peak side-lobe}}\right)$ dB | Higher A ⇒ smoother filter, wider transition |
| Transition width | $\Delta\omega = \omega_s - \omega_p$ | Inversely proportional to N |
| Normalized cutoff | $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c / F_s$ | $f_c$ in Hz, $F_s$ in Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off Cardinal Rule:** Increasing $N$ → narrower main lobe → sharper transition but more computation. Choosing a smoother window (Hamming, Blackman) → better stopband attenuation but wider transition. You **cannot** improve one without worsening the other — this is the **uncertainty principle of discrete signals**.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

- **ECG Filtering:** A 35-tap Hamming-windowed FIR LPF with $f_c = 40$ Hz is standard for suppressing EMG high-frequency noise in ambulatory Holter monitors.
- **EEG Filtering:** A Kaiser-windowed FIR BPF (0.5–30 Hz) extracts clinically meaningful brain rhythms (delta, theta, alpha, beta) from scalp recordings.
- **Hearing Aids:** FIR comb-notch filters cancel acoustic feedback in digital hearing aids.
- **MRI Reconstruction:** FIR filters are used as 1D apodization windows in k-space processing to control Gibbs ringing in reconstructed images.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Ideal Low-Pass FIR Filter Impulse Response

**Starting Point — The Ideal Frequency Response:**

$$H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} 1 \cdot e^{-j\omega\alpha}, & \vert\omega\vert \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \omega_c < \vert\omega\vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

We multiply by $e^{-j\omega\alpha}$ so that the resulting impulse response is **symmetric about $n = \alpha$** — this guarantees **linear phase** in the final FIR filter.

**Step 1 — Apply the Inverse DTFT:**

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi}^{\pi} H_d(e^{j\omega}) \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega$$

Since $H_d(e^{j\omega}) = 0$ outside $[-\omega_c, \omega_c]$, we can narrow the limits:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{-j\omega\alpha} \, e^{j\omega n} \, d\omega = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{j\omega(n-\alpha)} \, d\omega$$

**Step 2 — Combine Exponentials:**

We use the rule: $\int e^{au}\, du = \dfrac{e^{au}}{a}$. With $a = j(n-\alpha)$ and limits $\pm \omega_c$:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ \frac{e^{j\omega(n-\alpha)}}{j(n-\alpha)} \right]_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} = \frac{1}{2\pi \, j(n-\alpha)} \left( e^{j\omega_c(n-\alpha)} - e^{-j\omega_c(n-\alpha)} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Recognize Euler's Identity:**

Recall the identity $\sin(\theta) = \dfrac{e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta}}{2j}$. Applying this with $\theta = \omega_c(n-\alpha)$:

$$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi \, j(n-\alpha)} \cdot 2j \sin[\omega_c(n-\alpha)] = \frac{\sin[\omega_c(n-\alpha)]}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$$

**Step 4 — Final Result (Standard Sinc Form):**

$$\boxed{\,h_d(n) = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} \cdot \frac{\sin[\omega_c(n-\alpha)]}{\omega_c(n-\alpha)} = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi}\,\text{sinc}\!\left[\frac{\omega_c(n-\alpha)}{\pi}\right]\,}$$

This result holds for **all integers $n$** including $n = \alpha$, where by L'Hôpital's rule the value is $\omega_c / \pi$.

## 3.2 Derivation: High-Pass Filter from Low-Pass (Spectral Subtraction)

The high-pass filter is obtained from the LPF using the **complement property**:

$$H_{d,HPF}(e^{j\omega}) = e^{-j\omega\alpha} - H_{d,LPF}(e^{j\omega})$$

A delay line $e^{-j\omega\alpha}$ is the DTFT of $\delta(n - \alpha)$, so:

$$h_{d,HPF}(n) = \delta(n-\alpha) - h_{d,LPF}(n)$$

Substituting the LPF result:

$$\boxed{\,h_d(n) = \delta(n-\alpha) - \frac{\sin[\omega_c(n-\alpha)]}{\pi(n-\alpha)}\,}$$

**Verification:** At $n = \alpha$: $h_d(\alpha) = 1 - \omega_c/\pi$. This is the DC-blocked value, correct for a HPF (which must be zero at DC).

## 3.3 Derivation: Band-Pass Filter from Sum of Two LPFs

A BPF with passband $[\omega_{c1}, \omega_{c2}]$ is constructed as the difference of two LPFs:

$$H_{d,BPF}(e^{j\omega}) = H_{d,LPF,\omega_{c2}}(e^{j\omega}) - H_{d,LPF,\omega_{c1}}(e^{j\omega})$$

In the time domain:

$$h_{d,BPF}(n) = \frac{\sin[\omega_{c2}(n-\alpha)]}{\pi(n-\alpha)} - \frac{\sin[\omega_{c1}(n-\alpha)]}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board problems, you will often be given $N$, the sampling frequency $F_s$, and a cutoff in Hz $f_c$. The conversion $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c / F_s$ **must** be performed before substituting into the sinc formula. Failing to do so is the #1 source of zero marks.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Window Function Selection

This decision matrix tells you **which window to choose** for a given KTU problem:

| Requirement | Best Window | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum computation, sharpest transition | **Rectangular** | Narrowest main lobe ($4\pi/N$), but only **−21 dB** side lobes |
| Smooth response, moderate attenuation | **Hanning** | All side lobes tapered, ~**−44 dB** peak side lobe |
| ECG / EEG biomedical filtering (most common) | **Hamming** | First side lobe at **−53 dB** — optimal for medical signals |
| High attenuation, can afford wide transition | **Blackman** | **−74 dB** peak side lobe, ideal for EMG |
| Arbitrary attenuation, flexible | **Kaiser** | Adjustable $\beta$ parameter for any A |
| Zero endpoints (for overlap-add) | **Hanning / Blackman** | Endpoints touch zero, enabling perfect reconstruction |
| Power-of-2 FFT optimization | **Hamming / Blackman** | Coeffs often pre-tabulated in DSP libraries |

## 3.5 Comprehensive Worked Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem:** Design a low-pass FIR filter using the Fourier series (window) method with the following specifications:
- Sampling frequency $F_s = 8$ kHz
- Cutoff frequency $f_c = 1$ kHz
- Filter length $N = 7$ (use Hamming window)
- Type: Symmetric linear-phase (Type I)

**Step 1 — Convert to normalized digital frequency:**

$$\omega_c = 2\pi \cdot \frac{f_c}{F_s} = 2\pi \cdot \frac{1000}{8000} = \frac{\pi}{4} \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the delay parameter:**

$$\alpha = \frac{N-1}{2} = \frac{7-1}{2} = 3$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ideal impulse response** $h_d(n)$ for $n = 0, 1, \ldots, 6$:

Using $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin[\omega_c(n - \alpha)]}{\pi(n - \alpha)}$:

| $n$ | $n - \alpha$ | $\omega_c(n-\alpha)$ | $\sin[\cdot]$ | $h_d(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | -3 | $-3\pi/4$ | $-0.7071$ | $0.0750$ |
| 1 | -2 | $-\pi/2$ | $-1.0000$ | $0.1592$ |
| 2 | -1 | $-\pi/4$ | $-0.7071$ | $0.2251$ |
| 3 | 0 | $0$ | $0$ | $\omega_c/\pi = 0.2500$ |
| 4 | 1 | $\pi/4$ | $0.7071$ | $0.2251$ |
| 5 | 2 | $\pi/2$ | $1.0000$ | $0.1592$ |
| 6 | 3 | $3\pi/4$ | $0.7071$ | $0.0750$ |

**Step 4 — Compute the Hamming window values** $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\left(\dfrac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$ with $N-1 = 6$:

| $n$ | $2\pi n / 6$ | $\cos(\cdot)$ | $w(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $0$ | $1.0000$ | $0.0800$ |
| 1 | $\pi/3$ | $0.5000$ | $0.3100$ |
| 2 | $2\pi/3$ | $-0.5000$ | $0.7700$ |
| 3 | $\pi$ | $-1.0000$ | $1.0000$ |
| 4 | $4\pi/3$ | $-0.5000$ | $0.7700$ |
| 5 | $5\pi/3$ | $0.5000$ | $0.3100$ |
| 6 | $2\pi$ | $1.0000$ | $0.0800$ |

**Step 5 — Multiply to obtain the FIR coefficients** $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$:

| $n$ | $h_d(n)$ | $w(n)$ | $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $0.0750$ | $0.0800$ | $0.0060$ |
| 1 | $0.1592$ | $0.3100$ | $0.0494$ |
| 2 | $0.2251$ | $0.7700$ | $0.1733$ |
| 3 | $0.2500$ | $1.0000$ | $0.2500$ |
| 4 | $0.2251$ | $0.7700$ | $0.1733$ |
| 5 | $0.1592$ | $0.3100$ | $0.0494$ |
| 6 | $0.0750$ | $0.0800$ | $0.0060$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetry Check:** $h(0) = h(6) = 0.0060$, $h(1) = h(5) = 0.0494$, $h(2) = h(4) = 0.1733$. The coefficients are **symmetric about $n = 3$** — confirming **Type I linear phase** behavior. The system is causal, stable, and has constant group delay of **3 samples** in the passband.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — FIR Filter Design Pipeline (Fourier Series Method)

The complete design flow is shown in the Mermaid block diagram below. Each step is a **functional processing block**, and the arrows show how data flows from the ideal mathematical specification to the final hardware/software FIR coefficients.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SPECIFICATION: Passband Edge, Stopband Edge, Ripple, Fs"] --> B["DEFINE IDEAL Hd OF EJ OMEGA"]
    B --> C["STEP 1: IDTFT TO FIND hd OF N"]
    C --> D["STEP 2: APPLY WINDOW w OF N"]
    D --> E["STEP 3: COMPUTE hn = hdn TIMES wn"]
    E --> F["VALIDATE: SYMMETRY CHECK AND STABILITY"]
    F --> G["VALIDATE: LINEAR PHASE AND GROUP DELAY"]
    G --> H["VALIDATE: FREQUENCY RESPONSE PLOT"]
    H --> I["FINAL FIR COEFFICIENTS hn FOR 0 TO NMINUS1"]
    I --> J["IMPLEMENT IN DSP OR FPGA HARDWARE"]
    
    subgraph K["WINDOW FUNCTION LIBRARY"]
        K1["RECTANGULAR WINDOW"]
        K2["HANN WINDOW"]
        K3["HAMMING WINDOW"]
        K4["BLACKMAN WINDOW"]
        K5["KAISER WINDOW"]
    end
    
    K --> D
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Comparison of Analog and Digital Filter Realization

The two filter paradigms are shown as functional blocks. The analog filter is built from passive components; the digital filter is built from computational primitives.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["ANALOG FILTER REALIZATION"]
        A1["INPUT CONTINUOUS SIGNAL x OF T"] --> A2["RC OR RLC NETWORK"]
        A2 --> A3["OPAmp BUFFER"]
        A3 --> A4["OUTPUT CONTINUOUS y OF T"]
    end
    
    subgraph B["DIGITAL FILTER REALIZATION"]
        B1["INPUT ADC SAMPLED x OF N"] --> B2["DELAY LINE Z MINUS 1"]
        B2 --> B3["MULTIPLY ACCUMULATE UNIT"]
        B3 --> B4["OUTPUT DISCRETE y OF N"]
        B4 --> B5["DAC FOR ANALOG RECONSTRUCTION"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Frequency Domain Trade-off Plot — Window Choice vs. Side-Lobe Level

The diagram below illustrates the **uncertainty trade-off** between main-lobe width (transition sharpness) and side-lobe amplitude (stopband attenuation). A wider main lobe gives lower side lobes and vice versa.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P["WINDOW SELECTION TRADE OFF"] --> Q1["RECTANGULAR: NARROW MAIN LOBE, HIGH SIDELOBES, MINUS 21 dB"]
    P --> Q2["HAMMING: WIDE MAIN LOBE, LOW SIDELOBES, MINUS 53 dB"]
    P --> Q3["BLACKMAN: WIDEST MAIN LOBE, MINUS 74 dB SIDELOBES"]
    
    R["TRANSITION BAND WIDTH"] --> S1["NARROW: USE RECTANGULAR OR HAMMING"]
    R --> S2["WIDE: USE BLACKMAN OR KAISER"]
```

## 4.4 Direct-Form FIR Filter Realization (Hardware Block Diagram)

The actual hardware/software implementation of the FIR filter using multipliers, adders, and delay elements is shown in the block diagram below. This is the structure used inside a DSP processor.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    XN["x OF n"] --> D0["DELAY z MINUS 1"]
    D0 --> D1["DELAY z MINUS 1"]
    D1 --> D2["DELAY z MINUS 1"]
    D2 --> D3["DELAY z MINUS 1"]
    D3 --> D4["DELAY z MINUS 1"]
    
    D0 --> M0["MULTIPLY BY h OF 1"]
    D1 --> M1["MULTIPLY BY h OF 2"]
    D2 --> M2["MULTIPLY BY h OF 3"]
    D3 --> M3["MULTIPLY BY h OF 4"]
    D4 --> M4["MULTIPLY BY h OF 5"]
    D5["DELAY z MINUS 1"] --> M5["MULTIPLY BY h OF 6"]
    D5 --> D4
    XN --> M6["MULTIPLY BY h OF 0"]
    
    M0 --> SUM["ADDER TREE"]
    M1 --> SUM
    M2 --> SUM
    M3 --> SUM
    M4 --> SUM
    M5 --> SUM
    M6 --> SUM
    SUM --> YN["OUTPUT y OF n"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Compare the performance of FIR and IIR digital filters in terms of stability, phase response, and computational efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | FIR Filter | IIR Filter |
|---|---|---|
| Stability | Always stable (finite $h(n)$) | Conditionally stable (poles inside unit circle) |
| Phase Response | Can have **exact linear phase** | Non-linear phase in general |
| Computational Efficiency | Needs **more coefficients** for sharp response | Achieves same sharpness with **fewer coefficients** |
| Memory | Stores only past inputs | Stores past inputs **and** past outputs |
| Suitability for biomedical | **Preferred** (linear phase = no waveform distortion) | Used when efficiency is critical |

> **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 for stability, 1 for phase, 1 for efficiency comparison]**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of window functions in FIR filter design. Why is a rectangular window generally not preferred for biomedical signal filtering?**

**Model Answer:**

In the Fourier series method of FIR filter design, the ideal impulse response $h_d(n)$ is infinitely long. To obtain a finite-length causal FIR filter, we multiply $h_d(n)$ by a window $w(n)$ of finite length $N$:

$$h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n), \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

The choice of window governs two critical parameters: **main-lobe width** (controls transition sharpness) and **side-lobe level** (controls stopband attenuation). A rectangular window has the narrowest main lobe but its side lobes are only **−21 dB** below the main lobe, causing large **Gibbs ripples** in the passband and stopband. For biomedical signals (ECG, EEG), these ripples can mask low-amplitude features such as the **P-wave or QRS notch**, leading to diagnostic errors. Hence smooth windows (Hamming, Hanning) with side lobes at −44 dB to −74 dB are preferred.

> **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 for windowing concept, 1 for main/side-lobe trade-off, 1 for biomedical relevance]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the step-by-step procedure for designing an FIR low-pass filter using the Fourier series method with a window function. Include the relevant mathematical expressions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design an FIR low-pass filter with the following specifications using the Hamming window: $F_s = 10$ kHz, $f_c = 1.5$ kHz, $N = 9$. Compute all filter coefficients $h(0)$ to $h(8)$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Procedure [7 Marks]:**

1. **Specify the desired filter:** Identify passband edge $f_p$, stopband edge $f_s$, sampling frequency $F_s$, and filter length $N$.

2. **Convert to digital frequency:**
   $$\omega_c = \frac{2\pi f_c}{F_s}$$

3. **Define the ideal frequency response $H_d(e^{j\omega})$** with linear phase delay $\alpha = (N-1)/2$:
   $$H_d(e^{j\omega}) = \begin{cases} e^{-j\alpha\omega}, & \vert\omega\vert \le \omega_c \\ 0, & \omega_c < \vert\omega\vert \le \pi \end{cases}$$

4. **Compute the ideal impulse response** by applying the IDTFT:
   $$h_d(n) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{j\omega(n-\alpha)} d\omega = \frac{\sin[\omega_c(n-\alpha)]}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$$

5. **Choose a window function** $w(n)$ of length $N$. For biomedical applications, **Hamming** is preferred:
   $$w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{N-1}\right)$$

6. **Multiply to obtain FIR coefficients:**
   $$h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n), \quad 0 \le n \le N-1$$

7. **Validate:** Check coefficient symmetry $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ for linear phase, and confirm BIBO stability (automatic for FIR).

**Part (b) — Numerical Design [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1:** Normalize the cutoff:
$$\omega_c = 2\pi \cdot \frac{1500}{10000} = 0.3\pi \text{ rad/sample} = 0.9425 \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2:** Compute delay: $\alpha = (9-1)/2 = 4$

**Step 3:** Compute $h_d(n)$ for $n = 0$ to $8$ using $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin[\omega_c(n-4)]}{\pi(n-4)}$:

| $n$ | $n-4$ | $\omega_c(n-4)$ | $\sin[\cdot]$ | $h_d(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | -4 | $-4 \times 0.3\pi = -1.2\pi$ | $-0.9511$ | $0.0757$ |
| 1 | -3 | $-0.9\pi$ | $-0.3090$ | $0.0328$ |
| 2 | -2 | $-0.6\pi$ | $0.9511$ | $0.1514$ |
| 3 | -1 | $-0.3\pi$ | $0.8090$ | $0.2576$ |
| 4 | 0 | $0$ | $0$ | $0.3$ |
| 5 | 1 | $0.3\pi$ | $0.8090$ | $0.2576$ |
| 6 | 2 | $0.6\pi$ | $0.9511$ | $0.1514$ |
| 7 | 3 | $0.9\pi$ | $-0.3090$ | $-0.0328$ |
| 8 | 4 | $1.2\pi$ | $-0.9511$ | $-0.0757$ |

**Step 4:** Compute Hamming window $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n / 8)$:

| $n$ | $2\pi n/8$ | $\cos(\cdot)$ | $w(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 1.000 | 0.080 |
| 1 | $\pi/4$ | 0.707 | 0.215 |
| 2 | $\pi/2$ | 0.000 | 0.540 |
| 3 | $3\pi/4$ | -0.707 | 0.865 |
| 4 | $\pi$ | -1.000 | 1.000 |
| 5 | $5\pi/4$ | -0.707 | 0.865 |
| 6 | $3\pi/2$ | 0.000 | 0.540 |
| 7 | $7\pi/4$ | 0.707 | 0.215 |
| 8 | $2\pi$ | 1.000 | 0.080 |

**Step 5:** Multiply $h(n) = h_d(n) \cdot w(n)$:

| $n$ | $h_d(n)$ | $w(n)$ | $h(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0757 | 0.080 | 0.0061 |
| 1 | 0.0328 | 0.215 | 0.0071 |
| 2 | 0.1514 | 0.540 | 0.0818 |
| 3 | 0.2576 | 0.865 | 0.2228 |
| 4 | 0.3000 | 1.000 | 0.3000 |
| 5 | 0.2576 | 0.865 | 0.2228 |
| 6 | 0.1514 | 0.540 | 0.0818 |
| 7 | -0.0328 | 0.215 | -0.0071 |
| 8 | -0.0757 | 0.080 | -0.0061 |

**Verification:** $h(0) = h(8) = 0.0061$, $h(1) = -h(7) = -0.0071$, $h(2) = h(6) = 0.0818$, $h(3) = h(5) = 0.2228$, $h(4) = 0.3000$. The filter is **antisymmetric** — this corresponds to a **Type IV linear-phase FIR filter** (suitable for differentiators, Hilbert transformers, and high-pass filters with a zero at DC). To obtain a standard **Type I LPF**, the cutoff should be less than $\pi/2$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare digital filters with analog filters in detail. Explain with neat block diagrams why digital filters are preferred in modern biomedical instrumentation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Design an FIR high-pass filter using the Hamming window with $F_s = 4$ kHz, $f_c = 1$ kHz, $N = 7$. Tabulate all $h(n)$ values.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparison [7 Marks]:**

| Sl. No. | Parameter | Analog Filter | Digital Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Signal type | Continuous-time $x(t)$ | Discrete-time $x(n)$ |
| 2 | Building blocks | R, L, C, Op-amps | Multipliers, adders, delay (Z⁻¹) |
| 3 | Implementation | Hardware circuit | DSP / software algorithm |
| 4 | Stability | Depends on component tolerances | Guaranteed (for FIR); controllable (for IIR) |
| 5 | Phase linearity | Poor, requires all-pass equalizer | Easily designed for linear phase (FIR) |
| 6 | Adaptability | Manual component replacement | Software coefficient update |
| 7 | Noise performance | Susceptible to thermal & EMI noise | High noise immunity (digital logic levels) |
| 8 | Speed | Real-time, limited by op-amp slew rate | Limited by ADC and clock frequency |
| 9 | Cost for complex responses | High (many precision components) | Low (programmed in software) |
| 10 | Biomedical example | RC-coupled ECG amplifier | FIR digital 50 Hz notch filter |

**Why Digital Filters are Preferred in Biomedical Instrumentation:**

1. **Preservation of waveform morphology** — linear-phase FIR filters prevent time-shift distortion of QRS complexes, which is critical for accurate diagnosis.
2. **Software reconfigurability** — same hardware can implement LPF, HPF, BPF, or notch by simply updating coefficients.
3. **No component aging** — unlike analog R/L/C values that drift with temperature.
4. **Easy storage and reproducibility** — filters can be stored digitally and reproduced exactly.
5. **Integration with ADC/DSP pipelines** — modern biomedical systems are inherently digital after the front-end amplifier.

**Part (b) — FIR HPF Design [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1:** Normalize the cutoff:
$$\omega_c = 2\pi \cdot \frac{1000}{4000} = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad/sample}$$

**Step 2:** Compute delay: $\alpha = (7-1)/2 = 3$

**Step 3:** Compute the LPF response first (used as the building block for HPF):
$$h_{d,LPF}(n) = \frac{\sin[\omega_c(n-3)]}{\pi(n-3)} = \frac{\sin[\pi(n-3)/2]}{\pi(n-3)}$$

| $n$ | $n-3$ | $\omega_c(n-3)$ | $\sin[\cdot]$ | $h_{d,LPF}(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | -3 | $-3\pi/2$ | $1.000$ | $-0.1061$ |
| 1 | -2 | $-\pi$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 2 | -1 | $-\pi/2$ | $-1.000$ | $0.3183$ |
| 3 | 0 | $0$ | $0$ | $0.5$ |
| 4 | 1 | $\pi/2$ | $1.000$ | $0.3183$ |
| 5 | 2 | $\pi$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 6 | 3 | $3\pi/2$ | $-1.000$ | $-0.1061$ |

**Step 4:** Apply spectral subtraction: $h_{d,HPF}(n) = \delta(n-3) - h_{d,LPF}(n)$:

| $n$ | $\delta(n-3)$ | $h_{d,LPF}(n)$ | $h_{d,HPF}(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $0$ | $-0.1061$ | $0.1061$ |
| 1 | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 2 | $0$ | $0.3183$ | $-0.3183$ |
| 3 | $1$ | $0.5$ | $0.5$ |
| 4 | $0$ | $0.3183$ | $-0.3183$ |
| 5 | $0$ | $0$ | $0$ |
| 6 | $0$ | $-0.1061$ | $0.1061$ |

**Step 5:** Compute Hamming window $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/6)$:

| $n$ | $w(n)$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.080 |
| 1 | 0.310 |
| 2 | 0.770 |
| 3 | 1.000 |
| 4 | 0.770 |
| 5 | 0.310 |
| 6 | 0.080 |

**Step 6:** Final FIR HPF coefficients $h(n) = h_{d,HPF}(n) \cdot w(n)$:

| $n$ | $h_{d,HPF}(n)$ | $w(n)$ | $h(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.1061 | 0.080 | **0.0085** |
| 1 | 0 | 0.310 | **0** |
| 2 | -0.3183 | 0.770 | **-0.2451** |
| 3 | 0.5 | 1.000 | **0.5** |
| 4 | -0.3183 | 0.770 | **-0.2451** |
| 5 | 0 | 0.310 | **0** |
| 6 | 0.1061 | 0.080 | **0.0085** |

**Verification:** The coefficients are symmetric: $h(0)=h(6)=0.0085$, $h(1)=h(5)=0$, $h(2)=h(4)=-0.2451$, $h(3)=0.5$. DC gain $= \sum h(n) = 0.0085 + 0 - 0.2451 + 0.5 - 0.2451 + 0 + 0.0085 = 0.0268 \approx 0$ ✓ (correct for a high-pass filter).

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 3–5 Marks Per Question:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pi$ factor:** The correct sinc formula has $\sin[\omega_c(n-\alpha)] / \pi(n-\alpha)$, NOT $\sin[\omega_c(n-\alpha)] / (n-\alpha)$. Always divide by $\pi$.
> 2. **Skipping the unit conversion:** Failing to convert Hz to rad/sample using $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c / F_s$ is the #1 reason for full-mark loss. The cutoff **must be in radians/sample** before substitution.
> 3. **Not truncating the sinc properly:** $h_d(n)$ exists for all integers $n$ from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$. You must keep **only $N$ samples** (typically $n=0$ to $N-1$) and discard the rest. Showing $h_d(n)$ for $n < 0$ without shifting/cropping loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $\alpha$ with $N$:** $\alpha = (N-1)/2$ is the **delay**, not the filter length. Many students wrongly use $\alpha = N/2$.
> 5. **Window endpoint errors:** The window formula uses $N-1$ in the denominator, not $N$. For Hamming: $w(n) = 0.54 - 0.46\cos(2\pi n/(N-1))$.
> 6. **No symmetry check:** Always verify $h(n) = h(N-1-n)$ in your final answer. If symmetry holds, the filter has linear phase — a key KTU marking checkpoint.
> 7. **Forgetting to state the filter type:** A complete answer must declare "**This is a Type I linear-phase FIR filter**" or "**Type IV if antisymmetric**" to fetch the design-justification marks.

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two main filter types:** FIR (non-recursive, always stable, linear phase possible) and IIR (recursive, conditionally stable, sharp response with fewer coefficients).
- **Analog vs. Digital:** Digital filters win on flexibility, reproducibility, and phase linearity; analog filters win on real-time speed and simplicity.
- **FIR Design = 3 Steps:** (1) Define ideal $H_d(e^{j\omega})$, (2) IDTFT to get $h_d(n)$ which is a sinc function, (3) Multiply by window $w(n)$.
- **Master Formula (LPF):** $h_d(n) = \dfrac{\sin[\omega_c(n-\alpha)]}{\pi(n-\alpha)}$ where $\alpha = (N-1)/2$.
- **HPF = Delta minus LPF:** $h_{d,HPF}(n) = \delta(n-\alpha) - h_{d,LPF}(n)$.
- **BPF = LPF($\omega_2$) minus LPF($\omega_1$):** Two sinc functions subtracted.
- **Window Trade-off:** Narrow main lobe ↔ high side lobes (Rectangular = 4π/N, −21 dB). Wide main lobe ↔ low side lobes (Blackman = 12π/N, −74 dB).
- **Biomedical Default:** **Hamming window** — best balance of attenuation (−53 dB) and transition width.
- **Linear Phase Requirement:** Coefficients must be symmetric ($h(n) = h(N-1-n)$) or antisymmetric ($h(n) = -h(N-1-n)$).
- **Stability:** FIR filters are **always BIBO stable** because $\sum \vert h(n) \vert < \infty$ by construction.
- **Group Delay:** $\tau_g = (N-1)/2 = \alpha$ samples — a **constant** for symmetric FIR (this is the "linear phase" property).
- **Always convert units:** Hz → rad/sample using $\omega_c = 2\pi f_c / F_s$ **before** substitution.
- **KTU favorite question pattern:** "Design an FIR LPF/HPF/BPF using the window method for given $F_s$, $f_c$, and $N$" — practice the full 6-step procedure until it is automatic.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — IIR Digital Filters, Analog Approximations & Biomedical Project Domains

## 1.1 IIR Digital Filters — Core Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> An **IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) Digital Filter** is a discrete-time causal linear shift-invariant system whose output $y[n]$ depends not only on the current and past input samples $x[n], x[n-1], \dots, x[n-M]$ but also on **past output samples** $y[n-1], y[n-2], \dots, y[n-N]$. Because the impulse response theoretically extends to infinity, it is called *infinite* impulse response.

The general difference equation governing any IIR filter is:

$$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$

The corresponding transfer function in the $z$-domain is:

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k}}{\displaystyle 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Echo Room":**
> Imagine shouting inside a large empty hall. The echo you hear is the room "remembering" your past shouts. An IIR filter behaves exactly like this echo room: every output sample carries a *recursion* of past outputs (the echoes). An FIR (Finite Impulse Response) filter, in contrast, behaves like a pristine anechoic chamber — it only listens to the current and past inputs, with **no memory of its own outputs**. The recursive feedback term $\sum a_k y[n-k]$ is what gives IIR filters their characteristic "ringing" tail, and also their sharp roll-off with fewer coefficients compared to FIR filters.

### 1.2 Why IIR Filters for Biomedical Signals?

| Property | IIR Advantage in Biomedical DSP |
|---|---|
| **Sharp magnitude response** | Cleanly removes **50/60 Hz powerline interference** from ECG/EMG |
| **Low computational cost** | Real-time filtering on low-power wearable hardware |
| **Phase linearity trade-off** | Acceptable for diagnostic *envelope* analysis, not for waveform morphology |
| **Recursive structure** | Naturally models physiological feedback loops (e.g., respiratory–cardiac coupling) |

---

## 1.3 Analog Filter Approximations — The Design Philosophy

Since digital IIR filters are typically designed by transforming a well-understood **analog prototype**, we must first understand how an ideal analog filter is *approximated* by a real, physically realizable one.

The **ideal** low-pass filter magnitude response is a brick-wall:

$$\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert_{\text{ideal}} = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le \Omega \le \Omega_c \\ 0, & \Omega > \Omega_c \end{cases}$$

This ideal response is **not realizable** (Paley-Wiener condition fails). Therefore we *approximate* it using three classical analog prototype families:

1. **Butterworth Approximation** — maximally flat magnitude in the passband.
2. Chebyshev Approximation (Type I & II) — equiripple in passband/stopband.
3. Elliptic (Cauer) Approximation — equiripple in both bands.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Only the **Butterworth approximation** is mandated in Module 4. Chebyshev and elliptic are not in the syllabus but may be referenced for completeness.

---

## 1.4 The Butterworth Approximation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> The **Butterworth approximation** of order $N$ produces a magnitude-squared response that is *maximally flat* (all derivatives up to order $2N-1$ vanish) at $\Omega = 0$, and monotonically decreasing for $\Omega > 0$. The defining equation is:
> $$\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$$
> where $\Omega_c$ is the **cutoff frequency** (the $-3$ dB point) and $N$ is the filter order.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Smooth Hill":**
> Picture a gently rolling hill. Butterworth's response is exactly that — a single smooth mound that drops off gracefully, with **no ripples** anywhere. Chebyshev's response, by contrast, looks like a saw-toothed plateau — flat on top with ripples. Butterworth sacrifices a sharp transition for **smoothness**, which is medically valuable when you don't want to introduce waveform distortion (e.g., preserving ST-segment morphology in ECG).

---

## 1.5 Frequency Transformation Techniques — Bridging Analog to Digital

A frequency transformation (or mapping) converts a **continuous-time transfer function** $H_a(s)$ into a **discrete-time transfer function** $H(z)$. KTU 2024 mandates three classical techniques:

| # | Technique | Core Idea | Aliasing Risk |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Impulse Invariant Transformation** | Sample the analog impulse response $h_a(t)$ at $t = nT$ | **YES** — high frequencies fold |
| 2 | **Matched Z-Transform Technique** | Map analog poles at $p_k$ to $z = e^{p_k T}$; zeros at $z_k$ to $z = e^{z_k T}$ | Moderate |
| 3 | **Bilinear Transformation** | Algebraic substitution $s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ | **NO** — one-to-one mapping |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "Three Ways to Translate a Book":**
> - **Impulse Invariant** = literal translation — keeps the *sound* of words (samples match), but long words get clipped at the page edge (aliasing).
> - **Matched Z-Transform** = translation by matching key landmarks (poles and zeros), like matching chapters to chapters.
> - **Bilinear** = a complete rewrite that *preserves meaning but changes all the words* — every analog frequency is squashed via a *tangent* function before mapping, so nothing is lost, but you must "pre-warp" the critical frequencies.

---

# MODULE 4 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IIR Filter Architecture Recap

### 2.1.1 Direct Form-I Structure
The direct implementation of the IIR difference equation is:

**Step 1:** Compute the *non-recursive* (FIR-like) part: $w[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$

**Step 2:** Compute the *recursive* (feedback) part: $y[n] = w[n] - \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k]$

### 2.1.2 Why Stability Matters
The IIR filter is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if and only if **all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle**:

$$\vert z_k \vert < 1, \quad \forall k \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}$$

> [!TIP]
> This is a *high-yield* KTU question: "State and prove the stability condition for an IIR filter."

---

## 2.2 Butterworth Approximation — Theory

### 2.2.1 Deriving the Magnitude-Squared Function

The Butterworth response is built from the axiom: *"The first $2N-1$ derivatives of $\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2$ with respect to $\Omega$ should be zero at $\Omega = 0$."*

This produces the *unique* low-pass form:

$$\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$$

**Key observations:**

- At $\Omega = 0$: $\vert H_a(j0) \vert^2 = 1$ (unity DC gain).
- At $\Omega = \Omega_c$: $\vert H_a(j\Omega_c) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$, which is $-3.01$ dB (the cutoff point).
- As $\Omega \to \infty$: roll-off rate is $-20N$ dB/decade.

### 2.2.2 Determining the Filter Order $N$

For a given specification of passband ripple $A_p$, stopband attenuation $A_s$, passband edge $\Omega_p$, and stopband edge $\Omega_s$:

$$N \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{A_s/10} - 1}{10^{A_p/10} - 1}}\right)}{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** In biomedical applications, $A_p = 3$ dB is common for *envelope* filters, and $A_s = 40$–$60$ dB is typical when suppressing powerline harmonics.

### 2.2.3 Pole Locations of a Butterworth Filter

The poles of $H_a(s)H_a(-s)$ lie on a **circle of radius $\Omega_c$** in the $s$-plane, equally spaced, and *none on the imaginary axis* (for stability we keep only the left-half-plane poles):

$$s_k = \Omega_c \cdot e^{j\pi(2k + N - 1)/(2N)}, \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, 2N$$

The stable poles are those with $\text{Re}(s_k) < 0$ (left half-plane).

---

## 2.3 Frequency Transformation — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Impulse Invariant Transformation

**Mathematical Foundation:**
If $h_a(t)$ is the analog impulse response and $T$ is the sampling period, the digital impulse response is defined as:

$$h[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT)$$

If the analog transfer function is expanded in partial fractions:

$$H_a(s) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{R_k}{s - p_k}$$

then the digital transfer function becomes:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{T \cdot R_k}{1 - e^{p_k T} z^{-1}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Limitation — Aliasing:** Since $h_a(t)$ is generally band-limited only after design, any frequency content above $\pi/T$ will *fold back* into the baseband. This makes impulse invariant **unsuitable for high-pass filters**, but acceptable for low-pass filters with a cutoff well below $f_s/2$.

### 2.3.2 Matched Z-Transform

**Mapping Rule:**
For an analog transfer function $H_a(s)$ factored into poles $p_k$ and zeros $z_k$:

$$H_a(s) = K \cdot \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{M}(s - z_k)}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(s - p_k)}$$

the matched Z-transform yields:

$$H(z) = K_m \cdot \frac{\prod_{k=1}^{M}(1 - e^{z_k T} z^{-1})}{\prod_{k=1}^{N}(1 - e^{p_k T} z^{-1})}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** The "matching" name comes from the fact that each pole (or zero) in the $s$-plane at location $p_k$ is *matched* to a corresponding pole (or zero) in the $z$-plane at $z = e^{p_k T}$, preserving the system's natural modes exactly.

### 2.3.3 Bilinear Transformation

**Mapping Rule:**
The substitution is:

$$s = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}} = \frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{z - 1}{z + 1}$$

**Frequency Warping Relationship:**
The analog frequency $\Omega$ and digital frequency $\omega$ are related by:

$$\Omega = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega}{2}\right)$$

or equivalently:

$$\omega = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{\Omega T}{2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-warping is mandatory:** When designing a digital filter to meet a critical frequency $\omega_c$, you must first compute the *pre-warped analog frequency*:
> $$\Omega_c^{\text{prewarp}} = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_c}{2}\right)$$
> and use $\Omega_c^{\text{prewarp}}$ in the analog prototype design. Skipping this step is the **#1 cause of KTU design errors**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table for last-minute revision.** All exam-critical equations are condensed here. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Units / Boundary Conditions** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | IIR Difference Equation | $y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$ | $a_0 = 1$ by convention |
| 2 | IIR Transfer Function | $H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | BIBO stable iff $\vert z_{\text{pole}} \vert < 1$ |
| 3 | Butterworth Magnitude-Squared | $\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$ | $-3$ dB at $\Omega = \Omega_c$ |
| 4 | Butterworth Roll-off | $\text{Attenuation} = 20 N \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)$ dB | Asymptotic for $\Omega \gg \Omega_c$ |
| 5 | Required Filter Order | $N \ge \dfrac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{A_s/10} - 1}{10^{A_p/10} - 1}}\right)}{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}$ | $A_p$ in dB, $A_s$ in dB |
| 6 | Butterworth Pole Locations | $s_k = \Omega_c \cdot e^{j\pi(2k + N - 1)/(2N)}$ | Keep LHP poles only |
| 7 | Impulse Invariant Mapping | $H(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \dfrac{T R_k}{1 - e^{p_k T} z^{-1}}$ | $R_k$ = partial-fraction residues |
| 8 | Matched Z-Transform | $H(z) = K_m \prod_{k=1}^{M}(1 - e^{z_k T} z^{-1}) \big/ \prod_{k=1}^{N}(1 - e^{p_k T} z^{-1})$ | Direct pole-zero mapping |
| 9 | Bilinear Substitution | $s = \dfrac{2}{T} \cdot \dfrac{1 - z^{-1}}{1 + z^{-1}}$ | One-to-one stable mapping |
| 10 | Frequency Warping (Analog → Digital) | $\Omega = \dfrac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega}{2}\right)$ | Pre-warp before prototype design |
| 11 | Pre-warped Cutoff | $\Omega_c^{\text{pre}} = \dfrac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\omega_c}{2}\right)$ | Mandatory for bilinear design |
| 12 | QRS Detection Threshold (Pan-Tompkins) | $\text{Threshold} = 0.4 \cdot \max(\vert \text{derivative} \vert)$ | Empirical biomedical heuristic |

### 2.4.1 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wearable ECG patches** (e.g., Apple Watch, AliveCor): use Butterworth bandpass + notch (50/60 Hz) — a cascade of IIR stages designed via bilinear transform.
- **Implantable neural recorders**: use elliptic IIR filters for sharp transition bands to maximize neural signal bandwidth within strict power budgets.
- **ICU patient monitors**: use cascaded IIR low-pass Butterworth filters for SPO₂ plethysmographic signal smoothing.
- **EEG acquisition systems**: use IIR notch filters designed via matched Z-transform to remove 50 Hz mains hum.

# MODULE 4 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Working

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Butterworth Filter Order Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU 2024 typical 7-mark question):**
> Design a Butterworth low-pass filter satisfying:
> - Passband edge: $f_p = 1$ kHz, max passband ripple $A_p = 1$ dB
> - Stopband edge: $f_s = 2$ kHz, min stopband attenuation $A_s = 20$ dB
> - Sampling frequency: $f_{\text{samp}} = 8$ kHz

**Step 1 — Convert to radians/sec (for the analog prototype):**
$$\Omega_p = 2\pi f_p = 2\pi \times 10^3 = 2000\pi \;\text{rad/s}$$
$$\Omega_s = 2\pi f_s = 2\pi \times 2 \times 10^3 = 4000\pi \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the order formula:**
$$N \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{A_s/10} - 1}{10^{A_p/10} - 1}}\right)}{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p}\right)}$$

Compute numerator term:
$$10^{A_s/10} - 1 = 10^{2.0} - 1 = 100 - 1 = 99$$
$$10^{A_p/10} - 1 = 10^{0.1} - 1 = 1.2589 - 1 = 0.2589$$
$$\sqrt{\frac{99}{0.2589}} = \sqrt{382.39} = 19.554$$
$$\log_{10}(19.554) = 1.2913$$

Denominator term:
$$\frac{\Omega_s}{\Omega_p} = \frac{4000\pi}{2000\pi} = 2$$
$$\log_{10}(2) = 0.3010$$

**Step 3 — Compute and round up:**
$$N \ge \frac{1.2913}{0.3010} = 4.290$$
$$\boxed{N = 5}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 5th-order Butterworth filter is needed. Since $N$ must be an integer, we always **round up** to guarantee specifications are met.

**Step 4 — Compute the $-3$ dB cutoff $\Omega_c$ using passband spec:**
$$\Omega_c = \frac{\Omega_p}{(10^{A_p/10} - 1)^{1/(2N)}} = \frac{2000\pi}{(0.2589)^{1/10}}$$
$$(0.2589)^{0.1} = e^{0.1 \ln(0.2589)} = e^{0.1 \times (-1.3513)} = e^{-0.13513} = 0.8735$$
$$\Omega_c = \frac{2000\pi}{0.8735} = 2287.3\pi \;\text{rad/s} \approx 7186.4 \;\text{rad/s}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Bilinear Transformation with Pre-warping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Convert an analog Butterworth low-pass filter with cutoff $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/s into a digital filter with digital cutoff $\omega_c = 0.4\pi$ rad using $T = 1$ s and the bilinear transformation.

**Step 1 — Pre-warp the digital cutoff to the analog domain:**
$$\Omega_c^{\text{pre}} = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_c}{2}\right) = 2 \tan\!\left(\frac{0.4\pi}{2}\right) = 2 \tan(0.2\pi)$$

Compute:
$$\tan(0.2\pi) = \tan(36°) = 0.7265$$
$$\Omega_c^{\text{pre}} = 2 \times 0.7265 = 1.4531 \;\text{rad/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** If you had used $\Omega_c = 1$ rad/s *directly* (i.e., skipped pre-warping), the resulting digital filter would have its $-3$ dB point at $\omega = 2 \arctan(0.5) = 0.2956\pi$ rad — significantly *lower* than the desired $0.4\pi$. This is the most common error in KTU answers.

**Step 2 — Design the analog prototype with the pre-warped cutoff:**

The normalized analog transfer function for a 1st-order Butterworth is:
$$H_a(s) = \frac{\Omega_c^{\text{pre}}}{s + \Omega_c^{\text{pre}}} = \frac{1.4531}{s + 1.4531}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the bilinear substitution $s = \frac{2(1 - z^{-1})}{1 + z^{-1}}$:**

$$H(z) = \frac{1.4531}{\dfrac{2(1 - z^{-1})}{1 + z^{-1}} + 1.4531}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(1 + z^{-1})$:
$$H(z) = \frac{1.4531 (1 + z^{-1})}{2(1 - z^{-1}) + 1.4531(1 + z^{-1})}$$

Expand the denominator:
$$\text{Denominator} = 2 - 2z^{-1} + 1.4531 + 1.4531 z^{-1} = 3.4531 - 0.5469 z^{-1}$$

Therefore:
$$H(z) = \frac{1.4531 + 1.4531 z^{-1}}{3.4531 - 0.5469 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Normalize the denominator coefficient to 1 (standard form):**
$$H(z) = \frac{0.4208 + 0.4208 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.1584 z^{-1}}$$

This is a 1st-order IIR low-pass filter with:
- $b_0 = 0.4208, \; b_1 = 0.4208, \; a_1 = -0.1584$

**Step 5 — Verify pole location is inside the unit circle:**
$$z_{\text{pole}} = 0.1584, \quad \vert z_{\text{pole}} \vert = 0.1584 < 1 \;\checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> **Stability verified.** The filter is BIBO-stable.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Impulse Invariant Transformation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** An analog system has transfer function $H_a(s) = \dfrac{2}{(s+1)(s+2)}$. Use the impulse invariant method with $T = 0.5$ s to find $H(z)$.

**Step 1 — Partial fraction expansion:**

Let:
$$H_a(s) = \frac{A}{s+1} + \frac{B}{s+2}$$

Solve for $A$:
$$A = \left.(s+1) H_a(s)\right|_{s=-1} = \frac{2}{-1+2} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$$

Solve for $B$:
$$B = \left.(s+2) H_a(s)\right|_{s=-2} = \frac{2}{-2+1} = \frac{2}{-1} = -2$$

So:
$$H_a(s) = \frac{2}{s+1} - \frac{2}{s+2}$$

**Step 2 — Sample the impulse response:**

The analog impulse response is:
$$h_a(t) = 2 e^{-t} u(t) - 2 e^{-2t} u(t)$$

Sampled at $t = nT$ (with $T = 0.5$ s):
$$h[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT) = 0.5 \left[2 e^{-0.5n} - 2 e^{-n}\right] = e^{-0.5n} - e^{-n}$$

**Step 3 — Z-transform the sampled response:**

Using the pair $a^n u[n] \leftrightarrow \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$:
$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-0.5} z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - e^{-1} z^{-1}}$$

Compute exponentials:
$$e^{-0.5} = 0.6065, \quad e^{-1} = 0.3679$$

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.6065 z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - 0.3679 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into single fraction:**

$$H(z) = \frac{(1 - 0.3679 z^{-1}) - (1 - 0.6065 z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.6065 z^{-1})(1 - 0.3679 z^{-1})}$$

$$H(z) = \frac{0.2386 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.9744 z^{-1} + 0.2231 z^{-2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Both poles ($z = 0.6065$ and $z = 0.3679$) are inside the unit circle, so the filter is stable.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4: Matched Z-Transform

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** Apply the matched Z-transform to $H_a(s) = \dfrac{s + 2}{(s + 1)(s + 3)}$ with $T = 1$ s.

**Step 1 — Identify analog poles and zeros:**
- Poles: $p_1 = -1, \; p_2 = -3$
- Zero: $z_1 = -2$

**Step 2 — Apply the mapping $p \to e^{pT}$:**

Pole mapping:
$$z_{\text{pole},1} = e^{-1 \cdot 1} = 0.3679$$
$$z_{\text{pole},2} = e^{-3 \cdot 1} = 0.0498$$

Zero mapping:
$$z_{\text{zero},1} = e^{-2 \cdot 1} = 0.1353$$

**Step 3 — Form the digital transfer function:**

$$H(z) = K_m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{z_1 T} z^{-1}}{(1 - e^{p_1 T} z^{-1})(1 - e^{p_2 T} z^{-1})}$$

$$H(z) = K_m \cdot \frac{1 - 0.1353 z^{-1}}{(1 - 0.3679 z^{-1})(1 - 0.0498 z^{-1})}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the gain $K_m$ via DC matching:** $H_a(0) = H(z)\big|_{z=1}$

$$H_a(0) = \frac{0 + 2}{(0+1)(0+3)} = \frac{2}{3}$$
$$H(1) = K_m \cdot \frac{1 - 0.1353}{(1 - 0.3679)(1 - 0.0498)} = K_m \cdot \frac{0.8647}{0.6321 \cdot 0.9502} = K_m \cdot 1.4396$$

Setting equal: $K_m \cdot 1.4396 = 0.6667 \Rightarrow K_m = 0.4630$

**Step 5 — Final form:**
$$H(z) = \frac{0.4630 - 0.0627 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.4177 z^{-1} + 0.01832 z^{-2}}$$

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    F -->|Matched Z Transform| G2[Map Poles to e to the pT and Zeros to e to the zT]

    G3 --> H
```
