# MODULE 4 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
> **Define an IIR digital filter. Why is it called "infinite" impulse response?**

**Model Answer:**
An IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) digital filter is a discrete-time linear shift-invariant system whose output $y[n]$ depends on both current/past inputs **and** past outputs. The general difference equation is:

$$y[n] = -\sum_{k=1}^{N} a_k y[n-k] + \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$$

The impulse response $h[n]$ of such a system, when the filter is excited by a unit impulse $\delta[n]$, theoretically extends to **infinity** in duration (does not become exactly zero in finite time) because of the recursive feedback term $\sum a_k y[n-k]$. This non-terminating response is the reason for the name "Infinite Impulse Response." (3 marks)

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
> **State the stability condition for an IIR filter. What happens if a pole lies on the unit circle?**

**Model Answer:**
An IIR filter is **BIBO stable** (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output stable) if and only if **all poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly inside the unit circle** in the $z$-plane, i.e.,

$$\vert z_k \vert < 1, \quad \forall k = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

If a pole lies **exactly on the unit circle** ($\vert z_k \vert = 1$), the filter is **marginally stable**: it produces sustained oscillations (e.g., a pure sinusoid) in response to an impulse, and any bounded input may yield an unbounded output over time. Such filters are not useful in practical biomedical signal processing. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]`
>
> **(a)** Derive the magnitude-squared response of an N-th order Butterworth low-pass filter. Show that it is maximally flat at $\Omega = 0$. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Design a Butterworth low-pass digital filter using the bilinear transformation method for the following specifications:
> - Passband edge: $f_p = 100$ Hz
> - Stopband edge: $f_s = 300$ Hz
> - Passband ripple: $A_p = 1$ dB
> - Stopband attenuation: $A_s = 30$ dB
> - Sampling frequency: $f_{\text{samp}} = 1000$ Hz (7 marks)

---

**Part (a) — Model Solution:**

The Butterworth approximation is constructed such that the magnitude-squared function $\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2$ has its first $2N-1$ derivatives equal to zero at $\Omega = 0$ (maximal flatness condition). The unique closed-form solution satisfying this axiom and the constraints $\vert H_a(j0) \vert = 1$ and $\vert H_a(j\Omega_c) \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$ is:

$$\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + \left(\dfrac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}}$$

**Proof of Maximal Flatness:**

Define $f(\Omega) = \vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = [1 + (\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}]^{-1}$. Let $u = \Omega^2$. Then:

$$f(\Omega) = [1 + (u/\Omega_c^2)^N]^{-1}$$

**Derivative computation (1st derivative):**
$$\frac{df}{d\Omega} = -N \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N-1} \cdot \frac{2}{\Omega_c} \cdot \left[1 + \left(\frac{\Omega}{\Omega_c}\right)^{2N}\right]^{-2}$$

At $\Omega = 0$: $\left(\Omega/\Omega_c\right)^{2N-1} = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{df}{d\Omega}\bigg|_{\Omega=0} = 0 \;\checkmark$

By the chain rule, every derivative of the form $\dfrac{d^k f}{d\Omega^k}$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, 2N-1$ contains a factor of $\left(\Omega/\Omega_c\right)^{2N-k}$, which vanishes at $\Omega = 0$. Hence all $2N-1$ derivatives are zero — confirming maximal flatness. (7 marks)

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert specifications to digital radian frequencies:**
$$\omega_p = 2\pi f_p / f_{\text{samp}} = 2\pi \times 100/1000 = 0.2\pi \;\text{rad}$$
$$\omega_s = 2\pi f_s / f_{\text{samp}} = 2\pi \times 300/1000 = 0.6\pi \;\text{rad}$$

**Step 2 — Pre-warp both critical frequencies (since bilinear transform requires this):**
$$\Omega_p = \frac{2}{T} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_p}{2}\right) = 2 f_{\text{samp}} \tan\!\left(\frac{0.2\pi}{2}\right) = 2000 \tan(0.1\pi) = 2000 \times 0.3249 = 649.84 \;\text{rad/s}$$
$$\Omega_s = 2 f_{\text{samp}} \tan\!\left(\frac{\omega_s}{2}\right) = 2000 \tan(0.3\pi) = 2000 \times 1.3764 = 2752.8 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Determine filter order:**
$$N \ge \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{10^{3.0} - 1}{10^{0.1} - 1}}\right)}{\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2752.8}{649.84}\right)} = \frac{\log_{10}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{999}{0.2589}}\right)}{\log_{10}(4.236)}$$

Numerator: $\sqrt{999/0.2589} = \sqrt{3858.6} = 62.12$; $\log_{10}(62.12) = 1.7934$

Denominator: $\log_{10}(4.236) = 0.6270$

$$N \ge \frac{1.7934}{0.6270} = 2.860 \Rightarrow \boxed{N = 3}$$

**Step 4 — Determine the $-3$ dB cutoff from passband spec:**
$$\Omega_c = \frac{\Omega_p}{(10^{0.1} - 1)^{1/6}} = \frac{649.84}{(0.2589)^{1/6}}$$

$(0.2589)^{1/6} = e^{(1/6) \ln(0.2589)} = e^{(1/6)(-1.3513)} = e^{-0.2252} = 0.7984$

$$\Omega_c = \frac{649.84}{0.7984} = 813.93 \;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the analog Butterworth poles for N = 3:**

Pole angles: $\theta_k = \pi/2 + (2k-1)\pi/(2N)$ for $k = 1, 2, 3$, or equivalently use the formula:

$$s_k = \Omega_c \cdot e^{j\pi(2k + N - 1)/(2N)}, \quad k = 1, 2, 3$$

For $N = 3$: $s_k = 813.93 \cdot e^{j\pi(2k+2)/6}$

- $k=1$: $s_1 = 813.93 \cdot e^{j\pi \cdot 4/6} = 813.93 \cdot e^{j 2\pi/3} = -406.97 + j\,704.94$
- $k=2$: $s_2 = 813.93 \cdot e^{j\pi \cdot 6/6} = 813.93 \cdot e^{j\pi} = -813.93$
- $k=3$: $s_3 = 813.93 \cdot e^{j\pi \cdot 8/6} = 813.93 \cdot e^{j 4\pi/3} = -406.97 - j\,704.94$

**Step 6 — Form the analog transfer function (using stable LHP poles only — all three are LHP):**
$$H_a(s) = \frac{813.93^3}{(s + 813.93)(s^2 + 813.93 s + 813.93^2)}$$

(since the product of the complex pair $(s-s_1)(s-s_3) = s^2 + 813.93 s + 813.93^2$)

**Step 7 — Apply bilinear substitution $s = 2000 \cdot (1 - z^{-1})/(1 + z^{-1})$:**

This step, while straightforward, requires careful algebra. The final cascaded second-order sections (biquads) are typically reported in KTU answers. (7 marks for full working)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]`
>
> **(a)** Explain the Impulse Invariant Method of designing IIR digital filters from analog prototypes. Discuss its advantages and limitations. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Using the impulse invariant technique, convert the analog filter $H_a(s) = \dfrac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)}$ into a digital filter with $T = 0.5$ s. Verify the stability of the resulting digital filter. (7 marks)

---

**Part (a) — Model Solution:**

The **Impulse Invariant Method** is a frequency-domain sampling technique that produces a digital filter whose impulse response $h[n]$ is a *scaled, sampled version* of the analog prototype's impulse response $h_a(t)$:

$$h[n] = T \cdot h_a(nT), \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

If the analog transfer function is expressed in partial-fraction form:

$$H_a(s) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{R_k}{s - p_k}$$

then the corresponding digital transfer function is:

$$H(z) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} \frac{T \cdot R_k}{1 - e^{p_k T} z^{-1}}$$

The factor $T$ ensures that the digital filter's gain matches the analog filter's gain at DC.

**Advantages:**
1. The **time-domain impulse response** is preserved exactly at sampling instants — useful when transient behavior matters.
2. Mathematically simple — straightforward partial-fraction expansion.
3. Stable analog filters yield stable digital filters (since $\text{Re}(p_k) < 0 \Rightarrow \vert e^{p_k T} \vert < 1$).

**Limitations:**
1. **Aliasing:** Since $H_a(j\Omega)$ is generally not strictly band-limited, frequencies above $\pi/T$ fold back into $[0, \pi/T]$, distorting the frequency response.
2. **Unsuitable for high-pass and band-stop filters** — these have significant energy near the Nyquist frequency.
3. Frequency response shape is *only approximately* preserved, never exactly.

**Conclusion:** The method is best suited for **low-pass and band-pass filters** with cutoff frequencies well below $f_s/2$, e.g., anti-aliasing pre-filters in biomedical front-ends. (7 marks)

---

**Part (b) — Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Partial Fraction Expansion of $H_a(s)$:**

$$H_a(s) = \frac{1}{(s+2)(s+3)} = \frac{A}{s+2} + \frac{B}{s+3}$$

$$A = \left.\frac{1}{s+3}\right|_{s=-2} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$
$$B = \left.\frac{1}{s+2}\right|_{s=-3} = \frac{1}{-1} = -1$$

$$H_a(s) = \frac{1}{s+2} - \frac{1}{s+3}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Impulse Invariant Mapping:**

With $T = 0.5$ s, $e^{p_k T}$ values:
$$e^{p_1 T} = e^{-2 \times 0.5} = e^{-1} = 0.3679$$
$$e^{p_2 T} = e^{-3 \times 0.5} = e^{-1.5} = 0.2231$$

$$H(z) = \frac{T \cdot 1}{1 - 0.3679 z^{-1}} - \frac{T \cdot 1}{1 - 0.2231 z^{-1}}$$

$$H(z) = \frac{0.5}{1 - 0.3679 z^{-1}} - \frac{0.5}{1 - 0.2231 z^{-1}}$$

**Step 3 — Combine into single rational form:**

Common denominator:
$$H(z) = \frac{0.5(1 - 0.2231 z^{-1}) - 0.5(1 - 0.3679 z^{-1})}{(1 - 0.3679 z^{-1})(1 - 0.2231 z^{-1})}$$

Numerator:
$$= 0.5 - 0.1116 z^{-1} - 0.5 + 0.1840 z^{-1} = 0.0724 z^{-1}$$

Denominator:
$$= 1 - 0.2231 z^{-1} - 0.3679 z^{-1} + 0.0821 z^{-2} = 1 - 0.5910 z^{-1} + 0.0821 z^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{H(z) = \frac{0.0724 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5910 z^{-1} + 0.0821 z^{-2}}}$$

**Step 4 — Stability Verification:**

Find the poles by solving $1 - 0.5910 z^{-1} + 0.0821 z^{-2} = 0$, or equivalently $z^2 - 0.5910 z + 0.0821 = 0$:

$$z = \frac{0.5910 \pm \sqrt{0.5910^2 - 4(0.0821)}}{2} = \frac{0.5910 \pm \sqrt{0.3493 - 0.3284}}{2} = \frac{0.5910 \pm \sqrt{0.0209}}{2}$$

$$z = \frac{0.5910 \pm 0.1446}{2}$$

$$z_1 = \frac{0.7356}{2} = 0.3678, \quad z_2 = \frac{0.4464}{2} = 0.2232$$

Both pole magnitudes satisfy $\vert z_1 \vert = 0.3678 < 1$ and $\vert z_2 \vert = 0.2232 < 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification confirms:** As expected, the poles match $e^{p_k T}$ from Step 2. The filter is **BIBO stable**. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting pre-warping** in bilinear transform problems → loses 2–3 marks immediately. The KTU answer key *always* checks for the pre-warped cutoff before substitution.
> 2. **Using impulse invariant for high-pass filters** → method is mathematically applicable but practically unsuitable; KTU expects students to *mention the aliasing issue*.
> 3. **Rounding $N$ down** instead of up → specification not met; mark deduction of 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing $\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert$ vs $\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2$** in Butterworth design → the formula uses magnitude-**squared**; using the square root version gives wrong pole locations.
> 5. **In QRS detection project work:** failing to validate the algorithm on the **MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database** → KTU mandates use of a *standard, publicly available* database.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section for last-minute KTU exam revision.**

- [x] **IIR Filters** are defined by a difference equation with feedback: $y[n] = -\sum a_k y[n-k] + \sum b_k x[n-k]$.
- [x] **BIBO Stability** ⇔ All poles of $H(z)$ inside the unit circle: $\vert z_k \vert < 1$.
- [x] **Butterworth magnitude-squared**: $\vert H_a(j\Omega) \vert^2 = [1 + (\Omega/\Omega_c)^{2N}]^{-1}$.
- [x] Butterworth is **maximally flat** at $\Omega = 0$ (first $2N-1$ derivatives vanish).
- [x] $-3$ dB point occurs exactly at $\Omega = \Omega_c$.
- [x] Roll-off rate is **$-20N$ dB/decade** in the stopband.
- [x] Filter order formula: $N \ge \log\!\left(\sqrt{(10^{A_s/10} - 1)/(10^{A_p/10} - 1)}\right) / \log(\Omega_s/\Omega_p)$.
- [x] Butterworth poles lie on a **circle of radius $\Omega_c$** in the $s$-plane; keep LHP poles only.
- [x] **Impulse Invariant**: $H(z) = \sum T R_k / (1 - e^{p_k T} z^{-1})$; aliasing-prone; **not for high-pass**.
- [x] **Matched Z-Transform**: Direct pole-zero mapping $p \to e^{pT}, z \to e^{zT}$; preserves modal structure.
- [x] **Bilinear Transform**: $s = (2/T)(1 - z^{-1})/(1 + z^{-1})$; **no aliasing**, but **pre-warping mandatory**.
- [x] **Pre-warping formula**: $\Omega_c^{\text{pre}} = (2/T) \tan(\omega_c / 2)$.
- [x] **Project Domain 1 (Artefact Removal):** Low-pass for HF noise, high-pass for baseline wander, **notch (matched Z)** for 50/60 Hz.
- [x] **Project Domain 2 (QRS Detection):** Bandpass (5–15 Hz) → Derivative → Square → Moving-average → Thresholding (Pan-Tompkins).
- [x] **Standard Project Database:** MIT-BIH Arrhythmia Database (PhysioNet).
- [x] **Team Size:** Maximum 5 members per KTU 2024 project regulation.
- [x] **Deliverables:** Methodology, algorithm, code (MATLAB/Python), and validation results on chosen database.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: P Wave and T Wave Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** P Wave and T Wave Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **electrocardiogram (ECG)** is a bioelectric signal that records the electrical activity of the heart over time. Within a single cardiac cycle (P-QRS-T complex), the **P wave** and **T wave** are the two most clinically significant low-amplitude, low-frequency components, distinct from the high-amplitude QRS complex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **P Wave**: Represents **atrial depolarization** — the electrical wavefront that originates in the sinoatrial (SA) node and propagates through both atria, causing them to contract and push blood into the ventricles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **T Wave**: Represents **ventricular repolarization** — the recovery phase of the ventricular myocardium, where the cells return to their resting polarized state after contraction.

## 1.2 Standard Physiological Metrics

| Parameter | P Wave | T Wave |
|---|---|---|
| Normal Amplitude | **≤ 0.25 mV** | **0.1 – 0.5 mV** |
| Normal Duration | **80 – 100 ms** | **160 – 200 ms** |
| Dominant Frequency Band | **5 – 30 Hz** | **0.5 – 10 Hz** |
| Source Event | Atrial Depolarization | Ventricular Repolarization |
| Typical Morphology | Smooth, rounded, monophasic | Asymmetric, broad, often peaked |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the heart as a large orchestra playing a symphony:
- The **P wave** is the *quiet, polite introductory murmur* of the strings tuning up before the piece begins (atria preparing the ventricles).
- The **QRS complex** is the *loud, dramatic brass section striking the main chord* (ventricular contraction — the heart's main pumping action).
- The **T wave** is the *gentle, slow fade-out melody* played by the woodwinds as the orchestra winds down (ventricles relaxing and resetting).

To a DSP engineer, detecting the P and T waves is like trying to hear the softest instruments in the orchestra while the brass is still ringing. They are **masked by high-amplitude QRS energy**, suffer from **baseline wander**, and have **low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)**. This is precisely why P and T wave detection requires specialized algorithms — the famous Pan-Tompkins algorithm works for QRS, but P/T waves demand different filtering, derivative, or wavelet-based strategies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU 2024?** Most clinical arrhythmias (atrial fibrillation, T-wave alternans, long QT syndrome) are diagnosed ONLY through accurate P and T wave analysis. QRS detection alone is clinically insufficient.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why P and T Wave Detection Is Harder than QRS Detection

1. **Low Amplitude**: P wave is roughly **1/8th** the amplitude of the R peak.
2. **Low Frequency Content**: Overlaps with baseline wander (0.1 – 0.5 Hz) and motion artifact.
3. **Morphological Variability**: P wave shape changes with age, breathing, lead placement.
4. **Masking Effect**: T wave is often "buried" in the tail of the QRS — picking it out cleanly is a non-trivial DSP problem.
5. **Absence of Sharp Slopes**: Unlike the steep R wave, P and T waves have **gradual slopes**, defeating simple derivative-thresholding.

## 2.2 Categories of Detection Algorithms

### 2.2.1 Derivative-Based Methods
Compute the first/second derivative of the filtered ECG. Peaks in the derivative correspond to steep slopes (onset/offset of waves). Limited effectiveness for P/T due to gentle slopes.

### 2.2.2 Wavelet Transform-Based Methods (MOST POPULAR for P/T)
Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) decomposes ECG into frequency sub-bands. **Daubechies (db4, db6)** wavelets are widely used because they closely match ECG morphology. Local modulus maxima at specific scales pinpoint P and T wave boundaries.

### 2.2.3 Template Matching & Correlation
A reference P/T waveform is correlated against incoming signal. High correlation = wave detected. Sensitive to morphological changes.

### 2.2.4 Hilbert Transform & Envelope Detection
Computes the **analytic signal envelope**; the envelope peaks identify wave centers.

### 2.2.5 Machine Learning (Modern Approach)
LSTM, CNN classifiers trained on labeled PT databases (MIT-BIH, QT Database). Outside basic KTU 2024 scope, but good to mention.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mandatory** for KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) numerical/derivation questions. Memorize the table — examiners frequently test these directly.

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | First Difference (Derivative) | $y(n) = x(n) - x(n-1)$ | $x(n)$: ECG sample (mV) | Slope detection |
| 2 | Second Difference | $y(n) = x(n) - 2x(n-1) + x(n-2)$ | Same as above | Inflection/curvature |
| 3 | Squaring Function | $y(n) = [x(n)]^{2}$ | Output in $\text{mV}^{2}$ | Emphasizes large slopes |
| 4 | Moving Window Integration | $y(n) = \dfrac{1}{N} \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(n-k)$ | $N$: window length (samples) | Smooths energy over time |
| 5 | Signal Energy (Discrete) | $E = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(n) \vert^{2}$ | Joules equivalent | Waveform power |
| 6 | Mean Energy of Segment | $E_{m} = \dfrac{1}{N} \displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert x(n) \vert^{2}$ | $\text{mV}^{2}$ | Adaptive thresholding |
| 7 | Adaptive Threshold | $\text{THR} = E_{m} \cdot \alpha$ | $\alpha$: scaling constant (0.1 – 0.3) | Decision rule |
| 8 | CWT Definition | $C(a,b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \displaystyle\int x(t) \cdot \psi^{\ast}\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$ | $a$: scale, $b$: translation | Wavelet analysis |
| 9 | DWT Scaling Relation | $\phi(t) = \sqrt{2} \displaystyle\sum_{k} h(k) \phi(2t-k)$ | $h(k)$: low-pass filter | MRA basis |
| 10 | DWT Wavelet Relation | $\psi(t) = \sqrt{2} \displaystyle\sum_{k} g(k) \phi(2t-k)$ | $g(k)$: high-pass filter | Detail coefficients |
| 11 | Bandpass for P Wave | $H_{p}(z) = 1 - z^{-T_{1}}\left(1 - z^{-T_{2}}\right)$ | $T_{1}, T_{2}$: delay samples | Remove DC + HF |
| 12 | Pan-Tompkins Threshold | $\text{THR}_{i+1} = 0.4 \cdot P_{i} + 0.6 \cdot \text{THR}_{i}$ | $P_{i}$: peak amplitude | Adaptive updating |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Holter Monitors & Wearables**: Smartwatches (Apple Watch, Fitbit) use wavelet-based P/T detection to flag **atrial fibrillation** episodes.
- **ICUs / CCUs**: Continuous bedside monitoring of T-wave alternans predicts **sudden cardiac death**.
- **Stress Test Analysis**: Accurate T-wave detection during exercise treadmill testing aids ischemia diagnosis.
- **Sleep Studies (Polysomnography)**: ECG-derived respiration from P-wave amplitude modulation is used in sleep apnea detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: First and Second Derivative Operators for Slope Detection

The goal of the derivative step in any ECG detection pipeline is to **convert the gentle slopes of P and T waves into sharp peaks** that can be easily thresholded.

### 3.1.1 First Derivative

The continuous-time derivative of a signal $x(t)$ is defined as the limit of the difference quotient:

$$
x'(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{x(t + \Delta t) - x(t)}{\Delta t}
$$

In digital form, with sampling period $T_{s} = 1$, we approximate the limit by setting $\Delta t = 1$:

$$
y(n) = \frac{x(n) - x(n-1)}{1} = x(n) - x(n-1)
$$

**Logic step**: At the rising edge of a P wave, $x(n) > x(n-1)$, so $y(n) > 0$. At the falling edge, $y(n) < 0$. The **zero-crossing** of $y(n)$ between these corresponds to the **peak of the wave**.

### 3.1.2 Second Derivative

Differentiate $y(n)$ once more:

$$
y_{2}(n) = y(n) - y(n-1) = [x(n) - x(n-1)] - [x(n-1) - x(n-2)]
$$

Expanding the brackets:

$$
y_{2}(n) = x(n) - x(n-1) - x(n-1) + x(n-2)
$$

Grouping like terms:

$$
y_{2}(n) = x(n) - 2 \cdot x(n-1) + x(n-2)
$$

**Logic step**: The second derivative measures **curvature**. Its zero-crossings (maxima/minima) correspond to the **onset and offset** points of the P or T wave, giving precise wave boundaries.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Squaring and Moving Window Integration

### 3.2.1 Squaring Function

$$
y_{sq}(n) = [x(n)]^{2}
$$

**Logic step**: Squaring makes all values positive (removes polarity confusion) and **amplifies large slopes non-linearly**. A slope of magnitude 5 becomes 25, while a slope of 1 becomes 1. This suppresses small noise and enhances the wave of interest.

### 3.2.2 Moving Window Integration (MWI)

The MWI averages the squared signal over a sliding window of length $N$ samples:

$$
y_{mwi}(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} y_{sq}(n-k)
$$

Expanding the summation:

$$
y_{mwi}(n) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ y_{sq}(n) + y_{sq}(n-1) + y_{sq}(n-2) + \dots + y_{sq}(n-N+1) \right]
$$

**Logic step**: The window length $N$ is chosen to match the **expected duration of the wave**. For a P wave of ~100 ms sampled at 360 Hz, $N \approx 36$ samples. The output of MWI is a smooth waveform whose peaks mark the wave center, and whose width marks the wave duration.

### 3.2.3 Choice of $N$

Window length $N$ in samples relates to physical duration $T_{w}$ as:

$$
N = T_{w} \cdot f_{s}
$$

where $f_{s}$ is the sampling frequency.

**Example (KTU-style numerical)**: For a T wave of duration 180 ms sampled at 250 Hz:

$$
N = 0.180 \times 250 = 45 \text{ samples}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Adaptive Threshold Computation

Static thresholds fail because ECG amplitude varies with patient, lead, and motion. An **adaptive threshold** self-updates.

Let $P_{i}$ be the peak amplitude of the $i$-th detected wave. The threshold for the $(i+1)$-th wave is:

$$
\text{THR}_{i+1} = \beta \cdot P_{i} + (1 - \beta) \cdot \text{THR}_{i}
$$

where $\beta$ is the update coefficient (typically **0.1 to 0.4** in Pan-Tompkins).

**Step-by-step expansion**: Let $\beta = 0.4$. Suppose the previous peak was $P_{1} = 1.2$ mV and the previous threshold was $\text{THR}_{1} = 0.5$ mV.

$$
\text{THR}_{2} = 0.4 \times 1.2 + (1 - 0.4) \times 0.5
$$

$$
\text{THR}_{2} = 0.48 + 0.6 \times 0.5
$$

$$
\text{THR}_{2} = 0.48 + 0.30
$$

$$
\text{THR}_{2} = 0.78 \text{ mV}
$$

**Logic step**: The new threshold is a weighted blend — 40% of the latest peak, 60% of the old threshold. This makes the detector **track slow amplitude drift** (e.g., respiration) without losing sensitivity to real waves.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Wavelet Transform Multi-Resolution Decomposition

### 3.4.1 Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)

The CWT of a signal $x(t)$ with mother wavelet $\psi(t)$ at scale $a$ and translation $b$ is:

$$
C(a, b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot \psi^{\ast}\left( \frac{t - b}{a} \right) \, dt
$$

- $\vert a \vert$ in denominator normalizes the energy of $\psi$ at scale $a$.
- $\psi^{\ast}$ denotes the **complex conjugate** of $\psi$.
- $a$ controls **frequency resolution** (large $a$ = low frequency), $b$ controls **time location**.

### 3.4.2 Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) — Mallat's Algorithm

Mallat's pyramidal decomposition:

$$
A_{j}(k) = \sum_{n} h(n - 2k) \cdot A_{j-1}(n)
$$

$$
D_{j}(k) = \sum_{n} g(n - 2k) \cdot A_{j-1}(n)
$$

where:
- $A_{j}$ = approximation coefficients at level $j$ (low-frequency content)
- $D_{j}$ = detail coefficients at level $j$ (high-frequency content)
- $h$ = low-pass filter (scaling)
- $g$ = high-pass filter (wavelet)
- The factor of 2 in $n - 2k$ represents **dyadic downsampling** (↓2).

**Logic step**: At each decomposition level, the signal is split into two halves of the original bandwidth. For ECG sampled at 360 Hz:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Level 1:} \quad & A_{1}: 0 - 90 \text{ Hz}, \quad D_{1}: 90 - 180 \text{ Hz} \\
\text{Level 2:} \quad & A_{2}: 0 - 45 \text{ Hz}, \quad D_{2}: 45 - 90 \text{ Hz} \\
\text{Level 3:} \quad & A_{3}: 0 - 22.5 \text{ Hz}, \quad D_{3}: 22.5 - 45 \text{ Hz} \\
\text{Level 4:} \quad & A_{4}: 0 - 11.25 \text{ Hz}, \quad D_{4}: 11.25 - 22.5 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Detection rule** (for P wave, dominant in 5–30 Hz, use scales corresponding to $D_{2}$, $D_{3}$, $D_{4}$): A **modulus maximum** (large positive or negative peak) of the wavelet coefficient sequence at the chosen scale, occurring in the expected temporal window between two consecutive R peaks, marks the **P wave peak**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Daubechies-4 (db4) wavelet is the gold standard for ECG P/T wave detection because its shape closely resembles a single cardiac cycle. Always cite **db4 or db6** in KTU answers.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Method | Best For P Wave? | Best For T Wave? | Computational Cost | Noise Robustness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Derivative (1st/2nd) | Moderate | Poor | Low | Low |
| Pan-Tompkins (modified) | Moderate | Moderate | Low | Moderate |
| Hilbert Envelope | Good | Good | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Wavelet (db4) MRA** | **Excellent** | **Excellent** | High | **High** |
| Template Matching | Good | Good | Moderate | Low |
| LSTM/CNN (ML) | Excellent | Excellent | Very High | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic ECG Signal Pipeline (Block Diagram)

The Mermaid diagram below traces a single ECG sample from acquisition to a clean P/T detection decision — this is the exact pipeline you should draw when KTU asks for a "block diagram of ECG processing."

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Raw ECG Signal Acquisition] --> nodeB[Pre Amplifier]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Analog Anti Aliasing Filter 0.5 Hz to 150 Hz]
    nodeC --> nodeD[ADC Sampling at 360 Hz]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Digital High Pass Filter Removes Baseline Wander]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Digital Low Pass Filter Removes 50 Hz Powerline]
    nodeF --> nodeG[R Peak Detection via Pan Tompkins]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Define Search Window RR Interval Minus T P Exclusion]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Wavelet Decomposition Using db4]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Modulus Maxima Search in P Band and T Band]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[P Wave Peak and T Wave Peak Output]
```

## 4.2 Wavelet-Based P/T Detection Architecture (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeX[ECG Segment T0 to T0 plus 2 RR] --> nodeY1[Level 1 Decomposition 180 to 90 Hz]
    nodeY1 --> nodeY2[Level 2 Decomposition 90 to 45 Hz]
    nodeY2 --> nodeY3[Level 3 Decomposition 45 to 22 Hz]
    nodeY3 --> nodeY4[Level 4 Decomposition 22 to 11 Hz]
    nodeY4 --> nodeY5[Modulus Maxima Detection in D3 and D4]
    nodeY5 --> nodeZ1[Identify T Wave Peak]
    nodeY5 --> nodeZ2[Identify P Wave Peak]
    nodeZ1 --> nodeW[Annotate Boundaries and Output]
    nodeZ2 --> nodeW
```

## 4.3 Decision Logic for P Wave Detection (Flowchart)

This diagram explains the *if-then* logic for confirming a P wave after a candidate has been picked by the wavelet modulus maxima search.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeM1[Find Local Max in D3 Wavelet Coefficient] --> nodeM2{Is the Time Within P Search Window}
    nodeM2 -- No --> nodeM3[Discard and Mark as False Positive]
    nodeM2 -- Yes --> nodeM4{Is Magnitude Above Adaptive Threshold}
    nodeM4 -- No --> nodeM3
    nodeM4 -- Yes --> nodeM5{Is Inter Beat Interval RR Within 0.6 to 1.2 s}
    nodeM5 -- No --> nodeM3
    nodeM5 -- Yes --> nodeM6[Accept and Mark as Valid P Wave]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this diagram is KTU-relevant**: Examiners in the 2024 scheme frequently draw a 7-mark question like *"With a neat block diagram, explain P wave detection in ECG."* The above three flowcharts collectively answer that question end-to-end. Memorize the pipeline, the wavelet sub-band mapping, and the search-window logic.

## 4.4 Annotated Waveform Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA1[P Wave Atrial Depolarization 80 to 100 ms] --> nodeA2[PR Segment Isoelectric Delay]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[QRS Complex Ventricular Depolarization 80 to 120 ms]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[ST Segment]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5[T Wave Ventricular Repolarization 160 to 200 ms]
    nodeA5 --> nodeA6[U Wave Optional 0.04 percent Amplitude]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define P wave and T wave in ECG. Why is P wave amplitude low compared to QRS?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper Pattern]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **P wave (1 mark)**: Represents atrial depolarization — the electrical activity of both atria contracting, originating from the SA node. Normal amplitude ≤ **0.25 mV**, duration **80–100 ms**.
- **T wave (1 mark)**: Represents ventricular repolarization — the recovery of ventricular muscle cells to resting state. Amplitude **0.1–0.5 mV**, duration **160–200 ms**.
- **Why P is small (1 mark)**: The atria have a much smaller muscle mass (only ~2 mm thick) compared to the ventricles (~15 mm thick). Lower muscle mass → less total electrical current → smaller P wave amplitude (about **1/8th** of R wave).

---

### Question 2: List four challenges in detecting P and T waves compared to QRS detection.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper Pattern]**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**
1. **Low amplitude**: P wave ≈ **0.25 mV**, easily masked by baseline wander.
2. **Low frequency content**: P (5–30 Hz) and T (0.5–10 Hz) overlap with motion artifact and respiration frequencies.
3. **Gradual slopes**: No sharp transition like the R peak — defeats simple derivative thresholding.
4. **High morphological variability**: Shape changes with breathing, age, electrolyte levels, and lead placement.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a): Explain the Pan-Tompkins algorithm with a block diagram. How can it be modified for P and T wave detection? [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper Pattern]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Original Pan-Tompkins (designed for QRS) [3 marks]:**

The algorithm consists of five sequential stages:
1. **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz)**: Cascaded low-pass (cutoff 11 Hz) and high-pass (cutoff 5 Hz) to isolate QRS energy.
2. **Derivative Operator**: $y(n) = \dfrac{1}{8} \left[ 2x(n) + x(n-1) - x(n-3) - 2x(n-4) \right]$
3. **Squaring**: $y(n) = [x(n)]^{2}$ — emphasizes large slopes, makes all values positive.
4. **Moving Window Integration**: $y(n) = \dfrac{1}{N} \displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} x(n-k)$ with $N \approx 30$ samples (150 ms).
5. **Adaptive Thresholding**: Dual thresholds (signal level + noise level) updated beat-to-beat.

**Step 2 — Modifications for P and T wave detection [3 marks]:**

| Stage | Original (QRS) | Modified (P Wave) | Modified (T Wave) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bandpass | 5–15 Hz | 1–10 Hz | 0.5–15 Hz |
| Search Window | Entire record | Between T and QRS | Between QRS and next P |
| Window Integration $N$ | 30 samples (150 ms) | 20 samples (100 ms) | 36 samples (180 ms) |
| Threshold $\beta$ | 0.4 | 0.2 (more sensitive) | 0.3 |

**Step 3 — Key engineering insight [1 mark]:**
P/T detection is performed **after** R peak is located, restricting the search to physiologically valid windows. This drastically reduces false positives. The search window is typically defined as:
- P wave: $\left[ R_{i-1} + 0.20 \cdot RR, \, R_{i} - 0.20 \cdot RR \right]$
- T wave: $\left[ R_{i} + 0.10 \cdot RR, \, R_{i} + 0.55 \cdot RR \right]$

---

#### Part (b): Derive the first and second derivative operators used in ECG wave detection. A P wave of duration 100 ms is sampled at 500 Hz. What window length $N$ should be used in the moving average? [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — First Derivative (from continuous to discrete) [2 marks]:**

Continuous derivative definition:

$$
x'(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{x(t + \Delta t) - x(t)}{\Delta t}
$$

Discrete approximation with $T_{s} = 1/f_{s}$:

$$
y(n) = \frac{x(n) - x(n-1)}{T_{s}}
$$

Since $T_{s} = 1/500 = 0.002$ s, normalizing:

$$
y(n) = \frac{x(n) - x(n-1)}{0.002} = 500 \left[ x(n) - x(n-1) \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Second Derivative (differentiate again) [2 marks]:**

$$
y_{2}(n) = y(n) - y(n-1) = 500 \left[ x(n) - x(n-1) \right] - 500 \left[ x(n-1) - x(n-2) \right]
$$

$$
y_{2}(n) = 500 \left[ x(n) - 2x(n-1) + x(n-2) \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Moving Average Window Length Calculation [2 marks]:**

Given:
- P wave duration: $T_{w} = 100$ ms $= 0.1$ s
- Sampling frequency: $f_{s} = 500$ Hz

$$
N = T_{w} \cdot f_{s} = 0.1 \times 500 = 50 \text{ samples}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer [1 mark]:**
- First derivative: $y(n) = 500 \left[ x(n) - x(n-1) \right]$
- Second derivative: $y_{2}(n) = 500 \left[ x(n) - 2x(n-1) + x(n-2) \right]$
- Window length: $N = 50$ samples

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

#### Part (a): Explain the wavelet transform-based approach for P and T wave detection. Why is db4 wavelet preferred? [7 Marks]

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Why Wavelet Transform? [2 marks]**
Fourier Transform gives only frequency info, no time localization. Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT) has fixed resolution. **Wavelet Transform** offers:
- Multi-Resolution Analysis (MRA) — decomposes signal into time-frequency tiles.
- Variable window — narrow at high frequencies, wide at low frequencies (matches ECG structure).

**Step 2 — Continuous Wavelet Transform Equation [1 mark]:**

$$
C(a, b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \cdot \psi^{\ast}\left( \frac{t - b}{a} \right) \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Mallat's Pyramidal Decomposition for ECG [2 marks]:**

At each level $j$, signal splits via $h(n)$ (low-pass) and $g(n)$ (high-pass) followed by dyadic downsampling:

$$
A_{j}(k) = \sum_{n} h(n - 2k) \cdot A_{j-1}(n)
$$

$$
D_{j}(k) = \sum_{n} g(n - 2k) \cdot A_{j-1}(n)
$$

For ECG at 360 Hz, scale-to-frequency mapping:
- $D_{1}$ (90–180 Hz) → high-frequency noise
- $D_{2}$ (45–90 Hz) → EMG noise + QRS high components
- $D_{3}$ (22.5–45 Hz) → **QRS main energy + T wave edges**
- $D_{4}$ (11.25–22.5 Hz) → **T wave peak + P wave peak**

**Step 4 — Detection Rule [1 mark]:**
A **modulus maximum** in $D_{3}$ or $D_{4}$ occurring within the physiologically valid P or T window (defined from the R peak) is marked as the wave peak.

**Step 5 — Why db4 Wavelet? [1 mark]:**
The Daubechies-4 (db4) wavelet shape closely resembles one cardiac cycle of an ECG. It is:
- **Compactly supported** (finite duration, good for real-time).
- **Orthogonal** (no redundancy).
- **Has 4 vanishing moments** — meaning it ignores polynomial trends up to degree 3, naturally suppressing baseline wander.
- Empirically validated on MIT-BIH and QT databases.

---

#### Part (b): With a block diagram, explain the step-by-step P wave detection algorithm after R peak localization. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram Stages [7 marks — 1 mark each]:**

1. **Input Stage**: ECG record $x(n)$, sampling rate $f_{s} = 360$ Hz (MIT-BIH standard).
2. **Preprocessing**: High-pass filter (cutoff 0.5 Hz) to remove baseline wander; low-pass (cutoff 40 Hz) to remove 50 Hz powerline.
3. **R Peak Detection**: Apply Pan-Tompkins → obtain $R_{i}$ for each beat.
4. **Search Window Definition**: For each $R_{i}$, define P search window as:
   - Start: $S = R_{i} - 0.40 \cdot (R_{i} - R_{i-1})$
   - End: $E = R_{i} - 0.10 \cdot (R_{i} - R_{i-1})$
5. **Wavelet Decomposition**: Apply db4 wavelet up to 4 levels on the segment $[S, E]$.
6. **Modulus Maxima Search**: Find the largest peak in $D_{3}$ and $D_{4}$ detail coefficients within the window — this is the **P wave peak**.
7. **Boundary Detection & Validation**: Mark onset and offset as the zero-crossings of $D_{2}$ flanking the modulus maximum. Reject if amplitude < 25% of expected P wave amplitude or duration > 120 ms (indicative of pathology).

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in P/T Wave Detection Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "atrial depolarization" with "atrial contraction"** — depolarization is the *electrical* event; contraction is the *mechanical* consequence. Examiners deduct 0.5 marks for this.
> 2. **Using the original Pan-Tompkins bandpass (5–15 Hz) for P wave** — P wave energy lies BELOW 15 Hz, mostly in 5–10 Hz. Always state the *modified* bandpass.
> 3. **Forgetting to define a search window** — KTU answers must say *where* in the ECG you look for the P wave (between T and QRS) and *where* for the T wave (between QRS and next P). Vague answers lose 2 marks.
> 4. **Naming "wavelet" without specifying the type** — Always write **"Daubechies-4 (db4)"** or **"Daubechies-6 (db6)"**. Generic "wavelet" is incomplete.
> 5. **Skipping the units and numerical value in derivations** — If you derive window length, *always substitute the numbers* and state the final numerical answer. Partial-credit marking rewards the numerical conclusion.
> 6. **Writing "Fourier Transform" instead of "Wavelet Transform"** — Fourier alone CANNOT localize P/T waves in time. KTU answers requiring time-frequency analysis MUST use wavelets.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — memorize before entering the exam hall:**

- ⭐ **P wave** = Atrial depolarization; **T wave** = Ventricular repolarization.
- ⭐ **P wave**: amplitude ≤ **0.25 mV**, duration **80–100 ms**, frequency **5–30 Hz**.
- ⭐ **T wave**: amplitude **0.1–0.5 mV**, duration **160–200 ms**, frequency **0.5–10 Hz**.
- ⭐ The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm** is designed for QRS only — must be **modified** for P/T (different bandpass, different window, different threshold sensitivity).
- ⭐ **Window length formula**: $N = T_{w} \times f_{s}$.
- ⭐ **First derivative operator**: $y(n) = x(n) - x(n-1)$.
- ⭐ **Second derivative operator**: $y_{2}(n) = x(n) - 2x(n-1) + x(n-2)$.
- ⭐ **Squaring** removes sign and amplifies large slopes non-linearly.
- ⭐ **Moving Window Integration (MWI)** smooths the squared signal; $N$ matches expected wave duration.
- ⭐ **Adaptive threshold equation**: $\text{THR}_{i+1} = \beta P_{i} + (1 - \beta) \text{THR}_{i}$, with $\beta \in [0.1, 0.4]$.
- ⭐ **Wavelet transform** (especially **db4**) is the gold standard for P/T detection.
- ⭐ **CWT equation**: $C(a, b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\vert a \vert}} \displaystyle\int x(t) \psi^{\ast}\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$.
- ⭐ **Modulus maxima** in detail coefficients $D_{3}$ and $D_{4}$ (sampled at 360 Hz) mark T and P wave peaks respectively.
- ⭐ **Search windows** (after R localization):
  - P wave: $[R_{i-1} + 0.20 \cdot RR, \; R_{i} - 0.20 \cdot RR]$
  - T wave: $[R_{i} + 0.10 \cdot RR, \; R_{i} + 0.55 \cdot RR]$
- ⭐ Always cite **Daubechies-4 (db4)** wavelet in KTU answers.
- ⭐ **Signal energy**: $E = \sum \vert x(n) \vert^{2}$; **Mean energy**: $E_{m} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum \vert x(n) \vert^{2}$.
- ⭐ Common pitfalls: forgetting units, omitting search windows, using Fourier instead of wavelet, mixing up depolarization vs contraction.
- ⭐ **Clinical relevance**: P wave analysis → atrial fibrillation, AV block; T wave analysis → ischemia, long QT syndrome, sudden cardiac death risk.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** PPG: Dicrotic Notch Detection; Heart Sound Detection; EEG Rhythm Detection; Frequency Domain Characterisation of Biomedical Signal - Power Spectrum Density Estimation of Biomedical Signals; Wave Shape and Waveform Complexity Analysis - Normal and Ectopic Beat

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Photoplethysmography (PPG) and Dicrotic Notch Detection

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)
**Photoplethysmography (PPG)** is a non-invasive optical technique that measures volumetric changes in blood in the peripheral circulation. A PPG signal consists of a pulsatile *AC component* superimposed on a slowly varying *DC baseline*. The **dicrotic notch (DN)** is a small downward deflection occurring on the descending limb of the systolic peak, caused by the reflected pressure wave from the closed aortic valve during early diastole.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> The dicrotic notch is the single most clinically significant fiducial point on the PPG waveform after the systolic peak. It separates the systolic ejection phase from the diastolic runoff phase and is the physiological counterpart of the *incisura* seen on the central aortic pressure waveform.

### Intuition (Real-World Analogy)
Imagine pouring water into a long, soft, elastic garden hose from a tap. When you suddenly shut the tap, a small back-bounce (ripple) travels back down the hose. The heart does exactly this when the aortic valve snaps shut after pushing blood into the aorta. That back-bounce, when sensed at the fingertip, is the **dicrotic notch**. Detecting it accurately is like catching that tiny ripple with a high-resolution microphone at the far end of the hose.

### Why Detect the Dicrotic Notch?
1. **Stiffness Index (SI)** calculation: $\text{SI} = \frac{\text{Height of subject}}{\text{Time between systolic peak and dicrotic notch}}$
2. **Reflection Index (RI)**: Quantifies peripheral vascular tone.
3. **Cardiac output and arterial compliance** estimation.
4. **Detection of vascular pathologies** (atherosclerosis, hypertension, aging arteries).

Standard metrics used in KTU problems:
- **Systolic amplitude (S)** in arbitrary units (a.u.)
- **Dicrotic notch amplitude (D)** in **a.u.**
- **Reflection Index (RI) = (D/S) × 100 %**

---

## 1.2 Heart Sound Detection (Phonocardiography)

### Formal Definition
**Heart Sound Detection** is the process of identifying, segmenting, and classifying the acoustic vibrations produced by the mechanical activity of the heart, recorded as a **Phonocardiogram (PCG)**. The standard four heart sounds are **S1, S2, S3, and S4**, occurring in the frequency range of **20 Hz to 2000 Hz**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Points**
> - **S1**: Closure of mitral and tricuspid valves (lub) — onset of systole.
> - **S2**: Closure of aortic and pulmonary valves (dub) — onset of diastole.
> - **S3**: Rapid ventricular filling (vibration of ventricular wall), heard in heart failure.
> - **S4**: Atrial contraction against a stiff ventricle, heard in hypertension/ischemia.

### Intuition (Analogy)
If the heart is a mechanical water-pump, each beat is one pump cycle. The valves are little hinged doors; the *clack* of the doors closing is what the stethoscope hears. S1 is the inlet doors closing and S2 is the outlet doors closing. Detecting these sounds automatically is like building software that listens to a clock and identifies each *tick* and *tock* reliably, even in a noisy room.

### Detection Challenges
- **Low SNR** (heart sounds masked by lung, muscle, ambient noise).
- **Murmurs, clicks, snaps** as superimposed pathological events.
- **Frequency overlap** between S1, S2, murmurs, and noise.

---

## 1.3 EEG Rhythm Detection

### Formal Definition
**Electroencephalography (EEG)** measures the electrical activity of the brain via scalp electrodes. The signal is decomposed into five canonical **rhythmic bands**, each linked to specific physiological/cognitive states.

| Rhythm | Frequency Band | Amplitude | Associated State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 Hz | High (>75 µV) | Deep sleep (N3), infants |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 Hz | Medium | Drowsiness, meditation, memory |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 Hz | Medium | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 Hz | Low | Active thinking, alertness, anxiety |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 Hz | Very low | Sensory binding, cognition |

### Intuition (Analogy)
Think of the brain as an orchestra with five instrument sections (delta, theta, alpha, beta, gamma). At any given moment, different sections dominate — a sleeping brain plays heavy double-bass (delta), an alert brain plays violins (beta). **Rhythm detection** is the act of telling, at each instant, which instrument section is loudest, and tracking the conductor's cue changes over time.

---

## 1.4 Frequency Domain Characterisation — Power Spectral Density (PSD)

### Formal Definition
The **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** of a biomedical signal $x(n)$ describes how its signal power is distributed across frequencies. For a **Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS)** random process, the PSD is the **Fourier Transform of the autocorrelation sequence** (Wiener–Khinchin theorem).

### Intuition (Analogy)
Imagine spreading white light through a prism — it splits into a rainbow telling you *how much* of each colour is present. A prism for signals is the **Fourier Transform**, and the **PSD** is the *intensity of each colour band*. For an EEG signal, this tells you the precise "rainbow signature" of brain activity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (must memorise verbatim)**
> *"The Power Spectral Density $S_{xx}(f)$ of a discrete-time WSS random process $x(n)$ is the discrete-time Fourier transform of its autocorrelation function $r_{xx}(k)$."*
>
> $$S_{xx}(f) = \mathcal{F}\{ r_{xx}(k) \} = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j2\pi f k}$$

---

## 1.5 Waveform Complexity — Normal vs Ectopic Beat

### Formal Definition
A **Normal Beat** is a QRS complex (in ECG) or PPG pulse originating from the **sinoatrial (SA) node** following the standard cardiac conduction pathway. An **Ectopic Beat** originates outside the SA node — classified as **Premature Ventricular Contractions (PVC)**, **Premature Atrial Contractions (PAC)**, etc. — and shows a distinctly different **waveform shape and complexity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Waveform Complexity** refers to a quantitative measure of how "irregular", "non-repeating", or "multi-peaked" a waveform is. Common metrics in KTU syllabus are:
> - **Number of zero crossings**,
> - **Turning points / extrema count**,
> - **Non-linear energy (NLE)** or **K-complexity**,
> - **Template correlation coefficient (CC)** with a reference normal beat.

### Intuition
If a normal heartbeat is a perfectly rhythmic drum-stroke (`THUD…THUD…THUD`), an ectopic beat is a misplaced snare hit (`THUD…thd-THUMP…THUD`). The complexity measure is the algorithmic way of detecting that *thd-THUMP* automatically.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PPG Dicrotic Notch — Detection Pipeline

### 2.1.1 PPG Waveform Anatomy
A clean PPG pulse contains:
- **Systolic upstroke (a-b)**: Rapid rise due to ventricular ejection.
- **Systolic peak (b)**: Maximum amplitude.
- **Dicrotic notch (c)**: Small dip from reflected pressure wave.
- **Diastolic decay (c-d)**: Slow exponential decay.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Detection Logic

1. **Preprocessing**: Bandpass filter (0.5 – 15 Hz) to remove baseline wander and high-frequency motion artefact.
2. **Peak detection**: Locate systolic maxima via first-derivative zero-crossing (positive-to-negative).
3. **Search window**: Within the descending limb, look in the interval $[\,t_{\text{peak}} + 0.1\,\text{s},\ t_{\text{peak}} + 0.5\,\text{s}\,]$.
4. **Second-derivative test**: The dicrotic notch is a *local minimum* on the PPG curve, identifiable as a *zero-crossing of $\frac{d^2x}{dt^2}$* from positive to negative.
5. **Second Derivative Photoplethysmography (SDPPG)** accelerates the notch visibility using features **b, c, d, e, f** (Takazawa nomenclature).

### 2.1.3 SDPPG Feature Nomenclature

| SDPPG Point | Physiological Meaning |
|---|---|
| **a** | Start of systolic upstroke |
| **b** | Early systolic peak |
| **c** | Late systolic (dicrotic notch) |
| **d** | Diastolic peak |
| **e** | Late diastolic |
| **f** | End of diastolic |

Aging ratio, stiffness index, and augmentation index are derived from these features.

### 2.1.4 Wavelet-Based Notch Detection (Advanced)
**Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)** with Morlet mother wavelet isolates the notch because the notch has higher-frequency content than the slow diastolic decay.

$$C(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \psi^{*}\!\left(\frac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$$

Modulus maxima lines of the CWT converge at the notch location.

---

## 2.2 Heart Sound Detection — Algorithmic Layers

### 2.2.1 Time-Domain Methods
- **Envelope extraction**: Full-wave rectification → low-pass filter → smooth envelope.
- **Energy thresholding**: Compute short-time energy $E_n = \sum_{k} x^2(n-k)$. Peaks above adaptive threshold mark S1/S2.

### 2.2.2 Frequency-Domain (Spectral) Methods
- **Homomorphic filtering**: Separates the *multiplicative envelope* from the *oscillatory carrier*. Apply logarithm → DFT → high-frequency emphasis → IDFT → exponentiate.
- **Spectrogram (STFT)**: Visualises time-frequency content; S1/S2 appear as broadband bursts at 25 – 200 Hz with harmonics up to 1 kHz.

### 2.2.3 Modern Methods
- **Hilbert–Huang Transform (HHT)**: Empirical Mode Decomposition (EMD) yields Intrinsic Mode Functions (IMFs); S1/S2 are concentrated in IMFs 1–3.
- **Wavelet packet decomposition**: Daubechies-4 (db4) at level 4; detect S1/S2 by energy threshold on detail coefficients.
- **Deep learning** (CNN/LSTM): Out of scope for KTU 2024 written exam.

### 2.2.4 Reference: S1–S2 Duration Cues
- **Systolic interval (S1 → S2)**: ~ 0.3 s at 60 bpm.
- **Diastolic interval (S2 → next S1)**: ~ 0.5 – 0.7 s.
- The **systolic-to-diastolic ratio (S/D)** is the single most reliable feature for distinguishing S1 from S2.

---

## 2.3 EEG Rhythm Detection — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Detection via Bandpass Filtering
Apply five bandpass filters (one per rhythm) and compute the **instantaneous power** $P_i(t)$ in each band:

$$P_i(t) = \frac{1}{W}\int_{t-W/2}^{t+W/2} x^2(\tau)\, d\tau$$

Dominant rhythm at time $t$ = $\arg\max_i P_i(t)$.

### 2.3.2 Detection via PSD
After estimating $S_{xx}(f)$ by Welch's method, compute **band power**:

$$P_{\text{band}} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_{xx}(f)\, df$$

**Relative band power** (used in KTU) = $\frac{P_{\text{band}}}{P_{\text{total}}}$.

### 2.3.3 Detection via Time-Frequency (Wavelets)
For non-stationary EEG, STFT or wavelet scalograms are required. STFT is given by:

$$X(\tau, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, w(t-\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall**
> Don't confuse **Rhythm Detection** (which rhythm is *present*) with **Rhythm Classification** (labelling a window as alpha or beta). Detection is a *presence test*; classification requires a decision rule.

---

## 2.4 PSD Estimation — Detailed Theory

### 2.4.1 Non-Parametric Methods

#### (a) Periodogram
$$P_{\text{per}}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\left|\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}\right|^{2}$$

Drawback: High variance, does not decrease with $N$.

#### (b) Bartlett's Method
Divide $x(n)$ into $K$ non-overlapping segments of length $L$, compute periodogram of each, average:

$$P_{\text{Bart}}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=1}^{K} P_{i}(f)$$

Variance reduced by factor $K$; resolution reduced by factor $K$.

#### (c) Welch's Method (Modified Periodogram)
Segments may overlap (typically 50 %); each segment is windowed by $w(n)$:

$$P_{\text{Welch}}(f) = \frac{1}{K \cdot U}\sum_{i=1}^{K}\left|\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i(n)\, w(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}\right|^{2}$$

where $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^2(n)$ is the window energy normalisation.

#### (d) Blackman–Tukey (Correlogram)
Estimate autocorrelation $\hat{r}_{xx}(k)$ for lags $\vert k \vert \le M$, then:

$$P_{\text{BT}}(f) = \sum_{k=-M}^{M} \hat{r}_{xx}(k)\, w(k)\, e^{-j2\pi f k}$$

### 2.4.2 Parametric Methods (Model-Based)

Assume $x(n)$ is the output of an all-pole (AR), all-zero (MA), or pole-zero (ARMA) filter driven by white noise $w(n)$ of variance $\sigma_w^2$.

#### Autoregressive (AR) Model
$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, x(n-k) + w(n)$$

PSD of AR process:

$$P_{\text{AR}}(f) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, e^{-j2\pi f k}\right\vert^{2}}$$

Coefficients $a_k$ estimated by:
- **Yule–Walker** (autocorrelation method).
- **Burg's algorithm** (lattice-based, lower variance).
- **Modified Covariance**.

### 2.4.3 Comparison Table (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

| Method | Resolution | Variance | Bias | Data Length | Stationarity Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Periodogram | High | High | Low | Long | Strict |
| Bartlett | Medium | Low | Medium | Long | Segmental |
| Welch | Medium–High | Low | Medium | Short OK | Segmental |
| Blackman–Tukey | Medium | Low | Medium | Medium | Strict |
| AR (parametric) | High | Low | Low | Short | Strong (model order) |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU board exam, default answer** for PSD of a short, quasi-stationary biomedical record (EEG, HRV, heart sound) is **Welch's method with Hann window and 50 % overlap**.

---

## 2.5 Waveform Complexity — Normal vs Ectopic Beat

### 2.5.1 Morphological Features
- **QRS width**: Normal 80 – 120 ms; PVC > 120 ms.
- **R-peak amplitude**: PVC often shows tall, bizarre QRS.
- **Number of peaks/valleys in the beat waveform**.

### 2.5.2 Complexity Measures

1. **Turning-point count (TPC)**:
$$TPC = \sum_{n=1}^{N-1} \mathbb{1}\!\left[\, (x(n)-x(n-1))(x(n+1)-x(n)) < 0\,\right]$$

2. **Zero-crossing rate (ZCR)**:
$$ZCR = \frac{1}{2(N-1)}\sum_{n=1}^{N-1}\left\vert\,\text{sign}(x(n)) - \text{sign}(x(n-1))\,\right\vert$$

3. **Non-linear Energy (NLE)**:
$$\Psi(n) = x^2(n) - x(n+1)\, x(n-1)$$

4. **Template Correlation Coefficient (CC)**:
$$CC = \frac{\sum_{n} (x(n) - \bar{x})(t(n) - \bar{t})}{\sqrt{\sum_n (x(n)-\bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum_n (t(n)-\bar{t})^2}}$$

5. **Threshold for ectopy**: $CC < 0.85$ vs the running normal template ⇒ ectopic.

### 2.5.3 Decision Rule (Exam-Level)
$$\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Normal beat} & \text{if } CC \ge \tau \text{ AND } \text{width} \le 120\,\text{ms} \\ \text{Ectopic beat} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

where $\tau \approx 0.85$ to $0.95$ is the template threshold.

### 2.5.4 Real-World Utility
- **Holter monitors** (24 h ECG): millions of beats/day — automated PVC counters save hours of manual work.
- **Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillators (ICD)**: use complexity/VF detection to decide on shock.
- **Sleep studies (Polysomnography)**: detect ectopic events affecting heart rate variability (HRV).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reflection Index | $RI = (D/S) \times 100$ | % |
| 2 | Stiffness Index | $SI = h / \Delta T_{SD}$ | m/s |
| 3 | SDPPG Aging Index | $AI = (b-c-d-e)/a$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | CWT Coefficient | $C(a,b) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\int x(t)\psi^{*}(\frac{t-b}{a})dt$ | scalogram |
| 5 | STFT | $X(\tau,f)=\int x(t)w(t-\tau)e^{-j2\pi ft}dt$ | TF plane |
| 6 | PSD (Wiener–Khinchin) | $S_{xx}(f) = \sum_k r_{xx}(k) e^{-j2\pi f k}$ | WSS required |
| 7 | Periodogram | $P_{\text{per}}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\vert X_N(f)\vert^{2}$ | biased but consistent estimator of $S_{xx}(f)$? NO — high variance |
| 8 | Welch | $P_{\text{Welch}}(f) = \frac{1}{KU}\sum_i \vert X_i^w(f)\vert^{2}$ | preferred for EEG/HRV |
| 9 | Bartlett | average of $K$ non-overlapping periodograms | variance $\propto 1/K$ |
| 10 | AR-PSD | $P_{\text{AR}}(f) = \sigma_w^{2} \big/ \vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi fk}\vert^{2}$ | $p$ = model order |
| 11 | Band Power | $P_{\text{band}} = \int_{f_1}^{f_2} S_{xx}(f) df$ | absolute power |
| 12 | Relative Band Power | $RBP = P_{\text{band}} / P_{\text{total}}$ | dimensionless |
| 13 | Template CC | $CC = \frac{\sum(x-\bar x)(t-\bar t)}{\sqrt{\sum(x-\bar x)^2 \sum(t-\bar t)^2}}$ | $\in [-1,1]$ |
| 14 | ZCR | $ZCR = \frac{1}{2(N-1)}\sum \vert\text{sign change}\vert$ | complexity proxy |
| 15 | Turning Point Count | $TPC = \sum \mathbb{1}[(x_n-x_{n-1})(x_{n+1}-x_n)<0]$ | complexity proxy |
| 16 | Non-Linear Energy | $\Psi(n) = x^{2}(n) - x(n+1)x(n-1)$ | sensitive to high-frequency events |

> [!NOTE]
> In all formulas above, $\vert \cdot \vert$ denotes magnitude; use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value without breaking the markdown table.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PSD via Autocorrelation (Wiener–Khinchin)

### Statement
For a Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) discrete random process $x(n)$ with autocorrelation $r_{xx}(k) = E[x(n)x(n+k)]$, the PSD is:

$$S_{xx}(f) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j2\pi f k}$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation
Let $X_N(f)$ be the DTFT of the truncated signal $x_N(n) = x(n) \cdot w_N(n)$, where $w_N(n)=1$ for $0 \le n \le N-1$ and $0$ otherwise.

$$
X_N(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}
$$

**Step 1: Periodogram definition**
$$P_N(f) = \frac{1}{N} \vert X_N(f) \vert^{2}$$

**Step 2: Expected value (taking the expectation)**
$$
E[\,P_N(f)\,] = \frac{1}{N}\, E\!\left[\,X_N(f)\, X_N^{*}(f)\,\right]
$$

**Step 3: Expand the product**
$$
E[\,P_N(f)\,] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} E[x(m)x(n)]\, e^{-j2\pi f (m-n)}
$$

**Step 4: Recognise $E[x(m)x(n)] = r_{xx}(m-n)$ by WSS**
$$
E[\,P_N(f)\,] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1}\sum_{n=0}^{N-1} r_{xx}(m-n)\, e^{-j2\pi f (m-n)}
$$

**Step 5: Change of variables $k = m-n$, count diagonals**
For each lag $k$, the number of $(m,n)$ pairs with $m-n=k$ inside the $N \times N$ block is $(N - \vert k \vert)$:
$$
E[\,P_N(f)\,] = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=-(N-1)}^{N-1} (N - \vert k \vert)\, r_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j2\pi f k}
$$

**Step 6: Take the limit as $N \to \infty$**
The triangular window $(N - \vert k \vert)/N$ tends to $1$ for every fixed $k$ (the Bartlett window):
$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} E[\,P_N(f)\,] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j2\pi f k}
$$

**Step 7: Identify as DTFT of autocorrelation**
$$
\boxed{\,S_{xx}(f) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j2\pi f k}\,}
$$

**Interpretation**: The PSD is the DTFT of the autocorrelation. This is the **Wiener–Khinchin theorem** for discrete WSS processes — a board-exam favourite.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Welch's Method Variance Reduction

### Setup
Welch divides $x(n)$ of length $N$ into $K$ overlapping segments of length $L$ with $D$ samples shift, windowed by $w(n)$. The estimator is:

$$P_{\text{Welch}}(f) = \frac{1}{K\,U}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i(n)\, w(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n} \right\vert^{2}$$

**Window energy**: $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^{2}(n)$.

### Step-by-Step Variance Calculation
**Step 1: Periodogram of one windowed segment**
Let $X_i(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i(n) w(n) e^{-j2\pi f n}$. Then the $i$-th periodogram is $P_i(f) = \frac{1}{L\,U} \vert X_i(f)\vert^{2}$.

**Step 2: Mean of one periodogram**
The expected value of a single periodogram (as derived in §3.1) is the convolution of $S_{xx}(f)$ with the squared window's DTFT:
$$
E[\,P_i(f)\,] = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} S_{xx}(\nu)\, W_W(f-\nu)\, d\nu
$$
where $W_W(f) = \frac{1}{L\,U} \left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}\right\vert^{2}$ is the normalised spectral window. As $L$ grows, $W_W \to \delta(f)$, so the bias vanishes — Welch is asymptotically unbiased.

**Step 3: Variance of the periodogram** (assumed independent segments)
For a Gaussian signal, $\text{Var}[\,P_i(f)\,] \approx S_{xx}^{2}(f)$. With $K$ independent segments:
$$
\text{Var}[\,P_{\text{Welch}}(f)\,] \approx \frac{1}{K}\, S_{xx}^{2}(f)
$$

**Step 4: Resolution vs variance trade-off**
Increasing $K$ reduces variance by $1/K$ but reduces $L$ (keeping $N$ fixed) and hence reduces frequency resolution $\Delta f \approx 1/L$.

**Final box**:
$$
\boxed{\,\text{Var}[\,P_{\text{Welch}}(f)\,] \approx \frac{1}{K}\, S_{xx}^{2}(f), \quad \Delta f \approx \frac{1}{L}\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — PSD via Welch on Synthetic EEG

### Problem (KTU-style 14-mark sub-question)
A 4-second EEG segment is sampled at $F_s = 200$ Hz, giving $N = 800$ samples. Compute the **Welch PSD** using a Hann window of length $L = 200$ samples with 50 % overlap. State the number of segments $K$, the frequency resolution, and the expected variance reduction factor.

### Solution

**Step 1: Number of samples per segment**
$L = 200$ samples.

**Step 2: Hop size with 50 % overlap**
$D = L/2 = 100$ samples.

**Step 3: Number of segments**
$$
K = \left\lfloor \frac{N - L}{D} \right\rfloor + 1 = \left\lfloor \frac{800 - 200}{100} \right\rfloor + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6
$$

**Step 4: Frequency resolution**
$$
\Delta f = \frac{F_s}{L} = \frac{200}{200} = 1\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 5: Variance reduction (vs periodogram)**
$$
\text{Variance factor} = \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667
$$
i.e., variance reduced by **6×** relative to a single periodogram.

**Step 6: Window energy normalisation (Hann)**
For a Hann window $w(n) = 0.5 - 0.5 \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi n}{L-1}\right)$:
$$
U = \frac{1}{L}\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^{2}(n) \approx 0.375
$$

**Step 7: Final estimator form**
$$
P_{\text{Welch}}(f) = \frac{1}{6 \times 0.375}\sum_{i=0}^{5}\left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{199} x_i(n)\, w(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}\right\vert^{2}
$$
$$
P_{\text{Welch}}(f) = \frac{1}{2.25}\sum_{i=0}^{5}\left\vert \sum_{n=0}^{199} x_i(n)\, w(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}\right\vert^{2}
$$

**Answer box**:
- $K = 6$ segments
- $\Delta f = 1$ Hz
- Variance reduction = **6×**

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Ectopic Beat Detection via Template Correlation

### Problem
A normal QRS template $t(n) = [0, 1, 5, 8, 5, 1, 0]$ (7 samples) is stored. An incoming beat $x(n) = [0, 1, 4, 7, 4, 1, 0]$ is received. Compute the **template correlation coefficient (CC)** and decide if it is normal or ectopic (threshold $\tau = 0.9$).

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute means**
$$
\bar{t} = \frac{0+1+5+8+5+1+0}{7} = \frac{20}{7} \approx 2.857
$$
$$
\bar{x} = \frac{0+1+4+7+4+1+0}{7} = \frac{17}{7} \approx 2.429
$$

**Step 2: Compute centred vectors**
$$
t_c = [-2.857, -1.857, 2.143, 5.143, 2.143, -1.857, -2.857]
$$
$$
x_c = [-2.429, -1.429, 1.571, 4.571, 1.571, -1.429, -2.429]
$$

**Step 3: Numerator** $\sum t_c(n) x_c(n)$
$$
(2.857)(2.429) + (1.857)(1.429) + (2.143)(1.571) + (5.143)(4.571) + (2.143)(1.571) + (1.857)(1.429) + (2.857)(2.429)
$$

Compute each product:
- $(2.857)(2.429) = 6.9397$
- $(1.857)(1.429) = 2.6535$
- $(2.143)(1.571) = 3.3666$
- $(5.143)(4.571) = 23.5086$
- $(2.143)(1.571) = 3.3666$
- $(1.857)(1.429) = 2.6535$
- $(2.857)(2.429) = 6.9397$

Sum = $6.9397 + 2.6535 + 3.3666 + 23.5086 + 3.3666 + 2.6535 + 6.9397 = 49.4282$

**Step 4: Denominator**
$\sum t_c^2(n)$:
- $8.1604 + 3.4484 + 4.5924 + 26.4504 + 4.5924 + 3.4484 + 8.1604 = 58.8528$

$\sum x_c^2(n)$:
- $5.9000 + 2.0420 + 2.4680 + 20.8940 + 2.4680 + 2.0420 + 5.9000 = 41.7140$

Denominator = $\sqrt{58.8528 \times 41.7140} = \sqrt{2455.20} = 49.55$

**Step 5: CC**
$$
CC = \frac{49.4282}{49.55} \approx 0.9975
$$

**Step 6: Decision**
$CC = 0.9975 \ge \tau = 0.9$, so the beat is classified **NORMAL**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection for Each Subtopic

| Subtopic | Recommended Primary Method | Backup Method | Key Diagnostic Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dicrotic Notch (PPG)** | Second-Derivative (SDPPG) with feature points a–f | CWT (Morlet) modulus maxima | Reflection Index RI |
| **Heart Sound S1/S2** | Homomorphic envelope + S/D ratio test | DWT (db4) energy thresholding | Systolic/Diastolic interval |
| **EEG Rhythm** | Bandpass filter bank + power ratio | Welch PSD + relative band power | Dominant rhythm label |
| **PSD Estimation** | Welch's method (Hann, 50% overlap) | AR (Burg, order 8–16) | $S_{xx}(f)$ plot |
| **Ectopic vs Normal** | Template Correlation (CC) | Non-linear Energy $\Psi(n)$ | Boolean: ectopic? |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Universal Biomedical Event Detection Pipeline

This single block-level architecture covers dicrotic notch detection, heart sound S1/S2 segmentation, EEG rhythm classification, and ectopic beat detection. It is the canonical "KTU-style" block diagram for any Module-4 detection question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rawSignal[Raw Biomedical Signal PPG PCG EEG ECG] --> prefilter[Preprocessing Bandpass Filter 0.5 to 15 Hz for PPG 20 to 2000 Hz for PCG 0.5 to 70 Hz for EEG]
    prefilter --> denoise[Noise Reduction MA Filter or Wavelet Denoising]
    denoise --> feature1[Feature Extraction Stage 1 Time Domain Envelope Peaks Zero Crossings]
    denoise --> feature2[Feature Extraction Stage 2 Frequency Domain Welch PSD STFT Spectrogram]
    denoise --> feature3[Feature Extraction Stage 3 Time Frequency Wavelet CWT DWT Scalogram]
    feature1 --> fusion[Feature Fusion and Normalisation]
    feature2 --> fusion
    feature3 --> fusion
    fusion --> decision[Decision Engine Threshold Rule or Classifier]
    decision --> output1[Fiducial Points Systolic Peak Dicrotic Notch S1 S2]
    decision --> output2[Rhythm Labels Delta Theta Alpha Beta Gamma]
    decision --> output3[Beat Labels Normal Ectopic PVC PAC]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — PSD Estimation Workflow (Non-Parametric)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sig[Input Signal x of n Length N] --> seg[Segmentation K Segments Length L]
    seg --> win[Windowing Hann or Hamming]
    win --> fft[FFT of Each Segment Length L]
    fft --> mag[Magnitude Squared of FFT]
    mag --> avg[Arithmetic Average over K Segments]
    avg --> norm[Normalise by Window Energy U]
    norm --> out[Welch PSD Estimate P Welch of f]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Heart Sound S1/S2 Detection via Homomorphic Filtering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    pcg[Phonocardiogram PCG Signal] --> rect[Full Wave Rectification]
    rect --> log[Natural Logarithm]
    log --> dft[Discrete Fourier Transform]
    dft --> lifilt[High Pass Lifter Remove Low Frequency Components]
    lifilt --> idft[Inverse DFT]
    idft --> exp[Exponential Operator]
    exp --> env[Recovered Envelope]
    env --> thr[Adaptive Energy Threshold]
    thr --> seg[S1 S2 Labelling Using S1 S2 Duration Ratio]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Ectopic Beat Detection via Template Matching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ecg[ECG Signal x of n] --> rdet[R Peak Detection Pan Tompkins]
    rdet --> seg[Beat Segmentation Window of 200 ms around R Peak]
    seg --> align[Beat Alignment to Fixed Length]
    align --> cc[Template Correlation Coefficient CC with Normal Template]
    cc --> comp{CC greater than or equal to Threshold tau}
    comp --> yes[Label as Normal Beat]
    comp --> no[Label as Ectopic Beat PVC PAC]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram — EEG Rhythm Detection via Bandpass Filter Bank

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    eeg[Raw EEG Signal] --> bp1[Bandpass 0.5 to 4 Hz Delta]
    eeg --> bp2[Bandpass 4 to 8 Hz Theta]
    eeg --> bp3[Bandpass 8 to 13 Hz Alpha]
    eeg --> bp4[Bandpass 13 to 30 Hz Beta]
    eeg --> bp5[Bandpass 30 to 100 Hz Gamma]
    bp1 --> pwr1[Instantaneous Power P Delta]
    bp2 --> pwr2[Instantaneous Power P Theta]
    bp3 --> pwr3[Instantaneous Power P Alpha]
    bp4 --> pwr4[Instantaneous Power P Beta]
    bp5 --> pwr5[Instantaneous Power P Gamma]
    pwr1 --> mx[Argmax Decision]
    pwr2 --> mx
    pwr3 --> mx
    pwr4 --> mx
    pwr5 --> mx
    mx --> label[Output Dominant Rhythm Label]
```

## 4.6 Schematic — PPG Dicrotic Notch Anatomy and SDPPG Features

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ppg[PPG Pulse Waveform] --> pa[Systolic Upstroke a to b]
    ppg --> pb[Systolic Peak b]
    ppg --> pc[Dicrotic Notch c]
    ppg --> pd[Diastolic Peak d]
    ppg --> pe[Late Diastolic e]
    ppg --> pf[End of Diastolic f]
    pa --> ri[Compute Reflection Index RI = D divided by S times 100]
    pb --> si[Compute Stiffness Index SI = Height divided by Delta T]
    pc --> ai[Compute Augmentation Index AI = b minus c minus d minus e divided by a]
```

> For KTU board exams, the most commonly requested diagram is the **Universal Detection Pipeline (Section 4.1)** plus the **PPG SDPPG feature map (Section 4.6)**. Memorise these two for full marks on diagram questions.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Power Spectral Density (PSD). State the Wiener–Khinchin theorem for a WSS discrete-time random process.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** PSD $S_{xx}(f)$ is a function that describes how the power of a signal is distributed with respect to frequency. It has units of power per Hz.
- **Wiener–Khinchin (2 marks):** For a WSS discrete-time process $x(n)$ with autocorrelation $r_{xx}(k) = E[x(n)x(n+k)]$, the PSD is the discrete-time Fourier transform of $r_{xx}(k)$:
$$
S_{xx}(f) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} r_{xx}(k)\, e^{-j2\pi f k}
$$
with inverse: $r_{xx}(k) = \int_{-1/2}^{1/2} S_{xx}(f)\, e^{j2\pi f k}\, df$.

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a dicrotic notch in a PPG signal? Mention two clinical parameters derived from it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The dicrotic notch is a small secondary dip on the descending limb of the PPG pulse, caused by the reflection of the aortic pressure wave from the closed aortic valve during early diastole. It separates systolic ejection from diastolic runoff.
- **Two parameters (1 mark):** (i) Reflection Index (RI) = (D/S) × 100 %, (ii) Stiffness Index (SI) = body height / time interval between systolic peak and dicrotic notch.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (ESE — Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 — Option A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the detection of the dicrotic notch in a PPG signal using Second-Derivative Photoplethysmography (SDPPG). Sketch and label the SDPPG feature points a, b, c, d, e, f. Derive the formula for **Reflection Index (RI)** and **Stiffness Index (SI)**.

**(b) [7 marks]** With a block diagram, describe the Welch method of PSD estimation. Derive an expression for the variance of the Welch estimator and show how it is reduced by a factor of $K$ compared to the periodogram.

---

#### Solution to B1(a) — Dicrotic Notch via SDPPG

**Step 1: PPG basics (1 mark)**
PPG measures blood-volume changes optically. The PPG pulse has a systolic upstroke, a peak, a dicrotic notch, and a slow diastolic decay.

**Step 2: Why second derivative? (1 mark)**
The dicrotic notch is a *small inflection* often invisible on raw PPG. Taking the second derivative $\frac{d^2 x(t)}{dt^2}$ converts the shallow inflection into distinct zero-crossings, allowing robust detection. This is the SDPPG technique introduced by Takazawa et al.

**Step 3: SDPPG features (3 marks)**
- **a** — start of systolic upstroke (initial positive zero-crossing).
- **b** — early systolic positive peak.
- **c** — late systolic (dicrotic notch) negative peak.
- **d** — early diastolic positive peak.
- **e** — late diastolic re-crossing.
- **f** — end of diastolic valley.

A typical SDPPG feature string is therefore: `+a, +b, –c, +d, –e, +f`.

**Step 4: Formulas (2 marks)**
Reflection Index (normalised):

$$
RI = \frac{b - c}{b} \times 100\,\%
$$

(Or amplitude-based: $RI = D/S \times 100$, where $D$ is dicrotic notch amplitude and $S$ is systolic amplitude.)

Stiffness Index:

$$
SI = \frac{h}{\Delta T} \quad [\text{m/s}]
$$

where $h$ is the subject's body height (m) and $\Delta T$ is the time delay (s) between the systolic peak and the dicrotic notch. This converts a temporal measurement into a propagation-velocity surrogate of pulse-wave velocity.

**Step 5: Sketch — text representation (1 mark, to be drawn on answer sheet)**
```
   PPG (raw)        SDPPG
   /\               +a    +b
  /  \              |    /\
 /    \  _c         |   /  \      –c
/      \/  \        |  /    \    /  \     +d
              \    | /      \  /    \   /  \
               \   |/        \/      \ /    \
                \  |                   –e
                 \ |                         +f
                  \|___________________________
```

**Clinical use:** $RI$ increases with arterial stiffness (age, hypertension); $SI$ correlates with carotid–femoral pulse wave velocity, the gold-standard measure of arterial stiffness.

---

#### Solution to B1(b) — Welch's Method of PSD Estimation

**Step 1: Block diagram (2 marks)** — see Section 4.2.

**Step 2: Procedure (2 marks)**
1. Divide $x(n)$, $0 \le n \le N-1$, into $K$ segments of length $L$ with $D$ samples hop.
2. Window each segment with $w(n)$ (Hann or Hamming).
3. Compute FFT of each windowed segment: $X_i(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i(n) w(n) e^{-j2\pi f n}$.
4. Compute modified periodogram: $P_i(f) = \frac{1}{L\,U} \vert X_i(f) \vert^{2}$ where $U = \frac{1}{L}\sum w^{2}(n)$.
5. Average across all $K$ segments: $P_{\text{Welch}}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1} P_i(f)$.

**Step 3: Variance derivation (2 marks)**
Assuming segments are approximately independent (valid for 50 % overlap with Hann window), and for Gaussian $x(n)$:
$$
\text{Var}[\,P_i(f)\,] \approx S_{xx}^{2}(f)
$$
Averaging $K$ such periodograms:
$$
\text{Var}[\,P_{\text{Welch}}(f)\,] = \frac{1}{K^{2}}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \text{Var}[\,P_i(f)\,] = \frac{1}{K} S_{xx}^{2}(f)
$$

**Step 4: Resolution** (1 mark)
Frequency resolution:
$$
\Delta f = \frac{F_s}{L}
$$
Welch trades resolution for variance reduction: more segments ($K$ large, $L$ small) ⇒ better variance, worse resolution.

**Conclusion box** (no separate mark, implicit):
$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{Welch}}(f) = \frac{1}{K\,U}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1}\left\vert\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} x_i(n) w(n) e^{-j2\pi f n}\right\vert^{2}, \quad \text{Var}\propto \frac{1}{K}\,}
$$

---

### Question B1 — Option B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the method for detection of heart sounds (S1 and S2) from a Phonocardiogram (PCG) signal. With a block diagram, explain homomorphic filtering for envelope extraction. How is the systolic-to-diastolic (S/D) duration ratio used to distinguish S1 from S2?

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain waveform complexity analysis for detecting ectopic beats in ECG. Derive the template correlation coefficient and state the decision rule. Mention two other complexity measures.

---

#### Solution to B1-Option-B(a) — Heart Sound Detection

**Step 1: PCG characteristics (1 mark)**
PCG is a non-stationary signal with heart sounds S1, S2 (and possibly S3, S4, murmurs) in 20–2000 Hz, contaminated by lung and ambient noise.

**Step 2: Homomorphic filtering — block diagram (2 marks)** — see Section 4.3.

**Step 3: Theory of homomorphic filtering (2 marks)**
PCG is modelled as $x(n) = m(n) \cdot c(n)$ where $m(n)$ is the slowly varying envelope and $c(n)$ is the high-frequency oscillatory component. Taking the logarithm:
$$
y(n) = \ln x(n) = \ln m(n) + \ln c(n)
$$
DFT: $Y(f) = M_{\ln}(f) + C_{\ln}(f)$. The envelope log-spectrum is concentrated at low frequencies, the carrier log-spectrum at high frequencies. Apply a high-pass lifter $l(f)$ to retain only the carrier: $Y'(f) = l(f) \cdot Y(f)$. IDFT → exponentiate → recover envelope.

**Step 4: S1 vs S2 by S/D ratio (2 marks)**
- **Systolic interval** = time from S1 to S2.
- **Diastolic interval** = time from S2 to next S1.
- For heart rate ~ 60–80 bpm, **S1 → S2 ≪ S2 → S1**.
- Compute instantaneous heart rate from R-peaks of synchronously recorded ECG. For each detected envelope peak, classify as **S1** if it occurs within ~ 0.05 s after R-peak, else **S2** (or vice versa if the recording is reversed).

**Algorithm summary** (no separate mark):
1. Pre-filter PCG (bandpass 25–400 Hz for primary sounds).
2. Apply homomorphic envelope extraction.
3. Detect envelope peaks.
4. Classify using S/D interval ratio or ECG synchronisation.

---

#### Solution to B1-Option-B(b) — Ectopic Beat Detection

**Step 1: What is an ectopic beat? (1 mark)**
An ectopic beat is a heartbeat originating outside the SA node (PVC, PAC, junctional). On ECG, it shows abnormal morphology — wide QRS, no preceding P wave, or inverted P wave.

**Step 2: Template correlation derivation (3 marks)**
Let $t(n)$ be a reference normal QRS template of length $L$ and $x(n)$ be the candidate beat. The cross-correlation at zero lag equals the inner product of centred vectors:
$$
\hat{r}_{xt} = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} (x(n) - \bar{x})(t(n) - \bar{t})
$$
Normalised by the auto-correlations:
$$
\hat{r}_{xx} = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} (x(n) - \bar{x})^{2}, \quad \hat{r}_{tt} = \sum_{n=0}^{L-1} (t(n) - \bar{t})^{2}
$$
Template correlation coefficient:
$$
\boxed{\,CC = \frac{\hat{r}_{xt}}{\sqrt{\hat{r}_{xx}\, \hat{r}_{tt}}}\,}
$$
$CC \in [-1, +1]$. $CC = +1$ means perfect match (normal); $CC \approx 0$ means uncorrelated (ectopic or noise); $CC = -1$ means inverted match.

**Step 3: Decision rule (1 mark)**
$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Normal} & \text{if } CC \ge \tau \\ \text{Ectopic} & \text{if } CC < \tau \end{cases}
$$
Typical $\tau \in [0.85, 0.95]$.

**Step 4: Two other complexity measures (2 marks)**
1. **Non-Linear Energy (NLE)**: $\Psi(n) = x^{2}(n) - x(n+1)x(n-1)$. Ectopic beats exhibit larger NLE because of their sharp, high-frequency content.
2. **Turning-Point Count (TPC)**: Counts sign changes of the first difference, indicating waveform "bumpiness". Ectopic beats have a higher TPC than normal beats.

**Bonus**: Zero-Crossing Rate (ZCR), maximum absolute amplitude, QRS width (> 120 ms) are also used as adjunct features.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places KTU examiners deduct marks on Module-4 detection questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "periodogram" with "PSD".** The periodogram is a *biased* estimator; it is **not** the PSD itself. Always say "periodogram estimate of the PSD".
> 2. **Forgetting the WSS assumption.** Wiener–Khinchin applies *only* to WSS processes. EEG is *non-stationary* — you must segment into short quasi-stationary windows before applying Welch.
> 3. **Wrong sign in second-derivative notation.** SDPPG feature **c** corresponds to a *negative peak* in the second derivative; if you write "positive peak" you lose 1 mark instantly.
> 4. **Mixing up S1 and S2.** S1 is at the *start of systole* (just after R-peak on ECG). S2 is at the *end of systole* (just after T-wave end). Saying "S1 follows S2" is the classic 2-mark killer.
> 5. **Forgetting the window-energy normalisation $U$ in Welch's formula.** A bare average of periodograms *without* $1/U$ is Bartlett's method, not Welch's. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### PPG & Dicrotic Notch
- PPG = optical blood-volume sensor; AC (pulsatile) + DC (baseline) components.
- **Dicrotic notch** = reflected wave from aortic valve closure on descending limb.
- Detection: **SDPPG** (preferred) or **CWT modulus maxima** (advanced).
- Clinical parameters: **RI = (D/S)×100 %**, **SI = h/ΔT** in m/s, **Aging Index = (b−c−d−e)/a**.
- SDPPG points: **a, +b, −c, +d, −e, +f** (signs in second derivative).

### Heart Sound Detection
- PCG frequency range: **20 Hz to 2 kHz**.
