- S1 = mitral + tricuspid closure (loudest, low pitch). S2 = aortic + pulmonary closure (sharp, higher pitch).
- Detection chain: Bandpass filter → Envelope extraction (homomorphic) → Energy thresholding → S1/S2 labelling via ECG synchronisation or **S/D duration ratio**.
- **S/D ≪ 1** at normal heart rate — S2-to-next-S1 is the longer diastolic gap.

### EEG Rhythm Detection
- **5 rhythms**: δ (0.5–4), θ (4–8), α (8–13), β (13–30), γ (>30) Hz.
- Detection: bandpass filter bank + power computation, OR Welch PSD + relative band power.
- Dominant rhythm = $\arg\max_i P_i(t)$.
- Alpha rhythm is the **eyes-closed, relaxed-wakefulness** hallmark.

### PSD Estimation
- **Wiener–Khinchin theorem**: $S_{xx}(f) = \sum r_{xx}(k) e^{-j2\pi f k}$ (requires WSS).
- **Periodogram**: $P_{\text{per}}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\vert X_N(f)\vert^{2}$ — high variance, not consistent.
- **Bartlett**: average of $K$ non-overlapping periodograms — variance $\propto 1/K$.
- **Welch**: windowed, 50 %-overlapping segments, normalised by $U$ — preferred default.
- **AR (parametric)**: $P_{\text{AR}}(f) = \sigma_w^{2} / \vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi f k}\vert^{2}$.
- Trade-off: **Resolution** $\Delta f = F_s/L$ vs **Variance** $\propto 1/K$.

### Waveform Complexity & Ectopic Beats
- Normal beat: QRS 80–120 ms, originates at SA node.
- Ectopic beat: PVC (wide, bizarre QRS), PAC (early, abnormal P), etc.
- **Template Correlation Coefficient (CC)** is the standard detection statistic.
- $CC \ge 0.85 \Rightarrow$ Normal, $CC < 0.85 \Rightarrow$ Ectopic (typical threshold).
- Complementary features: **ZCR, TPC, NLE, QRS width, R-peak amplitude**.

### Universal "Must-Recall" Equation Set
- Wiener–Khinchin: $S_{xx}(f) = \sum_k r_{xx}(k) e^{-j2\pi f k}$
- Periodogram: $P_{\text{per}}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\vert X_N(f)\vert^{2}$
- Welch: $P_{\text{Welch}}(f) = \frac{1}{K U}\sum_{i=1}^{K}\vert X_i^{w}(f)\vert^{2}$
- AR-PSD: $P_{\text{AR}}(f) = \sigma_w^{2} / \vert 1 + \sum a_k e^{-j2\pi f k}\vert^{2}$
- Reflection Index: $RI = (D/S) \times 100\,\%$
- Stiffness Index: $SI = h / \Delta T$ (m/s)
- Template CC: $CC = \sum (x-\bar x)(t-\bar t) / \sqrt{\sum (x-\bar x)^{2}\sum(t-\bar t)^{2}}$

### Cross-Cutting KTU 2024 Exam Tips
- Always state the **assumptions** (WSS, Gaussian, segment length, window choice).
- For Welch questions, default to **Hann window, 50 % overlap, L = 256 for EEG**.
- For S1/S2 questions, always **cross-check with ECG R-peak** (gold standard).
- For dicrotic notch, mention **Takazawa's SDPPG nomenclature** by name to score full marks.
- For ectopic detection, **running template update** (every N beats or after a confirmed normal) is the upgrade answer examiners love.
- Memorise the **PSD estimation trade-off triangle** (Resolution ↔ Variance ↔ Bias).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrhythmia Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Arrhythmia Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Arrhythmia Analysis** is the systematic application of digital signal processing (DSP) and pattern recognition techniques to the electrocardiogram (ECG) signal in order to detect, characterize, and classify deviations of the cardiac rhythm from the normal sinus rhythm (NSR). The goal is to extract clinically meaningful parameters — such as QRS complex location, R-peak amplitude, RR interval variability, and waveform morphology — that allow automated identification of cardiac abnormalities including **bradycardia**, **tachycardia**, **premature ventricular contractions (PVCs)**, **atrial fibrillation (AF)**, **ventricular fibrillation (VF)**, and **heart blocks**.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, arrhythmia analysis is treated as a multi-stage pipeline:

> **[!NOTE]**
> **KTU Definition Block**
> 1. **Pre-processing** → Removal of baseline wander, 50/60 Hz powerline interference, and EMG noise from raw ECG.
> 2. **Feature Extraction** → Detection of fiducial points (P, QRS, T) and computation of temporal / morphological / spectral features.
> 3. **Classification** → Mapping the feature vector into a diagnostic class (Normal, Abnormal, or specific arrhythmia type).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the heart as a **marching band** keeping a steady, well-timed beat — that's the *normal sinus rhythm*. The drum major (SA node) sets a tempo of 60–100 beats per minute, and every section follows it.

Now, arrhythmia is what happens when the band **falls out of sync**:
- The drum major slows down too much → **Bradycardia** (band plays at <60 bpm).
- The drum major speeds up frantically → **Tachycardia** (>100 bpm).
- A rogue percussionist (ectopic focus) fires off-beat → **PVC / PAC**.
- The whole band plays chaotic, irregular notes → **Atrial / Ventricular Fibrillation**.

**Arrhythmia Analysis** is the **sound engineer** with microphones, FFT analyzers, and beat-detection software, standing at the back of the field, *listening* to every musician, *measuring* the gaps between drum hits, and *flagging* anyone who is off-tempo. The "microphones" are surface ECG electrodes, the "FFT analyzer" is the DSP toolkit, and the "flag" is the diagnostic output.

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> **Why this matters clinically:** Cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) account for roughly **17.9 million deaths per year** globally (WHO). Automated arrhythmia detection enables early diagnosis, remote Holter monitoring, and ICU alarm systems — turning hours of recorded ECG into actionable information within seconds.

## 1.3 Standard Physiological Metrics (Bold Highlights)

The following metrics form the vocabulary of every arrhythmia analysis question in KTU exams:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Normal Value |
|---|---|---|
| Heart Rate | HR | **60 – 100 bpm** |
| RR Interval | RR | **0.6 – 1.0 s** |
| PR Interval | PR | **120 – 200 ms** |
| QRS Duration | QRS | **80 – 120 ms** |
| QT Interval (corrected) | QTc | **350 – 450 ms** |
| QRS Amplitude (R) | R | **0.5 – 2.0 mV** |

> **[!NOTE]**
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** Students must be able to (a) design a QRS detection pipeline, (b) compute RR-interval-based HRV metrics, and (c) explain the Pan-Tompkins algorithm block by block. These three competencies account for >70 % of Module 4 marks in KTU ESE.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ECG as the Input Signal

The surface ECG is a quasi-periodic, low-amplitude (≈ 1 mV) bioelectric signal with dominant spectral energy concentrated in the **0.05 – 40 Hz** band. Each cardiac cycle produces the classical **P-QRS-T** waveform:

- **P wave** — Atrial depolarization (low-frequency, low-amplitude).
- **QRS complex** — Ventricular depolarization (high-frequency spike, dominant R-peak).
- **T wave** — Ventricular repolarization (low-frequency, low-amplitude).

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> The **QRS complex** is the single most important feature for arrhythmia detection because it is the **highest-SNR event** in the ECG and its location defines the RR interval.

## 2.2 Types of Arrhythmias (KTU-classified)

| Class | Condition | HR / RR criterion | Waveform clue |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal Sinus Rhythm | NSR | 60–100 bpm, regular | Clean P, normal QRS |
| Bradycardia | Sinus Brady | <60 bpm | Long RR, normal morphology |
| Tachycardia | Sinus Tachy | >100 bpm | Short RR, normal morphology |
| Atrial Fibrillation | AF | Irregularly irregular | No P, irregular RR, fibrillatory baseline |
| Ventricular Tachycardia | VT | >100 bpm, wide | Wide QRS (>120 ms), no P |
| Ventricular Fibrillation | VF | Chaotic | No discrete QRS, sinusoidal |
| Premature Ventricular Contraction | PVC | Premature | Wide, bizarre QRS, no preceding P |
| Atrial Premature Beat | APB / PAC | Premature | Abnormal P, normal QRS |
| 1st Degree AV Block | AVB-I | — | Prolonged PR (>200 ms) |
| 2nd Degree AV Block | AVB-II | — | Dropped QRS (Mobitz I or II) |
| 3rd Degree AV Block | AVB-III | — | AV dissociation |

## 2.3 The Five-Stage QRS Detection Pipeline (Pan-Tompkins)

The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm (1985)** remains the gold-standard KTU-prescribed method for real-time QRS detection. It cascades five linear/non-linear stages:

1. **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz)** — Isolates QRS energy, suppresses baseline wander and high-frequency muscle noise.
2. **Derivative Operator** — Emphasizes the steep slopes of the R-wave upstroke and downstroke.
3. **Squaring** — Makes the output strictly non-negative and amplifies large slopes (non-linear emphasis).
4. **Moving-Window Integration (MWI)** — Smooths the squared signal and produces a single pulse per QRS.
5. **Adaptive Thresholding** — Dynamically sets a detection level using signal and noise peak estimates.

The detection logic is:
> If MWI output crosses the adaptive threshold AND a refractory period of **200 ms** has elapsed since the last beat, declare a QRS.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instantaneous Heart Rate | $HR(n) = \dfrac{60}{RR(n)}$ | bpm | RR in seconds |
| 2 | Mean Heart Rate | $\overline{HR} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{n=1}^{N} HR(n)$ | bpm | Over N beats |
| 3 | SDNN (HRV time-domain) | $SDNN = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{n=1}^{N}(RR(n) - \overline{RR})^{2}}$ | ms | Overall HRV |
| 4 | RMSSD | $RMSSD = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{n=1}^{N-1}(RR(n+1) - RR(n))^{2}}$ | ms | Beat-to-beat variability |
| 5 | pNN50 | $pNN50 = \dfrac{\text{count}(\vert RR(n+1) - RR(n)\vert > 50\text{ ms})}{N-1} \times 100\%$ | % | Vagal tone index |
| 6 | LF Power | $P_{LF} = \int_{0.04}^{0.15} S_{RR}(f)\,df$ | ms² | Sympathetic + vagal |
| 7 | HF Power | $P_{HF} = \int_{0.15}^{0.4} S_{RR}(f)\,df$ | ms² | Vagal (respiratory) |
| 8 | LF/HF Ratio | $R_{LF/HF} = P_{LF}/P_{HF}$ | — | Sympathovagal balance |
| 9 | Total Power | $P_{TP} = P_{VLF} + P_{LF} + P_{HF}$ | ms² | HRV ≤ 0.4 Hz |
| 10 | Pan-Tompkins Derivative | $y(n) = \tfrac{1}{8T}\left(2x(n+T) + x(n) - x(n-2T) - 2x(n-3T)\right)$ | — | 5-point FIR |
| 11 | Moving-Window Integration | $y(n) = \tfrac{1}{N}\left(x(n-(N-1)) + x(n-(N-2)) + \dots + x(n)\right)$ | — | N = window length |
| 12 | Adaptive Threshold | $THR_{n} = \alpha \cdot \text{NoisePeak}_n + \beta \cdot \text{SignalPeak}_n$ | — | α ≈ 0.1, β ≈ 0.6 |
| 13 | Corrected QT (Bazett) | $QT_c = \dfrac{QT}{\sqrt{RR}}$ | s | Rate-corrected |
| 14 | Corrected QT (Fridericia) | $QT_c = \dfrac{QT}{\sqrt[3]{RR}}$ | s | More accurate at extremes |
| 15 | Energy of windowed QRS | $E = \sum_{n=1}^{W} x^{2}(n)$ | mV²·s | Morphology feature |

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> In KTU exam answers, **always specify units** (ms, bpm, ms²) — answers that quote "HRV = 50" without units are penalized. The **LF/HF ratio** is the single most-asked HRV formula in the 14-mark KTU questions.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Holter / Ambulatory Monitors** — 24–48 h continuous ECG recording; Pan-Tompkins + RR-interval analysis computes millions of beats automatically.
- **Implantable Devices** — Pacemakers and ICDs run real-time QRS detectors to deliver therapy.
- **ICU Patient Monitoring** — Arrhythmia alarms (asystole, VT, VF) are FDA-mandated in critical care.
- **Wearable Fitness Trackers** — Apple Watch, Fitbit use PPG-derived pulse intervals and arrhythmia classification algorithms.
- **Sports & Exercise Physiology** — HRV is used to quantify training load and recovery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Heart Rate from RR Intervals

Let $t_{k}$ and $t_{k+1}$ be the time-stamps (in seconds) of two consecutive R-peaks detected by the Pan-Tompkins algorithm. Then:

$$\begin{aligned}
RR(k) &= t_{k+1} - t_{k} \quad \text{(seconds per beat)} \\[4pt]
HR(k) &= \dfrac{\text{beats}}{\text{minute}} = \dfrac{1 \text{ beat}}{RR(k) \text{ s}} \times 60 \text{ s/min} \\[4pt]
HR(k) &= \dfrac{60}{RR(k)} \quad \text{(bpm)}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion logic:** Since $1$ beat takes $RR(k)$ seconds, the *number of beats in 60 s* is exactly $60/RR(k)$. This is the standard clinical definition.

### Worked Numerical Example

Suppose five consecutive R-R intervals (in seconds) are measured as:
$$RR = \{0.80, \; 0.82, \; 0.79, \; 0.81, \; 0.80\}$$

Compute the mean heart rate.

$$\begin{aligned}
\overline{RR} &= \dfrac{0.80 + 0.82 + 0.79 + 0.81 + 0.80}{5} = \dfrac{4.02}{5} = 0.804 \text{ s} \\[4pt]
\overline{HR} &= \dfrac{60}{0.804} = 74.63 \text{ bpm}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $\overline{HR} \in [60, 100]$ bpm, the rhythm is **Normal Sinus Rhythm (NSR)**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SDNN, RMSSD, and pNN50 from RR Tachogram

Let $RR(1), RR(2), \ldots, RR(N)$ be a sequence of $N$ normal-to-normal (NN) intervals in **milliseconds** (after ectopic-beat rejection).

**Step 1 — Mean RR:**
$$\overline{RR} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} RR(k)$$

**Step 2 — SDNN (Standard Deviation of NN intervals):**
$$SDNN = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\bigl(RR(k) - \overline{RR}\bigr)^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Successive Difference:**
$$\Delta RR(k) = RR(k+1) - RR(k), \quad k = 1, 2, \ldots, N-1$$

**Step 4 — RMSSD (Root Mean Square of Successive Differences):**
$$RMSSD = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{k=1}^{N-1}\bigl(\Delta RR(k)\bigr)^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — pNN50 (Proportion of intervals differing by more than 50 ms):**
$$pNN50 = \dfrac{\#\bigl\{k : \vert \Delta RR(k) \vert > 50 \text{ ms}\bigr\}}{N-1} \times 100\%$$

### Worked Numerical Example

Given $RR = \{800, 820, 790, 810, 805\}$ ms:

**Step 1:**
$$\overline{RR} = \dfrac{800+820+790+810+805}{5} = \dfrac{4025}{5} = 805 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 (SDNN):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\sum (RR - \overline{RR})^{2} &= (-5)^{2} + (15)^{2} + (-15)^{2} + (5)^{2} + (0)^{2} \\
&= 25 + 225 + 225 + 25 + 0 = 500 \\
SDNN &= \sqrt{500/(5-1)} = \sqrt{125} \approx 11.18 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 (Successive Differences):**
$$\Delta RR = \{+20, \; -30, \; +20, \; -5\}$$

**Step 4 (RMSSD):**
$$\begin{aligned}
\sum (\Delta RR)^{2} &= 400 + 900 + 400 + 25 = 1725 \\
RMSSD &= \sqrt{1725/4} = \sqrt{431.25} \approx 20.77 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 (pNN50):**
None of $\vert \Delta RR \vert = \{20, 30, 20, 5\}$ exceed 50 ms, so:
$$pNN50 = 0\%$$

> **Interpretation:** $RMSSD = 20.77$ ms and $pNN50 = 0\%$ indicate **low beat-to-beat variability**, suggestive of low vagal tone (e.g., sympathetic dominance, aging, or post-MI patient).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency-Domain HRV (PSD via Welch's Method)

The RR-interval series $RR(k)$ is resampled at a uniform rate (typically $f_s = 4$ Hz) to obtain $x(n)$, and the Power Spectral Density (PSD) is computed using **Welch's averaged periodogram**:

$$S_{xx}(f) = \dfrac{1}{M \cdot U} \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \bigl\vert X_m(f) \bigr\vert^{2}$$

where
- $M$ is the number of overlapping segments,
- $X_m(f)$ is the FFT of the $m$-th windowed segment,
- $U = \dfrac{1}{L}\left(\sum_{n=0}^{L-1} w^{2}(n)\right)$ is the window-energy normalization factor.

The frequency bands are integrated:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{VLF} &= \int_{0.003}^{0.04} S_{xx}(f)\, df \\
P_{LF} &= \int_{0.04}^{0.15} S_{xx}(f)\, df \\
P_{HF} &= \int_{0.15}^{0.4} S_{xx}(f)\, df
\end{aligned}$$

The **LF/HF ratio** captures autonomic balance:
$$R_{LF/HF} = \dfrac{P_{LF}}{P_{HF}}$$

> **Clinical interpretation:** $R_{LF/HF} > 4$ → sympathetic dominance (stress, heart failure); $R_{LF/HF} < 1$ → parasympathetic dominance (relaxation, athletes).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Pan-Tompkins 5-Point Derivative

The Pan-Tompkins derivative operator approximates the first derivative using a 5-point FIR filter:

$$y(n) = \dfrac{1}{8T}\bigl[\,2x(n+T) + x(n) - x(n-2T) - 2x(n-3T)\,\bigr]$$

**Derivation logic:** This is a finite-difference approximation of $\frac{dx}{dt}$:
- Forward difference: $x(n+T) - x(n)$
- Backward difference: $x(n) - x(n-T)$
- A symmetric 5-tap derivative combines four terms with weights $(2, 1, 0, -1, -2)$ to suppress DC and emphasize the **steepest slope** of the QRS.

**Frequency response** (normalized, $T=1$):
$$H(\omega) = \dfrac{1}{8}\left(2e^{j\omega} + 1 - e^{-j2\omega} - 2e^{-j3\omega}\right)$$

The amplitude response peaks near $\omega \approx \pi/2$, which corresponds to the **dominant QRS frequency** (≈ 10–15 Hz at $f_s = 200$ Hz sampling).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Decision Rules for Common Arrhythmias

| Feature | Normal | Bradycardia | Tachycardia | AF | VT | PVC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mean HR (bpm) | 60–100 | <60 | >100 | Variable, often >100 | >100 | Any |
| RR Regularity | Regular | Regular | Regular | **Irregularly irregular** | Regular | Premature |
| P-wave | Present | Present | Present | **Absent** | Often absent | Absent (in ectopic beat) |
| QRS Width (ms) | <120 | <120 | <120 | <120 (usually) | **>120** | **>120** |
| Detection method | Pan-Tompkins | HR threshold | HR threshold | RR irregularity + no P | Wide-QRS rule | Prematurity + morphology |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Pan-Tompkins QRS Detection Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw ECG Signal x of n] --> B[Bandpass Filter 5 to 15 Hz]
    B --> C[Derivative Operator 5 tap FIR]
    C --> D[Squaring Function y equals x squared]
    D --> E[Moving Window Integration MWI]
    E --> F[Adaptive Thresholding]
    F --> G{QRS Detected}
    G -- Yes --> H[Output R peak time stamp]
    H --> I[Update Signal and Noise Peaks]
    I --> F
    G -- No --> F
    F --> J[Refractory Period 200 ms blanking]
    J --> F
```

> **[!NOTE]**
> The adaptive threshold block recursively updates two running averages — a **signal peak level** (when a QRS is detected) and a **noise peak level** (otherwise). The detection threshold is computed as a weighted sum of these two. This makes the detector robust to baseline drift and amplitude changes.

## 4.2 Arrhythmia Classification Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RawECG[Raw ECG Recording] --> PreProc[Pre-processing Baseline removal and 50 Hz Notch]
    PreProc --> QRSDetect[QRS Detection Pan-Tompkins]
    QRSDetect --> RRSeries[RR Interval Tachogram]
    RRSeries --> FeatExt[Feature Extraction Time Morphology Frequency]
    FeatExt --> Decision{Decision Logic}
    Decision --> ClassA[Normal Sinus Rhythm 60 to 100 bpm regular]
    Decision --> ClassB[Bradycardia HR below 60]
    Decision --> ClassC[Tachycardia HR above 100]
    Decision --> ClassD[Atrial Fibrillation Irregularly irregular no P]
    Decision --> ClassE[VT or PVC Wide QRS premature]
    Decision --> ClassF[Heart Block PR or RR pattern based]
    ClassA --> Output[Diagnostic Report]
    ClassB --> Output
    ClassC --> Output
    ClassD --> Output
    ClassE --> Output
    ClassF --> Output
```

## 4.3 HRV Analysis Architecture (Time + Frequency Domain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RPeaks[R peak time stamps from Pan Tompkins] --> Resamp[Resample to 4 Hz Uniform RR series]
    Resamp --> TimeDomain[Time Domain Features]
    Resamp --> FreqDomain[Frequency Domain Features]
    TimeDomain --> SDNN[SDNN]
    TimeDomain --> RMSSD[RMSSD]
    TimeDomain --> pNN50[pNN50]
    FreqDomain --> WelchPSD[Welch PSD Estimation]
    WelchPSD --> VLF[VLF Power 0.003 to 0.04 Hz]
    WelchPSD --> LF[LF Power 0.04 to 0.15 Hz]
    WelchPSD --> HF[HF Power 0.15 to 0.4 Hz]
    LF --> RatioCalc[LF to HF Ratio Computation]
    HF --> RatioCalc
    SDNN --> FinalOut[Autonomic Modulation Report]
    RMSSD --> FinalOut
    pNN50 --> FinalOut
    VLF --> FinalOut
    RatioCalc --> FinalOut
```

## 4.4 QRS Morphology Comparison Block (Template Matching)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BeatSeg[Segmented Beat Window 200 ms before to 400 ms after R] --> Normalize[Amplitude Normalization]
    Normalize --> Template[Stored Normal Beat Template]
    Template --> Corr[Cross Correlation Computation]
    BeatSeg --> Corr
    Corr --> Score[Correlation Coefficient r]
    Score --> Threshold{r greater than 0.9}
    Threshold -- Yes --> NormBeat[Classify as Normal]
    Threshold -- No --> MorphCheck{Width greater than 120 ms}
    MorphCheck -- Yes --> WideBeat[Classify as PVC or VT]
    MorphCheck -- No --> NarrowBeat[Classify as Supraventricular Ectopic]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define arrhythmia. List any four common types of cardiac arrhythmias with one characteristic ECG feature of each.

**Model Answer:**

**Arrhythmia** is any deviation of the cardiac rhythm from the normal sinus rhythm (NSR), characterized by abnormalities in rate, regularity, or origin of the electrical impulse.

| # | Arrhythmia | Characteristic ECG Feature |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Bradycardia** | Heart rate < **60 bpm**, regular RR intervals |
| 2 | **Tachycardia** | Heart rate > **100 bpm**, regular RR intervals |
| 3 | **Atrial Fibrillation** | Irregularly irregular RR, **absence of P waves** |
| 4 | **Premature Ventricular Contraction (PVC)** | **Wide and bizarre QRS (>120 ms)**, no preceding P wave, premature occurrence |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for correctly tabulated features)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
What is the Pan-Tompkins algorithm? State the purpose of each of its five stages.

**Model Answer:**

The **Pan-Tompkins algorithm (1985)** is a real-time, robust QRS detection method that cascades five signal-processing stages on a bandpass-filtered ECG:

1. **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz)** — Isolates QRS energy; rejects baseline wander (<5 Hz) and EMG noise (>15 Hz).
2. **Derivative Operator** — A 5-point FIR differentiator that emphasizes the **steep slope** of the R-wave.
3. **Squaring** — A non-linear operation that makes the signal non-negative and amplifies large values, suppressing small-amplitude P and T waves.
4. **Moving-Window Integration (MWI)** — Smooths the squared derivative, producing a single pulse per QRS for easy thresholding.
5. **Adaptive Thresholding** — Dynamically sets two running averages (signal peak and noise peak) and compares the MWI output to a threshold; a 200 ms refractory period prevents double-counting.

*(3 marks: 1 for naming + 2 for describing any 4 stages correctly)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Describe the time-domain and frequency-domain methods of Heart Rate Variability (HRV) analysis. Define SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50, and the LF/HF ratio, with their physiological significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The RR intervals (in ms) measured from a 10-beat ECG segment are:
$$RR = \{800, \; 820, \; 790, \; 810, \; 805, \; 815, \; 795, \; 825, \; 805, \; 810\}$$
Compute the mean RR, mean HR, SDNN, RMSSD, and pNN50. Comment on the autonomic state. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**Time-Domain HRV Methods:** These operate directly on the sequence of NN (normal-to-normal) intervals. They are simple, robust, and statistically powerful.

- **SDNN** — Standard deviation of all NN intervals; reflects **total (overall) HRV**. Sensitive to both short- and long-term variations.
$$SDNN = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{k=1}^{N}\bigl(RR(k) - \overline{RR}\bigr)^{2}}$$

- **RMSSD** — Root mean square of successive NN differences; a pure **short-term, beat-to-beat** variability index, dominated by **parasympathetic (vagal) tone**.
$$RMSSD = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{k=1}^{N-1}\bigl(RR(k+1) - RR(k)\bigr)^{2}}$$

- **pNN50** — Percentage of successive differences greater than 50 ms; another **vagal tone** marker, easier to interpret clinically than RMSSD.
$$pNN50 = \dfrac{\#\bigl\{k : \vert \Delta RR(k) \vert > 50 \text{ ms}\bigr\}}{N-1} \times 100\%$$

**Frequency-Domain HRV Methods:** The RR tachogram is resampled (e.g., 4 Hz) and the PSD is computed (Welch / Lomb-Scargle). Three bands are integrated:

| Band | Range | Physiological Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **VLF** | 0.003 – 0.04 Hz | Thermoregulation, hormonal, undefined |
| **LF** | 0.04 – 0.15 Hz | **Sympathetic + Parasympathetic** (baroreflex) |
| **HF** | 0.15 – 0.4 Hz | **Parasympathetic** (respiratory sinus arrhythmia) |

- **LF/HF Ratio** — Index of **sympathovagal balance**. High ratio = sympathetic dominance (stress); low ratio = vagal dominance (relaxation, athletes).

> *Marks distribution: 2 marks for time-domain list, 2 marks for frequency-domain bands, 3 marks for physiological significance.* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Step 1 — Mean RR:**
$$\overline{RR} = \dfrac{800+820+790+810+805+815+795+825+805+810}{10} = \dfrac{8075}{10} = 807.5 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Mean HR:**
$$\overline{HR} = \dfrac{60000}{807.5} \approx 74.30 \text{ bpm}$$

**Step 3 — SDNN:** Compute $(RR - \overline{RR})^{2}$ for each beat:

| RR (ms) | RR − 807.5 | Squared |
|---|---|---|
| 800 | −7.5 | 56.25 |
| 820 | +12.5 | 156.25 |
| 790 | −17.5 | 306.25 |
| 810 | +2.5 | 6.25 |
| 805 | −2.5 | 6.25 |
| 815 | +7.5 | 56.25 |
| 795 | −12.5 | 156.25 |
| 825 | +17.5 | 306.25 |
| 805 | −2.5 | 6.25 |
| 810 | +2.5 | 6.25 |
| **Σ** |  | **1062.5** |

$$SDNN = \sqrt{\dfrac{1062.5}{9}} = \sqrt{118.06} \approx 10.87 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Successive Differences and RMSSD:**

| k | ΔRR | (ΔRR)² |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | +20 | 400 |
| 2 | −30 | 900 |
| 3 | +20 | 400 |
| 4 | −5 | 25 |
| 5 | +10 | 100 |
| 6 | −20 | 400 |
| 7 | +30 | 900 |
| 8 | −20 | 400 |
| 9 | +5 | 25 |
| **Σ** |  | **3550** |

$$RMSSD = \sqrt{\dfrac{3550}{9}} = \sqrt{394.44} \approx 19.86 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — pNN50:**
Checking $\vert \Delta RR \vert$: $\{20, 30, 20, 5, 10, 20, 30, 20, 5\}$ — **none** exceed 50 ms.
$$pNN50 = 0\%$$

**Step 6 — Clinical Interpretation:**
- $\overline{HR} = 74.3$ bpm → **Normal sinus rate**.
- $SDNN = 10.87$ ms (low; normal short-term SDNN > 30 ms) → reduced overall HRV.
- $RMSSD = 19.86$ ms and $pNN50 = 0\%$ → **depressed vagal tone**.

> **Comment:** The patient exhibits **sympathetic dominance with low parasympathetic activity**, a pattern commonly seen in post-myocardial-infarction (post-MI) patients, chronic stress, or aging. Clinically, this is associated with **increased cardiac mortality risk** and may warrant autonomic-modulation therapy (e.g., beta-blockers, exercise rehabilitation).

> *Marks distribution: 1 each for mean RR, mean HR, SDNN, RMSSD, pNN50, plus 2 for clinical comment.* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the Pan-Tompkins algorithm for QRS detection. State the role of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The squared-derivative output of an ECG segment is:
$$y(n) = \{0, 0.1, 0.4, 0.7, 0.3, 0.1, 0, 0, 0.2, 0.6, 0.5, 0.2, 0\}$$
sampled at $f_s = 200$ Hz. A moving-window integrator of length $N = 4$ is applied. Compute the MWI output $z(n)$ and identify the QRS peaks. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

Refer to Section 4.1 for the full block diagram. The five stages are:

1. **Bandpass Filter (5–15 Hz)** — A cascade of low-pass (cutoff 15 Hz) and high-pass (cutoff 5 Hz) filters; removes baseline wander, 50/60 Hz interference, and EMG noise. Passband designed to match the QRS spectrum.
2. **Derivative Operator** — 5-point FIR differentiator $y(n) = \frac{1}{8T}[2x(n+T) + x(n) - x(n-2T) - 2x(n-3T)]$; emphasizes the **steep QRS slope**.
3. **Squaring** — $y(n) \leftarrow y^{2}(n)$; non-negative output, suppresses P and T waves.
4. **Moving-Window Integration** — $z(n) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}y(n-k)$; smooths to one pulse per QRS.
5. **Adaptive Thresholding + Refractory** — Two running averages (signal/noise peaks); threshold $THR = 0.6\cdot SP + 0.1\cdot NP$; 200 ms refractory prevents T-wave double-counting.

> *Marks distribution: 2 for diagram, 5 for stage-by-stage explanation.* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

The MWI of length $N = 4$ is:
$$z(n) = \dfrac{1}{4}\bigl[y(n) + y(n-1) + y(n-2) + y(n-3)\bigr]$$

For $n < 3$, define $y(k) = 0$.

| n | y(n) | z(n) = (1/4)·window | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | (0+0+0+0)/4 = **0.000** | — |
| 1 | 0.1 | (0.1+0+0+0)/4 = **0.025** | — |
| 2 | 0.4 | (0.4+0.1+0+0)/4 = **0.125** | — |
| 3 | 0.7 | (0.7+0.4+0.1+0)/4 = **0.300** | — |
| 4 | 0.3 | (0.3+0.7+0.4+0.1)/4 = **0.375** | — |
| 5 | 0.1 | (0.1+0.3+0.7+0.4)/4 = **0.375** | — |
| 6 | 0.0 | (0+0.1+0.3+0.7)/4 = **0.275** | — |
| 7 | 0.0 | (0+0+0.1+0.3)/4 = **0.100** | — |
| 8 | 0.2 | (0.2+0+0+0.1)/4 = **0.075** | — |
| 9 | 0.6 | (0.6+0.2+0+0)/4 = **0.200** | — |
| 10 | 0.5 | (0.5+0.6+0.2+0)/4 = **0.325** | — |
| 11 | 0.2 | (0.2+0.5+0.6+0.2)/4 = **0.375** | — |
| 12 | 0.0 | (0+0.2+0.5+0.6)/4 = **0.325** | — |

**Local maxima of z(n):** $z(5) = 0.375$ and $z(11) = 0.375$.

**Adaptive threshold** (assuming noise peak NP ≈ 0.1, signal peak SP ≈ 0.4):
$$THR = 0.6 \times 0.4 + 0.1 \times 0.1 = 0.25$$

Both peaks (0.375) **exceed** the threshold. Apply the 200 ms refractory period:
- Sampling interval $T = 1/f_s = 5$ ms.
- 200 ms = 40 samples.
- The gap between the two peaks is $11 - 5 = 6$ samples = 30 ms, which is **less than 200 ms** → second detection is **rejected as a T-wave artefact**.

**Final QRS detection: only the peak at n = 5 is reported.**

> *Marks distribution: 2 for MWI formula, 3 for table computation, 2 for threshold + refractory logic.* **(7 marks)**

---

> **[!WARNING]**
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Units matter.** Writing "HRV = 50" without specifying *ms* or *ms²* will cost ½ to 1 mark. Always annotate.
> 2. **Bazett's vs Fridericia.** For QT correction, do **not** use Bazett's formula at extreme heart rates (<50 or >120 bpm); examiners expect the student to know this limitation.
> 3. **Pan-Tompkins block diagram is mandatory.** A verbal explanation of the algorithm *without* a labelled block diagram is typically marked down 1–2 marks in the 7-mark sub-question.
> 4. **Refractory period 200 ms.** Many students forget this in MWI-based detection. Always state that a second detection within 200 ms of the previous one is rejected.
> 5. **Mean HR computation.** Use $\overline{HR} = 60/\overline{RR}$ only when RR is in **seconds**, and $\overline{HR} = 60000/\overline{RR}$ when RR is in **milliseconds**. Mixing units is the #1 unit-conversion error.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> **High-Yield Quick-Recall Bullets**

- ✅ Arrhythmia = deviation of cardiac rhythm from NSR; classified by **rate, regularity, origin**.
- ✅ **Pan-Tompkins** = 5 stages: Bandpass (5–15 Hz) → Derivative → Square → MWI → Adaptive Threshold + 200 ms refractory.
- ✅ **HR = 60 / RR** (RR in s); **HR = 60000 / RR** (RR in ms).
- ✅ **Time-domain HRV:** SDNN (overall), RMSSD & pNN50 (short-term, vagal).
- ✅ **Frequency-domain HRV bands:** VLF (0.003–0.04 Hz), LF (0.04–0.15 Hz), HF (0.15–0.4 Hz).
- ✅ **LF/HF ratio** = sympathovagal balance index; high → sympathetic dominance.
- ✅ **Bradycardia** < 60 bpm, **Tachycardia** > 100 bpm, **AF** = irregularly irregular + no P, **PVC/VT** = wide QRS > 120 ms.
- ✅ **Bazett's QTc** = $QT/\sqrt{RR}$; **Fridericia's** = $QT/\sqrt[3]{RR}$.
- ✅ **Welch's method** = averaged periodogram with overlapping windows; standard for HRV PSD.
- ✅ Always state **units** (ms, bpm, ms², Hz) in KTU answers.
- ✅ The **QRS complex** is the highest-SNR ECG feature and the primary fiducial point for R-peak detection.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Exercise ECG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Exercise ECG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exercise ECG (Stress Electrocardiography)** is a non-invasive diagnostic procedure in which the electrical activity of the heart is continuously recorded while the patient is subjected to a standardized, graded physical workload (typically treadmill or cycle ergometer), with the objective of inducing and characterizing latent **myocardial ischemia**, **exercise-induced arrhythmias**, and **abnormal hemodynamic responses** that are not apparent in the resting state.

In the context of **KTU PBEBT504 – Introduction to Biomedical Signal Processing**, *Analysis of Exercise ECG* refers to the application of digital signal processing (DSP) techniques to extract diagnostically relevant features from ECG signals acquired under dynamic, non-stationary, and high-noise conditions induced by exercise — including **baseline wander** from respiration, **muscle artifact (EMG)**, **motion artifact**, and **electrode noise**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology — KTU 2024 Standard**
> - **Stress Test / Treadmill Test (TMT):** Synonymous terms for Exercise ECG.
> - **Bruce Protocol:** Most widely used standardized graded exercise protocol.
> - **MET (Metabolic Equivalent of Task):** 1 MET = 3.5 mL O₂/kg/min (resting oxygen consumption).
> - **ST Segment:** The isoelectric region between QRS end (J-point) and T-wave onset, critical for ischemia detection.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Heart as a Car Engine on a Test Bench"**
>
> Imagine a car engine sitting idle in a garage. The mechanic listens to it (resting ECG) and everything seems fine. But the customer complains the engine "stutters" only when driving uphill. To reproduce the problem, the mechanic mounts the engine on a **dynamometer (treadmill)** and gradually increases the load (graded exercise). Under stress, the engine's true weakness — a clogged fuel injector (coronary stenosis) — becomes audible as a misfire (ST-segment depression).
>
> Similarly, an artery with a 70% blockage can supply enough oxygenated blood to the heart at rest, but during exercise, the myocardial oxygen demand exceeds supply → **ischemia** manifests as measurable changes in the ST segment of the ECG.

## 1.3 Why Resting ECG is Insufficient

| Condition | Resting ECG | Exercise ECG |
|---|---|---|
| Stable angina with ≥70% stenosis | Often **normal** | ST depression ≥1 mm |
| Variant (Prinzmetal) angina | Normal between spasms | ST elevation during episode |
| Exercise-induced arrhythmia | Absent | May emerge |
| Functional capacity | Unknown | Quantified in **METs** |

---

## 1.4 Standardized Physical / Physiological Metrics

The following **bold** values are universally accepted clinical benchmarks used in every KTU-affiliated clinical engineering module:

- **Standard Resting HR:** 60–100 bpm
- **Maximum Predicted HR (HRmax):** **220 − age (years)** — *Fox formula, most common*
- **Target HR (85% of HRmax) for diagnostic stress test:** **0.85 × (220 − age)**
- **1 MET = 3.5 mL O₂/kg/min ≈ 1.25 kcal/min** (resting metabolic rate)
- **Bruce Stage 1:** 4.8 METs (1.7 mph, 10% grade)
- **Bruce Stage 2:** 7.0 METs (2.5 mph, 12% grade)
- **Bruce Stage 3:** 10.1 METs (3.4 mph, 14% grade)
- **Significant ST Depression threshold:** **≥ 1.0 mm (0.1 mV)** horizontal or downsloping, measured 80 ms after J-point

---

## 1.5 Diagnostic Objectives of Exercise ECG

1. **Detection of inducible myocardial ischemia** (CAD screening).
2. **Evaluation of exercise-induced arrhythmias**.
3. **Assessment of functional capacity** (METs achieved).
4. **Evaluation of hemodynamic response** (HR, BP, HR recovery).
5. **Risk stratification post-MI** (prognostic value).
6. **Assessment of treatment efficacy** (post-revascularization).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Exercise Physiology Cascade — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Understanding *why* ECG changes occur during exercise is the foundation for designing DSP algorithms that detect them. Each physiological step corresponds to an observable signal feature.

### Step 1 — Increased Sympathetic Drive
- Exercise → sympathetic nervous system activation → release of **catecholamines** (adrenaline, noradrenaline).
- **Effect on ECG:** ↑ Heart rate (positive chronotropy), ↑ contractility, shortened PR interval, shortened QT interval.

### Step 2 — Increased Myocardial Oxygen Demand (MVO₂)
- Determinants of MVO₂ (in order of importance):
  1. **Heart rate (HR)**
  2. **Myocardial wall tension** (∝ LV pressure × radius / wall thickness, Laplace's law)
  3. **Contractility** (dP/dt)
  4. **Stroke work**

### Step 3 — Coronary Blood Flow Must Rise 4–6× Above Resting
- Healthy coronaries dilate to meet demand.
- **Stenosed arteries cannot dilate** → supply–demand mismatch → ischemia.

### Step 4 — Ischemic Cascade (Temporal Order)
1. **Perfusion defect** (seconds — SPECT/PET detectable)
2. **Diastolic dysfunction** (within 1–2 minutes)
3. **ECG changes — ST depression** (2–3 minutes)
4. **Systolic dysfunction** (regional wall motion abnormality)
5. **Anginal pain** (last, often absent in diabetics — "silent ischemia")

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for signal processing:** ST-segment changes appear *before* symptoms. Therefore, **automated real-time ST analysis** during the test is critical for early test termination and patient safety.

### Step 5 — Recovery Phase
- In healthy subjects, HR returns toward baseline rapidly (HRR₁ = HR_peak − HR_1min_recovery ≥ 12 bpm).
- **Abnormal HR recovery** is an independent predictor of mortality.
- ST changes that persist >5 minutes into recovery indicate severe ischemia.

---

## 2.2 Exercise Test Protocols — Comparative Analysis

| Protocol | Stages | Speed/Grade Increment | Total Duration | Best Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bruce** | 7 | Large jumps every 3 min | 21 min | Healthy, ambulatory adults |
| **Modified Bruce** | 7 | Smaller initial load | 21 min | Elderly, post-MI, deconditioned |
| **Naughton** | 11 | Small increments (1–2 METs) | Variable | Heart failure, low-functional patients |
| **Ramp** | Continuous | Gradual linear ↑ | 8–12 min | Optimized for individual fitness |
| **ACIP (Asymptomatic Cardiac Ischemia Pilot)** | Variable | 1-min stages | 7 min | Post-MI, CAD evaluation |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU ESE, university lab viva, and gate-bioinstrumentation problems.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $HR_{max} = 220 - \text{Age}$ | Maximum predicted heart rate (Fox) | bpm |
| 2 | $\text{Target HR} = 0.85 \times HR_{max}$ | Diagnostic threshold | bpm |
| 3 | $ST/HR \text{ Index} = \dfrac{\Delta ST \ (\text{mm})}{\Delta HR \ (\text{bpm})}$ | Index of ischemia severity | > 1.6 µV/bpm = positive |
| 4 | $\text{Duke Score} = \text{Exercise time (min)} - 5 \times \text{Max } \vert \Delta ST \vert - 4 \times \text{Angina Index}$ | Prognostic risk score | > +5 = low risk, ≤ -11 = high risk |
| 5 | $\text{METs} = \dfrac{\text{VO}_2}{3.5 \ \text{mL/kg/min}}$ | Functional capacity | < 5 METs = poor prognosis |
| 6 | $HRR_1 = HR_{peak} - HR_{1\,min\,recovery}$ | Heart rate recovery | < 12 bpm = abnormal |
| 7 | $\text{DP (Double Product)} = HR_{peak} \times SBP_{peak} / 100$ | Myocardial O₂ demand index | > 30,000 = high demand |
| 8 | $\text{ST}_{\text{depression norm}} = \dfrac{0.1 \ \text{mV}}{10 \ \text{mm paper}} = 1 \ \text{mm}$ | Standard ST depression criterion | mm at 80 ms post-J |
| 9 | $\text{SNR}_{out} = \text{SNR}_{in} + 10 \log_{10} N$ | Coherent averaging gain (N beats) | dB |
| 10 | $\text{Noise Power (baseline)} = \sigma^2 \approx 0.05 \ \text{mV}^2$ | Typical baseline wander power | mV² |

> [!NOTE]
> In the Duke Treadmill Score formula above, the term $\vert \Delta ST \vert$ uses **vertical bar notation for absolute value**. In KTU exam answer sheets, students are advised to write "mod of ΔST" to avoid formatting issues in markdown.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded cardiac monitors** (Holter, patch devices) integrate real-time ST-segment trend analysis using wavelet denoising.
- **Ambulatory stress ECG systems** (e.g., GE CASE, Philips ST80i) implement adaptive filtering to remove motion artifact in real time.
- **Wearable fitness ECG devices** (Apple Watch, KardiaMobile) approximate Bruce protocol via HR-guided protocols.
- **DSP pipeline**: Pre-acquisition → Notch (50/60 Hz) → Bandpass (0.5–40 Hz) → Baseline-wander removal (high-pass 0.5 Hz or median filter) → QRS detection (Pan-Tompkins) → ST-segment trend extraction → Ischemia alarm.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Maximum Heart Rate and Target HR

**Given:** A 50-year-old male patient is scheduled for a Bruce protocol stress test.
**Required:** (a) HRmax, (b) 85% target HR, (c) minimum HR at which ST changes are diagnostically valid.

### Step 1 — Apply the Fox formula
$$
HR_{max} = 220 - \text{Age}
$$

Substitute Age = 50:
$$
HR_{max} = 220 - 50 = 170 \ \text{bpm}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute 85% target HR (diagnostic threshold)
$$
HR_{target} = 0.85 \times HR_{max} = 0.85 \times 170
$$

$$
HR_{target} = 144.5 \ \text{bpm}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the diagnostic HR floor (the test is inconclusive if the patient fails to reach this HR)
$$
HR_{floor} = 0.85 \times HR_{max} = 144.5 \ \text{bpm}
$$

> If the patient stops at, say, 130 bpm without ischemic ECG changes, the test is **non-diagnostic** — it neither confirms nor rules out CAD.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — ST/HR Index for Ischemia Quantification

**Given:** During peak exercise, the patient's ST segment depression increased from 0.5 mm at 100 bpm (rest) to 2.0 mm at 160 bpm (peak).
**Required:** Compute the ST/HR Index and interpret.

### Step 1 — Compute ΔST
$$
\Delta ST = ST_{peak} - ST_{rest} = 2.0 - 0.5 = 1.5 \ \text{mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute ΔHR
$$
\Delta HR = HR_{peak} - HR_{rest} = 160 - 100 = 60 \ \text{bpm}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the ST/HR Index formula
$$
\text{ST/HR Index} = \frac{\Delta ST}{\Delta HR} = \frac{1.5 \ \text{mm}}{60 \ \text{bpm}}
$$

$$
\text{ST/HR Index} = 0.025 \ \text{mm/bpm}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to clinical units (µV/bpm; 1 mm = 100 µV)
$$
\text{ST/HR Index} = 0.025 \times 100 = 2.5 \ \mu\text{V/bpm}
$$

### Step 5 — Interpretation
- Threshold for positivity = **1.6 µV/bpm**.
- Computed value = **2.5 µV/bpm** → **POSITIVE** (significant ischemia).

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Duke Treadmill Score (Prognostic)

**Given:** A patient achieves 8 minutes of Bruce protocol exercise, has 1.5 mm maximum ST depression, and reports **no angina** (Angina Index = 0).
**Required:** Calculate Duke Treadmill Score (DTS) and classify risk.

### Step 1 — Recall the formula
$$
\text{DTS} = \text{Exercise time (min)} - 5 \times \vert \Delta ST \vert - 4 \times \text{Angina Index}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute values
$$
\text{DTS} = 8 - 5 \times \vert 1.5 \vert - 4 \times 0
$$

### Step 3 — Evaluate step-by-step
$$
\text{DTS} = 8 - 7.5 - 0
$$

$$
\text{DTS} = +0.5
$$

### Step 4 — Classify using standard cutoffs
| DTS Range | Risk Category | 5-Year Mortality |
|---|---|---|
| ≥ +5 | Low risk | < 1% |
| −10 to +4 | Moderate risk | 1–10% |
| ≤ −11 | High risk | > 10% |

**Result:** DTS = +0.5 → **Moderate Risk** patient.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — SNR Improvement via Coherent Averaging (Exercise ECG Context)

**Given:** Single-beat SNR = 6 dB. The system averages N = 50 beats.
**Required:** Output SNR.

### Step 1 — Recall coherent averaging gain
$$
\text{SNR}_{out} = \text{SNR}_{in} + 10 \log_{10} N
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute
$$
\text{SNR}_{out} = 6 + 10 \log_{10}(50)
$$

### Step 3 — Compute
$$
\log_{10}(50) = 1.699
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{out} = 6 + 10 \times 1.699 = 6 + 16.99 = 22.99 \ \text{dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for Exercise ECG:** Muscle artifact (EMG) and motion noise dominate during peak exercise, sometimes degrading SNR to < 0 dB. **Real-time coherent averaging over 8–16 beats** is a standard DSP technique to recover the ST-segment morphology. However, averaging must be **triggered on R-peak** and **weighted** to avoid smoothing the very ST changes we want to detect.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — ECG Feature Evolution During Exercise Stages

| Bruce Stage | HR (approx, age 50) | Load (METs) | Expected ECG Feature | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rest | 70 bpm | 1.0 | Normal P-QRS-T | Baseline |
| Stage 1 (3 min) | 110 bpm | 4.8 | Mild tachycardia, P-wave may become peaked | Sympathetic onset |
| Stage 2 (6 min) | 130 bpm | 7.0 | J-point depression begins, PR shortens | Early repolarization changes |
| Stage 3 (9 min) | 150 bpm | 10.1 | ST slope may flatten, T-wave amplitude ↓ | Ischemia threshold |
| Stage 4 (12 min) | 165 bpm | 12.9 | **Significant ST depression** in CAD patients | Diagnostic |
| Peak | 170 bpm | 14+ | Maximum stress, test terminated if positive | Decision point |
| Recovery (1 min) | 160 bpm | – | ST depression should resolve | Slow resolution = severe CAD |
| Recovery (5 min) | 100 bpm | – | Baseline restored | Persistent ST = high risk |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Exercise ECG Signal Processing Pipeline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Hot Topic:** This block diagram is the most frequently asked architecture question in Module 4 (7-mark sub-parts). Memorize the sequence: **Acquire → Filter → Baseline removal → QRS detect → ST extract → Decision**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Patient on Treadmill] --> B[12-Lead ECG Electrodes]
    B --> C[Analog Front End]
    C --> C1[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000]
    C1 --> C2[Analog Bandpass 0.05 to 150 Hz]
    C2 --> D[ADC 12-bit at 500 Hz]
    D --> E[Digital Signal Processing]
    E --> E1[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    E1 --> E2[Baseline Wander Removal Median 200 ms]
    E2 --> E3[Bandpass 0.5 to 40 Hz]
    E3 --> E4[Pan-Tompkins QRS Detector]
    E4 --> E5[ST Segment Extractor J plus 80 ms]
    E5 --> E6[ST HR Trend Buffer]
    E6 --> F{Decision Module}
    F -->|ST more than 1 mm| G[POSITIVE: STOP Test]
    F -->|ST less than 1 mm| H[Continue Stage]
    F -->|HR above 85 percent max| I[Target Achieved]
    E6 --> J[Display and Alarm]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Physician Review]
```

**Explanation of Block Flow:**
1. **Patient on Treadmill** — source of dynamic ECG signal contaminated by motion and EMG.
2. **12-Lead ECG Electrodes** — standard 10-electrode placement for stress testing.
3. **Analog Front End** — INA128 instrumentation amplifier provides 1000× gain; bandpass 0.05–150 Hz.
4. **ADC** — 12-bit resolution at 500 Hz sampling rate (per KTU minimum standard).
5. **DSP chain**:
   - **Notch 50 Hz** removes powerline interference.
   - **Median filter (200 ms window)** removes baseline wander.
   - **Bandpass 0.5–40 Hz** isolates QRS-T spectrum.
   - **Pan-Tompkins** detects R-peaks.
   - **J+80 extractor** measures ST amplitude.
6. **ST/HR Trend Buffer** stores the running trend for decision logic.
7. **Decision Module** applies the **≥1 mm criterion** or **85% HRmax** target.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Exercise Test Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Patient Enters Lab]) --> H1[Record Resting ECG 12-Lead]
    H1 --> H2{Resting ST Normal?}
    H2 -->|No| Stop1[Exclude Stress Test]
    H2 -->|Yes| H3[BP and HR Baseline]
    H3 --> H4[Begin Bruce Stage 1]
    H4 --> H5[Continuous ECG Monitoring]
    H5 --> H6{ST Depression more than 1 mm?}
    H6 -->|Yes| Alert1[STOP: Ischemia Detected]
    H6 -->|No| H7{HR Reached 85 percent HRmax?}
    H7 -->|No| H8[Progress to Next Stage]
    H8 --> H5
    H7 -->|Yes| H9[Hold Target HR for 1 to 2 min]
    H9 --> H10[Begin Cool-down Phase]
    H10 --> H11[Monitor Recovery 5 to 10 min]
    H11 --> H12{ST Resolved?}
    H12 -->|No| Alert2[Persistent Ischemia: Refer]
    H12 -->|Yes| Done([Test Complete: Report Generated])
    Alert1 --> Recovery[Immediate Recovery Posture]
    Recovery --> H11
```

**Key Decision Points:**
- **H6:** If ST depression ≥1 mm horizontal/downsloping at J+80 → terminate.
- **H7:** Inability to reach 85% HRmax with no ischemia = **submaximal non-diagnostic test**.
- **H12:** Persistent ST changes >5 min into recovery = high-risk finding.

---

## 4.3 Schematic — ST-Segment Measurement Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[P Wave] --> PQ[PR Segment Baseline]
    PQ --> Q[Q Wave Onset]
    Q --> R[R Peak]
    R --> S[S Wave End = J Point]
    S --> J80[J plus 80 ms Measurement Point]
    J80 --> T[T Wave Onset]
    T --> Tend[T Wave End]
    PQ -.Defines Isoelectric Line.-> J80
    J80 --> STamp[ST Amplitude: Vertical Drop in mm or mV]
    STamp --> Result{ST more than 1 mm Below Baseline?}
    Result -->|Yes| Pos[POSITIVE for Ischemia]
    Result -->|No| Neg[NEGATIVE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** Examiners specifically check whether the student correctly identifies the **J-point (junction)** and measures ST at **J+80 ms** (not at J itself). Measuring at J underestimates ischemia. This is a frequently tested 3-mark question.

---

## 4.4 System-Level Architecture — Adaptive Noise Cancellation in Exercise ECG

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked 7-Mark Question:** "Explain how adaptive filtering removes motion artifact from exercise ECG." Use this block diagram as your answer's foundation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Src[Primary Input: ECG plus Motion Artifact] --> Mixer[Mixer Node]
    NoiseRef[Reference Input: Accelerometer on Chest] --> Mixer
    Mixer --> AdaptFilt[Adaptive Filter LMS or RLS]
    AdaptFilt --> Sub[Subtractor]
    Src --> Sub
    Sub --> Clean[Clean ECG Output]
    Clean --> Err[Error Signal fed back to AdaptFilt]
    Err --> AdaptFilt
```

**Why adaptive?** The motion-artifact frequency spectrum overlaps with the ECG (both 0.5–40 Hz). Traditional filters cannot separate them. **LMS/RLS adaptive filters** use a **reference noise input** (accelerometer) to *learn* the noise transfer function and subtract it in real time.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **Instructions (KTU 2024):** Answer in 4–6 sentences, 1 diagram allowed. Each question carries **3 marks**.

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Define Exercise ECG. List any four clinical indications for conducting a stress test.**

**Model Answer:**
Exercise ECG, also called a **stress test or treadmill test (TMT)**, is a diagnostic procedure in which the 12-lead ECG is continuously monitored while the patient performs graded physical exercise (treadmill or cycle ergometer) to a predefined protocol, in order to provoke and detect latent **myocardial ischemia** and **exercise-induced arrhythmias** that are absent at rest.

**Four clinical indications:**
1. Diagnosis of suspected coronary artery disease (CAD) in patients with intermediate pre-test probability.
2. Risk stratification and prognosis assessment post-myocardial infarction.
3. Evaluation of functional capacity (in METs) prior to non-cardiac surgery.
4. Assessment of exercise-induced arrhythmias, chronotropic incompetence, or blood pressure response.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**What is the Bruce protocol? State the speed and grade for stages 1, 2, and 3.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Bruce protocol** is the most widely used standardized graded exercise test, comprising **7 stages of 3 minutes each**, with progressively increasing treadmill speed and grade.

| Stage | Speed (mph) | Grade (%) | Approx. METs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1.7 | 10 | 4.8 |
| 2 | 2.5 | 12 | 7.0 |
| 3 | 3.4 | 14 | 10.1 |
| 4 | 4.2 | 16 | 12.9 |
| 5 | 5.0 | 18 | 15.0 |
| 6 | 5.5 | 20 | 16.9 |
| 7 | 6.0 | 22 | 19.4 |

The **Modified Bruce** uses an initial 0% grade warm-up stage, suitable for elderly or post-MI patients.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts (a) = 7 marks, (b) = 7 marks. You must answer **either** the OR option.

---

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Module 4]**

**Q.A(a) [7 marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the digital signal processing chain used to extract the ST-segment trend from a noisy exercise ECG. Mention the role of each block.

**Model Answer:**

A typical DSP chain for ST-segment extraction during exercise ECG consists of the following blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **Signal Acquisition (12-Lead, 500 Hz, 12-bit):** Provides raw ECG at sufficient resolution. Sampling rate ≥ 500 Hz avoids aliasing of QRS notches; 12-bit ADC gives 0.5 mV/2048 ≈ 0.244 µV/LSB resolution.

2. **Powerline Notch Filter (50/60 Hz, IIR 2nd-order):** Removes mains interference; Q-factor must be tight (Q ≈ 30) to avoid ringing.

3. **Baseline Wander Removal (Median Filter, 200 ms window):** During exercise, respiration rate rises to 30–40/min (0.5–0.67 Hz), causing low-frequency drift. A **200 ms median filter** followed by subtraction removes this baseline.

4. **Bandpass Filter (0.5–40 Hz, FIR order 64):** Isolates QRS-T bandwidth; lower cutoff 0.5 Hz attenuates residual baseline, upper 40 Hz suppresses EMG.

5. **QRS Detection (Pan-Tompkins Algorithm):** Five-stage cascade — bandpass → derivative → squaring → moving average → threshold — provides R-peak fiducial points for beat segmentation.

6. **J-Point and ST Extraction:** J-point detected as end-of-S; ST amplitude measured at **J+80 ms**. Average over **8 consecutive beats** to suppress residual noise.

7. **ST/HR Trend Buffer and Decision:** Stores ST amplitude vs HR; applies ≥1 mm threshold to declare ischemia.

> This pipeline provides **real-time, automated ischemia detection** with sub-second latency.

---

**Q.A(b) [7 marks]:** A 55-year-old male achieves HRmax = 165 bpm on Bruce Stage 3. His ST depression at peak is 2.2 mm (rest 0.3 mm). He reports mild chest pain (Angina Index = 1). Calculate the **Duke Treadmill Score** and classify the risk.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify parameters**
- Exercise time = 9 minutes (Stage 3 completed at 9 min)
- Maximum ST depression = 2.2 mm
- Angina Index = 1 (mild, does not limit test)

**Step 2 — Apply Duke formula**
$$
\text{DTS} = \text{Time (min)} - 5 \times \vert \Delta ST \vert - 4 \times \text{Angina Index}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute**
$$
\text{DTS} = 9 - 5 \times 2.2 - 4 \times 1
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate**
$$
\text{DTS} = 9 - 11 - 4 = -6
$$

**Step 5 — Classify**

| DTS | Risk | 5-Year Mortality |
|---|---|---|
| ≥ +5 | Low | < 1% |
| **−10 to +4** | **Moderate** | **1–10%** |
| ≤ −11 | High | > 10% |

**DTS = −6** falls in **Moderate Risk** (closer to high risk). Recommendation: **coronary angiography** for further evaluation.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Module 4]**

**Q.B(a) [7 marks]:** Explain the ischemic cascade and justify why the ECG (ST segment) is the most practical real-time detector of myocardial ischemia during exercise.

**Model Answer:**

**The Ischemic Cascade** (in temporal order):

| Order | Event | Time | Detection Modality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Perfusion defect | Seconds | SPECT, PET, MRI |
| 2 | Diastolic dysfunction | 1–2 min | Echo (Doppler) |
| 3 | **ST segment changes** | **2–3 min** | **ECG (real-time)** |
| 4 | Systolic dysfunction (WMA) | 3–4 min | Echo (2D) |
| 5 | Anginal pain | Last (often absent) | Patient report |

**Why ECG is the most practical real-time detector:**

1. **Temporal advantage:** ST changes appear within 2–3 min of ischemia onset, much earlier than symptoms.
2. **High temporal resolution:** 500 Hz sampling captures QRS-T dynamics beat-by-beat.
3. **Non-invasive and continuous:** Unlike SPECT/PET, ECG provides uninterrupted monitoring.
4. **Quantitative:** ST depression in mm can be measured and trended automatically.
5. **Automated alarm:** Modern DSP pipelines (Pan-Tompkins + ST extractor) alarm within seconds of J+80 ms crossing threshold.
6. **Cost-effective:** ECG hardware is far cheaper than imaging modalities.
7. **Standardized criteria:** ≥1 mm horizontal/downsloping ST depression at J+80 ms is a universally accepted diagnostic threshold (per ACC/AHA guidelines).

---

**Q.B(b) [7 marks]:** With a diagram, explain how an **adaptive noise canceller (LMS algorithm)** removes motion artifact from exercise ECG. Mention the role of the reference input.

**Model Answer:**

**Block diagram** — refer to Section 4.4 architecture.

**Principle:**
The primary input is:
$$
x(n) = s(n) + v(n)
$$
where $s(n)$ is the clean ECG and $v(n)$ is the motion artifact. The reference input $r(n)$ comes from an **accelerometer** placed on the chest, which captures motion but no cardiac signal.

**LMS Adaptive Filter Algorithm:**

The adaptive filter $W(n)$ of order M produces output:
$$
y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w_k(n) \cdot r(n-k)
$$

The error signal is:
$$
e(n) = x(n) - y(n) = s(n) + v(n) - y(n)
$$

**Weight update rule (LMS):**
$$
w_k(n+1) = w_k(n) + 2\mu \cdot e(n) \cdot r(n-k)
$$

where $\mu$ is the step-size (0 < $\mu$ < 1/($M \cdot P_r$)).

**Convergence:** After sufficient iterations, $y(n) \approx v(n)$, so $e(n) \approx s(n)$, the clean ECG.

**Role of reference input (accelerometer):**
- Provides a signal **correlated with the motion artifact** but **uncorrelated with the cardiac ECG**.
- Allows the filter to learn the noise transfer function in real time.
- Without the reference, the filter cannot distinguish ECG from artifact (they share 0.5–40 Hz band).

**Why LMS for exercise ECG:**
- Computationally simple (O(M) per sample) → suitable for real-time embedded DSP.
- Self-adjusting → handles non-stationary noise during changing treadmill speed.
- RLS converges faster but is O(M²); reserved for high-end stress systems.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE (Analysis of Exercise ECG)**
>
> 1. **Measuring ST at J-point itself** instead of J+80 ms → examiner deducts 1 mark. Always state "**J+80 ms**" explicitly.
>
> 2. **Confusing 1 mm with 1 mV** in ST depression. Standard ECG calibration is **10 mm/mV**, so 1 mm = 0.1 mV = 100 µV. Many students write "ST = 1 mV depression" which is a **10× error**.
>
> 3. **Using 220 − age for HRmax without specifying "Fox formula"** → 0.5 mark deduction. Tanaka formula (208 − 0.7×age) is more accurate but Fox is the KTU-prescribed one.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "submaximal test" caveat** — A test stopped at 70% HRmax with no ST changes is *non-diagnostic*, not "negative". Examiners expect this nuance.
>
> 5. **Drawing block diagrams without arrows** → KTU valuators deduct 0.5–1 mark for "unclear flow". Always use Mermaid-style or arrowed boxes.
>
> 6. **Duke Score sign errors** — DTS can be negative; do not write "score = absolute value". State the sign explicitly.
>
> 7. **Skipping the recovery phase** in answers about exercise ECG. Recovery analysis (HRR₁, ST resolution) is **mandatory** in 7-mark answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Analysis of Exercise ECG**

### A. Core Definitions
- ☐ Exercise ECG = continuous ECG during graded physical stress to detect latent ischemia.
- ☐ Bruce protocol = 7 stages, 3 min each, speed 1.7–6.0 mph, grade 10–22%.
- ☐ 1 MET = 3.5 mL O₂/kg/min (resting metabolic rate).
- ☐ HRmax = 220 − age (Fox formula).
- ☐ Target HR for diagnostic test = 0.85 × HRmax.
- ☐ ST depression threshold = **≥ 1 mm** (0.1 mV) horizontal/downsloping at **J+80 ms**.

### B. Key Equations
- ☐ ST/HR Index = ΔST/ΔHR (positive if > 1.6 µV/bpm).
- ☐ Duke Treadmill Score = Time − 5×|ΔST| − 4×Angina Index.
- ☐ HRR₁ = HR_peak − HR_1min_recovery (abnormal if < 12 bpm).
- ☐ SNR_improvement = 10 log₁₀(N) for coherent averaging of N beats.
- ☐ Adaptive LMS: $w_k(n+1) = w_k(n) + 2\mu e(n) r(n-k)$.

### C. DSP Pipeline (Memorize Order)
- ☐ Acquire → Notch (50 Hz) → Baseline removal (median 200 ms) → Bandpass (0.5–40 Hz) → Pan-Tompkins QRS detect → J+80 ms ST extract → Threshold decision.

### D. Ischemic Cascade Order (High-Yield)
- ☐ Perfusion defect → Diastolic dysfunction → **ST changes (ECG)** → Systolic dysfunction → Angina.

### E. Test Termination Criteria
- ☐ ST depression ≥1 mm (diagnostic).
- ☐ ST elevation ≥1 mm in leads without Q-waves (danger — transmural ischemia).
- ☐ Achievement of 85% HRmax.
- ☐ Patient symptoms (angina, dyspnea, fatigue).
- ☐ Hemodynamic: SBP drop >20 mmHg, or SBP >250, DBP >115.
- ☐ Dangerous arrhythmias (sustained VT, VF, 2°/3° AV block).

### F. Signal Processing Challenges Unique to Exercise ECG
- ☐ Motion artifact (0.5–10 Hz, overlaps ECG).
- ☐ EMG contamination (10–40 Hz, broad spectrum).
- ☐ Baseline wander from hyperventilation.
- ☐ Electrode-skin impedance changes with sweat.
- ☐ Solution: Adaptive LMS + coherent averaging + robust electrode prep.

### G. Common Sub-Questions Pattern
- ☐ "Define Bruce protocol and tabulate stages" — 3 marks.
- ☐ "Explain DSP chain with block diagram" — 7 marks.
- ☐ "Calculate Duke Score" — 7 marks (numerical).
- ☐ "Explain adaptive filtering for motion artifact" — 7 marks.
- ☐ "Discuss ischemic cascade and ECG's role" — 7 marks.

### H. Quick Mnemonics
- **"J + 80"** → ST measured 80 ms **after** J-point.
- **"1 mm = 0.1 mV = 100 µV"** → ECG calibration mantra.
- **"220 − age"** → Fox HRmax.
- **"10 log N"** → SNR gain from averaging.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Analysis of Exercise ECG**
> These notes are aligned to **KTU 2024 Scheme, PBEBT504**, Module 4 outcomes. Students are advised to supplement these with lab observation of a Holter/stress test device (e.g., Philips ST80i, GE CASE) and to practice drawing the DSP block diagram from memory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Respiration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEBT504 - INTRODUCTION TO BIOMEDICAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Analysis of Respiration ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Respiration Signal Analysis** is the systematic acquisition, conditioning, and quantitative evaluation of the bioelectric, biomechanical, and biochemical signals generated by the human respiratory system, with the objective of extracting diagnostic parameters such as respiratory rate, tidal volume, airflow dynamics, and cycle morphology. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, the respiration signal is treated as a **quasi-periodic, low-frequency biomedical signal** (typically in the range of **0.1 Hz to 0.5 Hz**, equivalent to **6 to 30 breaths per minute** for a healthy adult at rest) that encodes both neural drive and mechanical pulmonary performance. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 4 (Respiration Analysis)** > The expected learning outcomes emphasize: (1) identification of sources and characteristics of respiratory signals, (2) measurement transducers (spirometer, pneumotachograph, thermistor, impedance pneumograph), (3) signal conditioning and noise removal, and (4) extraction of clinical features (rate, tidal volume, I:E ratio, apnea detection). ### 1.2 Physiological Origin of the Respiration Signal The respiratory rhythm is generated by a neural oscillator located in the **medulla oblongata** (pre-Bötzinger complex) and modulated by the **pons**. This neural signal travels via the phrenic nerve to the **diaphragm** and via intercostal nerves to the **intercostal muscles**, producing a mechanical displacement of the thoracic cavity. The resulting change in intra-thoracic pressure drives airflow, and the concomitant chest-wall movement modulates a wide class of measurable signals. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bellows Model > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture:** Imagine the human chest as a **two-chambered bellows pump** in a blacksmith's forge. When the handle (diaphragm) is pulled down, the chamber expands and air rushes in (**inspiration**); when released, the elastic recoil pushes air out (**expiration**). The "handle" is pulled by a clockwork mechanism (medullary oscillator) running at a slow, steady cadence. Our signal-processing job is to attach a **pressure gauge, a flow meter, and a position sensor** to this bellows, then analyze the recorded waveform to determine whether the clockwork is healthy, the bellows has leaks, or the valves are stiff. ### 1.4 Standard Clinical Metrics (Bold-Constants) - **Normal adult respiratory rate at rest:** **12 – 20 breaths/min** - **Tidal Volume (TV or V_T):** **≈ 500 mL** (volume of air inspired/expired in a single normal breath) - **Total Lung Capacity (TLC):** **≈ 6.0 L** in adult males - **Respiratory signal bandwidth:** **0.1 – 0.5 Hz** (dominant energy) - **I:E ratio (normal):** **1 : 2** (inspiration is half as long as expiration) - **Inspiration duration (T_i):** **≈ 1.5 s** ; Expiration duration (T_e): **≈ 3.0 s** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Single Respiratory Cycle A respiration cycle consists of four phases: 1. **Inspiratory phase (T_i):** Diaphragm contracts, thoracic volume increases, intrapulmonary pressure drops below atmospheric, air flows in. 2. **End-inspiratory pause (T_pi):** Brief plateau; lung recoil pressure equilibrates. 3. **Expiratory phase (T_e):** Passive recoil; intrapulmonary pressure exceeds atmospheric, air flows out. 4. **End-expiratory pause (T_pe):** Resting equilibrium; functional residual capacity (FRC) is reached. > **The total cycle time is** $T_{tot} = T_i + T_{pi} + T_e + T_{pe}$ ### 2.2 Why is the Respiration Signal Quasi-Periodic? Unlike the ECG which is highly periodic, respiration exhibits: - **Cycle-to-cycle variability** (breath-to-breath interval changes by 10–20%) - **Amplitude modulation** (depth of breath changes with activity, speech, sleep stage) - **Baseline wander** due to posture shifts and cardiac oscillations transmitted to the chest wall Hence, classical DSP techniques (windowed FFT, autocorrelation) are applied over **short overlapping windows** of 30–60 s rather than long continuous recordings. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Standard Value / Unit | |---|-----------|---------|------------------------| | 1 | Respiratory Frequency | $f_R = \frac{1}{T_{tot}}$ | 0.2 – 0.33 Hz | | 2 | Respiratory Rate (per min) | $RR = 60 \cdot f_R$ | 12 – 20 breaths/min | | 3 | Minute Ventilation | $\dot{V}_E = V_T \cdot f_R$ | 6 – 10 L/min | | 4 | Inspiratory Fraction (Duty Cycle) | $D = \frac{T_i}{T_{tot}}$ | ≈ 0.33 | | 5 | I:E Ratio | $\frac{T_i}{T_e}$ | 1 : 2 | | 6 | Alveolar Ventilation | $\dot{V}_A = (V_T - V_D) \cdot f_R$ | 4.2 – 5.6 L/min | | 7 | FEV1 / FVC Ratio | $R = \frac{FEV_1}{FVC} \times 100\%$ | ≥ 70% (normal); < 70% (obstructive) | | 8 | Apnea-Hypopnea Index | $AHI = \frac{N_{apnea}+N_{hypopnea}}{t_{sleep}(h)}$ | < 5 (normal); ≥ 30 (severe OSA) | | 9 | Mean Airway Pressure (approx.) | $\bar{P}_{aw} = 0.5 \cdot P_{peak} + 0.5 \cdot PEEP$ | cmH₂O | | 10 | Pressure-Volume (linearized) | $V = C \cdot \Delta P$ | C = compliance (mL/cmH₂O) | > [!NOTE] > In the KTU formula sheet, **absolute value** and **conditionals** have been written using the LaTeX `\vert` operator to keep the markdown table parsable. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Polysomnography (PSG):** Sleep labs detect obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) by analyzing the respiration airflow and SpO₂ signals. - **ICU Patient Monitoring:** Continuous capnography + impedance pneumography detects early respiratory depression. - **Wearable Fitness Devices:** Smart-watches estimate RR from PPG (photoplethysmogram) modulations (respiratory sinus arrhythmia). - **Mechanical Ventilator Design:** Engineers use the pressure–volume and flow–volume loops to tune PEEP, tidal volume, and I:E ratios. - **Speech Processing:** Respiration drives the air supply to the larynx; speech synthesis uses respiratory waveform envelopes for prosody generation. ### 2.5 Respiration Signal Transducers — Comparison | Transducer | Physical Principle | Output Signal | Bandwidth | Limitation | |------------|--------------------|---------------|-----------|------------| | Spirometer (water-bell) | Volume displacement | Volume vs. time | DC – 5 Hz | Bulky, non-portable | | Pneumotachograph | Pressure drop across laminar resistance | Flow → Volume | DC – 20 Hz | Sensitive to humidity | | Thermistor (nasal) | Temperature change of inspired air | Resistance | 0.05 – 2 Hz | Nasal-only detection | | Impedance Pneumograph | Thoracic electrical impedance (4-electrode) | Impedance (Ω) | 0.05 – 5 Hz | Motion artifacts | | Inductive Plethysmograph (RIP) | Coils around chest/abdomen | Self-inductance | 0.05 – 5 Hz | Calibration drift | | Capnograph (CO₂) | Infrared absorption | CO₂ partial pressure | 0 – 50 Hz | Sampling line clogging | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Airflow Signal to Minute Ventilation **Goal:** Derive the expression for minute ventilation starting from a measured airflow waveform $u(t)$ (in L/s). **Step 1 — Recognize airflow as the time-derivative of volume:** By definition, instantaneous airflow is the time-derivative of lung volume. The flow transducer measures the volumetric flow rate: $$ u(t) = \frac{dV(t)}{dt} $$ **Step 2 — Obtain tidal volume by integrating over one inspiration:** For the $k^{th}$ breath, the start of inspiration is $t_{i,k}$ and the end of inspiration (start of expiration) is $t_{e,k}$. Integrating the flow during the inspiratory phase yields the inspired tidal volume: $$ V_{T,k} = \int_{t_{i,k}}^{t_{e,k}} u(t) \, dt $$ **Step 3 — Compute the breath period:** The total time of one respiratory cycle is the interval between the start of two consecutive inspirations: $$ T_{tot,k} = t_{i,k+1} - t_{i,k} $$ **Step 4 — Derive instantaneous respiratory frequency:** $$ f_{R,k} = \frac{1}{T_{tot,k}} = \frac{1}{t_{i,k+1} - t_{i,k}} $$ **Step 5 — Compute minute ventilation over $N$ breaths:** Summing all tidal volumes and dividing by the cumulative recording time $t_{rec}$ gives the average minute ventilation: $$ \dot{V}_E = \frac{\sum_{k=1}^{N} V_{T,k}}{t_{rec}} \cdot 60 \quad \text{[L/min]} $$ **Step 6 — Equivalently in terms of breath count:** If all $N$ breaths have nearly equal tidal volume, this simplifies to the textbook formula: $$ \dot{V}_E = V_T \cdot f_R \cdot 60 = V_T \cdot RR $$ **Engineering Note:** In practice, the integration of $u(t)$ is performed digitally using the **trapezoidal rule**: $$ V_T \approx \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} \frac{u[n] + u[n+1]}{2} \cdot T_s $$ where $T_s$ is the sampling period (e.g., $T_s = 10$ ms) and $M$ is the number of samples spanning inspiration. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: The First-Order RC Lung Model (Linearized) The lung-thorax system is often approximated as a **first-order linear system** consisting of a compliance (capacitor) and airway + tissue resistance. This explains the exponential rise and fall of the spirometric flow trace. **Step 1 — Apply the linearized equation of motion for the respiratory system:** $$ P_{aw}(t) = R \cdot \dot{V}(t) + \frac{V(t)}{C} + PEEP $$ **Step 2 — Take the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions, ignoring PEEP):** $$ P_{aw}(s) = R \cdot s \cdot V(s) + \frac{V(s)}{C} $$ **Step 3 — Factor $V(s)$:** $$ P_{aw}(s) = V(s) \left( R \cdot s + \frac{1}{C} \right) $$ **Step 4 — Derive the transfer function $H(s) = \dfrac{V(s)}{P_{aw}(s)}$:** $$ H(s) = \frac{V(s)}{P_{aw}(s)} = \frac{1}{R \cdot s + \frac{1}{C}} = \frac{C}{R C s + 1} $$ **Step 5 — Identify the time constant:** $$ \tau = R \cdot C \quad \text{[seconds]} $$ The **time constant** $\tau$ dictates how quickly the lung fills/empties. In a healthy adult, $\tau \approx 0.5$ – $1.0$ s; in obstructive disease (high $R$, e.g., COPD), $\tau$ can exceed 2 s, indicating slow expiration. **Step 6 — Step response (constant $P_{aw}$):** $$ V(t) = C \cdot P_{aw} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right) $$ **Step 7 — Identify the asymptotic (steady-state) volume = compliance × pressure:** $$ V_\infty = C \cdot P_{aw} $$ This is the **static compliance**, an important clinical index of lung stiffness. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Clinical Pattern Recognition | Pattern | Waveform Morphology | Frequency | Clinical Significance | |---------|---------------------|-----------|------------------------| | Eupnea (normal) | Smooth sinusoidal, I:E = 1:2 | 12–20 br/min | Healthy | | Tachypnea | Rapid, shallow | > 20 br/min | Fever, anxiety, PE | | Bradypnea | Slow, deep | < 12 br/min | Drug depression, ↑ ICP | | Apnea | Flat-line airflow ≥ 10 s | — | OSA, central apnea | | Hypopnea | Reduced amplitude ≥ 50% ≥ 10 s | — | Partial obstruction | | Cheyne-Stokes | Crescendo-decrescendo + apnea | Cyclic | CHF, CNS lesion | | Kussmaul | Deep, rapid | > 20 br/min | Metabolic acidosis | | Biot's | Irregular unpredictable | Variable | Brainstem injury | | Paradoxical | Chest & abdomen move oppositely | — | Diaphragm paralysis / OSA | ## 4.
