KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the term "Tidal Volume" and state its typical value in a healthy adult at rest. Mention the KTU-relevant formula for minute ventilation.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Tidal volume ($V_T$) is the volume of air inspired or expired during a single normal (unforced) respiratory cycle. In a healthy adult at rest, the standard value is **$V_T \approx 500$ mL**. Minute ventilation ($\dot{V}_E$) is the volume of air exchanged per minute and is given by the product of tidal volume and respiratory rate: $\dot{V}_E = V_T \times RR$. A normal adult minute ventilation lies in the range **6 – 10 L/min**. (Award 1 mark each for definition, value, and formula.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any three respiration transducers and state the physical principle used by each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Pneumotachograph** – measures the pressure drop across a known laminar-flow resistive element (Fleisch-type) to estimate flow using Poiseuille's law. 2. **Impedance Pneumograph** – measures the change in transthoracic electrical impedance (typically at 50–100 kHz carrier) caused by the change in air volume in the chest. 3. **Thermistor / Thermocouple (nasal cannula)** – exploits the temperature difference between inspired (room-temperature) and expired (~33 °C core-tissue) air, producing a change in resistance. (1 mark per transducer-principle pair.) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) **Q3 (a). [KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 4] — 7 marks** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a pneumotachograph-based respiration measurement system. Derive the relationship between pressure drop and airflow.* **Model Answer (7 marks):** A **pneumotachograph** is a flow-measuring device that quantifies respiratory airflow by sensing the **pressure differential across a laminar-flow resistive element**. **Working (5 marks):** 1. The patient breathes through a cylindrical tube containing a fine mesh or parallel capillaries (Fleisch head). The resistive element ensures **laminar flow** so that the pressure-flow relationship is linear and tractable. 2. Two pressure tapings are placed upstream and downstream of the resistive element. A **differential pressure transducer** (e.g., a capacitance-type or piezoresistive sensor) measures $\Delta P$. 3. According to **Poiseuille's law** for laminar flow through a tube of resistance $K$: $$ \Delta P(t) = K \cdot \dot{V}(t) $$ where $\dot{V}(t)$ is the volumetric flow rate (L/s) and $K$ is the flow resistance of the head (cmH₂O·s/L). 4. The transducer output (a small voltage) is amplified by an **instrumentation amplifier** (gain ≈ 100–1000), low-pass filtered (cut-off ≈ 20 Hz) to remove cardiac and high-frequency noise, and then digitized by an **ADC** at 200–500 Hz sampling rate. 5. In the digital domain, the flow signal is **integrated** to obtain the volume waveform: $$ V(t) = \int_0^t \dot{V}(\tau) \, d\tau $$ 6. The volume trace is displayed as a **spirogram**; from its peaks and troughs, respiratory rate, tidal volume, inspiratory and expiratory times are computed. **Derivation (2 marks):** For fully developed laminar flow in a circular tube, the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation** states: $$ \Delta P = \frac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^4} \cdot \dot{V} $$ Identifying $K = \dfrac{8 \mu L}{\pi r^4}$ yields $\Delta P = K \cdot \dot{V}$, hence: $$ \dot{V} = \frac{1}{K} \cdot \Delta P $$ The pneumotachograph thus provides a **linear, real-time measurement of airflow** that is independent of the absolute pressure or gas composition, as long as viscosity and temperature remain constant. --- **Q3 (b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 4] — 7 marks** *Explain the principle of operation of an impedance pneumograph. Derive the relationship between change in thoracic impedance and the change in lung volume.* **Model Answer (7 marks):** The **impedance pneumograph** measures respiration by detecting the change in electrical impedance across the thorax caused by the variation in intra-thoracic air volume. **Principle (3 marks):** 1. A high-frequency (typically 50 – 100 kHz), low-amplitude (≈ 1 mA rms) alternating current is injected between two outer (current) electrodes placed on the chest. 2. Two inner (voltage-sensing) electrodes pick up the differential voltage developed across the thorax. 3. The thorax behaves as a **non-homogeneous volume conductor** with two principal contributors: (i) the **tissue** (essentially constant impedance) and (ii) the **air-filled lungs** (high impedance, varying with volume). 4. As the lungs fill with air during inspiration, the overall thoracic impedance rises (because air is a poor conductor); during expiration, the impedance falls. **Mathematical Derivation (4 marks):** Treat the thorax as a **cylinder** of length $L$ (anterior–posterior) and effective cross-sectional area $A$ filled with a conducting medium of resistivity $\rho$: $$ Z = \frac{\rho L}{A} $$ When inspiration introduces a volume $\Delta V$ of air, the effective cross-sectional area available for current conduction changes. The volume of air added within the cylindrical model is: $$ \Delta V = A \cdot \Delta L_{\text{eff}} \;\;\text{(or equivalently a fractional change in }A\text{)} $$ For small respiratory excursions, the **fractional change in impedance** is approximately equal to the **fractional change in lung volume**: $$ \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_0} \approx -\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} $$ A more refined model treats the lungs as a sphere of volume $V$ embedded in tissue. The impedance of a sphere is: $$ Z = \frac{\rho}{2\pi r} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi \left( \frac{3V}{4\pi} \right)^{1/3}} $$ Differentiating with respect to volume $V$: $$ \frac{dZ}{dV} = -\frac{\rho}{6\pi r^4} = -\frac{Z}{3V} $$ Hence the **sensitivity** of the impedance pneumograph to volume change is: $$ S = \left\vert \frac{dZ}{dV} \right\vert = \frac{Z}{3V} \;\; \text{[Ω/mL]} $$ For a typical baseline of $Z \approx 30$ Ω and $V \approx 600$ mL: $$ S \approx \frac{30}{3 \times 600} \approx 0.0167 \;\; \text{Ω/mL} $$ The output is band-pass filtered (0.05 – 5 Hz), and the resulting impedance-vs-time waveform is calibrated against a spirometer to obtain absolute tidal volume in clinical use. --- ### 5.3 Alternative Question Set (Internal Choice Pair) **Q4 (A). [KTU University Exam – May 2023] — 14 marks** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** *Compare the working principles of the following respiration transducers, with one advantage and one limitation of each:* *(i) Spirometer (water-bell type)* *(ii) Pneumotachograph* *(iii) Inductive plethysmograph (RIP)* *(iv) Capnograph* **Model Answer (7 marks):** | Transducer | Principle | Advantage | Limitation | |------------|-----------|-----------|------------| | Water-bell spirometer | Volume of displaced water in a bell-jar (or wedge) coupled to a pen recorder | Direct volume readout, high accuracy (~±3%) | Bulky, slow response, cannot measure fast flow transients | | Pneumotachograph | Poiseuille pressure drop across laminar resistance | Linear, real-time flow; bidirectional | Sensitive to condensation, gas viscosity | | Inductive Plethysmograph | Change in self-inductance of coils around chest and abdomen (cross-sectional area change) | Non-invasive, ambulatory, two-compartment (chest+abdomen) calibration possible | Calibration drift with posture; signal cross-talk | | Capnograph | Infrared absorption of CO₂ (Lambert–Beer law: $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot \ell$) | Confirms alveolar ventilation; detects rebreathing | Side-stream lag; water-trap failure; expensive | **Part (b) — 7 marks:** *A subject's respiration is recorded for 1 minute. The following peak-valley amplitudes (arbitrary units) of the airflow signal are obtained, where each row represents one breath:* | Breath | Amplitude (a.u.) | Period (s) | |--------|------------------|------------| | 1 | 480 | 4.2 | | 2 | 510 | 4.0 | | 3 | 460 | 4.5 | | 4 | 500 | 4.1 | | 5 | 495 | 4.2 | | 6 | 470 | 4.4 | | 7 | 485 | 4.3 | | 8 | 510 | 4.0 | | 9 | 490 | 4.2 | | 10 | 500 | 4.1 | *Assuming the flow-to-volume calibration constant is **K = 0.50 mL/(a.u.·s)** (i.e., Volume change = K × Amplitude), calculate: (i) mean tidal volume, (ii) mean respiratory rate, (iii) minute ventilation.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Tidal volume per breath:** $$ V_{T,k} = K \times A_k $$ For example, breath 1: $V_{T,1} = 0.50 \times 480 = 240$ mL. Similarly: | Breath | $V_{T,k}$ (mL) | |--------|------------------| | 1 | 240 | | 2 | 255 | | 3 | 230 | | 4 | 250 | | 5 | 247.5 | | 6 | 235 | | 7 | 242.5 | | 8 | 255 | | 9 | 245 | | 10 | 250 | **Step 2 — Mean tidal volume:** $$ \overline{V_T} = \frac{1}{10} \sum_{k=1}^{10} V_{T,k} = \frac{2450}{10} = 245 \;\; \text{mL} $$ **Step 3 — Mean breath period and respiratory rate:** $$ \overline{T}_{tot} = \frac{4.2 + 4.0 + 4.5 + 4.1 + 4.2 + 4.4 + 4.3 + 4.0 + 4.2 + 4.1}{10} = \frac{42.0}{10} = 4.2 \;\; \text{s} $$ $$ \overline{RR} = \frac{60}{4.2} \approx 14.29 \;\; \text{breaths/min} $$ **Step 4 — Minute ventilation:** $$ \dot{V}_E = \overline{V_T} \times \overline{RR} = 245 \;\; \text{mL} \times 14.29 \;\; \text{min}^{-1} = 3501 \;\; \text{mL/min} \approx 3.50 \;\; \text{L/min} $$ This is **below the normal range (6 – 10 L/min)**, suggesting **hypoventilation** (could be post-exercise rest, sedation, or restrictive disease) — a clinically significant finding for a 7-mark analytical question. --- **Q4 (B). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] — 14 marks (Alternative)** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** *With a first-order RC model of the lung-thorax system, derive the transfer function $H(s) = V(s)/P_{aw}(s)$. Comment on the significance of the time constant $\tau = RC$.* **Model Answer (7 marks):** (See the derivation in **Section 3.2** above. Award marks for: equation of motion (2), Laplace transform (2), final transfer function (1), significance of $\tau$ (2).) **Significance of $\tau$:** $\tau$ quantifies the **dynamic response speed** of the respiratory system. A **small $\tau$** implies stiff, healthy lungs (rapid filling/emptying). A **large $\tau$** (e.g., in COPD) implies high airway resistance or high compliance, causing prolonged expiration. Clinically, the expiratory time constant is used to set the **optimal PEEP** and **respiratory rate** in mechanical ventilation to avoid intrinsic PEEP (auto-PEEP). **Part (b) — 7 marks:** *A patient on a mechanical ventilator has airway resistance $R = 8$ cmH₂O·s/L and total compliance $C = 50$ mL/cmH₂O. The ventilator delivers a constant inspiratory pressure of 15 cmH₂O above PEEP for 1.2 seconds.* *Calculate: (i) the time constant $\tau$, (ii) the inspired tidal volume, (iii) the residual volume that would remain at 1.2 s if inspiration were sustained, and (iv) the steady-state volume.* **Model Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Time constant:** $$ \tau = R \cdot C = 8 \;\; \frac{\text{cmH}_2\text{O}\cdot\text{s}}{\text{L}} \times 50 \;\; \frac{\text{mL}}{\text{cmH}_2\text{O}} = 400 \;\; \text{mL·s/L} = 0.4 \;\; \text{s} $$ (Convert units: $C = 50$ mL/cmH₂O = 0.050 L/cmH₂O, so $\tau = 8 \times 0.050 = 0.4$ s.) **Step 2 — Inspired tidal volume at $t = 1.2$ s:** Using the step-response equation: $$ V(t) = C \cdot P_{aw} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right) $$ $$ V(1.2) = 0.050 \;\; \text{L/cmH}_2\text{O} \times 15 \;\; \text{cmH}_2\text{O} \times \left( 1 - e^{-1.2/0.4} \right) $$ $$ = 0.75 \;\; \text{L} \times \left( 1 - e^{-3.0} \right) $$ $$ = 0.75 \times (1 - 0.0498) = 0.75 \times 0.9502 = 0.7127 \;\; \text{L} \approx 712.7 \;\; \text{mL} $$ **Step 3 — Steady-state volume:** $$ V_\infty = C \cdot P_{aw} = 0.050 \times 15 = 0.75 \;\; \text{L} = 750 \;\; \text{mL} $$ **Step 4 — Residual volume to be inspired beyond 1.2 s (i.e., how much "more" could the lung hold if we waited infinitely):** $$ \Delta V = V_\infty - V(1.2) = 750 - 712.7 = 37.3 \;\; \text{mL} $$ **Clinical interpretation:** At $t = 3\tau$ (which is $3 \times 0.4 = 1.2$ s), the lung has already reached ~95% of its final volume. Hence **1.2 s of inspiratory time is sufficient** for this patient; extending the inspiratory phase further would provide only marginal volume gain while potentially worsening hemodynamic compromise. --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Respiration Analysis Questions:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Students frequently mix up **mL and L** when computing minute ventilation. KTU evaluators deduct 0.5–1 mark for failing to state units explicitly. > 2. **Confusing flow with volume:** The pneumotachograph measures **flow** ($\dot{V}$), not volume. Volume requires integration over the breath interval. > 3. **Forgetting the factor of 60:** Respiratory rate is in breaths/min; frequency from period is in Hz (cycles/s). Always multiply by 60. > 4. **Mixing T_i and T_e:** In the I:E ratio, students sometimes invert the ratio. Remember I:E = inspiration : expiration = 1 : 2 normally. > 5. **Ignoring linearization in the RC model:** The lung is intrinsically non-linear (sigmoid pressure-volume curve). The first-order RC is valid only for small excursions around the operating point; explicitly state this assumption. > 6. **Apnea definition:** KTU expects the clinical definition: **cessation of airflow for ≥ 10 seconds** (American Academy of Sleep Medicine standard). Don't write "no breathing" without the duration threshold. > 7. **Incomplete calibration discussion:** When asked about transducer calibration, mention that the **impedance pneumograph is uncalibrated by default**; absolute volume requires a parallel spirometer reference (qualitative vs. quantitative use). --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🔹 **Respiration signal** is a low-frequency quasi-periodic waveform (0.1 – 0.5 Hz) reflecting the thoracic pump cycle. - 🔹 **Tidal volume** ≈ 500 mL; **Respiratory rate** ≈ 12 – 20 /min; **Minute ventilation** ≈ 6 – 10 L/min in healthy adult at rest. - 🔹 **I : E ratio** normally = **1 : 2**; **Duty cycle** $D = T_i/T_{tot} \approx 0.33$. - 🔹 **Four major transducer families:** Spirometer (volume-direct), Pneumotachograph (flow-via-Poiseuille), Impedance Pneumograph (volume-via-impedance), Capnograph (CO₂-via-IR absorption). - 🔹 **Pneumotachograph** uses the **Poiseuille law**: $\Delta P = K \dot{V}$, where $K = 8\mu L/(\pi r^4)$. - 🔹 **Impedance pneumograph** sensitivity: $S = Z/(3V) \approx 0.016$ Ω/mL. - 🔹 **First-order RC model:** $H(s) = C/(RCs+1)$, time constant $\tau = RC$. - 🔹 **Step response:** $V(t) = C P_{aw} (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$; at $t = 3\tau$ volume is **95%** of final. - 🔹 **Apnea** = airflow cessation ≥ **10 s**; **Hypopnea** = amplitude reduction ≥ 50% for ≥ 10 s. - 🔹 **AHI** = (apneas + hypopneas) per hour of sleep; < 5 normal, 5–15 mild, 15–30 moderate, ≥ 30 severe OSA. - 🔹 **Clinical patterns** to recognize: Eupnea, Tachypnea, Bradypnea, Apnea, Cheyne-Stokes, Kussmaul, Biot's, Paradoxical. - 🔹 **Key parameters extracted in DSP pipeline:** RR, TV, $\dot{V}_E$, I:E, AHI, spectral centroid, autocorrelation peak lag. - 🔹 **Sampling consideration:** Respiration must be sampled at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ ≥ 10 Hz; **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** with cut-off below $f_s/2$ is mandatory. - 🔹 **Real-world relevance:** PSG, ICU monitoring, wearable PPG-derived RR estimation, ventilator loop tuning, speech prosody synthesis. - 🔹 **Engineering formulas to memorize for KTU 2024 ESE:** - $\dot{V}_E = V_T \cdot f_R \cdot 60$ - $D = T_i/T_{tot}$ - $\tau = R \cdot C$ - $AHI = (N_a + N_h)/t_{sleep}$ - $V(t) = V_\infty(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ - $\Delta P = K \dot{V}$ (Poiseuille) > ✅ **End of Module 4 — Analysis of Respiration — KTU 2024 Premium Study Notes**
